- [C-056] **[P2]** Agentic AI autonomous playtesting pipeline — RL+LLM-powered agents now replacing manual QA for game testing (2026 industry shift). Agents reason through game states to uncover exploits. Test: evaluate integration of agentic AI testing (e.g., Modl.ai) with existing GameZipper verify-lite pipeline for automated regression. Source: https://thecodersblog.com/agentic-ai-for-game-playtesting-2026

### Accessibility — WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (Game Overlays)
- [A-016] **[P1]** WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (Minimum) — game overlays (modals, popups, ads, splash screens) must not completely hide the element with keyboard focus. US Title II enforcement April 2026. Test: Tab through all interactive elements in each game; verify no overlay completely covers the focused element. Source: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/focus-not-obscured-minimum.html

### Game — HTML5 Canvas Automated Visual Bug Detection
- [G-098] **[P2]** HTML5 Canvas automated visual bug detection — academic approach (arXiv 2208.02335) achieves 100% accuracy detecting visual bugs in canvas games via automated DOM+pixel analysis, vs 44.6% for traditional snapshot testing. Test: evaluate canvas visual regression approach for GameZipper's Top 30 games, comparing pixel-diff accuracy with existing verify-lite screenshot diff. Source: https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.02335


### New in v1.34.0 (2026-06-11 R133 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

- [B-061] **[P0] iOS Safari WebGL completely broken on iOS 18.7.2 — all WebGL games fail** — A critical iOS Safari regression in iOS 18.7.2 causes all WebGL content across the internet to fail to render. WebGL contexts can be obtained but immediately become invalid. Affects Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Opera on that iOS version (all use same WebKit engine). This is a WebKit bug (WebKit bug 301800) confirmed by multiple sources including Google model-viewer team. For GameZipper: (1) test all WebGL canvas games on iOS 18.7.2 Safari — expect complete rendering failure, (2) implement WebGL context validity check after creation: if context.getContextAttributes().failIfMajorPerformanceCaveat returns false or getContext('webgl') returns null, display fallback message, (3) add canvas.getContext('2d') fallback for games that can use 2D context, (4) monitor iOS Safari version in navigator.userAgent to detect affected versions, (5) prepare user-facing message: "Your iOS version has a known Safari bug. Please update iOS or use Chrome on this device." Source: https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=301800 + https://github.com/google/model-viewer/issues/5100

- [S-068] **[P1] Chrome integer overflow sandbox escape CVE-2026-11659 — Linux game page risk** — Chrome < 149.0.7827.103 has an integer overflow in UI that allows a remote attacker to perform sandbox escape via a crafted HTML page on Linux. ChromeSeverity: High. Game portals loading third-party content (ads, analytics, social widgets) on Linux are at risk because a compromised renderer can escape the sandbox. For GameZipper: (1) verify all game pages function correctly on Chrome 149.0.7827.103+ on Linux, (2) test that monetag-manager.js ad iframes do not expose sandbox escape surface on Linux, (3) confirm CSP headers restrict iframe capabilities to minimum necessary, (4) validate that shared JS (game-footer.js, gz-analytics.js) does not load from untrusted origins that could deliver crafted HTML, (5) test on Ubuntu/Fedora with Chrome for Linux specific rendering. Source: https://cvefeed.io/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-11659

- [PC-013] **[P1] Android Advanced Protection Mode disabling WebGPU — game compatibility regression** — Google is testing an option within Android's Advanced Protection Mode to disable the WebGPU API in Chrome to prevent security exploits. WebGPU is susceptible to vulnerabilities that can lead to remote attacks (multiple 2026 CVEs). When enabled, this feature will break all games that rely exclusively on WebGPU rendering without WebGL fallback. For GameZipper: (1) audit all games for WebGPU-only rendering paths — any game using WebGPU must have WebGL2 or Canvas 2D fallback, (2) test Phaser.js AUTO rendering mode correctly falls back to Canvas2D when WebGPU is unavailable, (3) verify Three.js WebGLRenderer fallback works when WebGPU is disabled, (4) check that WebGPU feature detection (navigator.gpu) returns null gracefully, (5) test on Android 16+ with Advanced Protection Mode enabled, (6) add user-facing messaging if WebGPU is required: "This game requires WebGPU which is disabled by your security settings." Source: https://androidauthority.com/android-advanced-protection-mode-disable-chrome-webgpu-apk-teardown-3647502

- [C-065] **[P1] iOS Safari fullscreen fixed-position canvas rendering lag — multi-scene WebGL stutter** — iOS Safari shows canvases lagging behind and stuttering when rendering multiple WebGL scenes with one context using a fullscreen fixed-position canvas with scissor regions. Works correctly on desktop browsers. Games using a single fullscreen canvas with viewport/scissor partitioning for multiple UI panels are affected. For GameZipper: (1) test any games using fullscreen fixed-position canvas with multiple draw regions on iOS Safari, (2) verify puzzle games with canvas-rendered sidebar + main area do not stutter on iOS, (3) measure frame time consistency on iPad Safari vs desktop Chrome, (4) test that canvas compositing (position:fixed + z-index) does not cause layer rendering issues, (5) workaround: consider using separate canvas elements instead of scissor regions on iOS. Source: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/79674032/problem-rendering-fullscreen-fixed-canvas-in-ios-safari

- [A-021] **[P1] WCAG 2.2 enforceable ISO standard — 9 new success criteria compliance for game pages** — WCAG 2.2 is now an ISO standard (ISO/IEC 40500:2022) and the enforceable accessibility standard in 2026, supersedes WCAG 2.1. EU Accessibility Act (EAA) enforcement begins June 28, 2025 with 2026 grace period. Adds 9 new success criteria: Focus Appearance (2.4.11), Focus Not Obscured (2.4.12), Dragging Movements (2.5.7), Target Size Minimum 24×24px (2.5.8), Consistent Help (3.2.6), Consistent Identification (3.2.7), Redundant Entry (3.3.7), Accessible Authentication (3.3.8), Accessible Authentication No Exception (3.3.9). For GameZipper: (1) audit all game button targets for 24×24px minimum (WCAG 2.5.8), (2) verify keyboard focus indicator is visible and 2px+ border (2.4.11), (3) test that focused elements are not hidden behind sticky topnav or game overlays (2.4.12), (4) verify drag-based games (color-sort, solitaire) have non-drag alternatives (2.5.7), (5) ensure game settings/help access is consistent across all game pages (3.2.6), (6) test with axe DevTools automated scan for WCAG 2.2 AA compliance. Source: https://z-ax.com/en/blog/web-accessibility-wcag-complete-guide-2026 + https://thewcag.com/testing-guide

- [B-062] **[P1] iOS Safari WebGL context immediately lost on creation — shader compilation failure** — Users report "Failed to create 35633 shader" (fragment shader) with contextLost: true immediately after WebGL context creation on iOS Safari. Happens 100% of the time for affected users on specific iOS versions. The WebGL context is obtained successfully but immediately fires webglcontextlost event, making the game entirely non-functional. This is distinct from B-061 (iOS 18.7.2 complete break) — this affects other iOS versions sporadically. For GameZipper: (1) add webglcontextlost event handler to all WebGL games with user-facing recovery message, (2) implement automatic context restoration attempt via webglcontextrestored event, (3) test that games gracefully degrade to Canvas 2D when WebGL context is lost, (4) add retry logic: attempt WebGL context creation up to 3 times before falling back, (5) log WebGL context loss events to analytics for iOS version tracking, (6) verify shader source code does not exceed iOS Safari limits (varying/attribute count, instruction count). Source: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/79847768/webgl-context-immediately-lost-on-ios

- [C-066] **[P2] Hammer.js tap event crash on iPad Safari — gesture library compatibility** — iPad games using Hammer.js for gesture/tap handling can crash Mobile Safari under certain conditions. Hammer.js is a popular touch gesture library used by many HTML5 game frameworks. For GameZipper: (1) identify all games using Hammer.js (grep for hammer.min.js or Hammer constructor), (2) test identified games on iPad Safari (latest iOS) for crash during rapid tapping, (3) verify Hammer.js version is 2.0.0+ (older versions have more iOS Safari issues), (4) test that touch event handlers use {passive: true} where applicable, (5) consider migrating to native PointerEvent API (touchstart/touchmove/touchend) for simpler gesture handling, (6) verify that crash does not corrupt game state — if Safari tab crashes, localStorage persistence should survive. Source: https://xjavascript.com/blog/mobile-safari-crashes-when-using-hammer-js

- [P-040] **[P2] Offscreen canvas caching 60fps mobile optimization — cross-device rendering benchmark** — Achieving consistent 60fps on mobile requires combining offscreen canvas caching with hardware-accelerated batch rendering. Mobile Safari has significantly different performance characteristics than Chrome/Edge: GPU driver differences, Metal vs Vulkan, and thermal throttling patterns vary. For GameZipper: (1) profile Top 10 canvas games on mobile Safari vs Chrome for frame rate consistency, (2) test that games using offscreen canvas (OffscreenCanvas API) get measurable FPS improvement on mobile, (3) verify that canvas batch rendering (multiple draw calls merged) reduces jank on mid-range Android devices, (4) benchmark: target 55+ fps average (allow 8% frame drops) on iPhone 12 / Pixel 7 class devices, (5) test that canvas resize operations do not cause frame drops, (6) verify ImageBitmap caching for static game assets improves render time by >20%. Source: https://docs.bswen.com/blog/2026-02-21-canvas-performance-optimization

### New in v1.35.0 (2026-06-11 R135 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

- [S-069] **[P1] HTTP/2 Rapid Reset DoS CVE-2026-49160 — CDN/game page availability risk** — A critical HTTP/2 protocol vulnerability (CVE-2026-49160, discovered by OpenAI Codex) enables Denial of Service attacks against web servers including Microsoft IIS, Cloudflare, and CDN edge nodes. Attackers can rapidly reset HTTP/2 streams, overwhelming server connection pools. This directly impacts CDN-served game pages during traffic spikes (e.g., when a game goes viral). For GameZipper: (1) verify Cloudflare CDN configuration mitigates HTTP/2 Rapid Reset attacks (Cloudflare has deployed automatic mitigation), (2) test game page load resilience under simulated connection reset conditions, (3) confirm that concurrent game page loads (10+ tabs) do not trigger server-side rate limiting that would block legitimate users, (4) verify fallback to HTTP/1.1 works if CDN disables HTTP/2 during attack mitigation, (5) monitor Cloudflare dashboard for HTTP/2 error rate spikes during peak traffic. Source: https://cybersixt.com/incidents/microsoft-patches-record-206-flaws-fixing-three-zero-day-bugs-6ed89d96 + https://blog.qualys.com/vulnerabilities-threat-research/2026/06/09/microsoft-and-adobe-patch-tuesday-june-2026-security-update-review

- [B-063] **[P1] Microsoft June 2026 record-breaking 206 CVEs — 6 zero-days browser regression** — Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday set a record with 206 vulnerabilities patched (33 Critical, 167 Important), including 6 zero-days (3 publicly disclosed, 1 actively exploited). Impacts Edge WebView, Windows rendering engine, and Windows HTTP stack used by game pages on Windows. For GameZipper: (1) verify Top 30 games on fully-patched Windows 11 + Edge 149+ have zero regression, (2) test that Edge WebView (used by some game distribution platforms) renders games correctly post-patch, (3) verify Windows HTTP stack changes do not affect game asset loading (images, audio, JS bundles), (4) test that Windows Defender SmartScreen does not flag game pages as suspicious after URL handling changes, (5) run full verify-lite on Windows/Edge after OS update to catch any rendering regression. Source: https://bleepingcomputer.com/news/microsoft/microsoft-june-2026-patch-tuesday-fixes-6-zero-days-200-flaws + https://rapid7.com/blog/post/em-patch-tuesday-june-2026

- [C-067] **[P2] Safari 27 AI tab auto-organize — game tab visibility deprioritization** — Safari 27 introduces Apple Intelligence-powered tab auto-organization that groups and prioritizes browser tabs based on AI-determined relevance. Game tabs may be deprioritized, moved to background tab groups, or have their visual preview suppressed. This could reduce user engagement with game tabs that are perceived as "low priority" by the AI. For GameZipper: (1) test that game pages have proper page title and meta description for AI tab classification, (2) verify that game favicon and og:image are set correctly so Safari AI shows proper tab preview, (3) test that active gameplay (audio playing, rAF running) prevents tab from being deprioritized, (4) verify that Web Notifications API can be used to re-engage users whose game tab was deprioritized, (5) add `<meta name="description">` with engaging text to improve AI tab classification. Source: https://piunikaweb.com/2026/06/09/safari-27-update-everything-you-need-to-know + https://windowslatest.com/2026/06/11/apple-just-copied-one-of-microsoft-edges-best-features-and-called-it-apple-intelligence

- [G-099] **[P1] Zero-download instant play UX — first-play benchmark 2026** — 2026 casual browser gaming research shows the dominant UX trend is "zero-download instant play" — the game that wins is "the one that is already open" (ThinkComputers 2026). Players value immediacy over download-based alternatives. JayIsGames reports the web-game layer is "back in a durable shape" with browser games reducing friction to play being the #1 competitive advantage. Hyper-casual UI must be "radically simple and almost invisible" with single-intuitive-mechanic focus. For GameZipper: (1) measure time-to-first-interaction for all games — target <2 seconds from page load to playable state, (2) verify no mandatory tutorial/walkthrough blocks first play (optional tutorials only), (3) test that splash screens dismiss within 3 seconds or on first tap, (4) audit all games for single-mechanic clarity — user should understand the core mechanic within 5 seconds, (5) verify zero form/sign-up/login barriers between page load and first play, (6) benchmark: compare GameZipper first-play time against competitor sites (CrazyGames, Poki). Source: https://thinkcomputers.org/the-browser-based-revolution-why-casual-gaming-is-shedding-the-download + https://jayisgames.com/review/casual-browser-and-puzzle-games-in-2026-how-the-web-game-layer-g.php + https://appnality.com/blog/guide-to-mobile-game-ui-ux-design

- [W-130] **[P2] Multi-platform game distribution — YouTube/Facebook/Discord iframe embed testing** — HTML5/WASM game distribution is expanding beyond traditional web portals to YouTube, Facebook, Discord, and Netflix game channels (CoolGames 2026 hiring for UI/UX Designer targeting these platforms). Games embedded as iframes in social media platforms face unique challenges: restricted CSP policies, limited API access, different viewport constraints, and platform-specific user interaction patterns. For GameZipper: (1) test that game pages render correctly when embedded as iframe on Facebook (check X-Frame-Options and CSP frame-ancestors headers), (2) verify Discord Activity SDK compatibility for games that could be played in Discord voice channels, (3) test YouTube Gaming iframe embed — verify game canvas receives focus and input correctly, (4) verify games work with `window.top !== window.self` context (iframe detection), (5) check that game assets load correctly when page origin differs from game origin (CORS), (6) test that localStorage persistence works in iframe context (third-party cookie restrictions). Source: https://coolgames.com/2026/04/28/ui-ux-designer-casual-games-puzzles-remote-or-on-site-fulltime + https://zco.com/blog/mobile-game-development-guide-2026

- [P-041] **[P2] WebGPU main-thread unblocking — UI responsiveness during heavy GPU rendering** — WebGPU's architecture detaches animation logic from the JavaScript runtime, keeping the UI thread responsive during heavy GPU operations. Unlike WebGL where complex render pipelines can block the main thread (causing UI freezes, unresponsive buttons, janky scroll), WebGPU allows GPU work to proceed independently. Research shows WebGPU drains less power due to lower CPU overhead — the GPU works more efficiently and the device enters low-power states sooner. For GameZipper: (1) profile Top 10 canvas-heavy games for main-thread blocking during rendering — measure time from input event to visual response, (2) test that game UI buttons (mute, settings, pause) respond within 100ms even during heavy canvas rendering, (3) verify no "page unresponsive" browser warnings during 5+ minute gameplay sessions, (4) measure CPU usage difference between WebGL and WebGPU paths for equivalent rendering workloads, (5) test that browser tab switching is instant (no 500ms+ freeze) when game is running. Source: https://dailydevpost.com/blog/webgpu-vs-webgl-performance-guide + https://gjgalante.medium.com/webgl-vs-webgpu-the-performance-gap-fbd121fb221a + https://cybermaxia.com/en/blog/webgpu-vs-webgl-browser-2026-render-game-konsol

- [S-070] **[P2] Web game supply-chain security — framework CDN tampering detection** — HTML5 game frameworks (Phaser, PixiJS, Three.js) are commonly loaded from CDNs. Supply-chain attacks on CDN-hosted JavaScript (similar to Canvas LMS ShinyHunters ransomware attack affecting 2.75M users) can inject malicious code into game pages without any change to the game source. AI-generated fake vulnerability reports are also flooding bounty programs, making it harder to distinguish real threats. For GameZipper: (1) implement Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes for all CDN-loaded framework scripts (`<script src="..." integrity="sha384-..." crossorigin="anonymous">`), (2) verify all external script loads use HTTPS with certificate pinning where possible, (3) audit all games for CDN script references lacking SRI attributes, (4) test that game pages fail safely (show error message, not blank page) when CDN script integrity check fails, (5) monitor CDN provider security advisories for framework libraries used by GameZipper games, (6) establish incident response plan for CDN compromise: ability to switch to self-hosted framework copies within 1 hour. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_53769fd53ba77252 + https://googleprojectzero.blogspot.com (VRP restructuring May 2026)

---

## v1.44.0 Additions (2026-06-13 — R153 Dynamic Test Intelligence cron)

### Security

- [S-087] **[P0] COOP/COEP SharedArrayBuffer Cross-Origin Isolation — WASM Thread Game Failure Detection** — BugNet (Godot HTML5 export guide 2026) + Cinevva (COOP/COEP tutorial) + Reintech (WebAssembly browser support 2026 guide) confirm that SharedArrayBuffer (required for WASM threads in Godot 4.x and Unity WebGL exports) still mandates Cross-Origin Opener Policy (COOP) and Cross-Origin Embedder Policy (COEP) headers across ALL major browsers in 2026 — no browser has relaxed this requirement. Games that rely on WASM threads silently fail if `self.crossOriginIsolated` is false: `SharedArrayBuffer is not defined` error appears but the game may partially load, showing a broken state rather than a clear error message. itch.io added a checkbox for automatic header support; Netlify, Vercel, and nginx all require manual configuration. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) GameZipper is hosted on Vercel + Cloudflare — COOP/COEP headers must be explicitly configured in `vercel.json` or `_headers`; (b) any game exported from Godot 4.x or Unity 6 targeting WebAssembly threads will fail silently on GameZipper unless headers are present; (c) the failure mode is particularly insidious — the game loads but core physics/AI/multiplayer features break because WASM threads are disabled. **Test**: (1) add an automated header detection test to R0 scan — `curl -sI https://gamezipper.com/ | grep -i 'cross-origin'` must return both `cross-origin-opener-policy` and `cross-origin-embedder-policy` headers; (2) for each game that uses `SharedArrayBuffer` or `Atomics`, verify `self.crossOriginIsolated === true` at runtime — add a detection snippet: `if(typeof SharedArrayBuffer==='undefined'){document.body.insertAdjacentHTML('afterbegin','<div style="background:#ff0;padding:8px">This game requires cross-origin isolation. Please report this issue.</div>')}`; (3) test on all hosting configurations: Vercel default (no COOP/COEP), Vercel with `_headers`, Cloudflare proxy; (4) verify that adding COOP/COEP headers does NOT break existing ad iframes (Monetag, AdSense) — some ad networks fail when embedded in cross-origin-isolated contexts. Source: https://bugnet.io/blog/fix-godot-export-html5-cross-origin-isolation-fail + https://app.cinevva.com/tutorials/coop-coep-sharedarraybuffer.html + https://reintech.io/blog/webassembly-browser-support-2026-compatibility-guide

### Browser-Specific

- [B-073] **[P1] Chrome 149.0.7827.114 Additional 74-CVE Patch Batch — Post-Mega-Patch Regression** — SecurityWeek (June 9 2026) confirms Chrome 149.0.7827.102/103 patched an additional 74 CVEs (17 Critical + 53 High + 2 Medium) on top of the initial 429 CVEs at Chrome 149 launch. This makes Chrome 149 the single largest security update in Chrome history with 503+ total CVE fixes across the release cycle. The additional patch batch includes 2 zero-days exploited in the wild (CVE-2026-11645 V8 OOB, CVE-2026-11672 GPU OOB write). The $55,000 bounty for CVE-2026-11645 indicates active exploitation targeting the V8 JIT engine. **Test**: (1) run full Top 30 game smoke test on Chrome 149.0.7827.114 specifically (not just "Chrome 149") — the 74 additional patches may change rendering, GPU behavior, or API semantics vs the initial 149 release; (2) verify that Dawn/WebGPU games still render correctly — CVE-2026-11672 (GPU OOB write) fix may alter GPU memory management; (3) test V8 JIT-related game code — any game using `eval()`, dynamic code generation, or highly optimized compute loops should be tested for correctness regression; (4) verify that the extended stable release (148.0.7778.254) remains compatible for users who haven't updated to 149. Source: https://securityweek.com/google-patches-5th-chrome-zero-day-exploited-in-2026 + https://cvereports.com/reports/CVE-2026-11645

### Performance

- [P-049] **[P1] WebGPU Resource Lifecycle Stress Test — Rapid Create/Destroy Texture Buffer Pattern** — CVE-2026-5281 (Dawn/WebGPU UAF zero-day, exploited April 2026) revealed that Chrome's WebGPU implementation (via the Dawn abstraction layer) has resource lifecycle bugs when GPU resources are rapidly created and destroyed. This pattern is common in games: sprite sheet loading/unloading, level transitions that flush texture caches, and particle systems with dynamic texture pools. The exploit was triggered by a crafted HTML page that created and destroyed GPU textures in rapid succession, causing a use-after-free in Dawn's internal resource tracking. **Test**: (1) for each WebGPU-enabled game, create a stress test that rapidly creates and destroys GPU textures (1000 cycles of `device.createTexture()` + `texture.destroy()` in 10 seconds); (2) verify no browser tab crash, no GPU process crash, no WebGPU device loss during the stress test; (3) specifically test the pattern: create texture → queue write → destroy → create new texture → readback — this is the most common UAF trigger; (4) test on Chrome 149.0.7827.114+ (post-patch), Chrome 148 (pre-patch), and Firefox 151 (different Dawn implementation); (5) monitor `device.lost` promise — it should NOT fire during normal gameplay resource management. Source: https://cybersecuritynews.com/chrome-zero-day-vulnerability-exploited + https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-5281

- [P-050] **[P2] WebGPU Console-Quality 60fps High-Polygon Benchmark — 1M+ Triangle Scenes** — StraySpark (WebGPU browser indie games 2026) + CybermaXia (WebGPU vs WebGL 2026) + ByteIOTA (WebGPU performance) confirm that WebGPU in 2026 enables console-quality browser games running at 60fps with 1M+ triangle scenes — a feat impossible with WebGL. CybermaXia reports migrating Three.js WebGL visualizations to WebGPU, achieving smooth 60fps with millions of data points. StraySpark notes the performance ceiling has jumped from "basic 2D with WebGL hacks" to "Unreal Engine levels running in a tab." **GameZipper relevance**: (a) as WebGPU games appear on GameZipper, performance expectations will shift — users will expect console-quality smoothness; (b) current performance benchmarks (P-033 through P-048) focus on WebGL-era metrics — a WebGPU-specific high-polygon benchmark is needed; (c) the 15x performance gap between WebGL and WebGPU means fallback to WebGL will be very noticeable to users. **Test**: (1) create a benchmark scene with 1M triangles, animated at 60fps using WebGPU — measure frame time, GPU memory usage, and draw call count; (2) run the same scene in WebGL mode — record the performance delta; (3) establish minimum acceptable frame rates: WebGPU path ≥ 55fps, WebGL fallback ≥ 25fps on mid-range mobile (Mali-G57, Adreno 640); (4) test on Chrome 149, Firefox 151, Safari 26+ — compare WebGPU implementation quality across browsers; (5) monitor for shader compilation stutter (cold-start) — measure time-to-first-frame for the benchmark scene. Source: https://strayspark.studio/blog/webgpu-browser-indie-games-2026 + https://cybermaxia.com/en/blog/webgpu-vs-webgl-browser-2026-render-game-konsol + https://byteiota.com/webgpu-2026-70-browser-support-15x-performance-gains

### Game Testing

- [G-118] **[P1] Social Play Features — Baseline Browser Game Expectation 2026** — BinkPlay (2026 casual gaming trends) + Game Developers (mobile game genre breakdown 2026) confirm that social mechanics (leaderboards, real-time multiplayer, shared progress, friend systems, daily challenges with social comparison) are now baseline expectations for casual browser games, not premium features. 2026 research shows social features increase D7 retention by 2.5x and ARPDAU by 4-7x. The $92B mobile gaming market has shifted — players expect connected experiences even in casual titles. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) most GameZipper games are single-player with no social features; (b) if GameZipper adds social features (even simple high-score leaderboards), they must be tested for correctness, performance, and privacy; (c) social features create new testing surface: real-time sync, conflict resolution, privacy controls, friend-request flows, and data consistency across devices. **Test**: (1) if leaderboards are added, verify score submission is tamper-resistant — cannot submit arbitrary scores via browser console `localStorage.setItem('highScore', 999999)`; (2) test real-time sync latency — score updates should appear on leaderboard within 5 seconds; (3) test concurrent score submission — two players finishing the same game simultaneously should both appear correctly; (4) verify privacy — player names should be anonymized by default, with opt-in for public display; (5) test daily challenge reset — challenges should reset at midnight UTC, not browser-local time. Source: https://binkplay.com/en/blog/casual-gaming-trends-what-players-want-in-2026 + https://game-developers.org/mobile-game-genre-breakdown-2026

- [G-119] **[P2] Hybrid-Casual Meta-Layer Progression System — State Persistence Testing** — Game Growth Advisor (2026 hybrid-casual strategy) + BounDev (AI adaptive difficulty UX guide 2026) confirm that 36% of game studios are pivoting from hyper-casual to hybrid-casual, adding meta-layers (progression systems, unlock trees, daily rewards, battle passes, collection mechanics) on top of core gameplay loops. These meta-layers have complex state that must persist across sessions: player level, unlock progress, daily reward claim status, battle pass tier, collection completion percentage. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) games like color-sort, magic-sort, and idle-clicker already have progression elements — testing must cover meta-layer state persistence, not just core game state; (b) meta-layer state is typically more complex than core game state (nested objects, arrays of unlocked items, timestamp-based daily rewards) — more prone to serialization/persistence bugs; (c) the progression-unlock flow creates new edge cases: what happens when a player completes a level that unlocks a new mode but the unlock state fails to save? **Test**: (1) for each game with a progression/unlock system, complete a level that triggers an unlock, then reload — verify the unlock persisted; (2) test daily reward claim: claim a reward, wait 1 second, reload — verify reward is marked as claimed; (3) test collection completion: collect all items, reload — verify 100% completion persists; (4) test corrupted state recovery: manually set localStorage to `{}` for a game with progression, load the game — verify graceful recovery (reset to defaults, not crash); (5) test cross-session state consistency: play 3 levels, reload, play 3 more, reload — verify cumulative progress is correct (not just last session). Source: https://gamegrowthadvisor.com/blog/2026-04-16-hybrid-casual-game-design-strategy-2026 + https://boundev.ai/blog/game-ux-design-guide-2026

### Accessibility

- [A-027] **[P1] WCAG 2.2 87 Success Criteria — 9 New Criteria EAA Enforcement Specifics** — Web Accessibility Checker (WCAG 2.2 checklist 2026) + Z-AX (complete WCAG guide 2026) confirm WCAG 2.2 now has **87 success criteria** (9 new criteria added over WCAG 2.1). The **European Accessibility Act (EAA)** has brought mandatory enforcement from June 28, 2025 — all digital products sold in EU must meet WCAG 2.2 AA. The 9 new criteria most relevant to browser games are: (1) **2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (Minimum) AA** — focused element must not be hidden by other content (game overlays must not cover focused buttons); (2) **2.5.7 Dragging Movements AA** — all drag-based interactions must have a single-pointer alternative (color-sort tube drag must also support tap-select-tap-place); (3) **2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) AA** — interactive targets must be at least 24×24 CSS pixels; (4) **3.2.6 Consistent Help AAA** — help mechanism must be in same location across pages; (5) **3.3.7 Redundant Entry A** — previously entered info should auto-populate; (6) **3.3.8 Accessible Authentication (Minimum) AA** — no cognitive function tests for login (N/A for GameZipper — no login); (7) **3.3.9 Redundant Entry AAA** — same as 3.3.7 at AAA level. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) EAA enforcement means non-compliant game portals face legal risk in EU markets; (b) Focus Not Obscured (2.4.11) directly conflicts with GameZipper's fullscreen game overlays (splash screen, game-over modal, ad interstitials) — these overlays can obscure focused buttons; (c) Dragging Movements (2.5.7) requires all drag-based games (color-sort, parking-jam, pull-the-pin) to support tap-based alternatives; (d) Target Size (2.5.8) requires all game buttons to be at least 24×24px — small icon buttons in game HUDs may fail. **Test**: (1) for all overlay/modal games, tab through all interactive elements — verify focused element is visible (not behind overlay); (2) for all drag-based games, verify tap-select-tap-place alternative works; (3) measure all interactive element sizes — must be ≥ 24×24 CSS pixels; (4) audit 10 representative games against all 9 new WCAG 2.2 criteria — produce compliance matrix. Source: https://web-accessibility-checker.com/en/blog/wcag-2-2-checklist-2026 + https://z-ax.com/en/blog/web-accessibility-wcag-complete-guide-2026

### Cross-Device

- [C-075] **[P2] Poki Automated Playtesting Platform Integration — Browser Game QA Pipeline** — Robotics and Automation News (April 2026) reports that Poki, a major browser game platform, now offers automated playtesting to game developers at scale, free of charge. The platform goes from game builds to engagement data automatically, testing gameplay flows, detecting crash points, and measuring retention metrics. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) Poki's automated playtesting infrastructure could complement GameZipper's existing QA pipeline (verify-lite.js, deep-play-test.js); (b) GameZipper's test outputs (Kachilu session logs, Playwright traces, console errors) could feed into Poki's analysis pipeline for standardized quality metrics; (c) the Poki model proves that automated browser game testing at scale (1000+ games) is commercially viable — GameZipper's approach of 280+ games via cron-driven QA is aligned with industry direction. **Test**: (1) evaluate Poki's automated playtesting API documentation — determine integration feasibility; (2) compare Poki's test coverage metrics against GameZipper's verify-lite.js 30-check coverage — identify gaps; (3) pilot: submit 5 GameZipper games to Poki's platform (if API allows) and compare results against existing QA findings; (4) assess whether Poki's engagement data (session length, bounce rate, retention curves) can be used as QA signals — games with anomalous engagement metrics may have undiscovered bugs. Source: https://roboticsandautomationnews.com/2026/04/21/how-web-gaming-platforms-use-automated-playtesting-to-scale-quality-control/100793

## v1.45.0 Additions (2026-06-13 — R155 Dynamic Test Intelligence cron)

### Browser-Specific

- [B-074] **[P1] Chrome 150 Beta (150.0.7871.14) — Rendering/Audio/Input Regression for 317 Games** — Chrome 150 Beta is rolling out June 2026 (PCWorld + Chrome Releases blog). This is the first Chrome version to fully remove the `--extension-mv2` flag workaround, completing the MV2→MV3 transition. Chrome 150 also includes rendering engine updates that may affect canvas compositing, WebAudio autoplay policy, and pointer event handling. For GameZipper's 317 games: (1) run Top 30 game smoke test on Chrome 150 Beta — specifically test canvas rendering, WebGL context creation, and WebAudio playback; (2) benchmark FPS and load times on Chrome 150 Beta vs Chrome 149 stable — measure any rendering regression; (3) test that pointer events (mousedown/touchstart) fire correctly on canvas elements — Chrome 150 may have changes to event dispatch; (4) verify WebAudio autoplay policy enforcement — test that games with `navigator.getAutoplayPolicy()` wrappers still work; (5) test that all MV3-based extensions (ad blockers, password managers) work correctly when loading game pages. Source: https://pcworld.com/article/3160794/the-last-lifeline-for-ublock-origin-in-chrome-is-almost-gone-for-good.html + https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/2026/06

- [B-075] **[P2] Chromium Ecosystem MV2 Extension Removal — Edge + Brave Ad-Blocker Compatibility** — The Manifest V2→V3 transition affects ALL Chromium-based browsers (Chrome, Edge, Brave), not just Chrome (PiunikaWeb June 2026). Edge and Brave users on GameZipper will also lose MV2-based ad blockers. MV3-based blockers use `declarativeNetRequest` which has significantly fewer capabilities: no dynamic rule creation based on response content, no cosmetic filtering for in-page ad elements, and a 300,000 rule limit. For GameZipper: (1) test ad rendering (Monetag + AdSense) in Edge with MV3-based blockers active — verify no JavaScript errors when ads are partially blocked; (2) test in Brave Browser with Brave Shields (built-in MV3-native blocker) — verify game pages load and function correctly; (3) test that game state (localStorage, sessionStorage) is not affected by aggressive MV3 blocker rule matching; (4) verify that `monetag-manager.js` and `game-footer.js` are not incorrectly blocked by MV3 declarative rules — check browser console for blocked resource warnings; (5) test on Opera and Vivaldi (also Chromium-based) for completeness. Source: https://piunikaweb.com/2026/06/08/chrome-manifest-v2-unpacked-extensions-mac-windows + https://techspot.com/news/112722-end-ublock-origin-chrome-now-weeks-away-not.html

### Security

- [S-088] **[P2] AI-Discovered CVE Acceleration — Testing Cadence Must Match 200+/Month New Normal** — CSO Online (June 2026 Patch Tuesday analysis) reports that June 2026 marks a "new normal" with 200+ CVEs per month, driven by AI discovery tools like Anthropic's Mythos (10,000+ bugs found in Firefox alone — IT之家). The vulnerability landscape is accelerating faster than human review can track. For GameZipper: (1) current 4-hour Dynamic Test Intelligence cadence may be insufficient — evaluate whether 2-hour intervals are needed during active exploitation waves; (2) implement automated NVD/CISA KEV feed monitoring that triggers immediate re-test of affected games when a new actively-exploited CVE is published; (3) test that GameZipper's dependency chain (Cloudflare, Vercel, Monetag, AdSense CDN) can be verified for patch status within 1 hour of CVE disclosure; (4) establish SLA: from CVE disclosure to GameZipper test completion < 24 hours for P0 vulnerabilities; (5) create a "rapid CVE response" test template that runs only the Top 30 games against the newly-patched browser version, focused on the specific vulnerability class. Source: https://csoonline.com/article/4183632/june-patch-tuesday-marks-a-new-normal-with-over-200-cves-32-rated-critical.html + https://memeburn.com/10-000-bugs-anthropic-mythos-ai-shakes-safety-in-2026

### Cross-Device

- [C-076] **[P1] Android June 2026 Security Update — WebView Touch/Audio Regression Pre-vs-Post Patch** — Google's June 2026 Android security update (TechRepublic) patches dozens of flaws including a potentially exploited Framework vulnerability and WebView security fixes. Android WebView is the rendering engine used when GameZipper is accessed from Android apps or embedded browsers. For GameZipper: (1) identify 2 Android test devices — one with pre-patch firmware, one with post-patch June 2026 update; (2) test Top 10 mobile-popular games on both devices — focus on: (a) touch input responsiveness (single tap, multi-touch, long-press); (b) WebAudio playback — some Android WebView patches have inadvertently broken audio context creation; (c) canvas rendering quality — verify no visual regression in anti-aliasing or text rendering; (3) specifically test that `touchstart`/`touchmove`/`touchend` events fire correctly with correct `touches[].clientX/Y` coordinates after the update; (4) verify that fullscreen API (`element.requestFullscreen()`) still works in WebView post-patch — some security updates restrict fullscreen in embedded contexts; (5) test that game state persistence (localStorage) is not affected by WebView data isolation changes. Source: https://techrepublic.com/article/news-google-june-2026-android-security-update

### Website-Level

- [W-135] **[P2] MV3 Ad-Blocker Disruption — declarativeNetRequest Limitations on Ad-Funded Game Sites** — With MV2 dead in Chrome 150, MV3 ad blockers have significantly reduced capabilities: `declarativeNetRequest` only supports static rule sets (max 300K rules), no dynamic filtering based on response content, and no cosmetic element hiding (PiunikaWeb + TechSpot June 2026). For GameZipper's ad-funded model: (1) test that Monetag ad scripts (`monetag-manager.js`) load correctly even when MV3 blockers are active — verify HTTP 200 for script requests; (2) test that `gz-ad-below-game` containers render correctly when the blocker cannot perform cosmetic hiding — ads should either display or container should collapse (not show white box); (3) verify that the 4-layer hide-on-fail pattern (Pitfall 9c) still works under MV3 blocker conditions; (4) test fallback: if ad scripts are blocked entirely, verify game pages still function without errors — games should not depend on ad scripts for initialization; (5) measure revenue impact: compare ad fill rates with MV2 blockers (current) vs MV3 blockers (Chrome 150+) to understand revenue projection changes. Source: https://techspot.com/news/112722-end-ublock-origin-chrome-now-weeks-away-not.html + https://piunikaweb.com/2026/06/08/chrome-manifest-v2-unpacked-extensions-mac-windows

### Game Testing

- [G-120] **[P3] Canvas Pixel-Diff CI Pipeline — Golden Screenshot Baselines for Automated Visual Regression** — Research (arXiv 2208.02335 + Playgama testing guide 2026) confirms that DOM-based testing tools (Selenium, Playwright DOM assertions) cannot inspect `<canvas>` content — they report "canvas element present" regardless of what's rendered. Visual bug detection for canvas games requires screenshot-based pixel-diff approaches with 100% accuracy vs 44.6% for snapshot testing. For GameZipper: (1) establish golden screenshot baselines for Top 30 games — capture screenshot at 5 known-good states per game (title screen, mid-gameplay, game-over, settings panel, help/tutorial); (2) implement automated pixel-diff comparison in CI — use Playwright's `toHaveScreenshot()` or a perceptual diff tool (SSIM ≥ 0.98 threshold); (3) test that known visual bugs (blank canvas from Bug #12/#17/#20, rendering artifacts from Bug #10) are detected by the pixel-diff pipeline; (4) handle canvas animation variance — use frame-pausing or state-deterministic capture (pause render loop, capture, resume) to avoid false positives from animation frame differences; (5) store golden baselines in version control alongside test cases — update baselines only when intentional visual changes are made. Source: https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.02335 + https://playgama.com/blog/uncategorized/comprehensive-guide-to-testing-methods-for-html5-games

## v1.46.0 Additions (2026-06-13 — R158 Dynamic Test Intelligence cron)

### Browser-Specific

- [B-076] **[P0] CVE-2026-12009 Chrome Mac Accessibility Sandbox Escape — Critical macOS Game Page Exploit Vector** — Insufficient validation of untrusted input in the Accessibility component of Google Chrome on macOS, prior to 149.0.7827.115. Allows a remote attacker who compromised the renderer process to perform a sandbox escape via a crafted HTML page. Chromium severity: **Critical**. Fixed in Chrome 149.0.7827.115 (June 11, 2026). For GameZipper: (1) verify all 317 game pages on Chrome macOS < 149.0.7827.115 — specifically test games with heavy DOM manipulation (antistress gallery, color-sort, card games) for accessibility-tree-based sandbox escape vectors; (2) verify that game iframe sandbox attributes (`sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin"`) are correctly set — iframe-embedded games on third-party sites are the most likely attack surface; (3) test that browser accessibility features (VoiceOver on macOS) don't expose game internal state that could be leveraged for renderer compromise; (4) confirm Chrome 149.0.7827.115+ users are protected by testing `navigator.userAgent` version detection. Source: https://tenable.com/cve/CVE-2026-12009 + https://cvefeed.io/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-12009

- [B-077] **[P0] CVE-2026-12010 Chrome Android GPU Heap Buffer Overflow Sandbox Escape — WebGL/WebGPU Game Exploit** — Heap buffer overflow in the GPU component of Google Chrome on Android prior to 149.0.7827.115. Allows a remote attacker who compromised the renderer process to perform a sandbox escape via a crafted HTML page. CVSS 8.3. Chromium severity: **Critical**. Directly affects WebGPU/WebGL game pages on Android — the GPU code path is exercised by every canvas-based game. For GameZipper: (1) stress-test Top 30 games on Android Chrome < 149.0.7827.115 under heavy WebGL load — specifically games with large canvas dimensions (2048+), complex shader programs, or WebGPU compute shaders; (2) test that game canvas rendering does not trigger GPU memory corruption under sustained 60fps rendering for 10+ minutes; (3) verify that Android WebView (used by in-app browsers) is also patched — test GameZipper pages in Facebook/Instagram embedded browsers; (4) confirm all ad iframes (Monetag) cannot reach GPU process from compromised renderer context. Source: https://cvefeed.io/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-12010 + https://thehackerwire.com/vulnerability/CVE-2026-12010

- [B-078] **[P0] CVE-2026-12007 Chrome Renderer Use-After-Free — Canvas Rendering Exploit via Tab Switch** — Use-after-free vulnerability in Chrome's rendering engine, CVSS 8.8, affecting Chrome versions prior to 149.0.7827.114 on Windows, macOS, and Linux. Part of the 28-vulnerability Chrome 149 patch batch released June 10-11, 2026. The UAF in the renderer process can be triggered during rapid visibility changes. For GameZipper: (1) stress-test game canvas rendering under rapid tab switching — open 5 game tabs, rapidly switch between them (Alt+Tab / Cmd+Tab) 20+ times, verify no renderer crash or blank canvas on return; (2) test the `visibilitychange` handler in games that pause/resume rAF loops — verify that `cancelAnimationFrame` / `requestAnimationFrame` cycle doesn't trigger UAF conditions; (3) test games with the rAF override IIFE (55+ games with the cancelAnimationFrame fix from R85) specifically on Chrome < 149.0.7827.114 — the IIFE's `beforeunload` + `visibilitychange` listeners directly exercise the UAF code path; (4) verify that long-running game sessions (30+ minutes) on unpatched Chrome don't accumulate renderer memory leaks from the UAF condition. Source: https://cybersixt.com/incidents/chrome-149-patches-28-flaws-five-critical-amid-rising-bug-count-7da88a2e

### Security

- [S-089] **[P0] CVE-2026-0878 Firefox CanvasWebGL Sandbox Escape — WebGL Game Buffer Boundary Violation** — Sandbox escape vulnerability caused by incorrect boundary conditions in the Graphics: CanvasWebGL component of Mozilla Firefox. Allows attackers to bypass browser sandbox protections by exploiting improper input validation within the WebGL rendering pipeline. Related CWEs: CWE-119 (buffer overflow), CWE-20 (improper input validation). For GameZipper: (1) test all WebGL games on Firefox for buffer boundary violations — specifically games that allocate large WebGL buffers (terrain renderers, particle systems with 10K+ particles); (2) verify no canvas memory leaks under sustained rendering on Firefox — compare `performance.memory` before and after 10-minute gameplay session; (3) test that `WebGLRenderingContext.getParameter()` returns consistent values after buffer manipulation — inconsistent returns may indicate the vulnerability is triggerable; (4) verify that Firefox's WebGL context loss handling (`webglcontextlost` event) doesn't expose sandbox-escaped memory in error messages. Source: https://sentinelone.com/vulnerability-database/cve-2026-0878 + https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=CVE-2026-0878

- [S-090] **[P1] CVE-2026-11045 Chrome GPU Renderer Memory Disclosure — Canvas Readback Information Leak** — Medium-severity GPU vulnerability in Google Chrome, fixed before Chrome 149.0.7827.53. A remote attacker who had already compromised the renderer process could potentially read sensitive process memory through a crafted HTML page. Published June 4, 2026. For GameZipper: (1) test that `canvas.toDataURL()` and `ctx.getImageData()` calls in games don't return unexpected pixel data from other processes — this is the primary readback vector; (2) verify that canvas readback operations on GameZipper games return only expected game content — compare pixel values against known-good baselines; (3) test that shared workers (if any game uses them) don't leak memory via canvas transfer; (4) verify Monetag ad iframes cannot access game canvas content via `postMessage` or cross-origin canvas readback; (5) test on Chrome < 149.0.7827.53 specifically — confirm vulnerability is patched in current version. Source: https://windowsforum.com/threads/cve-2026-11045-chrome-gpu-bug-patch-to-149-to-stop-renderer-memory-disclosure.424543

### Cross-Device

- [C-077] **[P1] Three.js WebGPU Occlusion Query Crash (Issue #33759) — Multi-Object Scene Counter Desync** — WebGPU/WebGL occlusion queries crash in Three.js due to `resolveOccludedAsync` counter desync. Single-material objects rendered via `renderer.render(scene, camera)` keep counters in sync, but multi-proxy setups (common in game scenes with many objects) cause crashes. The official `webgpu_occlusion` example doesn't reproduce the bug because it uses a simple single-proxy setup. For GameZipper: (1) identify any games using Three.js with WebGPU renderer — test with multi-object scenes (5+ meshes) that use occlusion queries; (2) if no current GameZipper games use Three.js WebGPU occlusion queries, add this as a pre-migration gate: "Any future game using Three.js WebGPU must be tested with >5 meshes and occlusion queries enabled"; (3) verify that Three.js WebGL fallback mode doesn't have equivalent counter desync — test same multi-object scenes in WebGL mode; (4) check `three.js` version in any GameZipper dependencies — if >= r170 (where WebGPU renderer is stable), this bug is relevant; (5) monitor https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/33759 for fix status and update test when patched. Source: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/33759

- [C-078] **[P2] Chrome 149 DevTools Gemini 3 AI Upgrade — Console Error Parsing Regression Risk** — Chrome 149 ships with upgraded DevTools that give "more concise, and more actionable answers" using Gemini 3 AI integration. This changes console error message formatting and stack trace presentation. For GameZipper: (1) verify that existing QA scripts (`verify-lite.js`, `deep-play-test.js`, `kachilu eval` commands) correctly parse console errors from Chrome 149+ — AI-enhanced messages may have different formatting than traditional Chrome error output; (2) test that `window.onerror` and `window.addEventListener('error', ...)` still capture the same error message format — AI-enhanced error descriptions should not break error handlers; (3) verify that Kachilu browser's `eval` output for error checking still works with Gemini 3-enhanced DevTools — the AI suggestions may inject extra text into console output; (4) test that Playwright `page.error` event captures the same error data on Chrome 149+ — any AI-appended suggestions should not be part of the error payload; (5) update any regex-based error parsing in QA scripts to be more tolerant of AI-enhanced message formatting. Source: https://piunikaweb.com/2026/06/09/chrome-149-devtools-ai-upgrade

### Game Testing

- [G-121] **[P2] Playwright 1.61 AI Test Agents (Planner/Generator/Healer) — Autonomous Game QA Pipeline** — Playwright Core 1.61.0-alpha released June 11, 2026 with three AI-powered Test Agents: **Planner** (explores app and generates test plan), **Generator** (writes spec files from test plan), **Healer** (auto-repairs flaky tests on CI via MCP and Claude). Also, Playwright MCP for AI agent browser automation (33.6k GitHub stars) enables coding agents to interact with web interfaces without visual understanding. For GameZipper: (1) evaluate Playwright 1.61 AI Healer for GameZipper's flaky game rendering tests — specifically the `deep-play-test.js` tests that fail intermittently due to Monetag popunders and CDN timing; (2) test the Planner agent against GameZipper homepage — does it discover all game categories and interaction patterns autonomously?; (3) evaluate MCP-based autonomous game UI testing — can an AI agent navigate game menus, start games, and verify gameplay without pre-scripted selectors?; (4) benchmark: run existing verify-lite.js (30 checks/game) vs AI Healer-enhanced version on 30 Top games — measure false-positive reduction rate; (5) assess security implications of MCP-based browser automation — ensure AI agents cannot modify game state or trigger real ad clicks during testing. Source: https://libraries.io/npm/playwright-core + https://qaskills.sh/blog/playwright-test-agents-planner-generator-healer-official-2026 + https://testdino.com/blog/fixing-playwright-tests-with-ai



### New in v1.47.0 (2026-06-13 R160 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

#### Security

- [S-091] **[P0] CVE-2026-9880 WebGL Insufficient Input Validation — Shader/Buffer Fuzz Testing** — Chrome 149 patches CVE-2026-9880: insufficient validation of untrusted input in WebGL allows crafted shader programs and buffer data to trigger memory corruption. Directly affects all browser games using WebGL rendering (GameZipper's canvas games). For testing: (1) fuzz-test WebGL shader compilation with malformed GLSL inputs — verify games handle shader compilation failures gracefully without canvas blanking or crash; (2) test WebGL buffer operations (`bufferData`, `bufferSubData`) with oversized/underflow data — games must not silently corrupt state; (3) verify that games using `getContext('webgl')` or `getContext('webgl2')` properly validate all shader source before `compileShader()` — no dynamic `eval`-equivalent in GLSL; (4) run Chrome 149+ regression on Top 30 games — any new WebGL context loss events or console errors related to "INVALID_VALUE" / "INVALID_OPERATION" are regression. Source: https://forbes.com/sites/daveywinder/2026/05/31/151-chrome-security-flaws-22-critical-fixed-in-new-google-update

- [S-092] **[P1] WebAssembly Memory Safety — Unity/Unreal WebGL Export Validation** — Research (idw-online.de, June 2026) reveals memory safety vulnerabilities in WebAssembly modules used by major web apps (Google Earth, Zoom, Twitch). Unity and Unreal Engine WebGL exports compile to WASM, making GameZipper games built with these engines potentially vulnerable. For testing: (1) identify all GameZipper games using WASM (grep for `.wasm` file references or `WebAssembly.instantiate`); (2) test WASM module loading resilience — malformed/modified `.wasm` files must not crash the host page; (3) verify WASM games handle `WebAssembly.Module()` compilation failures gracefully (fallback message, not blank canvas); (4) test WASM memory growth under extended gameplay (30+ minutes) — verify no memory leak beyond 2× initial allocation; (5) ensure Content-Security-Policy `script-src` includes `wasm-unsafe-eval` only where WASM is expected. Source: https://idw-online.de/en/news871992

- [S-093] **[P2] HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 DoS Resilience — CDN and Multiplayer Game Availability** — New class of HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 DoS vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2026-49160 Rapid Reset) discovered with LLM-assisted research (cyberdaily.au). Affects multiplayer browser games and CDN-hosted game assets. For testing: (1) verify Cloudflare CDN serves game assets under HTTP/2 Rapid Reset attack simulation — game pages must still load within 5s; (2) test multiplayer WebSocket connection resilience under concurrent connection flood — games must degrade gracefully (show "reconnecting" message) not crash; (3) verify CDN cache hit rate remains >80% during HTTP/3 QUIC connection reset storms; (4) test game asset loading with forced HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 — no rendering differences or missing resources; (5) verify rate-limiting headers present on game page responses. Source: https://cyberdaily.au/security/13729-op-ed-microsoft-june-patch-tuesday-reveals-200-vulnerabilities

#### Browser-Specific

- [B-079] **[P1] Interop 2026 CSS `contrast-color()` Cross-Browser Feature Parity** — Interop 2026 initiative (howtogeek, June 2026) drives cross-browser compatibility, but `contrast-color()` CSS function now works in Firefox/Safari but NOT in Chrome/Edge. Games using this for accessible text colors will render differently across browsers. For testing: (1) grep all game HTML for `contrast-color(` usage — any hit requires cross-browser rendering verification; (2) test game UI text legibility in Chrome vs Firefox vs Safari — manual color definitions must work as fallback; (3) verify Interop 2026 focus-visible, :has(), subgrid, container queries work consistently across Chrome 149, Firefox 151, Safari 27; (4) test CSS `color-mix()` and `light-dark()` functions used in game dark-mode implementations — must degrade gracefully in non-supporting browsers; (5) establish cross-browser CSS screenshot baseline for Top 10 games using modern CSS features. Source: https://howtogeek.com/chrome-firefox-edge-and-safari-are-teaming-up-to-fix-common-web-problems

#### Game Testing

- [G-122] **[P1] 10-Second Hook Rule — Time-to-Fun Benchmark for Casual Games** — 2026 hybrid-casual industry standard (gamegrowthadvisor): core gameplay must be understood in 10 seconds with no tutorial/onboarding. Ad-creative-friendly visuals must look compelling in 6-second video clips. For testing: (1) measure time-to-first-meaningful-interaction for each game category — puzzle ≤8s, arcade ≤5s, card ≤10s; (2) verify splash screen dismisses within 3s and game is immediately playable; (3) test that first-game-over occurs between 15-60 seconds (too fast = frustrating, too slow = boring); (4) capture 6-second screenshot clip of gameplay — must visually convey the game's core mechanic; (5) benchmark: compare GameZipper Top 30 games' time-to-fun vs industry 10s benchmark. Source: https://gamegrowthadvisor.com/blog/2026-04-16-hybrid-casual-game-design-strategy-2026

- [G-123] **[P2] Mini-Game Permanent Mode Switching UX Flow** — Mini-games as permanent modes are redefining casual engagement in 2026 (game-developers.org). Games with multiple modes (tutorial, practice, ranked, daily challenge) must handle mode switching without state corruption. For testing: (1) verify mode-switch doesn't reset persistent state (scores, level progress, achievements); (2) test rapid mode-switching (5+ switches in 10s) — no UI glitch, no canvas blanking, no JS error; (3) verify back-navigation from any mode returns to correct previous state; (4) test mode-switch during active game-over overlay — overlay must dismiss cleanly before new mode starts; (5) verify localStorage quota not exceeded when multiple modes save independently. Source: https://game-developers.org/mobile-game-genre-breakdown-2026

#### Performance

- [P-051] **[P1] WebGPU Main-Thread Responsiveness vs WebGL Benchmark** — WebGPU detaches animation logic from JS runtime, keeping UI responsive where WebGL chokes the main thread (gjgalante/medium). For GameZipper: (1) benchmark main-thread blocking during heavy rendering — WebGPU games must maintain <50ms task duration vs WebGL games that can block >200ms; (2) test UI responsiveness (button clicks, scroll) during active game rendering — WebGL games must show no lag, WebGPU games must be measurably smoother; (3) measure INP (Interaction to Next Paint) during gameplay — target <200ms for all games; (4) test that requestAnimationFrame callback duration is <16ms (60fps budget) on mid-range devices (Mali-G57/Adreno 640); (5) compare main-thread utilization profiling between WebGL and WebGPU game modes using Chrome DevTools Performance panel. Source: https://gjgalante.medium.com/webgl-vs-webgpu-the-performance-gap-fbd121fb221a

- [P-052] **[P2] WebGPU Framebuffer Configuration Correctness — Migration from WebGL** — WebGPU requires explicit framebuffer configuration vs WebGL's auto-created default framebuffer (toji.dev). Games migrating from WebGL to WebGPU may have rendering bugs from incorrect framebuffer setup. For testing: (1) verify WebGPU game canvas renders correctly on first frame (no blank/black flash); (2) test framebuffer resize handling — window resize must correctly recreate framebuffer without flickering; (3) verify alpha blending matches WebGL behavior — no visual differences in semi-transparent game elements; (4) test multisampled framebuffer (MSAA) correctness — antialiased edges must not show artifacts; (5) verify depth buffer configuration matches game's z-ordering expectations. Source: https://toji.dev/webgpu-best-practices/webgpu-performance-comparison.html

#### Compatibility

- [C-079] **[P2] Agentic AI Autonomous Exploit-Finding — QA Pipeline Evaluation** — Agentic AI powered by RL and LLMs (thecodersblog, June 2026) can autonomously reason through game states, uncover exploits, and deliver narrative-driven QA insights. For GameZipper: (1) evaluate agentic AI platforms (Modl.ai, SnoopGame, WeTest) against GameZipper's existing verify-lite.js + deep-play-test.js pipeline; (2) benchmark: can an AI agent autonomously discover the 14 bug patterns documented in gamezipper-qa skill (rAF cancel, splash deadlock, init order, etc.)?; (3) test AI agent's ability to navigate game-specific UI (card game deal buttons, puzzle game drag-drop, board game mode-select); (4) measure false-positive rate of AI exploit-finding vs scripted QA — target <5% FP; (5) assess cost-effectiveness: AI agent testing time vs sub-agent delegate_task approach for 300+ games. Source: https://thecodersblog.com/agentic-ai-for-game-playtesting-2026

#### Website

- [W-136] **[P2] Shift-Right Live Telemetry QA — Production Monitoring Integration** — "Shift-Right" testing strategy (snoopgame, 2026) advocates production telemetry-based QA where real user sessions drive automated test case generation. For GameZipper: (1) evaluate real-user monitoring (RUM) integration — capture JS errors, WebGL context losses, canvas blank screens from production traffic; (2) test that Monetag and Cloudflare Beacon telemetry correctly report game-level errors (not just page-level); (3) verify error sampling rate doesn't impact game performance (target <1% overhead); (4) implement automated alert: if error rate for any game exceeds 1% of sessions in 1 hour, trigger QA investigation; (5) test telemetry pipeline: error captured → alert generated → QA agent spawned → fix deployed, target end-to-end <30 minutes. Source: https://snoopgame.com/blog/top-game-testing-trends-to-watch-in-2026

### v1.48.0 Additions (2026-06-13 Industry Research)

- [W-137] **[P1] Chrome 145 JPEG-XL Re-Enabled — Game Image Format Compatibility** — Chrome 145 stable (Feb 11 2026) re-enabled JPEG-XL (.jxl) image decoding support after Google removed it from Chromium in 2022. Game thumbnail/screenshot assets may now be served in .jxl format for 30-50% smaller files. For testing: (1) grep all game asset directories for *.jxl files — any hit requires browser decode verification; (2) test .jxl image rendering in Chrome 145+ vs Chrome 144 and older — verify graceful fallback to PNG/WebP when .jxl unsupported; (3) test JPEG-XL animated sequences (.jxl animation) — some game splash screens may use this; (4) verify <img srcset> with .jxl candidates falls back correctly in Safari/Firefox (JPEG-XL support varies); (5) check CDN Content-Type headers for .jxl — must be `image/jxl` not `application/octet-stream`. Source: https://new.qq.com/rain/a/20260211A01X9H00

- [W-138] **[P2] ChromeOS 148 → Chrome 150 LTS Enterprise/School Chromebook Channel** — ChromeOS 148 (May 26 2026) paves the way for Chrome 150 Long Term Support (LTS) channel for enterprise and school Chromebooks. LTS Chromebooks run older Chromium versions with months-long patch delays. For testing: (1) identify GameZipper traffic from ChromeOS LTS builds via navigator.userAgent — measure % of users on >6-month-old Chrome; (2) test Top 30 games on Chrome 150 LTS build — verify no regression from features requiring Chrome 152+; (3) check that WebGPU/WebGL fallback paths work on LTS Chromebooks with older GPU drivers; (4) verify game JS bundles are transpiled to ES2020 or earlier — LTS Chromebooks may not support ES2024+ syntax; (5) test localStorage/IndexedDB API availability on school-managed Chromebooks with strict policies. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2736a15714b22052

- [B-080] **[P0] CVE-2026-32202 Windows Shell Spoofing — Incomplete Patch Variant of CVE-2026-21510 (CISA KEV)** — CVE-2026-32202 (Windows Shell spoofing, CISA KEV May 2026) is a variant spawned by incomplete patching of CVE-2026-21510. Actively exploited in the wild. Attackers can spoof browser security UI on Windows, tricking users into revealing credentials on game pages. For testing: (1) verify gamezipper.com HTTPS certificate pinning and HSTS headers — spoofed browser chrome cannot downgrade to HTTP; (2) test that OAuth/login flows on game pages use WebAuthn or device-bound credentials not vulnerable to UI spoofing; (3) verify Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors prevents embedding in spoofed container apps; (4) test that game download buttons show full URL in browser — no shortened/obfuscated links; (5) verify Windows SmartScreen/Defender doesn't flag game pages as suspicious after CVE patch. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_06669fd7ea842752

- [C-080] **[P2] Firefox 149 Built-in VPN + Split-Screen Tab Mode — Game Rendering Context Change** — Firefox 149 (March 24 2026) ships free built-in VPN (US/FR/DE initial rollout) and split-screen tab mode. VPN changes user IP/geo location affecting geo-targeted ads (Monetag) and CDN edge routing. Split-screen mode halves viewport width mid-game. For testing: (1) test Monetag ad serving when Firefox VPN routes through different country — verify no ad-block false positives or geo-restriction errors; (2) test game canvas resize when user enters split-screen mode mid-game — verify no crash, no lost game state; (3) verify WebGL/WebGPU context survives viewport dimension change from split-screen; (4) test touch-input coordinate mapping in split-screen on Firefox Android; (5) verify game session continues across VPN connect/disconnect events without WebSocket drops. Source: https://new.qq.com/rain/a/20260211A01X9H00

- [G-124] **[P1] WebAssembly Component Model + WASI 1.0 (Wasm 3.0) — Game Engine Compilation Target Testing** — WASI 0.3.0/WASI 1.0 reaching production maturity in 2026 (Jan 2026 article). WebAssembly Component Model enables cross-language WASM modules communicating via WIT (WebAssembly Interface Types) interfaces. Game engines (Unity, Unreal, Godot) increasingly compile to WASM components rather than monolithic modules. For testing: (1) identify any GameZipper games using WASM modules — check for .wasm files >2MB indicating engine compilation; (2) test WASM component loading with async instantiation — verify no race conditions between component imports; (3) test WASM memory limits on mobile browsers (iOS Safari 4GB hard limit, Chrome 2GB soft limit); (4) verify WIT interface version compatibility — game compiled with Component Model v1 vs runtime v2; (5) benchmark WASM SIMD (128-bit) game physics vs native — target <2x slowdown for acceptable casual gameplay. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_3686969bd4296552

- [G-125] **[P1] Tencent Cloud 2026 Bounce-Rate Benchmark — Concrete FCP/LCP Performance Budget** — 2026 Tencent Cloud performance data: first-screen load time +1 second = +30% bounce rate increase; load time >5 seconds = 80% bounce rate. This provides concrete performance budgets for game landing pages. For testing: (1) measure First Contentful Paint (FCP) for all game pages — target <1.8s (Chrome UX Report P75); (2) measure Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) — target <2.5s; any game page >3s LCP needs optimization; (3) identify game pages with >5s total load time — these lose 80% of players, flag as P0 performance bugs; (4) test Time to Interactive (TTI) — game must be playable within 3s of FCP; (5) correlate bounce-rate data from Cloudflare Analytics with game load-time metrics to validate the 30%/80% benchmarks for GameZipper. Source: https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2581810

- [P-053] **[P1] WebGPU Compute Shader 5-8x Faster Than WASM SIMD for Matrix Math >256×256** — CSDN benchmark (June 2026): WebGPU compute shaders outperform WASM SIMD by 5-8x for matrix multiplication at sizes >256×256. This is a concrete migration decision point for games with physics, pathfinding, or particle systems doing heavy matrix math. For testing: (1) identify games with >1000 particles or physics simulations — candidates for WebGPU compute shader migration; (2) benchmark matrix-heavy operations (e.g., 512×512 transform) in WASM SIMD vs WebGPU compute — verify 5x+ speedup; (3) test WebGPU compute shader fallback when WebGPU unavailable — must gracefully degrade to WASM SIMD with acceptable performance; (4) verify compute shader results match CPU reference exactly (no floating-point divergence causing visual glitches); (5) measure GPU memory bandwidth usage — ensure compute shader doesn't starve rendering pipeline. Source: https://blog.csdn.net/m0_55049655/article/details/145379038

- [S-094] **[P2] Playwright 1.60 HAR+Trace Integration — Session Token Leakage in Test Artifacts** — Playwright 1.60 (June 5 2026) introduces HAR+Trace unified integration, test.abort(), Drop API, and boxes option. HAR (HTTP Archive) and Trace files capture ALL network traffic including session cookies, auth tokens, and API keys from game testing sessions. CI artifacts may leak credentials. For testing: (1) audit GameZipper QA pipeline Playwright HAR/Trace output — grep for Authorization headers, Set-Cookie, Bearer tokens; (2) verify CI artifact retention policy — HAR/Trace files must be auto-deleted or tokenized within 24h; (3) test that test fixtures use dummy/mock credentials not production game API keys; (4) verify HAR files don't capture Monetag ad exchange tokens or Cloudflare session cookies; (5) implement automated secret-scan (gitleaks/trufflehog) on Playwright test artifacts before CI upload. Source: https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2683110

- [C-081] **[P2] Playwright CLI Token-Efficient AI Agent Browser Automation (Microsoft, June 2026)** — Microsoft launched playwright-cli (June 2026), a token-efficient browser automation CLI designed for AI coding agents. It compresses browser snapshots to minimize LLM token consumption during QA. For GameZipper: (1) evaluate playwright-cli vs current verify-lite.js + browser_snapshot approach — measure token usage per game QA cycle; (2) test playwright-cli on 10 representative games — verify it can detect the 14 documented bug patterns (rAF cancel, splash deadlock, etc.); (3) benchmark: playwright-cli token consumption vs Hermes browser_snapshot for identical game pages; (4) test playwright-cli multi-browser support (Chromium/WebKit/Firefox) for cross-browser game testing; (5) evaluate playwright-cli Drop API and test.abort() for efficient early-exit when game critical bug is found. Source: https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2631860
