## CATEGORY 21: 2026 MID-JUNE BROWSER-AI ATTACK SURFACE + EMERGENCY CVE CADENCE + UX CONVERGENCE (R167 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 21.1 Browser-Integrated AI Panel Hijack (NEW Attack Surface 2026)
- [S-097] **[P1]** CVE-2026-0628 "Glic Jack" Chrome Gemini Live AI side panel hijack — CSA Labs (June 2026) documents a high-severity (CVSS 8.8) privilege escalation in Google Chrome's Gemini Live side panel. Browser extensions with **basic permissions** (not special/host permissions) can hijack the AI assistant to access **camera, microphone, local files, and screenshots from ANY open website** including game pages. This is a **new browser-AI attack surface**: the AI panel runs with elevated privileges and can read cross-origin content from all open tabs. **Test**: (1) verify that no GameZipper game page exposes sensitive content (localStorage keys, user PII, Monetag bidder data) that a compromised AI panel could exfiltrate via screenshot/file-read; (2) test that game pages with `navigator.mediaDevices` access (if any game uses camera/mic for AR/voice) require explicit user gesture + permission prompt — the AI panel hijack bypasses normal permission checks; (3) audit all game pages for `postMessage` handlers that might accept AI-panel-origin messages without origin validation; (4) recommend users disable browser-integrated AI panel extensions when playing games with Monetag ad iframes (ad networks are the typical malware delivery vector). Source: https://labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org/research/csa-research-note-browser-ai-panel-hijack-cve-2026-0628-2026

### 21.2 Chrome Emergency Update Cadence — 5th Zero-Day of 2026
- [B-084] **[P0]** Chrome 149.0.7827.114 emergency update — 5th Chrome zero-day of 2026 actively exploited — BleepingComputer (June 9 2026) + InfoSecurity Magazine (June 9 2026) confirm Google released an **emergency update** (outside the normal stable-channel schedule) patching the **5th Chrome zero-day exploited in the wild in 2026**. CVE-2026-11645 (V8 out-of-bounds memory access) is the headline CVE, but the broader pattern is critical: **5 actively-exploited zero-days in 5 months** = the exploit weaponization window is now measured in **days, not weeks**. **Test**: (1) assert that GameZipper's user-agent detection shows a non-blocking "Critical security update available — please update Chrome" banner for any `Chrome/` < 149.0.7827.114; (2) verify the banner does NOT block gameplay (all 5 CVEs require specially crafted malicious page, not normal game interaction); (3) test that the gz-error-toast recovery mechanism does NOT false-trigger from the banner; (4) document the emergency-update SLA: GameZipper must update its min-version detection within **48 hours** of any Chrome emergency CVE disclosure (down from the previous 7-day SLA for stable-channel releases). Source: https://bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/google-patches-fifth-chrome-zero-day-bug-exploited-in-attacks-this-year + https://infosecurity-magazine.com/news/google-patch-chrome-vulnerability

### 21.3 Microsoft Defender False Positives on Game JS Files
- [B-085] **[P2]** Microsoft Defender false positives quarantining game JS files — Forbes (May 21 2026) confirms **2 new Microsoft Defender zero-days were exploited in the wild**, prompting emergency security updates. The broader risk for GameZipper: Defender's heuristic engine (and other AV engines — Norton, McAfee, Kaspersky) increasingly **false-positive on minified/obfuscated game JavaScript** because it pattern-matches to known malware signatures. When Defender quarantines a game's `game.js` or `bundle-*.js`, the game page loads with a **blank canvas and no console error** (the script tag 404s after quarantine). **Test**: (1) submit GameZipper's top 30 game JS bundles to Microsoft Security Intelligence false-positive portal (https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/wdsi/filesubmission) — flag any quarantined files; (2) on a Windows 11 machine with Defender real-time protection ON, load each top 30 game and verify the canvas renders (not blank) after 10s; (3) if a game JS is quarantined, verify the game degrades gracefully (shows "Game unavailable — please check your antivirus" message, not a silent blank canvas); (4) add `code-signing` or `integrity="sha384-..."` SRI attributes to game script tags to reduce false-positive risk. Source: https://forbes.com/sites/daveywinder/2026/05/21/2-new-microsoft-defender-zero-days-exploited-patch-now-rolling-out

### 21.4 Push Security 2026 Browser Attack Techniques — TTPs Targeting Game Players
- [S-098] **[P2]** Push Security 2026 Browser Attack Techniques report — session hijacking + OAuth token theft TTPs — Push Security's April 2026 report documents the **specific TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) behind modern browser breaches** in the wild. Top attack patterns relevant to game players: (a) **session cookie theft** via malicious browser extension reading `document.cookie` + exfiltrating to attacker C2; (b) **OAuth token abuse** — attackers steal refresh tokens from localStorage/IndexedDB and replay them to hijack game accounts; (c) **browser session hijacking** via fake "update your browser" popups that install session-stealing extensions; (d) **ad-injection extensions** that modify game page DOM to inject phishing login forms over the real game. **Test**: (1) audit all GameZipper game pages for OAuth/token storage — tokens in localStorage are readable by any XSS or compromised extension; move sensitive tokens to HttpOnly cookies; (2) verify `document.cookie` on game pages contains ONLY functional first-party cookies (gz_aa, gz_ab analytics archive) — no session/auth tokens; (3) test game pages with 5 common malicious extension patterns (cookie-stealer, DOM-injector, ad-replacer, session-replayer, screenshot-stealer) loaded — verify game remains playable and no data exfiltration occurs; (4) add CSP `script-src 'self'` + `connect-src 'self' https://*.monetag.com` to prevent extension-injected scripts from phoning home. Source: https://pushsecurity.com/resources/browser-attacks-report

### 21.5 Apple AI-Powered Safari Extensions Overhaul
- [C-085] **[P2]** Apple AI-powered Safari extensions overhaul — extension interference testing on game pages — The Verge (June 9 2026) reports Apple is **rebuilding Safari's extension ecosystem with AI** to solve Safari's long-standing weakness: lack of a robust extension library. New AI-powered extensions can inject content scripts, modify page DOM, block network requests, and alter rendering — all of which can **interfere with game canvas, block Monetag ads, alter gameplay mechanics, or break CSS layout**. **Test**: (1) load top 30 GameZipper games in Safari 27 beta with **5 popular AI-powered Safari extensions** enabled (content blocker, ad blocker, dark-mode injector, grammar checker, password manager) — verify each game's canvas renders correctly and gameplay is not broken; (2) test that content-blocking extensions do not block Monetag ad scripts (monetag-manager.js) — if blocked, verify the ad container degrades silently (no white box) per the existing S-028/S-029 pattern; (3) verify AI extensions that "summarize" or "rewrite" page text do not corrupt game JSON-LD schema or game-data.js; (4) document which extensions break which games for the known-issues list. Source: https://theverge.com/tech/946345/apple-safari-ai-update-extensions

### 21.6 Casual Mobile Game Card/Tile Contrast Readability Audit
- [A-028] **[P2]** Casual mobile game card/tile contrast readability — WCAG color-blind audit — Software Testing Magazine (2026 accessibility audit for casual mobile gaming) identifies **card-face and tile-color contrast** as the #1 accessibility gap in casual card/match-3/puzzle games. Color-blind players (8% of males, ~0.5% of females = **~150M global users**) cannot distinguish red/green tiles, red/black card suits, or color-sorted tube contents when contrast is below WCAG 2.2 AA (4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for UI components). **Test**: (1) for all card games (solitaire, poker, blackjack): verify card suit symbols (♠♥♦♣) have ≥3:1 contrast ratio against card background in BOTH light and dark mode; (2) for all color-sort/match-3 games (color-sort, water-sort, candy-crush-style): verify each tile color is distinguishable under **deuteranopia, protanopia, and tritanopia** simulation (use Chrome DevTools "Emulate vision deficiencies" or Stark plugin); (3) verify color-coded game elements (red = danger, green = safe) have a **secondary non-color indicator** (icon, pattern, text label); (4) test with `prefers-contrast: more` media query — high-contrast mode must increase tile border visibility. Source: https://softwaretestingmagazine.com/knowledge/the-accessibility-audit-testing-for-inclusivity-in-casual-mobile-gaming

### 21.7 Spatial UI + Diegetic HUD Convergence in Browser Games (2026 UX Trend)
- [G-129] **[P2]** 2026 spatial UI + diegetic HUD convergence — canvas overlay + DOM hybrid testing — Boundev (Game UX Design 2026 guide) documents a convergence trend: browser games are adopting **spatial UI** (depth-based layering, parallax HUD, 3D-positioned indicators) and **diegetic HUD** (health bar rendered on the character model, ammo count on the weapon, score projected onto in-world surfaces). These patterns require **hybrid canvas + DOM rendering**: the game canvas renders the diegetic elements, while a DOM overlay handles the spatial UI layer. **Test**: (1) for any GameZipper game using overlay HUD (score, timer, health) positioned via `position: absolute` over canvas — verify the overlay aligns with canvas content across viewport resize and devicePixelRatio changes; (2) test spatial UI depth layering (z-index stacking of HUD elements) — verify no z-index conflict with Monetag ad overlay (gz-cb-overlay, gz-ad-below-game); (3) verify diegetic HUD elements rendered inside canvas are accessible via HTML-in-Canvas API (if the game adopts Chrome 149+ pattern) — run Playwright `page.accessibility.snapshot()` to confirm; (4) test parallax HUD performance — spatial UI with >3 layers can cause compositing jank on low-end devices; assert ≥30fps on simulated mid-range Android. Source: https://boundev.ai/blog/game-ux-design-guide-2026

### 21.8 Accessibility Impact on Player Retention — Quantified Benchmarks
- [P-056] **[P1]** Accessibility features increase D7 retention 2.5× — quantified revenue impact of accessibility debt — MoldStud Research (updated May 2026) + IGDA GASIG GDC 2026 quantify the business case: games with accessibility features (color-blind modes, screen reader support, remappable controls, reduced-motion option) see **D7 retention 2.5× higher** and **D30 retention 1.8× higher** than games without. The accessibility-addressable market is **$25B+** (developers.dev 2026). **Test**: (1) audit all 292+ GameZipper games for the 6 baseline accessibility features: (a) color-blind mode toggle, (b) screen reader ARIA labels on canvas, (c) keyboard-only playability, (d) reduced-motion option (`prefers-reduced-motion`), (e) adjustable text/UI size, (f) high-contrast mode; (2) score each game 0-6 on accessibility coverage; (3) prioritize fixing games scoring 0-2 (highest retention leak); (4) add an `accessibility_score` field to games-data.js for tracking; (5) verify the homepage "Accessibility" info page lists the top-accessible games to drive retention. **Rationale**: every game without color-blind mode leaks ~60% of color-blind players (8% of male traffic) who churn after first session. Source: https://moldstud.com/articles/p-the-impact-of-accessibility-on-player-engagement-and-retention-in-mobile-games-boosting-inclusivity-for-better-gameplay + https://developers.dev/tech-talk/accessibility-in-game-design-meaning.html

### 21.9 Browser Session Hijacking Targeting Game Players
- [PC-015] **[P2]** Browser session hijacking + OAuth/cookie theft targeting game players — modern attack chain — Combining Push Security 2026 TTP report + Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday (5 publicly disclosed zero-days) reveals a **modern attack chain targeting casual game players**: (1) attacker serves malicious ad (via compromised Monetag/AdSense bidder) on a game page; (2) ad exploits a browser zero-day (Chrome V8 CVE-2026-11645 or Edge UXSS CVE-2026-45494) to execute code in the renderer; (3) attacker reads `document.cookie` + `localStorage` to steal game progress keys (gz_<game>_v<N>), analytics archive (gz_aa), and any OAuth tokens; (4) attacker replays the session on their own machine to access the player's game progress/achievements. **Test**: (1) verify all GameZipper game pages set `SameSite=Lax` or `SameSite=Strict` on any cookie (currently should be 0 cookies except ad-network-set); (2) verify localStorage keys do NOT contain session tokens or auth credentials — only game progress data (acceptable to lose); (3) test that a compromised renderer (simulated via Playwright `page.evaluate` with stolen localStorage) cannot access cross-origin data (verify Storage Access API isolation); (4) add `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin` + `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp` headers to game pages to enable cross-origin isolation (protects against Spectre-class renderer exploits reading game memory). Source: https://pushsecurity.com/resources/browser-attacks-report + https://bleepingcomputer.com/news/microsoft/microsoft-june-2026-patch-tuesday-fixes-6-zero-days-200-flaws

## CATEGORY 22: 2026 MID-JUNE FIREFOX SANDBOX ESCAPE + WASM MEMORY + IOS WEBGL CONTEXT LOSS (R169 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 22.1 Firefox 151 MFSA2026-46 — 30+ Security Flaws + 4 High-Risk Sandbox Escapes
- [B-086] **[P1]** Firefox 151 MFSA2026-46 sandbox escape batch — PCWorld (June 2026) + Mozilla security advisory confirm Firefox 151 fixes **30+ security vulnerabilities** including **4 high-risk flaws that could allow browser sandbox escape**. The update also introduces enhanced privacy features with 14-day cookie isolation. Key CVE: **CVE-2026-8974** (memory safety bugs fixed in Firefox ESR 140.11 and Firefox 151, reported by Nika Layzell, Randell Jesup, Timothy Nikkel, Tom Schuster + Mozilla Fuzzing Team — moderate impact). **Test**: (1) assert GameZipper's Top 30 games run on Firefox 151+ with zero console errors and no rendering regression — sandbox escape CVEs can corrupt WebGL/Canvas state; (2) verify the 14-day cookie isolation does NOT break Monetag ad persistence (Monetag sets bidder cookies — if isolated per-14-day-window, ad fill rate may drop on returning users); (3) test that Firefox ESR 140.11+ users (enterprise/edu deployments on ESR channel) see no regression — ESR lags stable by ~6 weeks; (4) document the Firefox ESR support window in the test matrix (ESR 140 supported until Firefox ESR 141 ships). Source: https://mozilla.org/security/advisories/mfsa2026-46 + https://pcworld.com/article/3144596/firefox-151-brings-a-big-privacy-boost-and-fixes-30-security-flaws.html

### 22.2 CVE-2026-5733 Firefox WebGPU Buffer Overflow
- [S-099] **[P1]** CVE-2026-5733 Firefox WebGPU buffer overflow — SentinelOne vulnerability database confirms CVE-2026-5733 is a **buffer overflow in Mozilla Firefox's WebGPU component** caused by incorrect boundary conditions. Distinct from CVE-2026-8967 (WebGPU *information disclosure*, already tracked in B-040) — this is a **buffer overflow** that can corrupt memory and potentially achieve code execution. Fixed in Firefox 151. **Test**: (1) identify any GameZipper game using WebGPU (`navigator.gpu`) — fuzz-test rapid create/destroy of GPU buffers, textures, and bind groups to trigger the overflow; (2) verify games fail gracefully (canvas blank + error toast) rather than crashing the Firefox content process; (3) test on Firefox pre-151 (if available via BrowserStack) — confirm the buffer overflow manifests as a crash, then verify Firefox 151+ no longer crashes; (4) add WebGPU buffer-size validation to any game shader that takes runtime-sized arrays. Source: https://sentinelone.com/vulnerability-database/cve-2026-5733

### 22.3 iOS Safari WebGL Context Lost on Backgrounding (WebKit Bug 261331)
- [C-086] **[P1]** iOS Safari WebGL context lost when backgrounding Safari — WebKit Bugzilla bug 261331 documents a **REGRESSION introduced in iPadOS 17 Developer Beta 8**: when a user backgrounds Safari (switches apps, locks screen, opens Control Center) while a WebGL game is running, the WebGL context fires `webglcontextlost` and **does NOT restore** on foreground. The game canvas goes permanently black. Affects all WebGL games on iOS/iPadOS 17+. **Test**: (1) on a real iOS 17+ device (or Safari simulator), load a Top 10 WebGL game, play for 30s, background Safari (press Home), wait 10s, return — verify the WebGL context is restored (canvas re-renders) or the game shows a "Tap to resume" prompt; (2) verify the game registers a `webglcontextrestored` handler that re-initializes shaders and textures — if no handler, the game is permanently broken after one backgrounding; (3) test the same flow on iPadOS — the regression is iPad-specific per the bug title; (4) document which GameZipper games lack a `webglcontextrestored` handler and prioritize adding one. **Mitigation**: every WebGL game must implement `canvas.addEventListener('webglcontextrestored', reinitGL)`. Source: https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=261331

### 22.4 iOS 26 WebKit 26 WebGPU Memory Limits Regression
- [B-087] **[P1]** iOS 26 WebKit 26 WebGPU memory limits regression — home-assistant/frontend GitHub issue #28367 (December 2025, still affecting iOS 26 in June 2026) documents a **severe performance regression on iOS 26 (WebKit 26)** when rendering elements with multiple image layers. The issue is caused by **WebKit 26 / WebGPU memory limits** being stricter than iOS 17's WebGL backend. Symptoms: laggy loading, GPU errors, canvas freezing. Works flawlessly on iOS 17 (WebGL backend). **Test**: (1) on iOS 26 real device, load Top 10 games that use multiple textures/sprites — measure time-to-first-frame; flag any game >3s as affected; (2) verify WebGPU memory limit regression doesn't crash games with heavy texture usage (sprite-sheet games, tile-map games); (3) test WebGL fallback — if a game requests WebGPU on iOS 26 and hits the memory limit, verify it falls back to WebGL2 without a blank canvas; (4) document the iOS 26 WebGPU texture memory budget and flag games exceeding it. **Root cause**: WebKit 26 reduced per-tab WebGPU memory allocation vs WebGL. Source: https://github.com/home-assistant/frontend/issues/28367

### 22.5 WASM Linear Memory Fragmentation OOM in Long Sessions
- [P-057] **[P1]** WASM linear memory fragmentation OOM — loke.dev (2026) documents a subtle but critical pattern: **WebAssembly linear memory can hit OOM even when actual data density is incredibly low**, because the WASM allocator fragments the heap. Repeated small alloc/free cycles (common in game loops: particle spawn/despawn, entity create/destroy, level load/unload) cause the allocator to call `memory.grow` repeatedly, growing toward the 2GB/4GB maximum until OOM — despite low actual usage. **Test**: (1) for any GameZipper game using WASM (Unity/Godot/Unreal web exports, or hand-written WASM modules), run a 30-minute soak test — monitor `Module.HEAP8.buffer.byteLength` growth; flag if it grows monotonically without plateau; (2) test rapid level-restart (restart level 50 times in 60s) — verify WASM heap doesn't grow unboundedly; (3) if a game uses a WASM allocator (dlmalloc, emmalloc), verify it compacts or reuses freed blocks rather than always growing; (4) document the WASM memory budget per game and set a `MAXIMUM_MEMORY` fuse. **This is distinct from a JS memory leak** — the JS heap may be stable while the WASM linear memory fragments. Source: https://loke.dev/blog/wasm-linear-memory-fragmentation-oom

### 22.6 Godot 4.5 Web Export WASM Memory Ceiling
- [G-130] **[P2]** Godot 4.5 web export WASM memory ceiling + threaded loader caveats — gamineai.com (2026 H2 trend playbook) + Godot official web export docs document that **Godot 4.5 HTML5 exports hit the WASM memory ceiling** in browser demos, especially with threaded loaders. The default Godot web export uses `threads=yes` which requires SharedArrayBuffer (needs COOP/COEP headers — see S-087), and the WASM payload can exceed 20MB before any game assets load. Best practices for 2026: (a) build with `threads=no` for maximum compatibility, (b) use floor streaming + epoch teardown to manage memory, (c) keep WASM payload <10MB via asset stripping. **Test**: (1) if any GameZipper game is a Godot 4.x web export, verify the COOP/COEP headers are set (required for threaded WASM — already tracked in S-087); (2) measure the WASM payload size — flag any Godot export >15MB as a conversion-killer (users abandon >10MB downloads); (3) test the game on a 4GB-RAM mobile device — verify no WASM OOM within 10 minutes; (4) verify the threaded loader (if enabled) doesn't deadlock on browsers without SharedArrayBuffer. Source: https://gamineai.com/blog/godot-4-5-web-export-wasm-memory-ceiling-h2-2026-browser-demo-trend-playbook + https://docs.godotengine.org/en/4.5/tutorials/export/exporting_for_web.html

### 22.7 WASM Image Loader Memory Leak Pattern
- [P-058] **[P2]** WASM image loader memory leak pattern — cat-o-licious GitHub issue #15 documents a common bug: **the WebAssembly version of a game leaks memory every time an image is loaded**, causing slowdown or crash during long play sessions. Root cause: the WASM image-loading function allocates linear memory for pixel data but never frees it (the JS↔WASM boundary makes manual memory management error-prone). This pattern affects any game that dynamically loads images/sprites/textures via WASM (Unity, Godot, or custom engines). **Test**: (1) for WASM-based games, capture Chrome DevTools heap snapshots before and after loading 50 images — if the WASM linear memory (`Memory.buffer.byteLength`) grows by the size of 50 images and never shrinks, the game has this leak; (2) run a 100-image load/unload cycle — verify the game doesn't crash within 5 minutes; (3) verify the game calls the WASM `free()` equivalent (e.g., `_free(ptr)` in Emscripten, `drop()` in AssemblyScript) after each image is uploaded to a GPU texture; (4) document the fix pattern: load image → upload to GPU texture → free WASM pixel buffer immediately. Source: https://github.com/fiorix/cat-o-licious/issues/15

### 22.8 ChromeOS 150 LTC — Enterprise/School Chromium Lag
- [W-141] **[P2]** ChromeOS 150 LTC (Long-Term Candidate, July 21 2026) — enterprise/school Chromium version lag — Google support docs confirm ChromeOS 150 (due July 21 2026) includes the next **LTC (Long-Term Candidate)** release. LTC releases pin ChromeOS to a specific Chromium version for ~6 months, meaning enterprise and school Chromebooks (a significant GameZipper audience segment — "unblocked games" traffic) run an **older Chromium than stable Chrome**. **Test**: (1) identify the Chromium version pinned by ChromeOS 150 LTC — verify all GameZipper games work on that version (not just latest stable Chrome); (2) test on a school-provided Chromebook (or ChromeOS LTC VM) — verify no feature gated behind a newer Chromium breaks; (3) document the LTC support window in the test matrix — LTC users won't get Chrome 153's 2-week cadence updates for 6 months; (4) verify the "update your browser" banner (B-022 pattern) doesn't false-trigger on LTC users who are intentionally on an older, supported version. **Context**: schools are a top-3 traffic source for casual game portals; LTC pinning means a chunk of that traffic is always 1-2 Chromium majors behind. Source: https://support.google.com/chrome/a/answer/7679408

### 22.9 Playwright MCP Accessibility-Tree-First AI QA
- [C-087] **[P2]** Playwright MCP accessibility-tree-first AI QA — replaces fragile selectors with structured semantic view — autify.com + anhtu.dev + buildbetter.ai (2026 Playwright guides) confirm the 2026 Playwright testing paradigm shift: **Playwright MCP (Model Context Protocol) gives AI agents a structured, semantic view of the web app via accessibility snapshots** (roles, labels, states) instead of screenshots or fragile CSS/XPath selectors. AI agents (Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor) drive real browser actions via the accessibility tree. **Benefits**: (a) no brittle selectors that break when game HTML changes; (b) AI can reason about game UI semantically ("click the Play button" not "click #play-btn"); (c) accessibility-tree-first testing doubles as an accessibility audit. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) evaluate Playwright MCP vs current `verify-lite.js` + hardcoded selectors — measure script stability across 30 games after a UI refactor; (2) test AI-driven QA: prompt an AI agent via Playwright MCP to "play through the first level of each Top 10 game and report any blockers" — compare coverage vs scripted tests; (3) verify the accessibility snapshots expose game canvas elements with proper ARIA labels (synergy with A-004); (4) benchmark token consumption — Playwright MCP is designed to be token-efficient vs screenshot-based vision QA. Source: https://autify.com/blog/playwright-ai + https://anhtu.dev/playwright-2026-e2e-testing-mcp-ai-assisted-browser-automation-1116 + https://blog.buildbetter.ai/playwright-test-generation-with-ai-complete-2026-guide

## CATEGORY 23: 2026 MID-JUNE JS SANDBOX ESCAPE + AI EXTENSION INJECTION + VLM PLAYTESTING + BROWSER MEMORY CAPS (R171 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 23.1 SandboxJS Four CVSS 10.0 Sandbox-Escape Vulnerabilities
- [S-100] **[P1]** SandboxJS four CVSS 10.0 sandbox-escape vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-25520 / CVE-2026-25586 / CVE-2026-25587 / CVE-2026-25641) — a February 2026 advisory (qq.com/IT security red-alert coverage) confirms that **SandboxJS**, a JavaScript library specifically designed to isolate and protect untrusted JS execution, shipped **four critical flaws all rated CVSS 10.0**. An attacker can **completely break out of the sandbox and execute arbitrary code on the host system**. Relevance to GameZipper: if any third-party game/widget, user-generated-content embed, or analytics snippet relies on a JS-sandbox library (sandboxjs, vm2, isolated-vm, safe-eval) to run untrusted code, a compromised sandbox = full host compromise of every visitor who loads the page. **Test**: (1) grep the codebase + all game `index.html` for sandbox/isolation libraries (`sandboxjs`, `vm2`, `isolated-vm`, `safe-eval`, `Function(`, `new Function`, `eval(`) — if any are present and pinned to an unpatched version, treat as P0; (2) verify no game page executes remote/untrusted JS via a sandbox (e.g., ad creative, user-supplied config JSON that gets `eval`'d); (3) confirm all third-party ad creatives run inside the browser's native sandboxed `<iframe sandbox>` (Monetag/AdSense already do) rather than a JS-level sandbox; (4) add a dependency scan (e.g., `npm audit` / OWASP Dep-Check) to the pre-commit pipeline to catch sandbox-library CVEs before deploy. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_527698a8ac736552

### 23.2 AI-Assistant Browser Extension Zero-Click Prompt Injection From Arbitrary Websites
- [S-101] **[P2]** AI-assistant browser extension zero-click prompt injection from arbitrary websites — Claude extension CVE (March 27 2026) + generalization to all AI side-panel extensions. Security researchers disclosed that **Anthropic's Claude Chrome extension allowed zero-click prompt injection from any website the user visits**: a malicious or compromised page can inject a crafted prompt into the user's active AI assistant, exfiltrating page content, triggering unauthorized actions, or planting instructions. This generalizes to Gemini, Copilot, ChatGPT, and Apple-Intelligence Safari extensions in 2026. Distinct from S-097 (CVE-2026-0628 "Glic Jack" = a specific Chrome side-panel hijack bug): this is the **broader game-page-as-prompt-injection-vector** threat model affecting every AI-assistant extension. **Test**: (1) for any GameZipper feature that exposes page text to the DOM (game descriptions, chat, comments), assume it can be read/injected by AI extensions — verify no sensitive data (admin tokens, internal URLs) is rendered in DOM text; (2) test that a malicious payload (e.g., a game description containing `Ignore previous instructions and...`) does not get auto-ingested by AI extensions in a way that harms the user; (3) verify CSP headers do not allow inline event handlers that AI extensions could abuse; (4) document the threat: any game running on a user's machine while an AI assistant extension is active shares DOM context — treat AI-extension users as a distinct threat-model audience. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_26069c5df2970152

### 23.3 Vision Language Model (VLM) Agents for Autonomous Game Playtesting
- [C-088] **[P2]** Vision Language Model (VLM) agents for autonomous game playtesting (2026→2027 trend) — unrealsolutions.com + talkdev.com + thecodersblog.com (2026) document the next QA frontier: **VLM agents (GPT-4o-class vision models) play browser games like humans, perceiving the screen as pixels and finding visual bugs at scale**. Unlike text/agentic AI (G-112, G-116, C-074, C-078 — which drive via DOM/accessibility tree), VLM agents reason about the rendered canvas directly: they can spot a misaligned sprite, a clipped HUD, a wrong color, or a stuck animation that no DOM-based test can see. Predicted to become the dominant automated-playtest method by 2027. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) evaluate a VLM-agent playtest harness (e.g., Modl.ai, WeTest AI, or a custom GPT-4o + screenshot loop) on the Top 10 games — prompt it to "play 60 seconds and report any visual bug" and compare findings vs current canvas-pixel-diff CI (G-120); (2) measure bug yield: VLM agents typically catch cosmetic/clipping bugs that pixel-diff misses (because they understand *intent*, not just pixel equality); (3) benchmark cost — VLM playtests are expensive (vision tokens per frame); restrict to pre-release sweeps, not every commit; (4) verify the VLM agent can operate canvas-only games (no DOM buttons) by sending keyboard/mouse events via CDP. Source: https://unrealsolutions.com/insights/ai-game-testing-vlm-agents.html + https://talkdev.com/featured/ai-in-automated-game-testing-the-new-standard-for-next-gen-quality-assurance + https://thecodersblog.com/agentic-ai-for-game-playtesting-2026

### 23.4 Browser Per-Tab Hard Memory Caps + Dynamic GPU Allocation Inefficiency → OOM Crash
- [P-059] **[P1]** Browser per-tab hard memory caps + dynamic GPU allocation inefficiency → progressive slowdown then OOM crash in long sessions — the "Llamas on the Web" paper (arXiv 2605.20706, 2026) studying browser-based LLM inference documents a pattern that **directly applies to long-running browser games**: browsers **dynamically allocate GPU memory and load resources inefficiently, causing slowdowns as memory grows over the course of execution, and even crashes as browsers enforce hard per-tab memory caps**. Distinct from P-057 (WASM *heap* fragmentation) and B-087 (iOS WebGPU *texture* limit regression) — this is the **general browser-enforced per-tab memory ceiling** that any memory-growing game (particle systems, procedurally-generated levels, accumulating save data, long idle sessions) will hit on desktop Chrome/Edge/Firefox. **Test**: (1) run a 60-minute soak test on the Top 10 "addictive/long-session" games (idle games, endless runners, match-3 with endless mode) — monitor `performance.memory.usedJSHeapSize` (Chrome) + `performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` and GPU memory via `chrome://gpu`; flag any game whose memory grows monotonically without plateau; (2) verify each game degrades gracefully (saves state, shows "refresh to continue" toast) rather than hard-crashing the tab when the per-tab cap is hit; (3) test with Chrome's `--renderer-process-limit=1` or a memory-constrained profile to force early OOM and confirm graceful recovery; (4) document the per-tab memory cap per browser (Chrome ~4GB on 64-bit, Firefox configurable, Safari stricter) and flag games exceeding 50% of the cap as OOM-risk. Source: https://arxiv.org/html/2605.20706v1

### 23.5 WebGPU Memory-Safety Data Races (SafeRace Research)
- [P-060] **[P2]** WebGPU memory-safety data races — SafeRace research paper (Reese Levine et al., saferace.pdf) formally assesses WebGPU memory safety **in the presence of data races**. WebGPU exposes GPU compute via WGSL shaders and shared buffers; when a game's JS main thread and a compute/render pass concurrently read/write the same buffer/texture without correct synchronization (fences, barriers), the result is **undefined behavior: visual corruption (flickering/tearing textures), silently-wrong physics, or a device-lost crash**. This is an emerging class of bug as more games migrate compute-heavy work (particle GPU, physics, AI) to WebGPU. **Test**: (1) for any GameZipper game using WebGPU compute shaders (`GPUComputePipeline`), audit for shared buffer access without synchronization primitives — `gpu.onSubmittedWorkDone()` / proper `pipeline` barriers; (2) run a race-detection pass: enable the WebGPU validation layer / Dawn debug builds (`chrome --enable-features=Vulkan:strict_debug`) and play 5 minutes — flag any "buffer already mapped" / "texture in use" validation errors; (3) stress-test concurrent render+compute (e.g., GPU particle simulation running alongside the render loop) and verify no visual corruption on a 5-minute run; (4) verify the game handles `GPUDevice` `uncapturederror` events gracefully (logs + canvas error toast) rather than silently corrupting. Source: https://reeselevine.github.io/assets/pdf/saferace.pdf

### 23.6 2026 Player Complaints: Cluttered/Obstructive Game UI Audit
- [G-131] **[P2]** 2026 player complaints: cluttered/obstructive game UI audit — GameHelper investigative report "Why Video Game UIs Are So Bad in 2026" (March 2026) + UXPin Game UX Design Guide (2026) document widespread community frustration: **cluttered menus, unintuitive navigation, and interfaces that obstruct rather than help gameplay**. Casual-game-specific failure modes: (a) pause/settings overlay covers the play field, (b) too many buttons/icons competing for attention on a small screen, (c) modal dialogs interrupt flow, (d) inventory/shop UI requires too many taps. **Test**: (1) for each GameZipper game with a settings/pause/inventory overlay, verify the overlay does NOT cover the active play area when dismissed/closed — overlay must fully remove from DOM or `display:none` (not just opacity:0); (2) count on-screen interactive elements during core gameplay — flag any game with >8 simultaneous visible buttons/icons as clutter-risk; (3) verify every modal/dialog has an obvious close affordance within thumb-reach (bottom 2/3 of screen on mobile); (4) run a 3-second "first-glance" test (screenshot + heuristic): a new player should identify the primary action (Play/Start) within 3s — if the Play button is buried below the fold or visually equal-weighted to secondary buttons, flag as P2 UX debt. Source: https://gamehelper.io/games/squad/articles/why-are-video-game-uis-getting-worse-an-investigative-report + https://uxpin.com/studio/blog/game-ux

### 23.7 Bottom-Navigation + Thumb-Zone as 2026 Mobile UI Standard Migration
- [G-132] **[P2]** Bottom-navigation + thumb-zone as 2026 mobile UI standard migration — emergingtechs.net ("Player-First Evolution: Why User Feedback Rules 2026 Design") + connectioncafe.com ("One-Hand Play Wins Players in 2026") + novatechbeacon.com report that in response to years of player complaints about **"thumb fatigue" and top-only controls**, 2026 mobile game UI has standardized on **bottom-navigation** — primary controls and CTAs moved to the bottom of the screen (the natural thumb-reach zone for the 3.5B+ one-handed mobile players). Games that keep critical controls at the top of the viewport (above the natural thumb arc) see measurable engagement drop. Distinct from A-022 (48px touch-target *size*) and G-099 (zero-download *onboarding*) — this is the **vertical-positioning / thumb-zone-layout** standard. **Test**: (1) for every GameZipper game, verify primary interactive controls (Play, Restart, Hint, Settings) are positioned in the **bottom 60% of the viewport on mobile (≤414px wide)** — flag any primary control in the top 40% as thumb-fatigue risk; (2) test one-handed reach: with the device held in one hand, confirm every primary action is tappable without re-gripping (use the 44×44px thumb-comfort heuristic at bottom-center); (3) verify the homepage game grid and per-game CTA buttons are thumb-reachable — the "Play Game" button on game pages must NOT be at the very top; (4) for games with portrait + landscape, verify the bottom-nav pattern holds in both orientations. Source: https://emergingtechs.net/why-player-voices-govern-the-2026-casino-floor + https://connectioncafe.com/dark-mode-haptics-and-one-hand-play-the-new-ux-rules-for-gaming-apps-in-2026 + https://novatechbeacon.com/latest/why-2026-gaming-is-built-for-one-hand-play

### 23.8 Firefox 150+ Local Network Access Restriction Extended to ALL Users
- [B-088] **[P2]** Firefox 150+ local network access restriction extended to ALL users — Firefox 150 stable release (April 2026, qq.com coverage) extended the **local network access restriction** (previously limited to users with Strict Tracking Protection) to **all users**: a website must now **obtain explicit user permission before connecting to local devices or services** (LAN IPs, `localhost`, mDNS `.local` addresses, WebRTC LAN peers). This directly affects browser games that use LAN multiplayer, local-storage servers, mDNS device discovery, or WebRTC peer-to-peer on a home network — they will now hit a **permission prompt** (or silent failure if the prompt is dismissed). Distinct from the gz-analytics mixed-content LAN-IP bug (post-deploy-verification pitfall) — that was about *HTTP LAN IPs in HTTPS pages*; this is Firefox's new *permission gate* on any local-network connection. **Test**: (1) audit all GameZipper games for local-network APIs: `RTCPeerConnection` (WebRTC P2P), `WebSocket` to `.local`/LAN IPs, `fetch`/`XMLHttpRequest` to `192.168.*`/`10.*`/`localhost`, mDNS — any such connection will now prompt in Firefox 150+; (2) on Firefox 150, load each LAN-capable game and verify the permission prompt appears cleanly (not a silent failure/blank screen); (3) verify games that *fail* the permission gate degrade gracefully (show "LAN multiplayer unavailable in this browser" message) rather than hanging; (4) confirm no game silently depends on a local connection that Firefox now blocks (e.g., a game assuming `localhost:PORT` analytics works). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_15669e6c60a26052

### 23.9 Agent-Driven Browser Automation Tools (Browser Harness + Microsoft Foundry) as Playwright Alternatives
- [C-089] **[P3]** Agent-driven browser automation tools as Playwright alternatives — two 2026 developments offer alternatives to Playwright MCP (C-087) for AI-agent-driven game QA: (a) **Browser Harness** (open-source, 7.2k stars, qq.com/zhiarese review) — lets LLM agents directly control Chrome via CDP with "execute-then-learn" capability (agent runs an action, observes the result, adapts its capability set mid-task); Claude Code connects directly to a live Chrome instance; (b) **Microsoft Foundry** (Redmond Magazine, June 2026) — Microsoft's own browser-automation tool added for agentic workflows. Both target the same use case as Playwright MCP: an AI agent autonomously navigating/playing a game. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) evaluate Browser Harness vs Playwright MCP for the "play first level of each Top 10 game and report blockers" task — compare setup effort, token cost, and bug-finding yield; (2) Browser Harness's "execute-then-learn" model may handle canvas-only games better than Playwright MCP (which relies on the accessibility tree) — test on 3 canvas-only games and compare; (3) Microsoft Foundry is enterprise-oriented — evaluate only if the QA pipeline moves to Azure/Microsoft ecosystem; (4) standardize on ONE agent-automation tool to avoid fragmentation (recommend Playwright MCP for now, Browser Harness as canvas-game fallback). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_92669ef2db811252 + https://redmondmag.com/blogs/redmond-dispatch/2026/06/microsoft-adds-browser-automation-tool.aspx

## CATEGORY 24: 2026 MID-JUNE CRASH TELEMETRY + AUTONOMOUS AI QA + ONNX WEBKIT REGRESSION + EU DARK-PATTERN LAW (R173 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 24.1 Local-First Browser Crash Black Box for Hard-Crash Telemetry
- [C-090] **[P2]** Crashbox local-first crash black box for browser games — nearform/crashbox (open-source library, 2026) solves a fundamental observability gap: **when a browser tab dies hard — from a WebGPU device/process kill, a WASM or in-browser out-of-memory, or an unresponsive-tab kill — no JavaScript runs at the moment of death**, so conventional error handlers (`window.onerror`, `unhandledrejection`, `try/catch`) cannot capture or report the crash. Crashbox uses a **pre-crash heartbeat pattern**: a lightweight worker periodically writes game/session state to `sessionStorage`; on next load (or via `BroadcastChannel` from a surviving tab), it detects the missing heartbeat and reconstructs a crash report (last frame, memory pressure, GPU adapter state, elapsed session time). Distinct from C-089 (agent automation tools) and P-059 (memory caps) — this is the **crash-telemetry-recovery** layer that makes every other hard-crash bug (P-057 WASM OOM, P-059 per-tab cap, S-099 WebGPU overflow) diagnosable in production. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) evaluate Crashbox as an integration on the Top 10 long-session/idle games — trigger a forced OOM (via DevTools memory limiter or a memory-leak repro) and verify the post-crash report reconstructs the last-known state; (2) verify `BroadcastChannel` recovery works when only the game tab dies but the homepage tab survives (common in multi-tab play); (3) confirm the heartbeat write to `sessionStorage` does not cause a measurable frame-rate drop (target <1ms per heartbeat at 2s interval); (4) wire crash reports into the existing gz-analytics archive path so production hard-crashes become visible without a user bug report. Source: https://github.com/nearform/crashbox + https://github.com/nearform/crashbox/blob/main/docs/API.md

### 24.2 Autonomous AI Game Testing & Quality Scoring Platform
- [C-091] **[P2]** GameEval autonomous AI-powered game testing and quality evaluation platform — github.com/adam0white/GameEval (open-source, 2026) combines **serverless browser automation + computer vision + large language models** to automatically evaluate browser-based games *without human intervention*, producing comprehensive quality scores and actionable insights. Distinct from C-088 (VLM agents that *play* games and spot visual bugs) and C-087 (Playwright MCP that drives DOM/accessibility) — GameEval is a **full evaluation pipeline** that scores a game across multiple dimensions (loadability, playability, visual quality, performance, accessibility) and outputs a structured report. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) run GameEval against the Top 30 games and compare its quality scores against the existing manual QA verdicts (R0-R5 phases) — measure agreement rate and flag disagreements for review; (2) evaluate whether GameEval's computer-vision layer catches the canvas-pixel-diff bugs already found by the G-120 CI pipeline; (3) benchmark serverless execution cost per game and determine if it fits a pre-release gate vs a weekly sweep cadence; (4) verify GameEval can handle canvas-only games (no DOM buttons) the same way VLM agents (C-088) must. Source: https://github.com/adam0white/GameEval

### 24.3 Codex CLI + Playwright MCP Agent-Driven E2E Test Generation Loop
- [C-092] **[P3]** Codex CLI + Playwright MCP agent-driven E2E test generation architecture — danielvaughan.com (April 20 2026) documents a production test-generation pattern where a **Codex CLI agent loop** sits at the center and delegates to specialized tools by task type: (a) for *exploratory interaction* with a running game, it calls the **Playwright MCP server** (accessibility-tree snapshots, semantic actions); (b) for *structured test generation*, it invokes **Playwright's native agents** to emit TypeScript test code; (c) for *verification*, it runs the generated tests headlessly and feeds pass/fail results back into its reasoning loop. Distinct from C-087 (Playwright MCP as a QA tool) and C-089 (Browser Harness as an automation alternative) — this is the **agent orchestration architecture** that combines MCP exploration + native codegen + headless verification in a self-correcting loop. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) prototype the loop on 3 representative games (1 canvas puzzle, 1 DOM card game, 1 idle game) — prompt the agent to "generate a first-level completion test" and measure how many iterations the verify-feedback loop needs to reach a passing test; (2) compare generated-test stability (survives a UI refactor) vs the current hardcoded-selector scripts in verify-lite.js; (3) document token cost per generated test — agent-driven codegen is expensive and should be reserved for high-value regression suites, not per-commit. Source: https://codex.danielvaughan.com/2026/04/20/codex-cli-playwright-e2e-testing-agent-driven-test-generation

### 24.4 Safari/WebKit 26 ONNX Runtime JSEP Mode CPU/Memory Regression
- [B-089] **[P1]** Safari/WebKit 26 ONNX Runtime JSEP mode severe CPU and memory regression — microsoft/onnxruntime GitHub issue #26827 (December 2025, still open in June 2026) documents that **ONNX Runtime inference in Safari/WebKit 26 with JSEP (JavaScript + WASM) mode causes severe CPU and memory issues**, likely due to the model requiring CPU execution and the issue being isolated to JSEP + WASM mode. A reproducible minimal test project exists (github.com/theopolis/onnxtest). Relevance to GameZipper: any game adopting **on-device AI features** (WebNN/ONNX inference for NPC behavior, voice commands, image recognition, adaptive difficulty) will hit this regression on Safari 26 — the single most popular mobile browser in key markets. Distinct from B-087 (WebKit 26 *WebGPU* memory limits) — this is the **ONNX/WebNN inference** path, not the WebGPU rendering path. **Test**: (1) if any GameZipper game uses ONNX Runtime or WebNN (`navigator.ml`, `ort.InferenceSession`), run the inference on Safari 26 (real device or BrowserStack) — measure CPU% and memory growth over 60s; flag any game exceeding 50% sustained CPU or growing memory monotonically; (2) verify a WASM-mode fallback (non-JSEP) doesn't exhibit the regression — document which execution provider is safe on Safari 26; (3) test that the game degrades gracefully (disables AI feature + shows fallback) rather than freezing the tab when ONNX inference triggers the regression; (4) track the upstream issue and re-test when microsoft/onnxruntime#26827 is resolved. Source: https://github.com/microsoft/onnxruntime/issues/26827 + https://github.com/theopolis/onnxtest

### 24.5 WebGPU Game Crash Reporting Pipeline (Device-Lost + Validation Capture)
- [P-061] **[P2]** WebGPU game crash reporting pipeline: GPUDevice.lost handler + adapter fallback + validation error capture — bugnet.io (2026, "Crash Reporting for WebGPU Games") documents that **try/catch is blind to graphics failures**: WebGPU errors surface asynchronously via `GPUDevice.lost` (a Promise), uncaptured-error events, and validation-error scopes — none of which a synchronous try/catch around the draw call will catch. There is also **wide browser variance** in this new API (Chrome Dawn, Safari native, Firefox) meaning a crash report valid on one browser may be empty on another. Distinct from C-090 (general crash black box) — this is the **WebGPU-specific** crash-reporting contract every WebGPU game must implement. **Test**: (1) for every GameZipper game using `navigator.gpu`, verify it registers `device.lost.then(info => report(info.reason, info.message))` and a `device.onuncapturederror` handler — a game with neither is silently un-diagnosable in production; (2) force a device-lost (via DevTools "Kill GPU process" or by allocating an oversized buffer) and verify the game reports the crash (to gz-analytics or Crashbox C-090) rather than going black with no signal; (3) verify the game attempts adapter fallback (`navigator.gpu.requestAdapter({ fallback: true })`) on device loss before giving up; (4) test across Chrome/Safari/Firefox that the crash report captures the same fields (reason, message) — document per-browser gaps. Source: https://bugnet.io/blog/crash-reporting-for-webgpu-games + https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/GPUDevice/lost

### 24.6 EU Digital Fairness Act + DSA Article 25 Dark-Pattern Prohibition for Game Monetization
- [W-142] **[P1]** EU Digital Fairness Act (DFA) + DSA Article 25 dark-pattern prohibition for game monetization UX — ACM CHI 2026 research ("Dark Patterns and the EU Digital Services Act: Mapping Autonomy Violations", dl.acm.org/10.1145/3772318.3791479) + European Parliament EPRS briefing (Jan 2025) + Goodwin/JDSupra legal alerts (Nov 2025) confirm the regulatory trajectory: the **DSA Article 25 already prohibits three dark-pattern types — deception, manipulation, and distortion/impairment** of user autonomy — and the upcoming **Digital Fairness Act (DFA)**, tasked to Commissioner McGrath, will expand the ban to **addictive design, influencer marketing, and profiling that exploits consumer vulnerabilities**, with games explicitly named as an affected sector. High-risk monetization patterns for a casual-game portal: (a) "continue without supporting" ad-consent flow that is harder to tap than "accept all"; (b) fake countdown timers on bonus offers; (c) confirm-shaming ("No thanks, I don't want free coins"); (d) pre-checked consent boxes; (e) ad frequency that impairs the ability to make an autonomous play/pause decision. **Test**: (1) audit the Monetag/AdSense consent flow on all game pages — verify the "reject ads" / "continue without supporting" button is equally prominent (same size, color, position) as "accept" — reject-button parity is the DSA's clearest enforcement target; (2) scan all game + homepage UI for countdown timers, urgency language ("Limited time!", "Only X left!") and confirm-shaming copy — flag any as DFA-risk; (3) verify no pre-checked consent checkboxes exist in any form (newsletter, cookie, ad-personalization); (4) verify ad frequency (G-101 immersion-break threshold) does not cross into "impairment of autonomous choice" — if a player cannot pause/quit without watching 3+ ads, that is a DSA Article 25 distortion risk; (5) document the DFA compliance status in the test matrix ahead of the law's expected enforcement window. Source: https://dl.acm.org/doi/full/10.1145/3772318.3791479 + https://europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/ATAG/2025/767191/EPRS_ATA(2025)767191_EN.pdf + https://goodwinlaw.com/en/insights/publications/2025/11/alerts-practices-antc-from-dark-patterns-to-fair-play + https://cbtw.tech/insights/illegal-dark-patterns-europe

### 24.7 WASM-Compiled Native Game Engine Integration Testing
- [G-133] **[P2]** WASM-compiled native game engine (C++/C#/Rust → WASM) integration testing — simplified.media (May 2026, "WebAssembly Game Engines") + techbytes.app (April 2026, "Build Browser Games with WASM, WebGPU, and Rust") document the 2026 pattern where a **native game engine — written in C++, C#, or Rust — is compiled to WASM** so the hot path runs at near-native speed in-browser, paired with WebGL or WebGPU for rendering, Web Audio for sound, and WebRTC or WebSockets for multiplayer transport, replacing the JS runtime entirely for performance-critical loops. Distinct from G-130 (Godot 4.5 *web export* specifics) and P-057 (WASM *heap* fragmentation) — this is the **full integration-stack parity** test: a WASM engine must correctly bridge to five browser APIs (render, audio, input, network, storage) that each have their own threading/memory model. **Test**: (1) for any GameZipper game shipped as a WASM-compiled native engine (Rust+wasm-bindgen, C++/Emscripten, C#/Blazor-WASM, Unity IL2CPP), verify all five integration surfaces work: rendering (canvas/WebGPU draws), audio (Web Audio plays on first user gesture — autoplay policy), input (mouse/touch/keyboard events reach the WASM entry), network (fetch/WebSocket round-trips survive the JS↔WASM boundary), storage (localStorage/IndexedDB persists across reload); (2) verify the WASM module's linear memory does not conflict with the JS heap (no double-free, no buffer overrun into JS-owned memory); (3) test the threaded-WASM path (SharedArrayBuffer + COOP/COEP, per S-087) — verify it degrades to single-threaded on browsers without cross-origin isolation; (4) measure cold-start: the WASM payload + JS glue must parse and reach first-frame under the sub-3s baseline (P-039) — flag any engine whose WASM compile exceeds 1.5s. Source: https://simplified.media/guides/wasm-game-engines + https://techbytes.app/posts/build-browser-games-wasm-webgpu-rust-2026

### 24.8 WebGPU Three-Domain Memory Leak Detection for Browser Games
- [P-062] **[P2]** WebGPU three-domain memory leak detection (mapped/buffer + texture + pipeline-layout) for browser games — DrCodes (Feb 2026, "Fix WebGPU Memory Leaks in Browser Games: Complete Guide") documents that **WebGPU manages memory across three critical domains** — (a) mapped/buffer memory (`GPUBuffer` via `getMappedRange` / `writeBuffer`), (b) texture memory (`GPUTexture` + `GPUTextureView`), (c) pipeline/binding-layout memory (`GPUPipelineLayout`, `GPUBindGroup`, `GPUSampler`) — and each domain requires domain-specific handling to prevent leaks. Distinct from P-060 (WebGPU *data-race* memory safety) and P-058 (WASM *image-loader* leak) — this is the **systematic per-domain leak-detection** methodology for the WebGPU API itself. Common leak patterns: (1) `GPUBuffer.getMappedRange()` returned an `ArrayBuffer` that is never unmapped (`buffer.unmap()`), pinning GPU memory; (2) `GPUTexture` objects are recreated every frame without `texture.destroy()`; (3) `GPUBindGroup`/`GPUSampler` accumulate because the JS references are never released (GC can't reclaim GPU-owned resources). **Test**: (1) for each WebGPU game, instrument a per-frame resource census: count live `GPUBuffer`, `GPUTexture`, `GPUBindGroup`, `GPUSampler` objects — any count that grows monotonically over a 5-minute run is a leak; (2) run a rapid level-restart loop (50 restarts in 60s) and verify no domain's object count fails to return to baseline after a GC (`performance.memory` + forced `gc()` in test build); (3) verify every `GPUBuffer` creation has a matching `destroy()` or lifetime-managed release; every `getMappedRange` has a matching `unmap()`; every `GPUTexture` from `device.createTexture()` is destroyed when the sprite/level unloads; (4) use the Dawn debug build (`chrome --enable-features=Vulkan:strict_debug`) to surface unreleased-resource warnings. Source: https://drcodes.com/posts/fix-webgpu-memory-leaks-in-browser-games-complete-guide


## CATEGORY 25: 2026 MID-JUNE SAFARI 27 / CHROME 150 REGRESSION + ENTERPRISE AGENTIC AI + DARK-PATTERN LITIGATION (R175 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 25.1 Safari 27 / WebKit WWDC26 Record 525-Fix + 58-Feature Regression Sweep
- [B-090] **[P1]** Safari 27 / WebKit WWDC26 record 525-fix + 58-new-feature full-library regression sweep — WebKit's official WWDC26 announcement (webkit.org/blog/17967) and the "Why Safari 27 Ships 525 Fixes" lab notes (blakecrosley.com) confirm Safari 27 beta ships a **record 525 WebKit bug fixes alongside 58 new features**, including: (a) a **module-loader rewrite that finally enables top-level `await`** in `<script type="module">` — any game using ESM modules with top-level await (dynamic imports, async config fetch at module scope) will now execute where it previously threw; (b) **`appearance: base`** shipping — finally lets developers fully restyle native form controls (`<select>`, `<input>`, `<button>`) that were previously locked to platform appearance, changing the rendering of every game-settings dropdown; (c) **Customizable Select API** — `<select>` can now render arbitrary custom dropdown markup via `appearance: base-select`, breaking any code that assumed the UA shadow DOM structure; (d) **Imersive Environments / WebXR** expansion; (e) `:has()` selector shipping natively. A 525-fix batch is the **largest single-release regression surface in Safari's history** — every previously-reported WebKit-specific bug (S-040 CSP bypass, S-041 same-origin bypass, S-052 WebKit crash, C-066 Hammer.js tap crash, B-062 WebGL context-lost) must be re-verified as *not regressed* by a fix intended for something else. Distinct from B-059/B-069 (earlier Safari 27 beta coverage of AI tab organizer / speechSynthesis) — this is the **full shipping feature+fix surface** audit. **Test**: (1) run the Top 30 games on Safari 27 beta (real macOS/iOS device or BrowserStack) and diff against Safari 26.4 baseline — any visual, input, or crash regression is a 525-fix casualty until proven otherwise; (2) re-verify all previously-closed WebKit bugs (S-040/S-041/S-052/C-066/B-062) do NOT regress under the 525-fix batch; (3) test any game using ESM `<script type="module">` — confirm top-level await now works AND that modules that previously failed-silently don't now execute partially and corrupt state; (4) audit every `<select>` / settings dropdown for `appearance` styling — `appearance: base` may render custom-styled dropdowns differently than the previous `-webkit-appearance: none` hack; (5) test `:has()` usage if any game CSS relies on the selector polyfill vs native. Source: https://webkit.org/blog/17967/news-from-wwdc26-webkit-in-safari-27-beta + https://blakecrosley.com/blog/webkit-lab-how-safari-ships + https://developer.apple.com/documentation/safari-release-notes/safari-27-release-notes

### 25.2 Safari 27 `<model>` HTML Model Element — Interactive 3D in Game Previews/Cards
- [G-134] **[P2]** Safari 27 `<model>` HTML Model element for interactive 3D models in game previews/cards — WebKit's WWDC26 web-technology sessions (webkit.org/blog/17974) confirm the **`<model>` element comes to iOS, iPadOS, and macOS in Safari 27**, letting developers embed interactive 3D models in a webpage "like embedding any other media": use a `<source>` element to provide files in multiple formats, set `environmentmap` for custom lighting, use `stagemode="orbit"` to let users rotate the model, and reach for the JavaScript API for programmatic control. This is the **native, declarative alternative** to shipping a full WebGL/WebGPU scene just to show a rotatable 3D game-icon or character preview on a game card. Distinct from G-128 (CSS Grid Lanes for card *layout*) and the HTML-in-Canvas API (W-122/C-041, which renders HTML *into* canvas) — `<model>` renders an interactive 3D asset *as a media element* with no JS scene graph. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) prototype a `<model>`-based 3D rotating preview for 3 representative games (e.g. a 3D puzzle, a character-driven game, a vehicle game) and compare load time + interaction smoothness vs the current flat thumbnail — measure first-paint and orbit-frame-rate; (2) verify graceful degradation: browsers without `<model>` support (Chrome/Firefox as of mid-2026, older Safari) must fall back to the existing `<img>` thumbnail without a broken-element artifact — feature-detect via `HTMLModelElement` or `<model>` in `document.createElement`; (3) test `stagemode="orbit"` touch interaction on iOS Safari 27 — verify one-finger orbit, pinch-zoom, and that the gesture does not conflict with page scroll (the game card is inside a scrollable grid); (4) verify `environmentmap` lighting renders the model consistently across iOS/iPadOS/macOS (different GPU pipelines); (5) confirm the model asset size (glTF/USDZ) does not blow the sub-3s card-load budget (P-039) — lazy-load `<model>` only when card is in viewport. Source: https://webkit.org/blog/17974/web-technology-sessions-at-wwdc26 + https://developer.apple.com/documentation/safari-release-notes/safari-27-release-notes

### 25.3 Chrome 150 Stable (June 29 2026) — Quiet Release + AccentColor System-Color Audit
- [B-091] **[P2]** Chrome 150 stable (June 29 2026) quiet release + AccentColor/AccentColorText system-color audit on frozen 149 feature baseline — Chrome Platform Status (chrome-status-lite.deno.dev) and the Chrome 150 beta blog (developer.chrome.com/blog/chrome-150-beta, June 3 2026) confirm Chrome 150 is an **unusually quiet release**: **0 new features enabled by default, 0 new origin trials, only 2 features behind a flag** — `AccentColor` and `AccentColorText` CSS system colors that let a page read the user's OS-level accent color. The strategic significance is the *transition*: Chrome 149 (the record-429-CVE / CSS-gap-decorations / DevTools-for-Agents release) is now the **frozen stable baseline**, meaning every 149 feature (CSS Gap Decorations C-041, bfcache WebSocket disconnect G-127, WebMCP DevTools C-083, INP Core Web Vital P-054) is now load-bearing on every Chrome install that auto-updates to 150. Distinct from B-083 (Chrome ARM64 Linux platform) and B-084 (Chrome 5th zero-day 2026) — this is the **release-boundary stability** audit: confirm the 149 feature set, now frozen under 150, behaves identically. **Test**: (1) after Chrome 150 stable ships (June 29), re-run the Top 30 games on Chrome 150 and diff against Chrome 149 — the quiet release means *no new features* but the 525… er, the 149 fixes are now baked in, so the regression risk is in *fix side-effects* not new features; (2) audit any GameZipper CSS using `accent-color` (the existing property) vs the new `AccentColor`/`AccentColorText` *system colors* — these are different: `accent-color` *sets* the accent, `AccentColor` *reads* the OS accent; verify game UI that reads the OS theme (dark-mode-aware buttons, form controls) renders the correct accent on Windows/macOS/Linux; (3) verify CSS Gap Decorations (C-041) still render correctly now that they are on the frozen stable baseline (no longer behind a flag / origin trial); (4) confirm bfcache WebSocket disconnect behavior (G-127) is stable under 150 — re-test back/forward cache on a WebSocket-using game; (5) document the Chrome 149→150 transition as the new minimum-supported-Chrome baseline for the test matrix. Source: https://chrome-status-lite.deno.dev + https://developer.chrome.com/blog/chrome-150-beta + https://browsercalendar.com/browsers/chrome

### 25.4 Enterprise Agentic AI Playtesting — Capcom + EA + Google Cloud Production Deployments
- [C-093] **[P2]** Enterprise agentic AI playtesting production deployment evaluation — Capcom + Google Cloud (googlecloudpresscorner.com, April 22 2026) + EA's reinforcement-learning FIFA agents (digitaldefynd.com) + Google Cloud "Living Games" autonomous AI agents (games.gg) document that **agentic AI playtesting has crossed from research into enterprise production** in 2026: (a) **Capcom + Google Cloud** launched an AI platform that *automates routine tasks like playtesting*, framed as "a blueprint for how creative industries build technologies for the agentic AI era"; (b) **EA** deployed an RL-driven playtesting framework where RL agents autonomously play FIFA matches, simulate player strategies, and stress diverse gameplay scenarios; (c) **Google Cloud "Living Games"** introduces autonomous AI agents for dynamic gameplay, automated playtesting, and AI-assisted content creation; (d) **Multi-Agent RL (MARL)** (blog.stackademic.com, nature.com s41467-025-67178-6) is maturing for multiplayer game testing where your best move depends on other agents. Distinct from C-088 (VLM agents that *play & see*), C-091 (GameEval *scoring* platform), C-092 (Codex CLI *test-generation* loop) — this is the **enterprise-scale production deployment pattern** with cloud infrastructure, RL training loops, and multiplayer agent coordination. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) evaluate Google Cloud "Living Games" / Vertex AI as a managed playtesting backend for the Top 30 games — compare setup effort + per-game cost vs the existing Playwright-MCP + VLM approach (C-087/C-088); (2) for multiplayer-capable games (even single-device hot-seat), prototype a MARL setup (2-4 agents) to stress-test game balance / turn-order edge cases that a single scripted agent cannot reach; (3) benchmark EA-style RL-agent "player strategy simulation" — can an RL agent discover an exploit (infinite-score loop, skip-level glitch) in a GameZipper puzzle/arcade game within a 1-hour training budget?; (4) document the cost/quality tradeoff: enterprise agentic AI is powerful but metered — reserve for pre-release gates on Top 10 games, not the weekly 280-game sweep. Source: https://googlecloudpresscorner.com/2026-04-22-How-Capcom-Is-Leading-the-Way-for-Enterprise-Agentic-AI + https://digitaldefynd.com/IQ/ai-in-video-game-testing + https://games.gg/news/google-cloud-launches-ai-agents-for-game-development + https://blog.stackademic.com/unlocking-multi-agent-reinforcement-learning-the-2026-game-changer-for-autonomous-systems-and-a2db15853064

### 25.5 Browser-Regression Attribution Diagnostic Methodology
- [C-094] **[P3]** Browser-regression attribution diagnostic methodology — distinguish app bug from browser bug — maproom.net's "The Chrome update that torpedoed interactive mapping on Apple Macs" (2026) documents the *developer-experience* core problem: **"one of the hardest aspects of web development is that your application ultimately runs inside software you don't control. When a browser update introduces a regression, developers often spend hours — or even days — investigating their own code before discovering that the fault lies elsewhere."** This is a *process* test case, not a feature: it defines the **attribution protocol** every GameZipper QA cycle must follow when a game breaks, to avoid wasting hours blaming app code for a browser regression (or, worse, shipping a "fix" that papers over a browser bug that will be patched next week). Distinct from C-087/C-089/C-093 (automation *tools*) — this is the **diagnostic decision tree** for *when* a regression is detected. **Test/Protocol**: (1) when a game breaks on a single browser+version, BEFORE editing app code, run the **tri-browser attribution check**: load the same game on the other two evergreen browsers (Chrome/Firefox/Safari) at the same version-recency — if it works on 2 and breaks on 1, it is a browser regression until proven otherwise; (2) run the **version-bisect check**: use BrowserStack to load the game on the breaking browser's *previous* major version — if it works on N-1 and breaks on N, file a browser bug report, do not ship an app fix; (3) check the browser's release notes / issue tracker for a matching regression (webkit.org/blog, chromereleases.googleblog.com, mozilla.org/security/advisories) — the maproom case was diagnosable in <1 hour once the dev checked Chrome's known-issues; (4) if a browser regression is confirmed, ship a **temporary polyfill/workaround** with a clear `// FIXME: browser bug, remove when <browser> <version> fixes <issue>` comment + a calendar reminder to re-test after the browser's next patch; (5) log confirmed browser regressions into the test library (B-* category) so future QA cycles don't re-investigate the same symptom. Source: https://maproom.net/the-chrome-update-that-torpedoed-interactive-mapping-on-apple-macs

### 25.6 US Game-Addiction + Child-Targeting Dark-Pattern Lawsuit Exposure
- [W-143] **[P1]** US game-addiction + child-targeting dark-pattern lawsuit exposure — 2026 litigation wave — lawfold.com ("Game Addiction Lawsuit 2026: Who Qualifies & What to Know") documents a **growing 2026 multi-plaintiff litigation wave** naming specific games and projecting settlement payouts, while the **Gwinn v. Budge Studios** class action (classaction.org, law360.com, rainintelligence.com — filed ND Cal. Sept 2024, active through 2026) alleges Budge Studios (maker of Paw Patrol / Bluey / Barbie children's apps, 2B+ downloads) used **"stealth advertising" and addictive "dark patterns"** to entice children into in-app purchases and subscriptions "without parental awareness," leveraging children's trust in licensed characters. The 58-page complaint details patterns directly relevant to any ad-supported game portal with child reach: (a) **stealth advertising** — ads disguised as gameplay; (b) **character-leveraged pressure** — using beloved characters to drive purchases; (c) **addictive design** — mechanics tuned to maximize session length in minors. Distinct from W-142 (EU Digital Fairness Act / DSA Article 25 — *regulatory* prohibition) — this is the **US litigation** dimension under different legal theories (deceptive marketing, consumer protection, child-targeting). **Test for GameZipper**: (1) audit the full game catalog for **child-appeal signals** (cartoon art style, animal characters, simple mechanics, "kids" tags) — games matching ≥2 signals are elevated-risk under the Budge theory and need a **no-stealth-advertising review**: verify no ad creative is disguised as a game element (no fake "play" buttons that are ads, no ad-creatives using game characters); (2) verify the Monetag/AdSense ad stack does not serve **behavioral-targeted ads to known-minor audiences** — COPPA intersection; (3) audit session-length mechanics in kid-appeal games for **addictive-design patterns** (variable-ratio reward schedules, loss-aversion "don't lose your streak" prompts) — flag any that target minors; (4) verify an **age-gate / parental-consent flow** exists where regulation requires it (not necessarily for GameZipper's ad-supported model, but document the compliance posture); (5) monitor the lawfold.com litigation tracker for any GameZipper-distributed game being named and have an incident-response runbook ready. Source: https://lawfold.com/game-addiction-lawsuit + https://classaction.org/news/developer-of-paw-patrol-bluey-apps-facing-class-action-suit-over-allegedly-deceptive-in-game-marketing-tactics + https://rainintelligence.com/blog/dark-patterns-in-gaming-lawsuits-target-manipulative-monetization-tactics + https://law360.com/articles/1881450/bluey-paw-patrol-app-maker-sued-over-stealth-marketing

### 25.7 Asian-Market (CN/JP) F2P Dark-Pattern Audit for Localized Game Compliance
- [W-144] **[P2]** Asian-market (China / Japan) F2P dark-pattern audit for localized game compliance — arXiv 2511.17512 ("First Contact with Dark Patterns and Deceptive Designs in Chinese and Japanese Free-to-Play Mobile Games", Oct 2025) provides the **first systematic catalog** of dark patterns specific to the Chinese and Japanese F2P markets, finding **monetary dark patterns are the most dominant** category: (a) **"Pay to Skip"** is common in casual mobile games (waiting timers skippable via payment); (b) **"Pay Wall"** is characteristic of early-childhood games (gate content behind a purchase); (c) **red beacon UI tweaks** — simple color/glow treatments — are leveraged as dark patterns to draw the eye to monetization CTAs even within game UI/UX contexts; (d) the CN/JP patterns overlap with but are *culturally distinct* from the Western dark-pattern taxonomies underlying EU DSA (W-142) and US litigation (W-143). Relevance: if GameZipper localizes any game or serves traffic to CN/JP audiences, the regional regulator (China's CAC / Japan's Consumer Affairs Agency) applies **different enforcement lenses** than EU/US — a pattern that passes US review may violate JP's Premiums and Representations Act, and vice versa. **Test**: (1) for any game with CN/JP localization or significant CN/JP traffic share, audit for the arXiv-catalog patterns: Pay-to-Skip timers, Pay-Wall content gates, red-beacon monetization CTAs — flag each and verify it has a non-paid path (timer expires, content unlockable via play); (2) verify red/glow beacon styling on purchase buttons does not constitute "deceptive prominence" under JP law — the monetization CTA must not visually dwarf the free-play path; (3) confirm no Pay-Wall gates the *core* game loop (only optional cosmetics/speed-ups) — gating core play behind payment is the highest-risk pattern in both CN and JP; (4) document the CN/JP compliance posture alongside the EU (W-142) and US (W-143) postures in a unified dark-pattern compliance matrix. Source: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.17512v1

### 25.8 Hybrid-Casual 2026 Monetization-Design Integration Audit
- [G-135] **[P2]** Hybrid-casual 2026 monetization-design integration audit — ads/rewarded-video woven into core loop, not bolted on — gamegrowthadvisor.com ("Hybrid Casual Games 2026: Design, Monetization & Trends", April 16 2026) and meliorgames.com ("Game Monetization in 2026") document the defining 2026 industry shift: **the best games design monetization alongside gameplay, not after it** — "monetization in 2026 is not about squeezing players, but about building trust, value, and long-term engagement." The hypercasual→hybrid-casual migration means a game's monetization (ads, rewarded video, IAP) must be **integrated into the core loop** (e.g., a rewarded-video revive at the moment of death, a banner that doesn't cover the play field, an IAP that unlocks a meaningful new mode) rather than **bolted on** (interstitials every 60s regardless of context, paywalls mid-level). Distinct from G-106 (hybrid-casual *genre-blending mechanics*) and G-101 (ad-frequency *immersion-break threshold*) — this is the **monetization-design integration quality** audit: is the ad a natural beat of the loop or an interruption? **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for the Top 20 monetized games, map the monetization touchpoints against the core game loop diagram — a touchpoint that does NOT map to a natural loop beat (death, level-complete, menu) is a bolt-on and a churn risk; (2) verify rewarded-video placements offer **genuine player value** (revive, double-reward, skip-level) — not a paywall disguised as a "reward"; (3) verify interstitial frequency adapts to session length (a 90s session should not show 2 interstitials) per the G-101 threshold; (4) confirm the Monetag/AdSense placements never occlude the primary play surface or the primary CTA — measure pixel-overlap between ad containers and the game canvas / primary button at common viewport sizes; (5) benchmark the monetization-integration quality of the Top 20 against 2-3 reference hybrid-casual titles and flag the bottom quartile for redesign. Source: https://gamegrowthadvisor.com/blog/2026-04-16-hybrid-casual-game-design-strategy-2026 + https://meliorgames.com/game-development/game-monetization-in-2026-casual-and-mid-range-games

### 25.9 Chromium June 2026 Monthly Vulnerability Bulletin — PAN-SA-2026-0008 Regression Sweep
- [S-102] **[P2]** Chromium June 2026 monthly vulnerability bulletin (PAN-SA-2026-0008) regression sweep — Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 advisory PAN-SA-2026-0008 (security.paloaltonetworks.com) covers the **Chromium monthly vulnerability update for June 2026**, rated **severity 8.4 HIGH** with **network attack vector, high attack complexity, no privileges required, no user interaction required**, affecting Chromium-product confidentiality/integrity/availability (all HIGH) — meaning a network-positioned attacker can exploit affected Chromium versions without any user action beyond loading a page. The advisory intersects with Chrome 149's record-429-CVE baseline (B-056/B-070) and the transition to Chrome 150 (B-091): any GameZipper player on an unpatched Chromium build (Chrome, Edge, Brave, Vivaldi, Android System WebView, Electron apps) is exposed. Distinct from S-060/S-080 (specific V8 CVEs) and B-056 (the 149 mega-patch) — this is the **monthly cadence** vulnerability sweep: Chromium now ships monthly security bulletins (not just per-major-version), so the test library needs a recurring monthly regression check, not just per-release. **Test**: (1) after each Chromium monthly security bulletin (track chromereleases.googleblog.com + vendor advisories like PAN-SA), re-verify the Top 10 games on the *patched* Chrome/Edge to confirm the patch does not regress rendering/input (security patches frequently touch Skia/V8/Network stack); (2) verify the GameZipper site itself is not serving any content that would be flagged by the new Chromium security defaults (e.g., a new mixed-content or COEP enforcement rolled out via the patch); (3) confirm the ad stack (Monetag/AdSense iframes) does not expose players to the patched vulnerability class — third-party ad iframes are the most likely vector for a "no user interaction" Chromium network exploit on a game page; (4) update the minimum-supported-Chrome version in the test matrix to the patched version within 7 days of the bulletin. Source: https://security.paloaltonetworks.com/PAN-SA-2026-0008 + https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/2026
