GameZipper currently ships a FIXED level order (per the `puzzle-level-generator` skill); G-146 demands a per-player adaptive curve. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **DDA signal-collection audit** — verify the GameZipper analytics pipeline (per the `gamezipper-analytics-pipeline` skill) collects the DDA-relevant signals per level per player: attempts-before-success, time-per-attempt, hint-usage, skip-rate, time-since-last-win; if any signal is missing from the schema, add it; (2) **fixed-curve churn-baseline measurement** — for 5 representative puzzle games, measure the per-level churn rate (players who reach level N and never reach level N+1); identify the "spike levels" where churn is >2x the median — these are the levels where a fixed curve is too hard for too many players; (3) **DDA feasibility POC** — for ONE puzzle game (water-sort), implement a per-player level-recommendation: if a player fails level N >3 times, offer them level N+1 from a different difficulty band (or offer a hint), instead of forcing them to repeat level N; A/B-test (treatment: DDA path, control: fixed path) and measure D1/D7 retention per G-143/G-145 methodology; (4) **invisible-DDA anti-pattern audit** — verify any DDA implementation does NOT visibly announce "we made this easier for you" (which triggers the manipulation feeling per StudioKrew); the adjustment must be in the level-selection or hint-availability layer, not in a visible "easy mode" toggle; (5) **flow-state proxy metric** — define a flow-state proxy (e.g., session length >5 min + >3 levels attempted + 0 rage-quits defined as <30s session after a fail); track this proxy per game per week; a rising flow-state proxy = DDA working; a falling one = DDA missing (or no DDA in place); (6) **per-genre DDA calibration** — the DDA parameters differ by genre (puzzle: level-skip + hint economy; arcade: enemy spawn rate + powerup frequency; idle: resource-tick rate); calibrate per-genre rather than applying a single DDA model across the catalog. (Source: studiokrew.com/blog/hybrid-casual-games-vs-traditional-mobile-games March 27 2026)


## CATEGORY 37: 2026-Q2 R187 DYNAMIC TEST INTELLIGENCE — AI-AGENT-CONFIG-FILE-PERSISTENCE + VERSION-HISTORY-FORGERY + AI-WORKFLOW-EXEC-RCE + DESKTOP-TOOL-CDN-POISONING + iOS-APT-IN-WILD + BUILD-TOOLCHAIN-MIGRATION + GOV-CLOUD-BREACH + AI-MUSIC-COPYRIGHT (June 18 2026)

### 37.1 TanStack / Mistral AI "Mini Shai-Hulud" TeamPCP Cross-Registry Supply-Chain Attack — AI-Agent-Config-File Persistence + Dead-Man's-Switch Wipe Daemon (May 11 2026)
- [S-130] **[P0]** TanStack/Mistral AI "Mini Shai-Hulud" TeamPCP attack (May 11 2026) — threat actor group TeamPCP (a.k.a. Shai-Hulud) simultaneously compromised **62 official packages across npm AND PyPI** with **404 malicious versions**, including `@tanstack/react-router` (12M+ weekly downloads alone), Mistral AI official SDK, UiPath enterprise RPA toolkit, and OpenSearch client; the campaign is the **largest cross-registry supply-chain attack of 2026** and introduces four dimensions not seen in any prior S-series entry (blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161035915 "2026年最大跨生态供应链攻击:TanStack/Mistral AI 62个包被投毒"; blog.csdn.net/weixin_47053301/article/details/161039802 "TanStack NPM攻击 揭秘及应对方案"; so.html5.qq.com May 14 2026 "OpenAI回应TanStack供应链攻击"; github.com/Cobenian/shai-hulud-detect — community detector tracking **3,290+ confirmed bad versions** across npm/PyPI/Composer/Crates). **(1) AI-AGENT-CONFIG-FILE-PERSISTENCE dimension** — distinct from EVERY prior npm case (S-104 IndonesianFoods escalation, S-105 Phantom Gyp, S-106 IronWorm, S-107 Red Hat Miasma, S-108 @antv/g-webgpu-engine, S-111 TrapDoor, S-114 Sigstore, S-116 codexui-android, S-121 Ghost campaign, S-125 PhantomRaven, S-126 eslint-config-prettier): those attacks persisted via `postinstall` scripts, system daemons (LaunchAgent/systemd), or IDE files; TanStack persists in **`.claude/settings.json`** (Claude Code agent config), **`.vscode/tasks.json`** (VS Code task runner), **Kiro AI agent tokens**, and **MCP-server OAuth tokens for external services** — files that live INSIDE the project tree (not in `node_modules`), so `npm uninstall` does NOT remove them, and they auto-execute on every project open. For GameZipper (which runs Hermes subagents + Claude Code + MCP servers), a single compromised dependency persists across every subsequent agent invocation. **(2) DEAD-MAN'S-SWITCH WIPE-DAEMON dimension** — Wiz analysis found that if the operator revokes tokens BEFORE isolating the host, a destructive daemon **wipes the entire home directory** (`~`, including `~/gamezipper.com` repo, `~/.hermes/`, `~/.ssh/`, `~/.aws/`) as anti-forensics; the standard incident-response playbook ("revoke credentials first, isolate second") TRIGGERS data destruction. **(3) GITHUB-ACTIONS-OIDC-TRUST-CHAIN BYPASS** — the attacker forked `TanStack/router` as `zblgg/configuration` (a name that does NOT appear in GitHub's fork-list search), opened a pull request that triggered the `pull_request_target` workflow (which checks out fork code with the maintainer's secrets), poisoned the GitHub Actions cache, and then drove the maintainer's existing OIDC-publish workflow to push to npm with **cryptographically valid SLSA Level 3 signatures** — bypassing every provenance-based scanner (per S-114 provenance was supposed to be the trust signal; TanStack proves even SLSA L3 is bypassable via OIDC trust-chain abuse). **(4) PROC-MEM CREDENTIAL EXTRACTION** — on Linux CI runners the worm reads `/proc/<pid>/mem` to extract **even MASKED secrets** from other processes' memory, defeating GitHub Actions' secret-masking. **First TeamPCP campaign to target password managers** (1Password, Bitwarden) alongside cloud creds. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **scan for Shai-Hulud IoCs** — run `shai-hulud-detector.sh` from github.com/Cobenian/shai-hulud-detect against `/home/msdn/gamezipper.com/` and every game subdirectory; expect 0 matches; any match = stop and follow the dead-man's-switch playbook (ISOLATE FIRST, then revoke); (2) **AI-agent-config-file integrity check** — `git ls-files | xargs grep -lE '\.claude/settings\.json|\.vscode/tasks\.json' 2>/dev/null` should be empty (these files are project-tree-persistence targets); add a pre-commit hook rejecting any commit that introduces `.claude/settings.json` or `.vscode/tasks.json` with executable content (`"type": "shell"`); (3) **GitHub Actions `pull_request_target` audit** — `grep -rn 'pull_request_target' .github/workflows/ 2>/dev/null` — any workflow using `pull_request_target` MUST NOT check out fork code with secrets in scope; replace with `pull_request` (fork code runs with read-only `GITHUB_TOKEN`); (4) **OIDC trust-boundary test** — verify the GameZipper publish workflow's `id-token: write` permission is scoped to a single intended audience (e.g., `npm:gamezipper`) and the workflow refuses to run for forks; (5) **dead-man's-switch response playbook** — write and drill the incident-response procedure: when a compromised dependency is detected, **ISOLATE THE HOST FIRST** (network disconnect, snapshot the disk) **BEFORE revoking any tokens** — the wipe-daemon triggers on token-revocation events; document this in the `post-deploy-verification` skill's incident-response section; (6) **/proc mem-read protection** — verify CI runners use seccomp profiles that deny `ptrace` and `/proc/*/mem` reads from untrusted processes; (7) **password-manager isolation** — verify no CI runner, dev-container, or Hermes subagent has filesystem access to `~/.1password/`, `~/.bitwarden/`, or any password-manager vault directory. (Sources: blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161035915 May 11 2026; blog.csdn.net/weixin_47053301/article/details/161039802 May 13 2026; so.html5.qq.com May 14 2026 OpenAI response; github.com/Cobenian/shai-hulud-detect)

### 37.2 Axios npm Supply-Chain Attack via VERSION-HISTORY-FORGERY — Clean Predecessor Disguises Malicious Bump (March 31 2026)
- [S-131] **[P1]** Axios npm version-history-forgery supply-chain attack (March 31 2026) — attacker hijacked the axios maintainer's npm account and published two malicious versions (`axios@1.14.1` + `axios@0.30.4`, axios has 300M+ weekly downloads) carrying a cross-platform RAT (macOS Mach-O binary, Linux Python `/tmp/ld.py`); C2 at `sfrclak.com:8000` / IP `142.11.206.73`; the **VERSION-HISTORY-FORGERY** technique is the novel dimension: the attacker first published a CLEAN version `plain-crypto-js@4.2.0` to establish a benign version-history trail, then 39 minutes later published the MALICIOUS `plain-crypto-js@4.2.1` (containing the RAT dropper) and then the backdoored axios versions — so automated review tools that diff a new version against its predecessor see only a small "routine bump" diff against the clean predecessor, not the full malicious payload (April 2026 weekly security report; StepSecurity / FreeBuf Mar 31 2026; CSDN download.csdn.net/blog/column/11568299/159802674). **Why this is the VERSION-HISTORY-FORGERY dimension distinct from S-126 (eslint-config-prettier maintainer-phishing)**: S-126 phished a human maintainer to get publish credentials; S-131 FORGED A CLEAN VERSION HISTORY that automated review tools trust — **no human was phished**, the attacker simply understood that scanners compute `diff(new_version, previous_version)` and published a clean predecessor first so the diff looks small. **Distinct from S-104 (IndonesianFoods escalation) and S-121 (Ghost campaign)**: those run malicious code in `postinstall` while printing fake install logs; S-131's forgery is at the **VERSION-METADATA layer** (semver bump + changelog), invisible to log-review. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's pipeline runs subagents that install npm packages during game builds (`game-dev-workflow`, puzzle-pipeline); a subagent that "reviews the version history" before installing axios (the recommended mitigation in S-126 step 5) is FOOLED by the version-history-forgery — the history looks clean. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **DO NOT trust version-history diffs** — extend S-126's exact-version pinning mitigation: pin not just the version but the **integrity hash** AND the **publish timestamp**; flag any dependency whose immediate predecessor was published <24h before the current version (a S-131 forgery signature); (2) **install axios from a private mirror** — `npm config set registry https://registry.internal.gamezipper.com/` where the mirror holds a curated allowlist of vetted versions; (3) **C2-domain blocklist in CI egress** — verify the CI sandbox network-egress allowlist blocks `sfrclak.com`, `142.11.206.73`, and any other known C2 from the April 2026 weekly report (Axios + Apifox campaigns); (4) **RAT-binary YARA scan** — add a YARA rule to the CI that flags known RAT Mach-O and Python-loader signatures in any npm package's `dist/` or `bin/` directory; (5) **version-publish-interval alert** — add an `npm install` pre-hook that queries the npm registry's `time` field for the package being installed; if the previous version was published <24h ago AND the current version's `postinstall` is non-empty, alert + require manual approval; (6) **axios pin audit** — `grep -rn '"axios"' /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/package.json /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/**/package.json 2>/dev/null` should either return empty (axios not used) OR pin an exact pre-1.14.1 version with an integrity hash. (Sources: download.csdn.net/blog/column/11568299/159802674 April 2026 weekly security report; StepSecurity / FreeBuf March 31 2026)

### 37.3 Langflow CVE-2026-33017 CVSS 9.3 — AI-Low-Code-Workflow-Platform exec() RCE Exploited in 20 Hours (Feb 26 2026)
- [S-132] **[P1]** Langflow CVE-2026-33017 (CVSS 9.3, exploited in the wild 20 hours after disclosure) — the open-source AI workflow platform Langflow (visual builder for AI agents + RAG applications) had an unauthenticated remote-code-execution vulnerability in the **`POST /api/v1/build_public_tmp/{flow_id}/flow` endpoint**: the endpoint was designed to build public flows without authentication, but when the optional `data` parameter is supplied, the endpoint uses the attacker-controlled flow data (which may include arbitrary Python code in node definitions) **instead of the stored flow data from the database**; that code is passed to Python's `exec()` with **no sandbox protection**, yielding unauthenticated RCE; researcher Aviral Srivastava reported it Feb 26 2026; CISA added to KEV catalog with fix-by date April 8 2026; affects Langflow ≤ 1.8.1, fixed in 1.9.0.dev8 (blog.csdn.net/qq_37865996/article/details/159549218 March 27 2026; blog.csdn.net/2302_79436907/article/details/159896254 June 10 2026; so.html5.qq.com March 23 2026; blog.csdn.net/weixin_45635831/article/details/159563953 daily intel report March 28 2026). **Why this is the AI-WORKFLOW-PLATFORM-EXEC-RCE dimension distinct from S-119 (Marimo CVE-2026-39987 + LLM-agent post-exploitation)**: S-119 documents an attacker who FIRST compromises a server (via Marimo CVE) and THEN uses the LLM agent as the post-exploitation tool; S-132 documents a vulnerability where the **AI workflow platform itself is the exec() surface** — no separate compromise needed, the platform's design (`exec()` on user-supplied node code) IS the vulnerability. **Distinct from CVE-2025-3248 (CVSS 9.8, prior Langflow RCE)**: that targeted `/api/v1/validate/code`; CVE-2026-33017 reuses the SAME root-cause `exec()` call via a DIFFERENT endpoint (`/api/v1/build_public_tmp/...`), confirming the pattern that **once a platform has an `exec()`-on-untrusted-input design flaw, additional endpoints exposing the same call surface keep appearing**. **Why the 20-hour weaponization window matters**: confirms S-113 (Anthropic Mythos AI 100 Chromium zero-days in 15 days) and C-102 (AI-discovered-CVE cadence) — once a CVSS 9+ CVE in an AI-adjacent platform is disclosed, the time-to-weaponization is now <1 day, not the traditional week. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's pipeline runs **Hermes subagents** with broad tool access; if GameZipper EVER embeds an LLM-agent/workflow platform (Langflow, Flowise, n8n, Dify, anything that lets users define agent behavior in Python/JS), the platform's exec()-on-untrusted-input surface becomes a P0 unauthenticated RCE vector. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **inventory AI-workflow platforms in GameZipper infra** — `grep -rln 'langflow\|flowise\|n8n\|dify\|autogen\|crewai' /home/msdn/ --include="*.yml" --include="*.yaml" --include="*.json" --include="*.py" --include="*.toml" 2>/dev/null` should return empty; if any are present, patch to the latest fixed version AND audit every HTTP endpoint for `exec(` / `eval(` on user-supplied data; (2) **exec()/eval() audit on any unauthenticated endpoint** — for any GameZipper service exposing an unauthenticated HTTP endpoint, run `grep -nE 'exec\(|eval\(|subprocess\.|os\.system\(' server.py` and verify NONE of those code paths receive user-supplied input; (3) **20-hour-CVE-response SLA** — extend C-102's methodology to AI-adjacent platforms: any CVSS 9+ CVE in a platform GameZipper uses (Langflow, n8n, Hermes itself, Claude Code) triggers a **mandatory patch-or-disable within 20 hours**; (4) **unauthenticated-endpoint inventory** — `grep -rn '@app.route\|@router\.\|app.get\|app.post' /home/msdn/gamezipper-bi/ /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/api/ 2>/dev/null | grep -v auth` to enumerate any endpoint without auth middleware; each one is a candidate for a future exec()-RCE; (5) **CISA KEV subscription** — subscribe to CISA's KEV catalog RSS; any GameZipper-used-software entry triggers an immediate patch-or-disable workflow. (Sources: blog.csdn.net/qq_37865996/article/details/159549218 March 27 2026; blog.csdn.net/2302_79436907/article/details/159896254 June 10 2026; blog.csdn.net/weixin_45635831/article/details/159563953 March 28 2026; so.html5.qq.com March 23 2026)

### 37.4 Apifox Desktop-Client CDN Poisoning — 18-Day Credential Harvest Via Compromised Update Channel (April 2026)
- [C-109] **[P1]** Apifox desktop-client CDN poisoning (April 2026 weekly security report) — the API-testing desktop tool Apifox (popular in the Chinese dev ecosystem, comparable to Postman) had its **CDN update channel compromised for 18 days**, during which every download of the desktop client (regardless of version selected by the user) shipped a credential-stealing payload that exfiltrated **SSH private keys** and **Kubernetes service-account tokens**; the attack window was 18 days because the CDN compromise was not detected until a downstream victim reported anomalous outbound traffic (download.csdn.net/blog/column/11568299/159802674 April 2026 weekly security report). **Why this is the DESKTOP-DEV-TOOL-CDN-POISONING dimension distinct from EVERY npm-registry case (S-104 ~ S-131)**: npm-registry attacks poison a specific package version that a developer explicitly installs; the developer can pin a known-good version to avoid the bad one. CDN poisoning poisons **the delivery channel itself** — every download during the window is compromised regardless of which version the user selects, because the CDN serves the attacker's binary for every requested version. The user's "pin to a safe version" mitigation (recommended in S-126, S-131) is **completely ineffective** against CDN poisoning. **Distinct from S-070 (web game supply-chain framework CDN tampering detection)**: S-070 covers CDN tampering for GAME ASSETS served to players; C-109 covers CDN tampering for DEVELOPER TOOLS that build/deploy the games — a poisoned dev tool can backdoor every game it builds. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's pipeline uses desktop and CLI dev tools (Hermes CLI, Claude Code, kachilu-browser, possibly Apifox-equivalents for API testing of the BI dashboard); if ANY of these tools' auto-update channels are compromised, the tool becomes a persistent backdoor inside the build environment, and every game built during the compromise window is suspect. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **dev-tool auto-update audit** — inventory every dev tool used in the GameZipper pipeline that has an auto-update mechanism (Hermes CLI `hermes update`, Claude Code auto-update, kachilu-browser npm update, Homebrew, apt); for each, document the update source (URL, registry, CDN) and whether the update is signature-verified; (2) **signature-verification enforcement** — every auto-updating dev tool MUST verify the update's GPG/codesign signature against a pinned publisher key BEFORE executing the update; `hermes update` should refuse unsigned updates; if a tool lacks signature verification, disable auto-update and pin to a manually-verified version; (3) **18-day-detection SLA** — if any GameZipper dev tool's update CDN is reported compromised, the maximum detection-to-response time must be **<18 hours, not 18 days** (the Apifox window); subscribe to security advisories for every dev tool and trigger an immediate version-freeze + integrity-rescan when a compromise is reported; (4) **SSH-key + K8s-token rotation drill** — quarterly, run a drill that rotates every SSH key (`~/.ssh/id_*`), every K8s service-account token, and every cloud CLI credential, simulating a CDN-poisoning discovery; measure drill completion time; target <2 hours; (5) **build-environment reproducibility** — verify that a clean-room rebuild of any GameZipper game (from source on a freshly-provisioned, never-touched-by-auto-update machine) produces a byte-identical artifact to the production-deployed one; if NOT byte-identical, the production artifact may have been backdoored by a poisoned tool. (Source: download.csdn.net/blog/column/11568299/159802674 April 2026 weekly security report)

### 37.5 TA446 Russian APT First In-the-Wild iOS/iCloud Exploit-Kit Deployment — DarkSword (April 2026)
- [B-105] **[P1]** TA446 (Russian-nexus threat actor) first in-the-wild deployment of an iOS/iCloud exploit kit codenamed "DarkSword" (April 2026 weekly security report) — prior to April 2026, iOS exploit kits were nation-state-developed, used against a handful of high-value targets (journalists, dissidents, executives), and NEVER deployed by profit-driven APT groups against the general population; TA446's DarkSword deployment marks the **transition of iOS exploit kits from state-only to APT-criminal deployment**, meaning the mobile-Safari WebKit attack surface (which every casual-game iOS player uses) is now a real, in-the-wild target (download.csdn.net/blog/column/11568299/159802674 April 2026 weekly security report). **Why this is the iOS-APT-IN-THE-WILD dimension distinct from B-087 (iOS 26 WebKit 26 WebGPU memory-limits regression — a platform BUG) and B-097 (iOS 26.5 full security advisory 80+ vulns — Apple's disclosure of BUGS)**: B-087/B-097 document BUGS that Apple patched; B-105 documents an OFFENSIVE KIT that a threat actor is ACTIVELY DEPLOYING against iOS users — the difference between "there is a hole in the fence" (bug) and "there is a thief walking through the hole" (APT deployment). **Distinct from S-052 (Safari 26.5 WebKit crash + user-data exposure)**: S-052 is a single CVE; B-105 is a deployed multi-exploit kit (likely chains several CVEs, some of which may be 0-day at deployment time). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's iOS traffic (mobile Safari on iPhone/iPad) is a meaningful share of casual-game players; a player whose iOS device is compromised via DarkSword while visiting GameZipper may blame GameZipper (reputational damage) and may have their iCloud-stored GameZipper game saves / credentials exfiltrated. More critically, if a DarkSword-style kit chains a WebKit 0-day that triggers via a crafted canvas/WebGL context, **a malicious ad creative (per S-118 DoubleClick/DesckVB abuse) could deliver the DarkSword kit to GameZipper's iOS players via the Monetag/AdSense ad slot**. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **iOS-Safari exploit-resistance test** — on the latest iOS (currently 27 per W-155), open GameZipper's top-20 games in mobile Safari with Web Inspector + a proxy intercepting ad creatives; verify no ad creative triggers a WebKit crash or unexpected process exit (an exploit-kit fingerprinting signal); (2) **ad-creative sandbox hardening on iOS** — verify the `gz-ad-below-game` iframe on every game page has `sandbox="allow-scripts"` (NO `allow-same-origin`, NO `allow-top-navigation`) so a malicious ad cannot reach the parent page's DOM or trigger cross-origin navigation that an exploit kit could chain; (3) **iOS WebKit CVE monitoring** — subscribe to Apple's security-advisories RSS + Google Project Zero + iOS-exploit trackers; any iOS WebKit CVE disclosed with "exploited in the wild" triggers a re-test of the top-20 games on the unpatched iOS version to verify GameZipper does not enable the exploit path; (4) **canvas/WebGL input-validation audit** — verify no GameZipper game passes untrusted (ad-served, URL-parameter, user-input) data into a WebGL shader source or canvas context attribute without sanitization (a common WebKit exploit-entry vector); `grep -rn 'createShader\|shaderSource\|getContext.*webgl' /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/*/index.html 2>/dev/null | head` to find the surface; (5) **iOS player-security-notice** — add a one-time dismissible notice on GameZipper game pages for iOS users: "Keep iOS updated for the best security; this site does not request iCloud access" — sets player expectations and reduces blame-attribution if a player's device is compromised via a different vector while visiting. (Source: download.csdn.net/blog/column/11568299/159802674 April 2026 weekly security report)

### 37.6 Rolldown 1.0 + Vite 8 Build-Toolchain Migration — rollupOptions → rolldownOptions, esbuild → oxc (June 2026)
- [W-159] **[P2]** Rolldown 1.0 + Vite 8 build-toolchain migration (Rolldown RC Jan 21 2026, Vite 8 Beta Dec 4 2025, stable release June 2026 per JavaScript Weekly Week 20) — Rolldown is a Rust-written JavaScript/TypeScript bundler that replaces BOTH Rollup (production bundling) AND esbuild (dev transform + dep pre-bundling) in Vite 8, delivering 10-30x faster production builds than Rollup and matching esbuild's dev performance; the migration is a **CONFIG-API SHAPE CHANGE**: `build.rollupOptions` → `build.rolldownOptions`, the `esbuild` config option → `oxc` option (because Rolldown uses the Oxc parser/transformer/minifier instead of esbuild); most Rollup plugins continue to work but some require migration (antfu/skills rolldown-migration.md; blog.csdn.net/xfy196/article/details/157909765 April 15 2026 "尤雨溪官宣:Vite8来啦,Rolldown 1.0 RC 发布"; blog.csdn.net/qq_31470439/article/details/155553842; cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2679559 JS Weekly Week 20 2026; VoidZero / Evan You announcement). **Why this is the BUILD-TOOLCHAIN-MIGRATION dimension distinct from B-103 (Basemark Web 3.0 → gpuscore.com URL migration)**: B-103 is a TOOL-URL change (a benchmark URL moved); W-159 is a CONFIG-API SHAPE change (the configuration keys themselves change name) — a build config that worked on Vite 7 silently uses default values on Vite 8 because `rollupOptions` is ignored (the new key is `rolldownOptions`), producing a DIFFERENT bundle (different chunk splitting, different tree-shaking, different minification) without any error. **Distinct from G-137 (2026 web-game tech-stack decision matrix WebGL vs WebGPU vs WASM)**: G-137 covers GAME-RENDERING tech choice; W-159 covers BUILD-TOOL choice — a build-tool change does not affect what the game renders but does affect the bundle shape, which affects every downstream consumer (CDN cache keys, subresource-integrity hashes, bundle-size budgets). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper games are mostly single-file HTML+JS (not Vite-built), BUT (a) any game library that GameZipper imports (e.g., a Vite-built minified bundle from npm) changes bundle shape when its upstream author migrates to Vite 8, breaking subresource-integrity hashes and CDN cache keys silently; (b) the GameZipper pipeline's build tools (if any Vite is used for tooling pages like tools.gamezipper.com) must migrate `rollupOptions` → `rolldownOptions` or the config is silently ignored; (c) ad-stuff third-party bundles (Monetag, AdSense) that are Vite-built may change shape, affecting the post-deploy-verification ad-container-fill detection. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **Vite-usage audit** — `grep -rln 'rollupOptions\|vite\|esbuild' /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/ --include="*.js" --include="*.ts" --include="*.json" --include="*.mjs" 2>/dev/null | head` to find any Vite/Rollup/esbuild config; document each; (2) **subresource-integrity hash pinning** — for any third-party script loaded with `integrity="sha384-..."`, add a CI check that re-computes the hash weekly and alerts on change (a Vite-8-upstream migration will silently change the hash, breaking the integrity check — which is the desired behavior: alert on unexpected bundle-shape change); (3) **if GameZipper adopts Vite 8** — migrate `rollupOptions` → `rolldownOptions` and `esbuild` → `oxc` per the antfu/skills migration guide; verify the production build is byte-identical-or-documented-different before deploying; (4) **bundle-size-budget regression test** — capture the bundle size of every game's main JS file; alert if any game's bundle size changes >5% without a corresponding game-code change (catches silent upstream-bundler-migration-induced bloat or tree-shaking regressions); (5) **CDN cache-key strategy** — verify the CDN cache key for any Vite-built asset includes a content hash (not just a version number) so a Vite-8-migration-induced bundle-shape change automatically invalidates the cache (otherwise players get stale bundles). (Sources: antfu/skills blob/main/skills/vite/references/rolldown-migration.md May 28 2026; blog.csdn.net/xfy196/article/details/157909765 April 15 2026; cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2679559 JS Weekly Week 20; VoidZero / Evan You Vite 8 Beta announcement Dec 4 2025)

### 37.7 ShinyHunters EU Commission AWS Breach — 350GB Exfiltrated, Public-Sector Cloud-Compromise Risk (April 2026)
- [W-160] **[P2]** ShinyHunters breach of the European Commission's AWS cloud infrastructure (April 2026 weekly security report) — the threat actor ShinyHunters exfiltrated **350GB of data** from the European Commission's AWS environment, demonstrating that even a major government institution with dedicated security teams can have its cloud infrastructure comprehensively compromised; the breach exposes a risk dimension for any organization in the public-sector supply chain (download.csdn.net/blog/column/11568299/159802674 April 2026 weekly security report). **Why this is the GOVERNMENT-CLOUD-BREACH-PUBLIC-SECTOR-RISK dimension distinct from A-034 (ADA Title II April 2026 deadline) and A-035 (post-ADA-Title-II enforcement wave)**: A-034/A-035 cover COMPLIANCE deadlines (the legal requirement to be accessible); W-160 covers ACTIVE COMPROMISE (the security reality that public-sector infrastructure is under active attack) — a GameZipper customer in the .gov/.edu space (a library, a school district, a municipality) can have their Cloudflare/CDN credentials exfiltrated via a breached upstream provider, and GameZipper's content served THROUGH that customer's proxy/CDN becomes a vector or a casualty. **Distinct from S-052/S-118 (specific CVEs and ad-platform abuse)**: those are technical exploits; W-160 is a **supply-chain-trust dimension** — the question is not "is GameZipper's code secure?" but "are GameZipper's UPSTREAM PROVIDERS (Cloudflare, GitHub Pages, AWS if used for the BI dashboard, Monetag/AdSense) secure, and what happens to GameZipper when one of them is breached?". **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's infrastructure depends on Cloudflare (CDN), GitHub Pages (origin), and potentially AWS (BI dashboard backend per the OpenAPI methodology in `post-deploy-verification`); a breach of any of these providers exposes GameZipper's configuration, secrets, and deployment pipeline. For public-sector GameZipper customers (libraries running GameZipper games in K-12 labs, municipalities embedding GameZipper on intranet portals), the ShinyHunters precedent means their OWN cloud infrastructure is a target, and a breach of their infrastructure can pivot to compromising the GameZipper content they serve to their users. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **upstream-provider breach-response playbook** — document the procedure for when Cloudflare / GitHub / AWS announces a breach: (a) rotate every API key/OAuth token issued to that provider within 4 hours, (b) audit git history for any commit signed during the breach window, (c) re-deploy from a known-good commit, (d) notify users if any player data may have been exposed; (2) **public-sector-customer incident-response addendum** — for any GameZipper customer in .gov/.edu, add a clause to the embedding agreement: "If your infrastructure is compromised, GameZipper content served through your proxy must be re-verified against the canonical GameZipper origin (gamezipper.com) before being served to your users"; (3) **cloud-provider security-posture monitoring** — subscribe to security advisories from Cloudflare, GitHub, AWS; any "we detected unauthorized access" announcement triggers the upstream-provider breach-response playbook (step 1); (4) **defense-in-depth for public-sector embedding** — for .gov/.edu embeds, recommend the customer pull GameZipper games from a GameZipper-controlled CDN (not through their own proxy), so a breach of their infrastructure does not compromise the game content; (5) **350GB-exfiltration detection** — verify the BI dashboard backend (if on AWS) has data-exfiltration detection (GuardDuty, CloudTrail anomalous-egress alerts); a 350GB egress should trigger an alert, not pass unnoticed for days. (Source: download.csdn.net/blog/column/11568299/159802674 April 2026 weekly security report)

### 37.8 Suno / Udio AI-Music Copyright Lawsuit — UMG + Warner + Sony vs AI Music Generators (June 24 2024, Ongoing 2026)
- [W-161] **[P2]** Suno / Udio AI-music copyright litigation (filed June 24 2024 by Universal Music Group + Warner Music Group + Sony Music Entertainment against Suno AI and Udio, ongoing through 2026) — the three major record labels sued the two leading AI music-generation startups alleging "massive" copyright infringement of sound recordings used to train the generative models; the case is the **first major AI-GENERATED-AUDIO copyright litigation** and its outcome will determine whether AI-generated music is derivative work (infringing) or transformative fair use (non-infringing); the litigation has expanded through 2026 with additional plaintiffs and amicus briefs (toutiao.com/topic/7484078372046407732/ 2026; toutiao.com/topic/7488894961577035812/ 2026; huanqiu.com via toutiao 2026; sourceforge.net/software/ai-music-generators 2026 — Suno listing notes "free tier = non-commercial use only, paid tier required for commercial use"). **Why this is the AI-GENERATED-AUDIO COPYRIGHT LITIGATION dimension distinct from W-146 (FTC Honda non-tech dark-pattern precedent)**: W-146 is consumer-protection (deceptive UX); W-161 is INTELLECTUAL-PROPERTY (who owns the output of an AI trained on copyrighted input). **Distinct from S-127 (AI chatbot cryptojacking recommendation)**: S-127 is a SECURITY vector; W-161 is a LEGAL vector — the two are unrelated except both involve AI. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper games commonly use background music (BGM) and sound effects; per the `mlops/models/audiocraft-audio-generation` and `creative/songwriting-and-ai-music` (Suno) skills, AI-generated audio is a documented GameZipper tooling path. If a GameZipper game ships BGM generated by Suno/Udio (or any AI music model trained on copyrighted recordings), and the Suno/Udio litigation concludes that AI-generated music IS derivative infringement, then: (a) every shipped AI-generated track becomes retroactively infringing, (b) the original rightsholders can issue DMCA takedowns against GameZipper game pages, (c) GameZipper may face secondary-infringement claims for distributing the infringing tracks. Even if the AI-music vendor (Suno) has an indemnification clause, the vendor's liability cap may be far below GameZipper's actual damages. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **AI-generated-audio inventory** — `grep -rln 'suno\|udio\|musicgen\|audiocraft\|soundraw\|mubert\|aiva\|boomy' /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/ --include="*.html" --include="*.js" --include="*.md" --include="*.json" 2>/dev/null` and audit every game's BGM source; document which tracks are AI-generated and by which model; (2) **AI-audio licensing-terms audit** — for every AI-generated audio asset in GameZipper, verify the generation tool's terms-of-service permit commercial use AND provide indemnification (Suno paid tier permits commercial use; free tier does NOT); any track generated on a free tier MUST be replaced or licensed; (3) **DMCA-takedown-response playbook** — document the procedure for when GameZipper receives a DMCA takedown notice for an AI-generated audio asset: (a) remove the asset within 24 hours, (b) replace with a public-domain or properly-licensed alternative, (c) audit all other games using the same generation tool for the same risk; (4) **AI-audio-provenance manifest** — maintain a manifest of every AI-generated audio asset: (asset path, generation tool, generation date, prompt used, ToS version at generation time, commercial-use-licensed: yes/no); this manifest is the defense if a rightsholder challenges an asset's provenance; (5) **public-domain / royalty-free BGM preference** — default to public-domain (Kevin MacLeod incompetech.com CC-BY, Free Music Archive, Musopen for classical) or properly-licensed royalty-free libraries for new GameZipper BGM; use AI-generated audio only when the tool's ToS explicitly permits commercial use AND the litigation outcome is resolved (post-2026); (6) **litigation-outcome trigger** — when the Suno/Udio case reaches a final judgment or settlement, re-audit every AI-generated audio asset against the judgment's scope; if the judgment narrows commercial-use rights, replace affected assets within 30 days. (Sources: toutiao.com/topic/7484078372046407732/ 2026; toutiao.com/topic/7488894961577035812/ 2026; huanqiu.com via toutiao 2026; sourceforge.net/software/ai-music-generators 2026 — Suno commercial-use ToS)

## CATEGORY 38: 2026-Q2 R188 DYNAMIC TEST INTELLIGENCE — AI-PLATFORM-STACK-RSC-RCE + DEV-ENVIRONMENT LOCAL-NETWORK RCE + AI-AGENT-COMPANY NPM-WORM DECENTRALIZED-BLOCKCHAIN-C2 + LEGACY-DAEMON PROTOCOL-PARSER OVERFLOW + IDENTITY-INFRASTRUCTURE UNAUTH-RCE + AI-CHATBOT OFFICIAL-SPONSORED-CONTENT + WASM-WebGPU SHARED-MEMORY BOUNDARY + DEVTOOLS-CONSOLE-RUNNING-UNTRUSTED-JS (June 18 2026)

### 38.1 React2Shell CVE-2025-55182 — React Server Components Flight-Protocol CVSS-10.0 Unauthenticated RCE Exploited in AI Platforms (Jan + May 2026)
- [S-133] **[P0]** React2Shell CVE-2025-55182 (CVSS 10.0, CISA KEV Dec 5 2025, fix deadline Dec 26 2025) — Meta's React core team + Vercel's Next.js team jointly disclosed an unauthenticated remote-code-execution vulnerability in **React Server Components (RSC)** Flight Protocol: the server deserializes client-supplied form/flight payloads containing `$`-prefixed reference markers WITHOUT validating them, allowing an attacker to construct a malicious serialized payload that triggers prototype-pollution or indirect invocation of dangerous modules (`child_process`, `vm`) on the server, yielding unauthenticated RCE; affects `react-server-dom-webpack/parcel/turbopack` versions 19.0 / 19.1.0 / 19.1.1 / 19.2.0, fixed in 19.0.1 / 19.1.2 / 19.2.1; the sibling CVE-2025-66478 (Next.js App Router variant) was WITHDRAWN as a duplicate. **Actively exploited Jan 2026** against insurance/e-commerce/IT industries (new.qq.com/rain/a/20260128A02COA00 Jan 28 2026 "攻击者利用React2Shell漏洞针对IT行业发起攻击") and **May 2026 mass-exploitation of Dify** (blog.csdn.net/wwwzhouhui/article/details/155789490 Dec 10 2025; blog.csdn.net/A19911511842/article/details/158615197 May 21 2026): the open-source LLM-app platform Dify self-hosted instances (Dify v1.9.x-v1.10.1, which use Next.js App Router) were compromised for cryptomining, AI API-key theft (OpenAI/Claude), and persistence backdoors. **Sources**: aliyun.com/notice/117771 (Dec 5 2025); segmentfault.com/a/1190000047451749 (Dec 5 2025); keenlab.tencent.com (Tencent Keen Lab analysis Dec 8 2025); blog.csdn.net/sinat_17584329/article/details/155698219 (Dec 9 2025); download.csdn.net/download/segwy/92666654 (CISA BOD 22-01 KEV entry). **Why this is the AI-PLATFORM-STACK-RSC-RCE dimension distinct from S-132 (Langflow CVE-2026-33017 exec() RCE)**: S-132's exploit surface is an `exec()`-on-untrusted-input DESIGN FLAW (the platform deliberately runs user-supplied Python); S-133's exploit surface is the React Flight WIRE PROTOCOL's deserialization — the server never intended to execute attacker code, the framework's serialization layer was supposed to be safe. **Distinct from S-130/S-135 (npm supply-chain)**: those are MALICIOUS PACKAGES; S-133 is a CVE in shipped, legitimate library code that every RSC app already has installed. **Distinct from CVE-2025-3248 (prior Langflow CVSS 9.8)**: different ecosystem entirely (React not Langflow). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's live game catalog is single-file HTML+JS and does NOT run RSC, so the production game pages are NOT directly vulnerable. HOWEVER, the GameZipper tooling stack (BI dashboard, any future admin panel, the Hermes/Claude Code agent harness, and any Next.js/Vite-built internal tool) IS the attack surface — if any internal GameZipper service uses Next.js App Router or RSC frameworks, an unauthenticated HTTP POST to a server-function endpoint yields CVSS-10.0 RCE, and the Dify precedent shows attackers specifically target self-hosted AI platforms to steal AI API keys (the same OpenAI/Anthropic keys GameZipper's pipeline uses). **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **inventory every internal service for RSC/Next.js App Router usage** — `grep -rE 'react-server-dom|next/app|use server|"use server"' /home/msdn/ --include='*.js' --include='*.ts' --include='*.json'` and confirm which (if any) GameZipper BI/admin/agent services run RSC; (2) for any RSC service found, verify `react-server-dom-webpack/parcel/turbopack` version is ≥ 19.0.1 / 19.1.2 / 19.2.1 (the fixed versions) — `npm ls react-server-dom-webpack` must show a patched version; (3) verify the service is NOT exposed to the public internet without auth (the CVE is UNAUTHENTICATED — an RSC endpoint behind Cloudflare Access / mTLS is safe, one on 0.0.0.0:3000 is not); (4) scan WAF/access logs for the Flight-Protocol payload signature (`$`-prefixed markers + `child_process`/`vm` references in POST bodies) to detect prior exploitation; (5) rotate any AI API keys (OpenAI/Anthropic) stored on hosts that run RSC services, per the Dify compromise pattern.

### 38.2 CVE-2025-11953 React Native Metro Dev-Server Local-Network Unauthenticated RCE (CVSS 9.8) — JFrog Nov 5 2025
- [S-134] **[P1]** CVE-2025-11953 React Native Metro dev-server RCE (CVSS 9.8, JFrog disclosure Nov 5 2025) — the `@react-native-community/cli-server-api` package (used by React Native's Metro development server, >2M weekly downloads) versions 4.8.0 through 20.0.0-alpha.2 allow an unauthenticated attacker **on the same local network** as a developer running `npx react-native start` / `npm start` (Metro's default settings) to send a malicious HTTP request that executes arbitrary OS commands on the developer's machine; primarily affects Windows (e.g., launches `calc.exe` as proof-of-concept) but theoretically macOS/Linux too; fixed in `@react-native-community/cli` 20.0.0+. **Sources**: new.qq.com/rain/a/20251105A01ZF400 (Nov 5 2025 "JavaScript 库 React 高危漏洞威胁数百万开发者"); so.html5.qq.com (Nov 5 2025); blog.csdn.net/2408_89348881/article/details/154571976 (Nov 8 2025 "React Native 开发服务器漏洞 紧急安全提示"). **Why this is the DEV-ENVIRONMENT LOCAL-NETWORK RCE dimension distinct from S-133 (React2Shell production-server RCE)**: S-133 attacks the PRODUCTION server (the deployed app exposed to the internet); S-134 attacks the DEVELOPER's machine (the Metro dev server running on localhost during `npm start`). The threat model is fundamentally different — S-134 requires network adjacency (coffee-shop WiFi, shared office LAN, compromised build agent on the same VPC) but yields control of the BUILD machine, meaning every game built on that machine afterward is suspect (the attacker can inject a backdoor into the game bundle before it ships). **Distinct from S-109 (GitHub npm v12 disables install scripts)**: S-109 is a package-install-time defense; S-134 is a RUNTIME dev-server compromise that no package-config change prevents (only upgrading the cli package or binding Metro to 127.0.0.1 prevents it). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's current pipeline does NOT use React Native (games are pure web), BUT (a) the DIMENSION generalizes to ANY dev tool that binds an HTTP server to 0.0.0.0 or the LAN interface during development (Vite dev server, Webpack DevServer, Parcel, Live Server, the Hermes dev harness, kachilu-browser's control port) — if any GameZipper dev tool listens on a non-loopback interface without auth, a network-adjacent attacker takes over the dev machine; (b) if a future GameZipper mobile-companion app or any subagent-built tool uses React Native, this CVE applies directly. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **scan every dev tool for non-loopback bind** — `ss -tlnp 2>/dev/null | grep -v '127.0.0.1\|::1' | grep LISTEN` on the dev/build machine must show ONLY intentionally-exposed ports; any dev tool (Vite, Webpack, Metro, Live Server) binding to 0.0.0.0 or a LAN IP is a CVE-2025-11953-class risk; (2) verify dev servers are configured with `host: 'localhost'` / `--host 127.0.0.1` not `--host 0.0.0.0`; (3) if React Native tooling exists anywhere in the toolchain, run `npx @react-native-community/cli --version` and confirm ≥ 20.0.0; (4) ensure the build machine is NOT on a shared/coffee-shop WiFi during builds (the local-network adjacency requirement means a wired/VPN-only build environment is the architectural mitigation).

### 38.3 CanisterWorm / Namastex Labs AI-Agent-Company-Targeted npm Worm — ICP Decentralized-Blockchain C2 Exfil Endpoint (April 24 2026)
- [S-135] **[P1]** CanisterWorm / Namastex Labs npm supply-chain worm (Socket + StepSecurity disclosure, April 24 2026) — a self-propagating "CanisterWorm"-class malware compromised multiple npm packages associated with **Namastex Labs** (an agentic-AI company); the worm collects tokens, credentials, API keys, SSH keys, plus cloud / CI/CD / container-registry / Kubernetes / Docker / LLM-platform configuration, then exfiltrates the stolen data via TWO channels: (1) a conventional webhook, and (2) — the novel dimension — an **Internet Computer Protocol (ICP) canister container endpoint with hardcoded container ID `cjn37-uyaaa-aaaac-qgnva-cai`**; the payload references a "TeamPCP/LiteLLM method" for `.pth` file injection (cross-registry, npm + PyPI lineage). **Source**: so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_85669eb09b517552 (April 24 2026 "npm供应链蠕虫病毒持续蔓延 窃取开发者敏感数据" — Socket/StepSecurity disclosure). **Why this is the AI-AGENT-COMPANY SUPPLY-CHAIN + DECENTRALIZED-BLOCKCHAIN-C2 dimension distinct from S-130 (TanStack/Mistral TeamPCP cross-registry attack May 11 2026)**: S-130 is the same broader TeamPCP campaign family but (a) S-130's exfil was via conventional C2 (sfrclak.com-style domains + GitHub Actions OIDC) that can be sinkholed/taken down via domain registrar + hosting-provider abuse; (b) CanisterWorm specifically **profiles AI-agent VENDORS** (Namastex Labs is an "智能体AI公司" / agentic-AI company) — attackers now target the AI-agent-company ecosystem specifically because those vendors hold aggregated AI API keys + agent-tool credentials with broad access; (c) CanisterWorm's ICP canister C2 is a **DECENTRALIZED-BLOCKCHAIN endpoint** — the canister lives on the Internet Computer blockchain, which has no single registrar, no single hosting provider, no single legal jurisdiction to serve a takedown on, making the C2 nearly impossible to disrupt via traditional domain/IP blocking. This is the first documented use of a blockchain canister as a malware exfil endpoint in the npm ecosystem. **Distinct from S-108 (@antv/g-webgpu-engine)**: that targeted a WebGPU rendering library; CanisterWorm targets the AI-agent tool chain. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's pipeline runs Hermes subagents + Claude Code + MCP servers, all of which hold AI API keys + tool credentials; if a compromised npm package reaches the GameZipper build environment and exfils via an ICP canister, (a) traditional egress filtering (block known-bad domains/IPs) FAILS because the ICP canister endpoint is a legitimate blockchain endpoint that egress allowlists may permit, (b) the stolen AI keys can be monetized by the attacker via LLM-API resale or used to run further agent-driven attacks attributed to GameZipper's account, (c) the `.pth` file injection vector means a single compromised PyPI dep can persist across Python interpreter restarts (the `.pth` auto-loads on every `python` invocation). **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **block ICP/blockchain canister egress at the network layer** — add `*.ic0.app`, `*.icp0.io`, `*.raw.ic0.app`, and the raw canister host pattern to the build-environment egress DENY list (these are the Internet Computer canister-access domains); any outbound connection to an ICP endpoint from a build/CI host is anomalous; (2) extend S-130's AI-AGENT-CONFIG-FILE audit (`.claude/settings.json`, `.vscode/tasks.json`, MCP OAuth tokens) to also scan for `.pth` files dropped in Python `site-packages` (the CanisterWorm persistence vector); (3) treat any npm/PyPI package whose description or author references an "agentic AI" / "agent framework" / "LLM tooling" vendor as elevated-risk (the attacker profile specifically targets these); (4) scan package-lock.json + requirements.txt for the TeamPCP/LiteLLM/Namastex indicators before every build.

### 38.4 CVE-2026-32746 GNU InetUtils Telnetd LINEMODE SLC Out-of-Bounds Write — CVSS 9.8 Unauthenticated RCE (March 11 2026)
- [W-162] **[P1]** CVE-2026-32746 GNU InetUtils Telnetd OOB write (CVSS 9.8, Israeli security firm Dream discovered March 11 2026) — the LINEMODE "set local characters" (SLC) suboption handler in GNU InetUtils Telnetd contains an out-of-bounds write that yields a buffer overflow and code execution; affects ALL versions of the telnetd implementation up to and including 2.7; fix expected before April 1 2026. **Source**: so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_20169ba7a7d29552 (March 18 2026 "GNU InetUtils Telnetd被发现严重漏洞CVE-2026-32746"). **Why this is the LEGACY-DAEMON PROTOCOL-PARSER OVERFLOW dimension** — distinct from prior server-side cases (S-086 NGINX heap overflow, S-069 HTTP/2 Rapid Reset) because those are MODERN high-traffic services; W-162 covers a LEGACY daemon (telnetd) that should never run in production but frequently lingers on internal/management networks, build agents, and embedded/IoT devices. The dimension generalizes: any server-side daemon with a legacy text-protocol parser (telnet, FTP, SMTP, SNMP, legacy RPC) is a CVSS-9.x unauthenticated-RCE candidate. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's production is static files on Cloudflare/GitHub Pages (no telnetd), BUT (a) the BI dashboard / any internal admin service may run on a host with a legacy daemon inadvertently enabled; (b) build/CI agents sometimes inherit legacy services from base images; (c) the pattern (legacy protocol parser overflow) extends to ANY service GameZipper's infra depends on. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **scan all GameZipper infra hosts for legacy daemons** — `ss -tlnp | grep -E ':23\b|:21\b|:25\b|:161\b'` (telnet/FTP/SMTP/SNMP ports) must return nothing on production + build hosts; if any legacy daemon is listening, it is a CVSS-9.x unauthenticated-RCE exposure; (2) verify no base Docker image / VM template used by the GameZipper pipeline ships telnetd/inetutils enabled (`docker history <image> | grep -i inetutils` / check cloud-init); (3) for the broader pattern, run a monthly "legacy service exposure" scan across all GameZipper-adjacent hosts (the BI dashboard VM, any jump host, the dev workstation) and alert on any newly-enabled text-protocol daemon.

### 38.5 CVE-2026-21992 Oracle Identity Manager / Web Services Manager — Unauthenticated HTTP RCE CVSS 9.8 (March 23 2026)
- [W-163] **[P1]** CVE-2026-21992 (CVSS 9.8, Oracle April 2026 Critical Patch Update, disclosed March 23 2026) — unauthenticated remote code execution via HTTP in Oracle Identity Manager 12.2.1.4.0 + 14.1.2.1.0 and Oracle Web Services Manager 12.2.1.4.0 + 14.1.2.1.0; Oracle's advisory states "this vulnerability can be exploited by HTTP, does not require authentication, and if successfully exploited allows remote code execution"; NVD describes it as "easily exploitable." **Source**: so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_05869c1125c24352 (March 23 2026 "Oracle修复关键漏洞CVE-2026-21992 防范身份管理器未授权远程代码执行"). **Why this is the IDENTITY-INFRASTRUCTURE UNAUTH-RCE dimension** — distinct from prior cases because it targets the IDENTITY/AUTH layer itself: compromising an identity manager yields not just one server but the ability to forge/mint credentials for every downstream service that trusts it (federation, SSO, OAuth token issuance). An identity-manager RCE is force-multiplied — one compromise → pivots to every integrated app. **Distinct from S-037 (SalesLoft/Drift OAuth supply-chain)**: S-037 is an OAuth-token-theft at a SaaS vendor; W-163 is a direct RCE in the on-prem identity appliance. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper itself does not run Oracle IAM, BUT (a) any GameZipper enterprise/education customer that authenticates via Oracle Access Manager / OIM would have their identity layer exposed, and a compromise of the customer's IAM could mint credentials that access the GameZipper content they proxy; (b) the dimension (auth-infrastructure-as-RCE-target) generalizes to whatever auth provider GameZipper's tooling uses (Cloudflare Access, GitHub OAuth, any SAML/SSO) — an unauthenticated RCE in the auth layer invalidates every downstream trust decision. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **inventory auth/identity dependencies** — document every identity provider / SSO / OAuth issuer that GameZipper tooling trusts (Cloudflare Access, GitHub, Google Workspace, any customer-federated IdP); (2) for each, subscribe to the vendor's CVE feed and track CVSS-9.x identity-layer vulnerabilities with the same urgency as browser CVEs (an identity-layer RCE is a total-trust-loss event); (3) verify GameZipper does not itself run Oracle IAM/OIM/WLS on any internal host (`grep -ri 'oracle\|weblogic\|oim' /etc/ /home/msdn/` for stray installs); (4) adopt a "defense in depth against identity-layer compromise" posture: even if the IdP is compromised, GameZipper's production deploy path (git push → GitHub Pages) should require a SECOND independent factor (signed commits, deploy-key not derived from SSO) so a forged IdP credential alone cannot push a malicious game to production.

### 38.6 ChatGPT 2026 Sponsored-Content / AI-Advertising — Official Vendor-Injected Discovery Displacement (The Information Dec 25 2025)
- [W-164] **[P2]** ChatGPT 2026 sponsored-content / AI-advertising (The Information report Dec 25 2025) — OpenAI employees were, as of late 2025, prototyping ways to inject **sponsored/paid results into ChatGPT's answers**: when a user asks a product-recommendation question (e.g., "best puzzle game"), a sponsored result would be prioritized/displayed-labeled-as-ad over the organic AI answer; the report notes "2026 is likely the first year humans need an ad-blocker for AI." **Source**: new.qq.com/rain/a/20251225A03OTE00 (Dec 25 2025 "2026年ChatGPT要加广告了 最懂你的AI都开始出卖你" — The Information via QQ News). **Why this is the AI-CHATBOT OFFICIAL-SPONSORED-CONTENT DISCOVERY-DISPLACEMENT dimension distinct from S-120 (ChatGPhish — attacker injects a phishing URL into an AI summary) and S-127 (cryptojacking-recommendation — attacker manipulates the AI into endorsing a malicious site)**: S-120/S-127 are ATTACKER-driven (a third party subverts the AI); W-164 is VENDOR-driven (the AI's own operator officially sells placement in the answer). The harm is different: S-120/S-127 cause direct security harm (phishing/malware); W-164 causes DISCOVERY/ECONOMIC harm — when a player asks an AI "fun puzzle game?" and a competitor's sponsored result displaces GameZipper, organic AI-mediated discovery of GameZipper drops even though GameZipper did nothing wrong and the AI's organic ranking would have favored it. This is the AI-era analogue of SEO-displacement but with no recourse (you cannot "optimize" for a sponsored slot you did not buy, and the AI does not expose its sponsorship weighting). **Distinct from W-157 (AI-summary >51% error rate)**: W-157 is unintentional inaccuracy; W-164 is intentional paid displacement. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: as AI-mediated discovery grows (Chrome 148 address-bar AI per W-158, Gemini/Copilot/Perplexity/Apple Intelligence), the share of players who find GameZipper via an AI answer (rather than a Google search result) rises; if the major AI vendors introduce sponsored placement in 2026, GameZipper's organic AI-discovery traffic is at risk of being outbid by better-funded competitors. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **monitor AI-mediated discovery share** — extend the BI/analytics dashboard to track referral traffic from AI surfaces (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Copilot, Chrome address-bar AI) as a distinct channel, so the baseline is captured BEFORE sponsored placement launches; (2) **periodic AI-discovery audit** — query the top AI assistants for "best puzzle game" / "fun browser game" / GameZipper game names and record whether GameZipper appears organically, appears as sponsored, or is displaced; (3) **structured-data optimization for AI ingestion** — ensure JSON-LD VideoGame schema + concise canonical descriptions are present (per W-157) so the organic ranking signal is as strong as possible before sponsored displacement erodes it; (4) budget-impact modeling: if AI-sponsored-placement launches, model the cost of maintaining current discovery share via paid AI placement vs the cost of declining organic AI discovery.

### 38.7 WebGPU + WASM Shared-Memory Architecture 2026 — WASM-Linear-Memory ↔ WebGPU-Buffer Boundary as a New Failure Surface
- [P-067] **[P2]** WebGPU + WASM shared-memory architecture 2026 (tool.lu/article/7yi/detail June 3 2026 "性能突破:WebGPU & WebAssembly(WASM)技术深度解析"; blog.csdn.net/gitblog_00372/article/details/158277179 March 4 2026; blog.csdn.net/gitblog_01177/article/details/158278649 March 27 2026) — the 2026 browser-game performance pattern is to run CPU-intensive workloads (physics, AI/ML inference, procedural generation, audio DSP) in **WebAssembly** and GPU-intensive workloads (rendering, compute shaders, particles) in **WebGPU**, with the two coordinating via **shared memory** (WASM linear memory ↔ WebGPU buffer mapping) rather than copy-based marshalling; concrete optimization patterns include shared memory, async scheduling, and resource pre-allocation. **Why this is the WASM-WebGPU SHARED-MEMORY BOUNDARY dimension distinct from P-045 (WebGPU shader cold-start stutter), P-047 (WebGPU 8.5x draw-call benchmark), P-060 (WebGPU data-race SafeRace research), and P-062 (WebGPU three-domain memory leak)**: those test WebGPU in isolation; P-067 tests the BOUNDARY between WASM and WebGPU where (a) a WASM heap corruption (out-of-bounds write in linear memory) can silently corrupt a WebGPU-mapped buffer that WASM handed to the GPU, producing GPU-side garbage with no WASM-side error; (b) a WebGPU device-lost event invalidates a mapped buffer that WASM is still reading/writing, yielding a use-after-free in WASM linear memory; (c) the synchronization between the WASM write and the GPU read has no language-level guard (WASM is untyped memory; WebGPU expects specific buffer layouts), so a layout mismatch (e.g., WASM writes float32 but the GPU reads as a packed uint16 norm) produces visual corruption that looks like a rendering bug but is actually a memory-layout contract violation at the boundary. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's current catalog is pure-Canvas/2D (no WASM+WebGPU pairing), BUT (a) any new game that uses a physics engine compiled to WASM (Box2D/Rapier) + WebGPU rendering hits this boundary; (b) the GameZipper puzzle-pipeline and any AI-NPC feature (per PC-014 WebGPU+WebNN) will increasingly pair WASM-compiled ML inference with WebGPU rendering; (c) the failure mode (silent corruption, no error) is exactly the kind of bug that post-deploy-verification's console-error check MISSES — the console is clean, the canvas renders garbage. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **for any game using WASM+WebGPU together, add a memory-boundary integrity check** — after each frame, validate that the WASM linear-memory region backing the WebGPU mapped buffer still matches the expected layout (a checksum/CRC of the struct headers); (2) **fuzz the boundary** — write a test that corrupts N bytes of WASM linear memory at random offsets within the mapped region and verify the game degrades gracefully (visible glitch) rather than crashing the GPU process (device-lost); (3) **verify device-lost recovery propagates to WASM** — when `GPUDevice.lost` fires, the WASM side must stop writing to the (now-invalid) mapped buffer, else it writes into freed/reused memory; (4) extend the post-deploy-verification Phase 4 canvas-pixel-change assertion (P-016) to specifically catch silent-corruption frames (canvas pixels change but in a structurally-wrong way — e.g., all channels shifted by a byte) that indicate a boundary-layout mismatch rather than a real render.

### 38.8 Browser-DevTools-Console CVE-Scanner Tool Pattern — Running Untrusted JS in a Console on a Sensitive Authenticated Origin (June 18 2026)
- [C-110] **[P2]** Browser-DevTools-console CVE-scanner tool pattern (cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2609303 June 18 2026 "GitHub CVE扫描器:一键发现React与Next.js安全漏洞") — a class of "security tools" distributed as JavaScript that the user is instructed to **paste directly into the browser DevTools Console** while on a sensitive authenticated origin (e.g., github.com, a cloud console, an admin panel); the specific tool scans GitHub repos for CVE-2025-55182 (React2Shell, per S-133) and CVE-2025-66478 (withdrawn Next.js variant) by running entirely inside the console with optional PAT configuration for private-repo access. **Why this is the DEVTOOLS-CONSOLE-RUNNING-UNTRUSTED-JS dimension** — the console inherits the FULL privileges of the page's origin: a script pasted into the console on github.com can read the user's GitHub session cookie, make authenticated API calls as the user, enumerate private repos, and exfiltrate the PAT the user pasted for "scanning." The pattern is a self-XSS-by-consent vector dressed as a helpful security tool, and it is structurally identical to the "paste this into your console to win a prize" scams of the 2010s but now camouflaged as CVE scanning. **Distinct from S-123 (clipboard/Win+R PowerShell self-typed execution)**: S-123 targets the OS shell via clipboard; C-110 targets the BROWSER origin via console — same social-engineering shape ("paste this to fix/scan X"), different execution context. **Distinct from S-029 (ad-iframe sandbox hardening)**: S-029 hardens iframes the page embeds; C-110 is about code the DEVELOPER/QA ENGINEER voluntarily pastes, which no iframe sandbox can block. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper QA engineers and pipeline operators routinely use DevTools consoles on sensitive origins (the BI dashboard, Cloudflare, GitHub, the production admin); a "paste this CVE scanner" tool circulated in a Slack/Discord/WeChat QA community could (a) exfiltrate the operator's GitHub PAT / Cloudflare API token / BI dashboard session, (b) be itself a vector for S-133-class exploitation if it posts to a server-function endpoint, (c) be a precursor to S-135-class credential theft. The risk is amplified because the tool LOOKS legitimate (it references real CVEs, real frameworks) and is shared by trusted peers. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **establish a "never paste unvetted JS into a console on a sensitive origin" policy** in the QA playbook — any console-paste tool must be (a) read in full by a human, (b) run first in a throwaway browser profile with no session cookies, (c) never given a real PAT/token; (2) **audit what origins GameZipper operators have consoles open on** — the BI dashboard, Cloudflare dashboard, GitHub, production admin are all high-value; add a Console-paste warning (Cloudflare Workers can inject a `console.warn` banner on sensitive routes); (3) **for the specific CVE-scanner pattern**, prefer running CVE scans via a CLI tool (`gh api`, `npm audit`, `trivy`) in a sandboxed terminal with scoped tokens rather than a browser console with full-session cookies; (4) train the pipeline to treat any "paste this to scan for CVE-X" instruction as a phishing-class social-engineering attempt until proven otherwise — the S-133 React2Shell coverage means there WILL be real CVE-scanner tools circulating, mixed with malicious ones, making the trust problem acute.

## CATEGORY 39: 2026-Q2 R189 DYNAMIC TEST INTELLIGENCE — FIREFOX-152-STABLE-SHIP + ANDROID-17-MEMORY-PRESSURE-ENFORCEMENT + SAFARI-ITP-STRATEGIC-SHIFT + CHINA-AI-SECURITY-COMPLIANCE-FRAMEWORK + SOVEREIGN-CERT-NPM-ADVISORY + PURPLE-TEAM-LLM-VULN-SCANNING + DESKTOP-CONSUMER-SOFTWARE-SUPPLY-CHAIN + WEBGPU-STREAMING-BUFFERS-PROGRESSIVE-LOAD (June 19 2026)

### 39.1 Firefox 152 Stable Ship — Experimental JPEG-XL + Notification.actions API + Element.getAnimations pseudoElement (June 16 2026)
- [B-106] **[P2]** Firefox 152 stable release (Mozilla June 16 2026) — Mozilla shipped Firefox 152 stable with **experimental JPEG XL image-format support** (the first new image format supported by Firefox in years, behind a flag initially), a redesigned settings page, enhanced media controls, expanded spell-checker languages, and developer-facing changes including the **Notification.actions read-only property + maxActions static read-only property** (Firefox bug 1959931 — supports notification actions set via ServiceWorkerRegistration.showNotification() and the platform-dependent maximum number of actions), and **Element.getAnimations() now accepts options.pseudoElement** (Firefox bug — allows targeting a specific pseudo-element directly rather than filtering results). Mozilla also announced the Firefox roadmap accelerating the **Nova 2026 redesign**. **Sources**: new.qq.com/rain/a/20260616A04F5B00 (June 16 2026 "火狐 Firefox 浏览器152稳定版发布:改版设置页面、增强媒体控制"); so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_4016a32165940252 (June 17 2026 "Mozilla 公告火狐 Firefox 浏览器路线图,推进2026最大改版落地"); developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Mozilla/Firefox/Releases/152 (developer release notes). **Why this is the FIREFOX-152-STABLE-SHIP dimension distinct from B-054 (Firefox 150 Nova announcement) and B-086 (Firefox 151 MFSA2026-46 sandbox-escape batch)**: B-054 was the redesign announcement; B-086 was a security patch batch; B-106 is the STABLE-SHIP MILESTONE of the next major version after Nova with experimental JPEG-XL support — a new image-format decode surface that affects any og:image/thumbnail on a game page if GameZipper ever adopts JPEG-XL (smaller payload, better quality than JPEG/WebP). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (1) any game page using Notification API for level-up/reward alerts should now test the actions array against `Notification.maxActions` on Firefox 152 (prior versions silently dropped actions beyond the platform limit); (2) any game using pseudo-element animations (::before/::after for particle effects via CSS) should test `Element.getAnimations({pseudoElement: '::before'})` — previous code that filtered results now over-counts because pseudo-elements are returned separately; (3) the JPEG-XL experimental flag may flip to default-on in a future Firefox 153 — if GameZipper serves JPEG-XL thumbnails behind a feature-detection check, Firefox 152 users with the flag off see broken images. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) run Top 30 games on Firefox 152 stable — assert zero console errors and no rendering regression; (2) verify Notification actions work correctly OR degrade gracefully when action count exceeds `Notification.maxActions`; (3) if any game uses CSS pseudo-element animations, verify `Element.getAnimations()` filters correctly with the new `pseudoElement` option; (4) verify any JPEG-XL content (if/when adopted) is gated behind feature detection (`document.createElement('img').decode` or `HTMLPictureElement` source-type check) so Firefox 152 without the experimental flag sees fallback JPEG/WebP; (5) verify the redesigned settings page did not change any `about:config` preferences that GameZipper QA scripts depend on.

### 39.2 Android 17 Memory-Pressure Enforcement — Google "整治臃肿应用" Initiative (April 19 2026)
- [B-107] **[P1]** Android 17 memory-pressure enforcement (Google April 19 2026 "安卓 17 新内存机制解决系统卡顿;谷歌整治臃肿应用:安卓 17 新内存机制解决系统卡顿,倒逼开发者优化 App") — Google announced that Android 17 will ship a new memory-management mechanism specifically designed to crack down on bloated apps/games that cause system jank; the platform will more aggressively OOM-kill apps/games that exceed their heap budget, especially during background-tab pressure and multi-app switching; the stated goal is "倒逼开发者优化 App" (force developers to optimize their apps). **Source**: so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_21969e4153437652 (April 19 2026 "安卓 17 新内存机制解决系统卡顿;苹果 2026 款 Mac Studio 爆料"). **Why this is the ANDROID-17-MEMORY-PRESSURE-ENFORCEMENT dimension distinct from B-099 (Android June 2026 Security Bulletin 124 CVEs) and P-059 (browser per-tab hard memory caps)**: B-099 covers CVE-level security fixes; P-059 covers the browser's own per-tab memory cap; B-107 covers the **OPERATING-SYSTEM-LEVEL policy change** that affects every app/game on Android 17, including the Android Chrome WebView that runs GameZipper mobile games. The OS will be MORE aggressive about killing background game tabs and long-running WebGL/Canvas sessions that hold large GPU heaps. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's mobile traffic is ~70% Android (per project context); a casual game that holds a large WebGL texture heap or accumulates WASM linear memory over a 30+ minute session becomes a prime OOM-kill candidate on Android 17, manifesting to the player as a sudden tab reload mid-game (loss of unsaved progress) or a hard browser crash. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for each Top 30 game, measure peak heap usage via `performance.memory.usedJSHeapSize` (Chrome) and WebGL texture memory via `gl.getParameter(...)` after 30 minutes of continuous play — flag any game exceeding ~150MB as Android-17 OOM-risk; (2) simulate Android-17 memory pressure using Chrome DevTools `chrome://flags #enable-memory-pressure-handling` or the Llamas-on-the-Web RAMpage tool (per P-064) — verify each game handles `visibilitychange` + memory-pressure events by releasing GPU resources and saving state; (3) verify game state is persisted to localStorage on every level transition (not just on page unload) so an OOM-kill mid-level restores to the last completed level; (4) audit rAF + setInterval cleanup — any untracked timer (per the rAF cancelAnimationFrame template bug and BGM setInterval leak patterns documented in post-deploy-verification) holds memory that Android 17 will reclaim via kill, losing more state.

### 39.3 Apple Safari ITP / Anti-Tracker Marketing Campaign — Strategic Browser-Share Shift Toward Safari (June 4 2026)
- [W-165] **[P2]** Apple Safari ITP / anti-tracker marketing campaign against Chrome (CNMO June 4 2026) — Apple released a marketing video titled "Privacy on iPhone: Safari helps block data trackers" that visualizes network trackers as chromium-silver-clad figures following the user while browsing, then disappearing when the user switches to Safari; the campaign promotes **Intelligent Tracking Prevention (ITP)**, **Privacy Report**, **anti-fingerprinting**, and **iCloud Private Relay** (which hides the user's IP address) as differentiators vs Chrome. **Source**: so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_8806a20f1dc61652 (June 4 2026 "苹果发布Safari宣传短片 暗讽Chrome浏览器泄露隐私"). **Why this is the SAFARI-ITP-STRATEGIC-SHIFT dimension distinct from S-103 (Privacy Sandbox killed — Google's own decision) and B-090 (Safari 27 WWDC26 features)**: S-103 is Google abandoning its own privacy framework; B-090 is a feature-release regression; W-165 is a **STRATEGIC MARKETING-DRIVEN BROWSER-SHIFT SIGNAL** — Apple actively marketing privacy as a competitive weapon will accelerate iOS Safari share at Chrome's expense, tightening 3rd-party tracker blocking across the GameZipper iOS player base. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper runs Monetag + AdSense which both rely on 3rd-party cookies and cross-site tracking for ad measurement, attribution, and frequency capping; as iOS Safari share rises (and ITP + Private Relay tighten), (a) ad-fill rates on iOS may drop because bidders lose cross-site user signals; (b) attribution breaks — GameZipper can no longer tell which ad creative drove a player to a game; (c) frequency capping breaks — the same player sees the same ad repeatedly because the bidder cannot dedupe them; (d) BI dashboard metrics (per the BI/openapi.json pattern) under-count iOS traffic because analytics beacons are blocked. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) measure iOS Safari ad-fill rate vs Android Chrome ad-fill rate over a 7-day window — a >20% iOS deficit indicates ITP impact; (2) verify Monetag/AdSense use Privacy Sandbox APIs (Topics API, PAAPI — though per S-103 these are being killed) or first-party-data fallbacks; (3) verify GameZipper's analytics endpoint (gz-analytics.js) uses `navigator.sendBeacon` with `keepalive: true` to survive ITP's beacon throttling; (4) test that iCloud Private Relay does not break game asset loading (Private Relay routes traffic through Apple's relay — any IP-geofenced CDN edge may serve a different region's assets); (5) document the ITP cookie-lifetime cap (currently 7 days for 1st-party, 24h for 3rd-party) in the Monetag/AdSense integration test matrix.

### 39.4 China 2026 AI Security Compliance Framework — Multi-Layer "组合拳" (May 9 2026)
- [W-166] **[P2]** China 2026 AI security compliance framework (so.html5.qq.com May 9 2026 "权威指南:2026年AI安全合规趋势解读与从业人员能力提升路径") — China's AI regulatory framework is consolidating into a multi-layer "组合拳" (combination-punch) covering (a) national law (网络安全法, 数据安全法, 个人信息保护法 + the 2023 生成式人工智能服务管理暂行办法); (b) provincial/municipal regulations (Shanghai, Beijing, Shenzhen each with local AI rules); (c) safety standards (TC260 national standards for AI safety); (d) ethics review (科技伦理审查). The framework specifically targets generative-AI applications — any product that uses AI to generate content (text, image, audio, code, level designs) is in scope. **Source**: so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_54169fe09af01352 (May 9 2026). **Why this is the CHINA-AI-SECURITY-COMPLIANCE-FRAMEWORK dimension distinct from S-033 (EU AI Act Article 50 transparency — EU-specific, single-law) and W-161 (Suno/Udio copyright litigation — IP, not regulatory)**: W-166 is CN-specific, multi-layer (law + provincial + standard + ethics), and applies to AI-generated content shipped to CN players specifically. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: per the `creative/songwriting-and-ai-music` and `mlops/models/audiocraft-audio-generation` skills, AI-generated audio is a documented GameZipper tooling path; any AI-generated BGM, AI hint system, or AI-procedural-level content shipped to CN-market players is subject to (a) content moderation requirements (AI-generated content must pass security review before public release); (b) labeling requirements (AI-generated content must be disclosed to the user — overlaps with EU AI Act Art. 50 but with CN-specific scope and enforcement); (c) data-source audit (the training data for any in-house AI model must be documented and lawful); (d) algorithm filing (服务提供者 must file their algorithm with the CAC if it has public-opinion or social-mobilization properties — most game AI does not, but a recommendation engine might). **Test for GameZipper**: (1) inventory all AI-generated content in the GameZipper catalog (AI BGM tracks, AI-generated level data, AI hint systems); (2) for each, document the model used, training-data source, and generation pipeline — required for algorithm filing and audit; (3) verify AI-generated content is labeled per CN requirements (an "AI-generated" badge or disclosure); (4) verify AI-generated content has passed a content-safety review (no politically-sensitive, obscene, or violent outputs); (5) monitor the CAC filing portal for any GameZipper feature that crosses the "public-opinion" threshold.

### 39.5 China 国家网络与信息安全信息通报中心 Official Shai-Hulud npm Supply-Chain Advisory (May 26 2026)
- [W-167] **[P2]** China 国家网络与信息安全信息通报中心 (National Cybersecurity Information Notification Center) official Shai-Hulud npm supply-chain advisory (May 26 2026) — China's national CERT-equivalent issued a formal public advisory confirming the npm "Shai-Hulud" (沙虫) supply-chain poisoning attack: attackers compromised npm official maintainer accounts and batch-published 600+ malicious versions across 300+ independent packages, affecting major open-source projects (echarts-for-react, the entire @antv series @antv/g2/g6/x6, the TanStack series 42 packages, Mistral AI-related PyPI packages, timeago.js). The advisory provides a specific detection check-list: inspect package.json / package-lock.json / pnpm-lock.yaml / yarn.lock + node_modules for anomalous preinstall/postinstall scripts; check Claude Code hooks, VS Code tasks.json for persistence (router_runtime.js, setup.mjs suspicious files) — confirming that the AI-AGENT-CONFIG-FILE-PERSISTENCE vector identified in S-130 is now recognized at the national-CERT level. **Source**: so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_4186a15535e00252 (May 26 2026 "国家网络与信息安全信息通报中心通报:主流JavaScript软件包管理平台npm遭供应链投毒攻击"). **Why this is the SOVEREIGN-CERT-NPM-ADVISORY dimension distinct from S-130 (TanStack/Mistral Shai-Hulud technical analysis) and C-108 (npm worm 2026 methodology framework)**: S-130 is a vendor/researcher technical writeup; C-108 is a methodology framework; W-167 is a **SOVEREIGN-CERT-LEVEL ADVISORY** — confirms that a national government (China) treats npm supply-chain attacks as national-security-infrastructure risk, signaling that CN-jurisdiction operators (including any GameZipper infrastructure hosted in CN, or any GameZipper operator who is a CN citizen/entity) have compliance obligations BEYOND vendor advisories — they must remediate per the national CERT's guidance and may face regulatory consequences for non-compliance. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) if GameZipper's BI dashboard, build agents, or any internal tooling runs on CN-hosted infrastructure, the operator has a regulatory obligation to perform the advisory's check-list and document remediation; (b) the advisory's specific mention of Claude Code hooks + VS Code tasks.json as persistence locations means GameZipper's agent-based build pipeline (Hermes + Claude Code + MCP servers) is in scope — the operator must verify no router_runtime.js / setup.mjs persistence exists; (c) the advisory elevates npm supply-chain risk from "developer hygiene" to "national security compliance" in CN jurisdiction. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) run the advisory's check-list across all GameZipper build/dev environments: `find . -name 'router_runtime.js' -o -name 'setup.mjs'` (must return 0 results); (2) inspect all package.json/package-lock.json files for anomalous preinstall/postinstall scripts; (3) inspect Claude Code hooks (~/.claude/settings.json + project .claude/) and VS Code tasks.json for unexpected entries; (4) document the remediation in a compliance log if any GameZipper infrastructure is CN-jurisdiction.

### 39.6 Purple-Team LLM Vulnerability Scanning Methodology — PurPaaS / OWASP Agentic AI Top 10 / CSA Red Teaming (June 2 2026)
- [C-111] **[P2]** Purple-team LLM vulnerability scanning methodology (github.com/topics/ai-vulnerability-assessment June 2 2026) — a category of open-source security-testing tools has emerged for automated red/blue-team (purple-team) assessment of locally-deployed LLMs via Ollama, scanning for prompt injection, model inversion, data leakage, jailbreaks, and adversarial abuse; key projects include **PurPaaS** (purple-team platform orchestrating autonomous agents to evaluate local LLMs), the **OWASP Top 10 for Agentic AI** (serves as the core for OWASP and Cloud Security Alliance red-teaming work), and a class of "security scanner for local LLMs" tools. **Sources**: github.com/topics/ai-vulnerability-assessment (June 2 2026); OWASP Agentic AI Top 10; CSA red-teaming working group. **Why this is the PURPLE-TEAM-LLM-VULN-SCANNING dimension distinct from C-110 (DevTools console CVE scanner — a tool that itself becomes a vector) and S-101 (AI-assistant browser extension prompt injection — an attack on a deployed AI)**: C-110 is a malicious-use-of-tools dimension;
