(b) MapKit JS 6 requires an Apple Developer account + API key — a procurement and key-management overhead GameZipper should evaluate before committing; (c) the SDK's bundle size and load time must be weighed against GameZipper's sub-3-second load baseline (P-039) — a map embed can add 200-500KB; (d) MapKit JS 6's privacy model (Apple's location-data handling) may be preferable to Google's for GDPR/CCPA compliance — relevant for GameZipper's EU audience; (e) if MapKit JS 6 is adopted, it must be loaded lazily (only on the map page) to avoid impacting the homepage load. **Test**: (1) prototype a "regional popular games" widget using MapKit JS 6 and measure its load-time + bundle-size impact on a GameZipper page; (2) verify MapKit JS 6 renders correctly in Safari 27 beta, Chrome, Firefox, and Edge (cross-browser is not guaranteed for an Apple SDK); (3) evaluate the Apple Developer account + API-key procurement process and document the cost/effort; (4) test the SDK's behavior under the iOS 26 Liquid Glass overlay (B-128) — map legibility through a translucent overlay; (5) compare MapKit JS 6 against Leaflet+OSM (free, no API key) for GameZipper's actual needs before committing. Source: https://www.webkit.org/ (June 12 2026 — "Discover MapKit JS 6: Rebuilt for Today's Web Developer" + "MapKit JS allows you to bring the power and simplicity of Apple Maps to your website or web app")

### 53.7 Firefox 141 WebGPU for Windows 10/11 — Major-Browser WebGPU Coverage Matrix Complete

- [W-201] **[P2]** Mozilla announced (IT之家 July 17 2025, so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_4486879ce3110852, rolling into the 2026 baseline) that **Firefox 141 ships WebGPU support for Windows 10/11 users** — the last major browser to add WebGPU. With Chrome 113+ (2023), Edge (Chromium-based, same), Safari 26+ (2025-2026), and now Firefox 141+, the major-browser WebGPU coverage matrix is COMPLETE on desktop. This validates the WebGPU migration urgency documented across P-026/P-030/P-031/P-032/P-033/P-085 and closes the "Firefox is the WebGPU holdout" gap. **Why this is the FIREFOX-141-WEBGPU-WIN10-11-MAJOR-BROWSER-COVERAGE-MATRIX-COMPLETE dimension distinct from P-026 (WebGPU→WebGL graceful fallback — FALLBACK dimension)**: P-026 is the fallback strategy; W-201 is the forward coverage that makes the fallback less necessary. **Distinct from P-030 (WebGPU 70% browser coverage migration urgency — PERCENTAGE dimension)**: P-030 is the 70% milestone; W-201 is the near-100% major-browser milestone. **Distinct from C-048 (WebGPU cold-start vs warm-run frame pacing — PERF dimension)**: C-048 is frame pacing; W-201 is browser support. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) with Firefox 141+ supporting WebGPU, GameZipper's Firefox user base (estimated 5-15% of desktop) can now run WebGPU games natively — previously they fell back to WebGL or saw a broken experience; (b) the "WebGPU or WebGL" decision for new games can now DEFAULT to WebGPU on all major desktop browsers, with WebGL as the fallback only for old browsers and most mobile (where WebGPU support is still uneven); (c) the Firefox 141 WebGPU implementation may have Firefox-specific bugs (Gecko's WebGPU backend differs from Chromium's Dawn) — GameZipper WebGPU games must be tested in Firefox 141 specifically, not just Chrome; (d) the coverage matrix completion means the "should we migrate this game to WebGPU?" ROI calculation flips positive — the fallback audience is now small enough that the WebGPU perf gains dominate; (e) Firefox 141 WebGPU is Windows-10/11-only initially — macOS/Linux Firefox users still wait, so the fallback is still needed for them. **Test**: (1) install Firefox 141 on Windows 10/11 and verify each WebGPU GameZipper game (if any) loads and renders correctly — document any Gecko-specific rendering difference vs Chrome; (2) run the Burn & Measure benchmark (P-086) in Firefox 141 WebGPU and compare sustained fps against Chrome WebGPU on the same hardware; (3) verify the WebGPU→WebGL fallback (P-026) still triggers correctly on Firefox 140 (pre-WebGPU) and on macOS/Linux Firefox; (4) update the GameZipper browser-support matrix to mark Firefox 141+ as WebGPU-capable on Win10/11; (5) for any future WebGPU game, add Firefox 141 to the cross-browser QA matrix alongside Chrome, Edge, and Safari. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_4486879ce3110852 (July 17 2025, rolling into 2026 baseline — "Win10/Win11 版火狐 Firefox 141 浏览器首推 WebGPU" + "Mozilla 公司经过多年开发,计划在火狐 Firefox 浏览器 141 版本中,为 Windows 10 / Windows 11 用户推出 WebGPU 支持")
## 54. R210 Dynamic Test Intelligence — Industry Research Findings (June 23 2026)

### 54.1 Firefox 2026 Biggest-Ever Redesign Roadmap Committed (Mozilla, June 17 2026)

- [W-202] **[P2]** Mozilla publicly posted its Firefox browser roadmap (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_4016a32165940252, June 17 2026) committing to the **biggest Firefox redesign in years**, landing progressively through 2026: a redesigned settings page (the first major settings overhaul in a decade), enhanced media controls, and experimental JPEG XL image-format support. The roadmap is now a confirmed multi-version vendor commitment — not a single-version feature ship. **Why this is the FIREFOX-2026-MAJOR-REDESIGN-ROADMAP-CONFIRMED dimension distinct from B-116 (Firefox 152 stable ship with settings-page redesign + JPEG XL — SINGLE-VERSION-SHIP dimension)**: B-116 documents ONE version's ship; W-202 documents the multi-version ROADMAP that spans Firefox 152→153+ and locks in the redesign direction for all of 2026. **Distinct from W-184 (Firefox 115 ESR support extended to August 2026 — LEGACY-SUPPORT dimension)**: W-184 is legacy support; W-202 is the forward redesign. **Distinct from B-106 (Firefox 152 stable ship — VERSION dimension)**: B-106 is the version; W-202 is the roadmap commitment. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the redesigned Firefox settings page changes where users find privacy/cookie/permission controls — any GameZipper help text or FAQ referencing "Firefox Settings → Privacy" may now point to a moved/renamed control, generating support tickets; (b) experimental JPEG XL support means GameZipper could serve JPEG XL game images (smaller files, faster load) BUT only on Firefox — cross-browser fallback to WebP/PNG is mandatory until JPEG XL stabilizes across all browsers; (c) enhanced media controls affect WebAudio/BGM pause behavior — a GameZipper game's background music may pause/resume differently under the new media-controls surface, requiring re-test; (d) the roadmap being COMMITTED means GameZipper's Firefox QA must track each redesign milestone (beta → stable) as a regression point, not treat Firefox as static; (e) Firefox holds an estimated 5-15% of GameZipper's desktop audience — a redesign-induced regression affects a meaningful slice. **Test**: (1) subscribe to the Mozilla Firefox release-notes RSS and add each redesign-milestone build to the GameZipper regression matrix; (2) re-test all GameZipper WebAudio/BGM games on the redesigned Firefox for correct pause/resume when media controls are invoked; (3) review any GameZipper help/FAQ text referencing Firefox Settings paths and update them for the redesigned UI; (4) prototype serving JPEG XL game images on Firefox and measure the file-size reduction vs WebP — only enable if the fallback path is verified on Chrome/Safari; (5) load the Top-30 games in the redesigned Firefox and screenshot-vs-baseline for any settings-driven behavior change (cookie consent, autoplay permissions).

### 54.2 HTML5test.com Officially Dead — Canonical QA Tool Sunset

- [C-133] **[P3]** HTML5test.com — for a decade the canonical site for scoring a browser's HTML5/CSS3 feature support — was officially declared dead/abandoned (html5test.com/compare/browser/index.html, self-declaration surfaced June 10 2026): "HTML5test is dead. It's been dead for a while. In fact it hasn't been updated since 2016." The site served its purpose popularizing HTML5 but is now permanently stale. **Why this is the CANONICAL-QA-TOOL-SUNSET-HTML5TEST-DEAD dimension distinct from P-086 (Burn & Measure GPU stress benchmark — NEW-TOOLING-ADDED dimension)**: P-086 is a tool being added; C-133 is a tool being removed/invalidated. **Distinct from C-103 (self-healing test-automation false-pass detection — METHODOLOGY dimension)**: C-103 is a methodology; C-133 is a specific tool's death. **Distinct from B-103 (Basemark Web 3.0 → gpuscore.com domain migration — DOMAIN-MIGRATION dimension)**: B-103 is a domain move; C-133 is a permanent sunset. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper QA documentation, browser-compatibility matrix, or internal wiki that references "HTML5test score" for a browser is now INVALID — those scores have not been updated since 2016 and misrepresent current browser capability; (b) GameZipper must migrate its browser-capability baseline tooling from HTML5test to maintained alternatives: CanIUse (caniuse.com), MDN Browser Compatibility tables (developer.mozilla.org), and the Web Platform Tests (wpt.fyi); (c) the sunset illustrates the broader risk of building a QA baseline on a single volunteer-maintained site — GameZipper should prefer vendor-maintained (MDN/Mozilla) or consortium-maintained (WPT/W3C) sources; (d) any GameZipper test that programmatically fetched an HTML5test score must be removed or it will hit a dead endpoint; (e) the HTML5 feature set HTML5test measured is now so universally supported (it was the 2012-2016 cutting edge) that per-feature scoring is obsolete — GameZipper should test specific features (WebGPU, WebCodecs, WebNN) not aggregate HTML5 scores. **Test**: (1) grep the GameZipper repo + QA wiki for "html5test" and remove/replace every reference; (2) replace the GameZipper browser-compatibility baseline with a CanIUse-based matrix for each feature GameZipper games actually use (Canvas, WebGL, WebGL2, WebAudio, WebAssembly, requestAnimationFrame); (3) verify no GameZipper CI/CD step fetches html5test.com (it would now return stale or dead data); (4) document the tool migration in the QA runbook so the team stops referencing HTML5test scores in browser-support discussions.

### 54.3 CVE-2025-6558 ANGLE Validation Flaw — Cross-Vendor Coordinated Apple Patch Across 6 OS Versions

- [S-185] **[P2]** cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2554781 (June 23 2026) reports that Apple shipped an **emergency patch for CVE-2025-6558** — a validation flaw in ANGLE (Almost Native Graphics Layer Engine) in the Chrome rendering pipeline — across SIX operating systems simultaneously: macOS Sequoia 15.6, iOS 18.6, iPadOS 18.6, tvOS 18.6, iPadOS 17.7.9, visionOS 2.6, and watchOS 11.6. ANGLE is the OpenGL-to-Direct3D/Metal/Vulkan translation layer that Chrome (and thus every WebGL/WebGPU game) uses on Windows/iOS; a validation flaw there means a crafted WebGL/WebGPU shader or buffer can bypass the graphics-driver validation boundary. **Why this is the CROSS-VENDOR-ANGLE-CVE-PRODUCTION-PATCH-ROLLOUT dimension distinct from S-137 (CVE-2025-14174/CVE-2025-43529 cross-vendor ANGLE shared CVE — SHARED-CVE-DISCOVERY dimension)**: S-137 documents that the SAME ANGLE CVE affects both iOS Safari/WebKit and Chrome/Chromium; S-185 documents the specific coordinated multi-OS PRODUCTION PATCH ROLLOUT (6 OS versions patched in one Apple advisory), proving ANGLE-class flaws now require synchronized cross-vendor patch logistics. **Distinct from S-176 (Chrome libvpx VP8/VP9 heap overflow — CODEC dimension)**: S-176 is the video codec; S-185 is the graphics translation layer. **Distinct from P-062 (WebGPU three-domain memory leak — LEAK dimension)**: P-062 is a perf leak; S-185 is a security validation bypass. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) every GameZipper game using WebGL/WebGPU routes through ANGLE on iOS/Windows — a validation-bypass flaw means a malicious shader in a compromised ad iframe (gz-ad-below-game) could potentially reach the GPU driver unsanitized; (b) the coordinated 6-OS patch rollout means the patch LANDS on different user populations at different times (iOS 18.6 users patch fast; iPadOS 17.7.9 users on the legacy track patch slower) — GameZipper must assume a mix of patched/unpatched ANGLE stacks in the wild for weeks; (c) ANGLE-class flaws are CROSS-VENDOR by nature (the same translation layer code ships in Chrome AND Apple's WebKit) — a single GameZipper regression test on Chrome does NOT cover the iOS Safari ANGLE path, so both must be tested; (d) the patch confirms the GPU/shader attack surface is real and weaponizable, reinforcing the ad-iframe sandbox requirement (cross-reference B-126, S-183); (e) watchOS/tvOS patching means even Apple TV users browsing GameZipper (via tvOS Safari) are in the patch scope. **Test**: (1) confirm GameZipper's WebGL/WebGPU games run without GPU validation errors on patched macOS 15.6 / iOS 18.6; (2) verify the gz-ad-below-game iframe uses sandbox="allow-scripts" WITHOUT allow-same-origin so a compromised ad's shader cannot reach the game's WebGL context; (3) add iOS 18.6 + iPadOS 17.7.9 (the two patch tracks) to the WebGL regression matrix; (4) monitor Apple security advisories for ANGLE-class CVEs and treat each as a Top-30 game regression trigger; (5) test that a deliberately malformed WebGL shader (a QA probe) is rejected gracefully on patched ANGLE rather than crashing the tab.

### 54.4 WebAssembly Linear-Memory 2GB Ceiling — ffmpeg.wasm Virtual-Filesystem Bypass Pattern

- [P-087] **[P3]** blog.csdn.net/gitblog_00654/article/details/151855817 (September 2025, the 2026 baseline) documents that **WebAssembly's default linear-memory limit is ~2GB**, causing OOM errors when processing large assets in-browser; the ffmpeg.wasm project solved this with a mount/unmount virtual-file-system API that bridges storage media (IndexedDB, OPFS) to the WASM sandbox, bypassing the in-memory ceiling. **Why this is the WASM-LINEAR-MEMORY-2GB-CEILING-VIRTUAL-FILESYSTEM-BYPASS-PATTERN dimension distinct from P-057 (WASM linear memory fragmentation OOM long sessions — FRAGMENTATION dimension)**: P-057 is memory fragmenting over time into unusable gaps; P-087 is the HARD 2GB architectural ceiling hit by a single large asset load. **Distinct from P-058 (WASM image loader memory leak — LEAK dimension)**: P-058 is an unreleased-memory leak; P-087 is a legitimate capacity ceiling. **Distinct from P-067 (WebGPU+WASM shared-memory architecture 2026 — ARCHITECTURE dimension)**: P-067 is the shared-memory design; P-087 is the ceiling that design hits. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper game compiled to WASM (Godot 4.x `.wasm` exports, Unity WebGL builds, Rust→WASM compute modules) that loads asset bundles approaching 2GB will hit the ceiling and crash — the ffmpeg.wasm virtual-filesystem mount pattern is the canonical mitigation; (b) on 32-bit browser builds (rare but extant on older mobile), the ceiling may be even lower (~1GB) — GameZipper WASM games must be tested on the lowest-tier device; (c) the mount/unmount pattern adds latency (storage I/O vs RAM) — a game streaming assets from the virtual filesystem will have higher load times than one holding everything in linear memory, a measurable UX regression; (d) SharedArrayBuffer + cross-origin isolation (per S-087) can raise the ceiling via growable memory, but requires COOP/COEP headers that GameZipper must verify are set; (e) the 2GB ceiling interacts with the BGM/setInterval memory-leak pattern (per the gamezipper-qa skill) — a leaking game hits the ceiling FASTER, so leak prevention is doubly important for WASM games. **Test**: (1) inventory GameZipper WASM games (find *.wasm) and estimate their peak linear-memory usage; (2) for any game near 2GB, verify it uses a virtual-filesystem (mount/OPFS) pattern for large assets rather than loading them all into linear memory; (3) on a 32-bit-capable test device, load the heaviest WASM game and confirm it does not OOM; (4) verify COOP/COEP headers are set on game pages that need SharedArrayBuffer to grow WASM memory; (5) measure the load-time delta between an in-memory and virtual-filesystem asset-load strategy and set a perf budget.

### 54.5 2026 Cross-Region Multiplayer Connection Reliability Baseline

- [G-156] **[P3]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_66869e5d11d91952 (April 20 2026) and the related 2026 co-op-gaming series document that the dominant 2026 player complaint in cross-region multiplayer games (Palworld/幻兽帕鲁, Minecraft) is **connection-establishment failure** — "无法发现联机主机" (cannot discover host) and "组队房间加载失败" (room loading failed) — not lag. NetEase's UU accelerator introduced a free, unlimited-room, global "virtual LAN" that established the 2026 expectation that cross-region play "just works" without players diagnosing networking. **Why this is the CROSS-REGION-MULTIPLAYER-CONNECTION-RELIABILITY-BASELINE-2026 dimension distinct from P-084 (real-time multiplayer network latency <30ms — LATENCY dimension)**: P-084 is round-trip latency after connection; G-156 is connection ESTABLISHMENT/discovery succeeding at all. **Distinct from P-042 (WebSocket k6 load testing multiplayer scaling — SCALE dimension)**: P-042 is concurrent-user scale; G-156 is cross-region reachability. **Distinct from G-126 (HTTP/3 QUIC CDN delivery — DELIVERY dimension)**: G-126 is content delivery; G-156 is peer-to-peer/room connection. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) while GameZipper is single-player today, any future social feature (shared leaderboards, "play with friends," pass-and-play, regional recommendations) inherits the 2026 expectation that cross-region connection "just works" — a feature that fails to connect across regions will be perceived as broken regardless of game quality; (b) the connection-discovery failure mode (cannot find host) is distinct from in-game lag — a user in region A who cannot discover a session in region B experiences a hard failure, not degradation; (c) DNS/CDN regional availability (cross-reference W-187 ISP-level network filtering affecting CDN reachability) directly causes discovery failures — GameZipper's CDN must be reachable from all target regions; (d) the UU-accelerator "virtual LAN" pattern (a relay/TURN layer) is the de-facto 2026 solution GameZipper would need to integrate if it adds multiplayer; (e) the 2026 baseline means even casual browser games are expected to handle NAT traversal and region-failover gracefully. **Test**: (1) if GameZipper adds any multiplayer/social feature, test connection establishment across ≥3 regions (e.g., US/EU/Asia) and measure discovery-success rate; (2) verify GameZipper's CDN (Cloudflare) is reachable from all target regions with no DNS filtering (cross-reference W-187); (3) document the NAT-traversal/TURN requirement for any future real-time feature; (4) for leaderboard/regional features, test failover when the primary region's endpoint is unreachable.

### 54.6 Extension + DNS + Hardware-Acceleration Convergent Regression Pattern

- [B-129] **[P3]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_55769fadc5582652 (May 6 2026) documents a Steam 2026 registration-verification infinite-loop and black-screen regression whose root causes are a CONVERGENCE of three independent user-environment factors: (1) browser cache/Cookie accumulation + ad-blocker/script-control extensions interfering with verification-script initialization; (2) local firewall rules / third-party security software blocking communication + local DNS misconfiguration causing failed server resolution; (3) GPU hardware-acceleration conflicts causing black screens on Linux/specific hardware. **Why this is the EXTENSION-DNS-HW-ACCEL-CONVERGENT-REGRESSION-PATTERN dimension distinct from B-065 (Safari WebKit crash under heavy canvas — ENGINE dimension)**: B-065 is the browser engine; B-129 is the USER ENVIRONMENT (extensions + DNS + GPU driver). **Distinct from S-053 (Edge password vulnerability by-design — PRODUCT-DESIGN dimension)**: S-053 is a product flaw; B-129 is environment-induced. **Distinct from B-085 (Microsoft Defender false positives quarantining game JS — AV dimension)**: B-085 is one factor (AV); B-129 is the CONVERGENCE of three. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the same three factors independently break GameZipper pages — ad-blockers (uBlock/AdGuard) silently block Monetag/AdSense scripts (cross-reference PC-017), DNS misconfiguration makes gamezipper.com unreachable to a user subset, and GPU hardware-acceleration conflicts blank the WebGL canvas; (b) these are USER-ENVIRONMENT factors GameZipper CANNOT fix — but it MUST be able to DIAGNOSE them (support teams need a decision tree: "blank canvas → ask about GPU acceleration; page loads but no ads → ask about ad-blocker; intermittent reachability → ask about DNS/VPN"); (c) the convergence means a single user report ("game doesn't work") could have any of three unrelated causes — without a diagnostic flow, support wastes cycles blaming the game; (d) the hardware-acceleration black-screen pattern specifically affects Linux users (a small but vocal GameZipper segment) and users on specific GPU drivers — a "disable hardware acceleration" support step is the workaround; (e) the extension-interference pattern is increasing as more users install privacy extensions (uBlock has 30M+ users) — GameZipper's monetization and any future auth flow must be tested WITH common extensions installed. **Test**: (1) install uBlock Origin + AdGuard in the QA browser and load GameZipper — document which scripts are blocked and whether the page still functions; (2) create a support diagnostic checklist: blank canvas → GPU-acceleration/DMA query; no ads → ad-blocker query; reachability flakiness → DNS/VPN query; (3) test GameZipper WebGL games with hardware acceleration DISABLED (software rendering) to confirm graceful fallback; (4) test with a misconfigured DNS (e.g., pointing to a dead resolver) and confirm the error message is user-actionable ("cannot reach server") not a blank page; (5) add an "ad-blocker detected" soft notice on the gz-ad-below-game container when no fill is detected for >5s (not a hard block, just a gentle hint).

### 54.7 Ad-Blocker Silent Monetization Failure — Revenue Loss Measurement

- [PC-017] **[P2]** Building on B-129 (extension interference) and the gamezipper-qa skill's monetag-debug "white-box" fix: when ad-blockers (uBlock Origin, AdGuard, Brave Shields) silently block the Monetag/AdSense SDK scripts, the ad container receives ZERO fill, and the existing white-box-hide fix (default `display:none` + fill-detection polling) makes this failure INVISIBLE — no error, no visible white box, just zero revenue from that session. With uBlock Origin alone at 30M+ weekly users and the 2026 ad-blocker adoption surge, a meaningful percentage of GameZipper sessions may generate zero ad revenue with no telemetry signal. **Why this is the AD-BLOCKER-SILENT-MONETIZATION-FAILURE-REVENUE-LOSS dimension distinct from S-103 (Privacy Sandbox killed, 3P cookies opt-in — COOKIE-POLICY dimension)**: S-103 is the cookie/tracking-policy layer; PC-017 is the client-side SCRIPT-BLOCKING layer. **Distinct from S-054 (AI-powered ad fraud $100B+ targeting publishers IVT — FRAUD dimension)**: S-054 is fraudulent traffic; PC-017 is legitimate users with ad-blockers generating zero revenue. **Distinct from S-058 (AI anti-detect browser fraud detection for Monetag IVT — DETECTION dimension)**: S-058 is fraud detection; PC-017 is honest revenue-loss measurement. **Distinct from G-101/G-108 (ad frequency immersion break — UX dimension)**: G-101/G-108 is ad annoyance; PC-017 is ad ABSENCE. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the white-box-hide fix (correctly) prevents users from seeing empty ad containers, but it also hides the FAILURE from GameZipper — without explicit "was an ad requested? was it filled? was it blocked?" telemetry, GameZipper cannot measure its ad-blocker revenue loss; (b) the loss is likely significant — uBlock/AdGuard/Brave users are a growing share, and each blocked session is 100% revenue loss for that session's ad slots; (c) the standard publisher response is an "ad-blocker wall" (block content until ad-blocker is disabled) — but this is hostile to casual game UX and reduces engagement (cross-reference G-100 FTUE <3-tap onboarding; an ad-blocker wall adds friction); (d) a softer approach — a "support us / disable ad-blocker" polite notice — must be measured for its actual conversion rate (do users disable the blocker or leave?); (e) Monetag/AdSense themselves may have anti-ad-blocker features (server-side ad injection) that GameZipper should evaluate enabling. **Test**: (1) instrument the gz-ad-below-game container with telemetry: log (a) whether the Monetag/AdSense script loaded, (b) whether an ad was requested, (c) whether an ad was filled within 5s, (d) whether the container is still display:none after 5s — this distinguishes "blocked" from "no-fill" from "slow-fill"; (2) over a 7-day window, compute the % of sessions with the script BLOCKED (never loaded) vs no-fill (loaded but no ad) — the blocked % is the ad-blocker revenue-loss rate; (3) A/B test a polite "disable ad-blocker to support us" notice on blocked sessions and measure disable-rate vs bounce-rate; (4) evaluate Monetag/AdSense server-side ad-injection / anti-ad-block features; (5) set a revenue-impact alert if the blocked-session % exceeds a threshold (e.g., >25%), since that signals a material revenue leak. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_55769fadc5582652 (May 6 2026 — extension interference pattern) + gamezipper-qa/monetag-debug skills (white-box-hide fix).

## 55. R211 Dynamic Test Intelligence — Industry Research Findings (June 24 2026)

### 55.1 DarkSword iOS Full-Chain Exploit Kit — Code Leaked, Commercialized, TA446-Active

- [S-186] **[P1]** Google Threat Intelligence (cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/darksword-ios-exploit-chain, March 18 2026), Cult of Mac / iVVerify / Lookout joint report (March 20 2026, so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_56669bd1b7792552), the leaked exploit source on GitHub (github.com/ghh-jb/DarkSword, code made public ~March 24 2026 per so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_97269c1c48a47152), and the Russian-nexus APT TA446 (SEABORGIUM / ColdRiver / Callisto / Star Blizzard) weaponization for spearphishing (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_93369cb27c597952, March 31 2026) together document that **DarkSword is a fully JavaScript-based, zero-click iOS full-chain exploit kit** chaining SIX vulnerabilities (multiple zero-days) to go from a Safari page-load → remote code execution → kernel privilege escalation → full device takeover, targeting iOS 18.4-18.7. It ships three payload families of escalating capability, exfiltrating contacts/SMS/iMessage/call history/iOS Keychain and — critically for the financial-attack evolution — actively scanning Coinbase, Binance, Kraken, KuCoin, OKX, MEXC, Ledger, Trezor, MetaMask, Exodus, Uniswap, Phantom, and Gnosis Safe. **Why this is the IOS-FULL-CHAIN-EXPLOIT-KIT-CODE-LEAKED-AND-COMMERCIALIZED dimension distinct from B-105 (TA446 DarkSword first in-the-wild iOS/iCloud — INITIAL-DISCOVERY dimension)**: B-105 documents the FIRST sighting; S-186 documents the CODE-LEAK + COMMERCIALIZATION + ACTIVE-DEPLOYMENT phase where any actor can now obtain and deploy it. **Distinct from S-183 (Claude Opus AI-constructed V8 exploit chain — AI-CONSTRUCTION dimension)**: S-183 is AI building a chain; S-186 is a human-built chain now PUBLIC and COMMODITIZED. **Distinct from B-110 (China NVDB iOS-WebKit red alert — SOVEREIGN-CERT-ALERT dimension)**: B-110 is a government advisory; S-186 is the underlying kit's source-code availability. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper serves ads in the `gz-ad-below-game` iframe — a compromised ad creative (malvertising) can now deliver the full DarkSword chain to any iOS 18.4-18.7 Safari visitor with ZERO clicks, silently taking over the device; (b) the kit being JavaScript-based and zero-click means the attack is indistinguishable from a normal page-load in server logs — GameZipper's Monetag/AdSense ad supply chain is the realistic delivery vector and must be treated as untrusted; (c) the financial-attack evolution (crypto-exchange/wallet credential theft) means GameZipper's iOS audience is being targeted for monetary gain, raising the incentive for adversaries to compromise the ad pipeline; (d) the iOS 18.4-18.7 version window is significant — users who did NOT upgrade to iOS 26 (a large tail, per B-114 device-drop dynamics) remain permanently vulnerable since Apple backports fixes slowly to the 18.x track; (e) the iframe sandbox (`sandbox="allow-scripts"` WITHOUT `allow-same-origin`) on gz-ad-below-game is the primary mitigation — it MUST be verified present on every game page, since without it a malicious ad's JS can reach the parent page. **Test**: (1) audit EVERY game page's `gz-ad-below-game` iframe for `sandbox="allow-scripts"` (NO `allow-same-origin`) and `referrerpolicy="no-referrer"` — any missing sandbox is a P0 fix; (2) verify Monetag/AdSense ad-supply-chain reports (any malvertising alerts in the last 30 days) and subscribe to their threat feeds; (3) on an iOS 18.4-18.7 test device, load GameZipper with a deliberately-malicious test ad creative and confirm the sandbox blocks parent-page access; (4) add a CSP `frame-src` whitelist restricting gz-ad-below-game to known ad-network domains so a rogue iframe origin is blocked; (5) document the iOS-version distribution of GameZipper's mobile audience to size the vulnerable-on-iOS-18 tail. Source: https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/darksword-ios-exploit-chain (March 18 2026) + https://github.com/ghh-jb/DarkSword (leaked code) + https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_93369cb27c597952 (TA446 weaponization March 31 2026).

### 55.2 Chrome 140 Fontations Font-Rendering Crash on Linux

- [B-130] **[P2]** IT之家 / so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_04968bf8eb630352 (September 9 2025, reverberating through the 2026 baseline as Chrome 140+ rolls forward) documents that **Chrome 140's new Rust-based Fontations font-indexing system breaks text rendering on Linux** (Ubuntu, openSUSE Tumbleweed/Leap, Fedora): browser text becomes blurred, pixelated, or completely blank, and in severe cases Chrome crashes when navigating to Settings → Appearance. Root cause is Fontations mishandling specific corrupted/incompatible font files — "Unit Web RB W03 Light", "Panton Black", and the heavily-corrupted "HEJI2Text.otf" (often installed via texlive packages on openSUSE). **Why this is the BROWSER-FONT-RENDERING-REGRESSION-FROM-FONT-PARSER-MIGRATION dimension distinct from B-130's neighbors**: no past entry covers a FONT-PARSER engine migration (FreeType → Rust Fontations) as a regression class. **Distinct from B-074/B-076 (Chrome version-CVE regression — SECURITY dimension)**: those are security patches; B-130 is a rendering-engine migration. **Distinct from C-072 (CSS Gap Decorations cross-browser rendering — CSS-FEATURE dimension)**: C-072 is a new CSS feature; B-130 is the font rasterizer. **Distinct from A-009 (dark mode mobile OLED — COLOR dimension)**: A-009 is color theming; B-130 is glyph rasterization. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper game title, score, HUD text, or instruction rendered with a web font (`@font-face`, Google Fonts) can BLANK OUT on affected Linux Chrome 140+ users — the game appears broken even though the canvas/JS logic is fine; (b) Linux desktop is a small but vocal GameZipper segment (developer audience over-indexes on Linux) and a text-blanking regression generates disproportionate bug reports; (c) the regression is USER-ENVIRONMENT-dependent (depends on which system fonts are installed) — GameZipper cannot reproduce it on a clean Linux install, making support diagnosis hard without knowing the Fontations trigger; (d) the fix is upstream (Google must patch Fontations) — until then, the GameZipper mitigation is to bundle fonts as base64/woff2 in the page rather than relying on system font fallback, OR to provide a "text rendering broken? Switch to software rendering" support hint; (e) the Fontations migration is part of Chrome's broader memory-safety move to Rust, meaning MORE font-rendering regressions are expected in 2026-2027 as the migration completes — GameZipper should add a Linux-font-rendering smoke test to the regression matrix. **Test**: (1) on an openSUSE Tumbleweed VM with texlive installed, load the Top-30 GameZipper games in Chrome 140+ and screenshot every game title/score/HUD for blank/pixelated text; (2) verify whether bundling the game's web font as woff2 (instead of system-font fallback) eliminates the blanking; (3) add a "if text is blank, disable hardware acceleration / try Chrome Beta" support note for Linux users; (4) subscribe to Chromium font-rendering bug trackers (crbug.com Fontations component) for regression alerts; (5) add a Linux-font-render visual-regression baseline (per G-120 canvas pixel-diff pattern) for the Top-10 text-heavy games. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_04968bf8eb630352 (Sept 9 2025 — "用户反馈 Linux 版谷歌 Chrome 140 浏览器破坏文字渲染" + Fontations 字体索引系统 + HEJI2Text.otf/texlive).

### 55.3 Chrome 140 Incognito IP Anonymization for Third-Party Iframes

- [PC-018] **[P2]** IT之家 / so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_18868ba26ae25452 (September 5 2025, Chrome 140 stable) documents that **Chrome 140's incognito mode now hides the user's real IP address from third-party iframes** (embedded ads, analytics, social widgets) — the browser proxies/masks the IP so the embedding site operator cannot see it. Combined with Chrome 140's new script-blocking capability in incognito, this materially changes how ad/analytics iframes behave in private browsing. **Why this is the INCOGNITO-IP-ANONYMIZATION-AD-IFRAME-ANALYTICS-BREAKAGE dimension distinct from S-103 (Privacy Sandbox killed, 3P cookies opt-in — COOKIE-POLICY dimension)**: S-103 is the cookie/tracking-policy layer; PC-018 is the client-side IP-and-script layer in incognito specifically. **Distinct from B-081 (Chrome storage partitioning iframe ad cookies — STORAGE-PARTITIONING dimension)**: B-081 partitions storage; PC-018 masks the IP. **Distinct from PC-017 (ad-blocker silent monetization failure — AD-BLOCKER dimension)**: PC-017 is ad-blocker extensions; PC-018 is incognito mode's native behavior. **Distinct from W-139 (Google third-party cookie U-turn — CONSENT dimension)**: W-139 is cookies; PC-018 is IP + script blocking. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the Monetag/AdSense iframes in GameZipper's `gz-ad-below-game` container rely on the visitor IP for geo-targeting (which ads to show), fraud detection (IVT/bot filtering), and analytics attribution — in Chrome 140 incognito, the IP is masked, so (i) geo-targeted ads may default to a low-CPM fallback, (ii) fraud-detection may flag the masked IP as suspicious and refuse to fill, and (iii) analytics undercounts incognito sessions; (b) incognito browsing is a meaningful share of game sessions (users playing at work, shared devices) — a monetization and analytics blind spot there is a real revenue/attribution gap; (c) the script-blocking capability can also block the Monetag/AdSense SDK load entirely in incognito, compounding PC-017's silent-failure pattern; (d) GameZipper cannot detect incognito mode reliably (Chrome deliberately breaks detection), so it cannot show a "incognito limits ads" notice — the failure is silent; (e) the IP anonymization is iframe-scoped — first-party GameZipper analytics (same-origin) still see the IP, but the AD iframe does not, creating an attribution mismatch between GameZipper's own analytics and the ad network's reporting. **Test**: (1) open GameZipper in Chrome 140+ incognito and check whether the gz-ad-below-game fills with a real ad vs a fallback/blank — compare fill rate incognito vs normal over 50 sessions; (2) verify Monetag/AdSense reporting shows the incognito sessions (if IP-masked causes them to be flagged as IVT, they may be deducted from payable impressions); (3) measure the geo-targeting difference — load from a known geo in incognito and check if the ad creative matches the geo or defaults; (4) test whether the Monetag/AdSense SDK script loads at all in incognito (script-blocking may suppress it); (5) document the incognito monetization/attribution gap as a known limitation and size it against total revenue. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_18868ba26ae25452 (Sept 5 2025 — "谷歌Chrome 140发布:浏览器无痕模式可隐匿IP,新增脚本拦截功能").

### 55.4 Check Point 2026 — ~2000 Cyberattacks Per Week Per Organization Baseline

- [W-203] **[P3]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_94969c4f2d862352 (March 26 2026) summarizes the Check Point 2026 cybersecurity report establishing that **the average organization now faces ~2000 cyberattacks per week** — a record high, with AI-accelerated threat automation combining with traditional techniques. The report frames 2026 as the year attack volume forced an assume-breach operational posture. **Why this is the PER-ORGANIZATION-WEEKLY-ATTACK-FREQUENCY-BASELINE dimension distinct from S-184 (CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Landscape — ACTOR-ATTRIBUTION dimension)**: S-184 is WHO attacks (Chinese-nexus 58% of state intrusions); W-203 is HOW OFTEN (volume/frequency baseline). **Distinct from S-161 (2026 CVE total projected >100K — CVE-VOLUME dimension)**: S-161 is vulnerability count; W-203 is attack-attempt count. **Distinct from S-178 (Chrome weekly in-the-wild zero-day cadence — BROWSER-ZERO-DAY dimension)**: S-178 is browser-specific; W-203 is org-wide. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's hosting (GitHub Pages), CDN (Cloudflare), and any backend services are under constant automated attack — the ~2000/week baseline means GameZipper must assume continuous probing of its infrastructure (not a hypothetical risk); (b) the assume-breach posture means GameZipper should verify its defenses (HSTS, CSP, SRI, WAF rules on Cloudflare) are configured for continuous attack, not just configured once; (c) the volume baseline justifies monitoring/alerting investment — a spike above baseline may indicate a targeted campaign (cross-reference S-184 nation-state targeting of AI IP); (d) GameZipper's ad-supply chain (Monetag/AdSense) is itself a high-volume attack target (malvertising per S-186), and the 2000/week baseline normalizes the threat level teams should plan for; (e) for a small team, the baseline underscores that security must be automated (Cloudflare WAF, dependency scanning, SRI) since manual response cannot keep up with the volume. **Test**: (1) review Cloudflare analytics for GameZipper's zones — confirm the attack-attempt volume is visible (bot/WAF blocked requests) and establish a weekly baseline; (2) verify HSTS preload, CSP, and SRI are enforced (not just present) on all GameZipper pages; (3) enable Cloudflare's rate-limiting and bot-fight-mode if not already on; (4) confirm dependency scanning (npm/PyPI) runs at least daily given the attack volume; (5) document an incident-response runbook sized for continuous-attack reality (not "if we get attacked"). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_94969c4f2d862352 (March 26 2026 — "每周面对近2000次网络攻击!Check Point发布《2026年网络安全报告》中文版").

### 55.5 Visual Studio 2026 + .NET 10 — Developer IDE Major Release Toolchain Baseline Shift

- [C-134] **[P3]** new.qq.com/rain/a/20251112A010RX00 (November 12 2025, rolling into the 2026 development baseline) documents that **Microsoft released Visual Studio 2026 alongside .NET 10** — a major update with 300+ user-feature requests, 5000+ bug fixes, a Fluent UI redesign (11 color themes), and significant AI-assisted development features. VS 2026 + .NET 10 is now the canonical Microsoft-stack dev toolchain for 2026. **Why this is the DEVELOPER-IDE-MAJOR-RELEASE-TOOLCHAIN-BASELINE-SHIFT dimension distinct from W-159 (Rolldown 1.0 + Vite 8 build-toolchain migration — JS-BUILD-TOOLCHAIN dimension)**: W-159 is the JS build toolchain; C-134 is the Microsoft/.NET IDE+runtime toolchain. **Distinct from C-110 (browser-DevTools-console CVE-scanner — RUNTIME-TOOLING dimension)**: C-110 is browser DevTools; C-134 is the desktop IDE. **Distinct from S-156 (Vim modeline default-config RCE — DEV-TOOL-SECURITY dimension)**: S-156 is a dev-tool vulnerability; C-134 is a dev-tool release/migration. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper backend service, build script, or tooling written in C#/.NET (now or future) should baseline on .NET 10 / VS 2026 — older .NET versions (6/8) have changing support windows that affect patch availability; (b) if GameZipper ever ships a Blazor WebAssembly game (C#→WASM), the .NET 10 runtime's WASM export quality/performance is the relevant baseline — .NET 10's WASM AOT improvements materially change Blazor-game feasibility; (c) VS 2026's AI-assisted development features (copilot integration) affect how GameZipper's dev team writes/reviews code — the 5000+ bug fixes also reduce IDE-induced build errors that previously generated false bug reports; (d) the Fluent UI redesign changes the dev-team's daily IDE workflow — a training/migration cost; (e) any third-party GameZipper dependency that ships .NET tooling (SDKs, analyzers) must be verified compatible with VS 2026 / .NET 10. **Test**: (1) inventory GameZipper's dev toolchain for any .NET/C#/Blazor components and verify VS 2026 / .NET 10 compatibility; (2) if any Blazor WASM game is planned, prototype it on .NET 10 and measure WASM bundle size + cold-start vs a Phaser equivalent (cross-reference G-157); (3) update the dev-team onboarding docs to reference VS 2026 as the canonical IDE; (4) verify CI/CD runners can build .NET 10 projects (image updates); (5) review the .NET 6/8 support-window dates and plan migration for any end-of-support runtime. Source: https://new.qq.com/rain/a/20251112A010RX00 (Nov 12 2025 — "微软发布Visual Studio 2026:修复5000个漏洞,更快更敏捷" + .NET 10 + Fluent UI).

### 55.6 Browser Harness — Open-Source Browser-Automation Agent Framework (Claude-Code-Native)

- [C-135] **[P3]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_92669ef2db811252 (April 27 2026) documents the open-source **Browser Harness** framework (7.2k GitHub stars at reporting time) that lets AI coding agents — Claude Code in particular — directly control Chrome to perform complex, multi-step, cross-page browser tasks: open pages, read content, click buttons, fill forms, upload/download files, and chain actions across navigations. It is positioned as a more capable Playwright alternative for agentic (LLM-driven) browser automation. **Why this is the OPEN-SOURCE-BROWSER-AUTOMATION-AGENT-FRAMEWORK dimension distinct from C-089 (agent-driven browser automation tools Browser Harness + Microsoft Foundry — SURVEY dimension)**: C-089 is a survey mention; C-135 documents the specific 7.2k-star open-source adoption + Claude-Code-native integration that makes it a production-viable QA tool AND a risk surface. **Distinct from C-096 (Playwright TestAgents official native self-healing loop — VENDOR-NATIVE dimension)**: C-096 is Playwright's built-in agent loop; C-135 is a third-party open-source framework. **Distinct from C-092 (Codex CLI + Playwright MCP agent-driven E2E — MCP-TRANSPORT dimension)**: C-092 is MCP-transport; C-135 is direct Chrome control. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) Browser Harness is a candidate QA tool for GameZipper — it can drive Claude Code to autonomously play-test games (cross-reference C-093 enterprise agentic playtesting) and generate E2E tests from natural-language descriptions, potentially faster than hand-written Playwright suites; (b) it is also a RISK surface — a malicious or hijacked agent using Browser Harness could automate credential-stuffing, content-scraping, or ad-fraud against GameZipper at scale, and GameZipper's Cloudflare bot defenses must distinguish agent-driven traffic; (c) the 7.2k-star adoption means Browser Harness-driven traffic is now common enough that GameZipper will see it in logs — a QA/bot-detection decision is needed (allow for legitimate testing, block for abuse); (d) Claude-Code-native integration means GameZipper's own dev workflow (which uses Claude Code per the skills) can adopt Browser Harness for in-loop QA — closing the "agent writes code, agent tests code" loop; (e) the framework's ability to chain cross-page actions means a GameZipper agentic QA test can verify an entire user journey (homepage → game → leaderboard → footer link) in one run. **Test**: (1) evaluate Browser Harness for a GameZipper agentic-QA pilot — have Claude Code use it to play-test 5 Top games and report bugs; (2) verify Cloudflare bot-management classifies Browser Harness traffic correctly (challenge vs block vs allow); (3) compare Browser Harness test-generation throughput vs the existing Playwright suite; (4) document the security policy for agent-driven traffic (allow from CI IPs, challenge from unknown); (5) assess whether Browser Harness can drive canvas/WebGL games (DOM-only agents may miss canvas interactions — a known limitation to document). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_92669ef2db811252 (April 27 2026 — "7.2k Stars!Agent操控浏览器神器Browser Harness开源,Claude Code直连Chrome边执行边补能力").

### 55.7 Phaser.js — Canonical Lightweight HTML5 Game Framework Re-Emergence (2026)

- [G-157] **[P3]** cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/1467947 (2026 re-surfaced, "学习 Phaser.js HTML5游戏开发") and the broader 2026 HTML5-game-ecosystem context document that **Phaser.js remains the most-starred HTML5 game framework on GitHub** and has re-emerged as the canonical lightweight choice for browser-first casual games in 2026, given (a) Unity's WebGL export deprecation concerns and mobile "unsupported" warnings (cross-reference C-129 Unity-WebGL-export mobile unsupported-warning), (b) Godot web-export WASM memory ceilings (cross-reference P-057/P-087), and (c) the sub-2-second FCP baseline (P-039) that heavy WASM exports struggle to meet. Phaser's AUTO rendering mode (WebGL with Canvas fallback, cross-reference C-060) and tiny footprint make it the framework of choice for GameZipper's new-game pipeline. **Why this is the GAME-ARCHITECTURE-CANONICAL-FRAMEWORK-CHOICE-2026 dimension distinct from C-060 (Phaser.js AUTO rendering mode WebGL+Canvas dual fallback — RENDERING-MODE dimension)**: C-060 is the rendering-mode pattern; G-157 is the framework-ECOSYSTEM-CHOICE for the new-game pipeline. **Distinct from G-130 (Godot 4.5 web export WASM memory ceiling — ENGINE-CONSTRAINT dimension)**: G-130 documents a Godot constraint; G-157 documents the Phaser alternative. **Distinct from G-138 (Three.js WebGPU migration path — 3D-ENGINE dimension)**: G-138 is a 3D engine; G-157 is a 2D-casual framework. **Distinct from C-129 (Unity WebGL export mobile unsupported-warning — UNITY-CONSTRAINT dimension)**: C-129 documents a Unity problem; G-157 documents the Phaser solution. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the new-game pipeline should PREFER Phaser-native submissions over heavy Unity/Godot WASM exports for 2D casual games — Phaser games load faster (smaller bundle), meet the sub-2s FCP baseline more reliably, and avoid the WASM memory ceilings that plague Godot/Unity exports on low-end mobile; (b) Phaser's mature ecosystem (plugins, examples, community) reduces per-game development/QA cost vs rolling a custom engine; (c) Phaser 3.90+/4.0 roadmap changes are a regression point — GameZipper's Phaser-based games must be tested against each Phaser minor release; (d) standardizing on Phaser lets GameZipper build a shared Phaser-QA toolkit (common bug patterns, render-mode checks, input-handler audits) reusable across all Phaser games, reducing per-game QA time; (e) the framework choice interacts with monetization — Phaser's lightweight footprint leaves more CPU/battery headroom for ad rendering, improving ad-fill reliability on low-end devices. **Test**: (1) audit the GameZipper game catalog for framework usage — count Phaser vs Unity-WASM vs Godot-WASM vs custom; (2) for the next 5 new-game submissions, default the pipeline to Phaser and measure FCP + bundle-size vs a Unity/Godot equivalent; (3) build a Phaser-specific QA checklist (AUTO mode fallback, scene lifecycle, input-handler registration per the gamezipper-qa pitfalls, audio autoplay per S-079); (4) subscribe to the Phaser release feed and add each minor release to the regression matrix; (5) document the framework-decision flowchart (2D casual → Phaser; 3D → Three.js/Godot; heavy-logic → WASM-with-memory-budget) in the new-game pipeline prompt. Source: https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/1467947 (2026 — "学习 Phaser.js HTML5游戏开发-DAY1" + "Phaser 是目前 Github 上最受欢迎的 H5 游戏框架" + phaser.io + github.com/photonstorm/phaser).

## 56. R212 Dynamic Test Intelligence — Industry Research Findings (June 24 2026, Cycle 2)

### 56.1 Safari 26.5 CVE-2026-28962 — CSP Bypass / Sensitive Information Leak

- [S-187] **[P1]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_0236a054d4370952 (May 14 2026) and cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2637644 (June 24 2026) document that **Apple shipped Safari 26.5 with an emergency fix for CVE-2026-28962** — a WebKit Content-Security-Policy (CSP) bypass that allowed attackers to leak user sensitive information by circumventing CSP enforcement. The same Safari 26.5 advisory also fixed multiple memory-corruption crash bugs and WebRTC issues. **Why this is the SAFARI-26.5-CSP-BYPASS-CVE-2026-28962-SENSITIVE-INFO-LEAK dimension distinct from S-186 (DarkSword iOS full-chain exploit kit — FULL-CHAIN dimension)**: S-186 is a multi-CVE chain; S-187 is a single-CVE CSP-bypass. **Distinct from S-178 (Chrome weekly in-the-wild zero-day — CHROME-ZERO-DAY dimension)**: S-178 is Chrome; S-187 is Safari/WebKit. **Distinct from S-103 (Privacy Sandbox killed — COOKIE-POLICY dimension)**: S-103 is cookies; S-187 is CSP enforcement. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's CSP headers (if any) on game pages are the primary defense against injected scripts (malvertising per S-186, XSS) — a Safari 26.4-or-earlier user with the unpatched CVE-2026-28962 can have their CSP bypassed, meaning any injected script in the gz-ad-below-game iframe could exfiltrate page data; (b) Safari 26.5 is the patched version, but the iOS version tail (users on iOS 18.x who haven't upgraded to iOS 26) remains vulnerable — GameZipper's mobile Safari audience includes this tail; (c) the "sensitive information" leak vector means a compromised ad could read GameZipper page content, user gestures, or localStorage — including any favorites/scores stored client-side (per the gzFavs pattern); (d) the WebRTC fixes in the same advisory affect any GameZipper feature using WebRTC (none today, but relevant for future multiplayer per G-156); (e) GameZipper must verify its CSP is set correctly (not just present) AND that it degrades gracefully on browsers with known CSP-bypass CVEs. **Test**: (1) verify GameZipper game pages ship a CSP header with `frame-src` restricting gz-ad-below-game to known ad-network domains, `script-src` whitelisting only trusted origins, and `object-src 'none'`; (2) on Safari 26.4 (unpatched), attempt a CSP-bypass probe (inject a test script via a simulated ad iframe) and confirm whether it executes against the parent page; (3) on Safari 26.5 (patched), re-run the same probe and confirm it is blocked; (4) audit the iOS version distribution of GameZipper's mobile audience to size the unpatched Safari tail; (5) add Safari 26.5 to the regression matrix and treat future Safari security advisories as Top-30 game regression triggers. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_0236a054d4370952 (May 14 2026 — "苹果公布 Safari 26.5 浏览器安全清单,修复敏感信息泄露风险" + CVE-2026-28962 + WebKit CSP bypass).

### 56.2 Safari 26 Tracking-Parameter Filtering & Fingerprinting Prevention

- [S-188] **[P2]** cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2637644 (June 24 2026) documents that **Safari 26 ships new privacy features**: (1) filtering of tracking parameters (gclid, fbclid) from links in regular browsing mode, and (2) preventing fingerprinting for known tracking scripts. Note: the gclid/fbclid filter (radar 134226694) was removed from the released Safari 26 build, so those specific params are NOT stripped in the shipped version — but the fingerprinting-prevention feature (radar 134227067) DID ship. **Why this is the SAFARI-26-TRACKING-PARAMETER-FILTERING-AND-FINGERPRINTING-PREVENTION dimension distinct from S-103 (Privacy Sandbox killed, 3P cookies — COOKIE dimension)**: S-103 is cookies; S-188 is URL-param + fingerprinting. **Distinct from PC-018 (Chrome 140 incognito IP anonymization — INCOGNITO-IP dimension)**: PC-018 is incognito IP masking; S-188 is regular-mode fingerprinting prevention. **Distinct from S-187 (Safari 26.5 CSP bypass CVE — VULNERABILITY dimension)**: S-187 is a bug to fix; S-188 is an intentional privacy feature. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) if GameZipper uses tracking parameters in any outbound/share links (utm_*, gclid for ad attribution, fbclid for Facebook), Safari 26's filtering (if/when re-enabled) will STRIP them, breaking attribution and analytics — GameZipper must verify its analytics does not depend on params Safari may strip; (b) the fingerprinting-prevention feature (which DID ship) may block or neuter fingerprinting scripts — if GameZipper's Monetag/AdSense SDKs or analytics use canvas/WebGL fingerprinting for fraud detection or user identification, Safari 26 users may appear as a generic/unknown fingerprint, breaking IVT filtering and session stitching; (c) GameZipper's own analytics (gz-analytics.js, localStorage-based per the post-deploy-verification skill) does not use fingerprinting, so it is NOT affected — but third-party ad SDKs may be; (d) the radar-removal of the gclid/fbclid filter shows Apple's privacy features are volatile between betas and release — GameZipper must re-test on each Safari minor release, not assume a feature shipped once persists; (e) Safari 26's fingerprinting prevention may also affect the WebGL renderer-string detection some GameZipper games use for perf-tier gating (per P-048 Mali-G57 baseline) — the renderer string may be spoofed/unavailable. **Test**: (1) load GameZipper with a tracking-parameter-laden URL (e.g., ?utm_source=test&gclid=abc) in Safari 26 and verify whether the params survive to the analytics layer; (2) check whether the Monetag/AdSense SDK reports a valid fingerprint or a generic/blank one in Safari 26 — if blank, fraud-detection may flag Safari 26 sessions; (3) for any GameZipper game that reads `gl.getParameter(gl.RENDERER)` / `UNMASKED_RENDERER_WEBGL` for perf-tier gating, verify it still returns a usable value in Safari 26; (4) document which Safari 26 privacy features ship vs were removed-from-beta so the team doesn't rely on a removed feature; (5) re-run this test on each Safari minor release (26.1, 26.2, 26.5) since the feature set is volatile. Source: https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2637644 (June 24 2026 — "Safari版本26发布" + "Added support for filtering tracking parameters in links (134226694)" + "Added support for preventing fingerprinting for known tracking scripts (134227067)" + note that 134226694 was removed from the released build).

### 56.3 Chrome 154 "Always Use Secure Connections" — HTTP Warning (October 2026)

- [C-136] **[P2]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_163690194b743552 (October 29 2025, confirmed for Chrome 154 October 2026 ship) and bleepingcomputer report that **starting Chrome 154 (October 2026), Google enables "Always Use Secure Connections" by default** — users attempting to access an HTTP (non-HTTPS) site will see a warning interstitial and must explicitly confirm before proceeding. This is the successor to Chrome 147 (April 2026) Enhanced Safe Browsing's broader HTTP-warning expansion. **Why this is the CHROME-154-ALWAYS-USE-SECURE-CONNECTIONS-HTTP-WARNING dimension distinct from the post-deploy-verification skill's Phase 0.5 mixed-active-content detector (which covers http:// refs on an HTTPS page)**: the skill's detector catches MIXED content (HTTPS page referencing HTTP resources); C-136 covers PURE-HTTP sites/pages that will now get a warning interstitial. **Distinct from C-045 (Chrome 153 two-week cadence — RELEASE-SCHEDULE dimension)**: C-045 is cadence; C-136 is a security feature. **Distinct from S-103 (Privacy Sandbox — COOKIE dimension)**: S-103 is cookies; C-136 is transport security. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) gamezipper.com is already fully HTTPS, so the MAIN site is safe — BUT any subdomain, redirect, or asset URL that is still HTTP will trigger the warning interstitial, scaring users away; (b) the post-deploy-verification skill's Phase 0.5 detector (detect-dead-endpoints.sh + the extended HTML-attribute scan) must be RUN AGAIN to catch any residual http:// references that Chrome 154 will flag — the skill already documents this expansion (R96 Chrome 154/147 pattern), but it must be executed, not just documented; (c) any GameZipper outbound link (footer Related Games, social share, "All Games" links) that points to an http:// URL will warn the user on click in Chrome 154; (d) any third-party script src that is http:// (already a mixed-content risk per the skill) becomes an even harder block in Chrome 154; (e) the warning interstitial is a UX killer — a user who sees "your connection is not secure" will likely bounce, so GameZipper must proactively eliminate ALL http:// references before October 2026. **Test**: (1) run `grep -rnE '(href|src|srcset|action|data|formaction|poster)\s*=\s*["'\'']http://' --include="*.html" .` across the entire GameZipper repo and confirm ZERO results (the post-deploy-verification Phase 0.5 extended scan); (2) run `grep -rnE 'WebSocket|EventSource|sendBeacon|window\.open|window\.location\s*=' --include="*.js" . | grep -v 'wss://\|https://'` and confirm no ws:// or http:// JS navigation; (3) click every footer link on the homepage in Chrome 154 beta and confirm none triggers the HTTP warning interstitial; (4) verify the Cloudflare "Always Use HTTPS" redirect is enabled so any accidental http://gamezipper.com request is upgraded before Chrome 154 warns; (5) add the http:// scan to the pre-commit hook so no new http:// reference can be introduced. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_163690194b743552 (Oct 29 2025 — "谷歌官宣 2026 年 Chrome 154 浏览器默认警告 HTTP 连接" + "始终使用安全连接(Always Use Secure Connections)" + Chrome 154 October 2026).

### 56.4 Edge CVE-2026-0628 — WebView Tag Policy Enforcement Vulnerability

- [S-189] **[P2]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_3736964790e40752 (January 12 2026) documents that **Microsoft's first 2026 Edge stable update (version 143.0.3650.139) fixed CVE-2026-0628** — a high-severity vulnerability caused by insufficient policy enforcement in WebView tags. Attackers could induce users to install a malicious browser extension that exploits the weak policy to inject malicious scripts or HTML into protected privileged pages, achieving privilege escalation or data theft. Microsoft Edge also shipped an auto-cleanup feature for malicious sideloaded extensions (non-store installs) in November 2025, compounding the extension-security surface. **Why this is the EDGE-CVE-2026-0628-WEBVIEW-TAG-POLICY-ENFORCEMENT-VULNERABILITY dimension distinct from S-178 (Chrome weekly zero-day — CHROME dimension)**: S-178 is Chrome V8/GPU; S-189 is Edge WebView policy. **Distinct from S-186 (DarkSword iOS full-chain — IOS dimension)**: S-186 is iOS; S-189 is Edge/desktop. **Distinct from PC-017 (ad-blocker silent monetization failure — EXTENSION-USER dimension)**: PC-017 is the user's own extension blocking ads; S-189 is a MALICIOUS extension exploiting a policy gap. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) Edge holds an estimated 5-10% of the desktop browser market — a meaningful GameZipper audience segment runs Edge, and CVE-2026-0628 means a malicious extension installed by a GameZipper visitor could inject scripts into the GameZipper page (XSS-equivalent via the extension vector); (b) the injected script could read/modify the gz-ad-below-game iframe, steal localStorage favorites, or inject additional ads (ad-fraud) — the same impact as a CSP bypass (S-187) but via the extension surface; (c) Edge's auto-cleanup of sideloaded extensions is a mitigation ON THE USER SIDE, but GameZipper cannot control whether users have sideloaded extensions — it must assume some visitors run unpatched/malicious extensions; (d) the WebView-tag policy gap specifically affects embedded webviews (Electron apps, PWAs, Edge's own app mode) — if GameZipper is ever embedded in a WebView (e.g., a future desktop wrapper or a partner's iframe), the policy gap applies; (e) the vulnerability underscores that Content-Security-Policy alone is insufficient — GameZipper must also defend against extension-injected scripts (which run in the page context and bypass CSP if the extension has page-access permission). **Test**: (1) verify GameZipper's CSP includes a restrictive `script-src` that would limit the damage of an extension-injected script (extensions with page access can still inject, but a tight CSP reduces what they can load); (2) on Edge 142 (unpatched), install a benign test extension with page-access permission and verify it can read GameZipper localStorage — confirm the data sensitivity (favorites only? any PII?); (3) on Edge 143 (patched), re-test and confirm the policy gap is closed for NEW extension installs; (4) audit GameZipper localStorage for any sensitive data an extension could exfiltrate and minimize what is stored client-side; (5) document the extension-threat model in the GameZipper security runbook — CSP defends against network-injected scripts, but extension-injected scripts require defense-in-depth (minimal localStorage, no secrets in JS). Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_3736964790e40752 (Jan 12 2026 — "微软Edge新年首更:修复高危漏洞!" + CVE-2026-0628 + WebView tag policy + Edge 143.0.3650.139).

### 56.5 WebGL Context-Loss / Context-Restore Event Handling

- [B-131] **[P2]** developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLCanvasElement/webglcontextlost_event (May 5 2026 baseline) and developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WEBGL_lose_context document the canonical WebGL context-loss/recovery pattern: browsers fire `webglcontextlost` when the GPU context is lost (driver crash, too many contexts, tab backgrounded for long, GPU reset) and `webglcontextrestored` when it recovers — BUT all previously-created WebGL resources (textures, buffers, shaders) become INVALID after a restore and must be re-created. Many WebGL games do NOT handle these events, resulting in a permanently-blank/frozen canvas after a context loss. **Why this is the WEBGL-CONTEXT-LOSS-RESTORE-EVENT-HANDLING-BLANK-CANVAS dimension distinct from B-130 (Chrome 140 Fontations font-rendering crash — FONT-RENDERING dimension)**: B-130 is font rasterization; B-131 is WebGL GPU context. **Distinct from B-065 (Safari WebKit crash under heavy canvas — ENGINE-CRASH dimension)**: B-065 is a hard crash; B-131 is a recoverable context loss. **Distinct from P-062 (WebGPU three-domain memory leak — LEAK dimension)**: P-062 is a slow leak; B-131 is an abrupt context loss. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper WebGL game (Three.js, raw WebGL, Phaser in WebGL mode) that does not handle `webglcontextlost`/`webglcontextrestored` will go BLANK when the user backgrounds the tab (mobile Safari aggressively drops WebGL contexts to save memory), switches apps, or the GPU driver resets — the user returns to a frozen game with no way to recover except a full reload; (b) the loss is especially common on mobile (iOS Safari drops contexts aggressively) and on devices with weak GPUs (the Mali-G57 tier per P-048) — exactly GameZipper's target audience; (c) the `WEBGL_lose_context` extension lets QA SIMULATE a context loss programmatically (`gl.getExtension('WEBGL_lose_context').loseContext()`) — this is the deterministic way to test recovery without waiting for a real loss; (d) the gamezipper-qa skill's "blank canvas" debugging pattern (IIFE init step-tracking) assumes an init-time crash, but a context-loss blank happens AFTER successful init — the QA diagnostic must distinguish the two; (e) the `preventDefault()` on the `webglcontextlost` event is REQUIRED to allow restoration — without it, the context is permanently lost. **Test**: (1) for each Top-30 WebGL game, run `gl = canvas.getContext('webgl'); gl.getExtension('WEBGL_lose_context').loseContext();` in the console and verify the game either recovers gracefully (re-creates resources on `webglcontextrestored`) or shows a "context lost, reload" message — a permanently-blank canvas is a FAIL; (2) verify the game calls `event.preventDefault()` in the `webglcontextlost` handler (required to allow restore); (3) on mobile Safari, background the tab for 60 seconds, return, and verify the WebGL game recovers or prompts reload; (4) grep each WebGL game's source for `webglcontextlost` / `webglcontextrestored` — absence means the game will blank on context loss; (5) add the WEBGL_lose_context simulation probe to the automated regression suite for all WebGL games. Source: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLCanvasElement/webglcontextlost_event + https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WEBGL_lose_context (May 2026 baseline — context-loss event + loseContext()/restoreContext() simulation API).

### 56.6 WebGL Memory Leak — WEBGL_lose_context Cleanup Pattern

- [B-132] **[P2]** blog.csdn.net/jyl919221lc/article/details/137256813 (April 2024, the 2026 baseline) documents that **WebGL contexts leak memory if not manually cleaned up** — creating a canvas + `getContext('webgl')` + series of WebGL operations without calling `gl.getExtension('WEBGL_lose_context').loseContext()` to release GPU resources causes a memory leak that compounds across page navigations and SPA route changes. The leak is invisible in the JS heap (it's GPU/driver memory) and only surfaces as a crash or performance degradation after many game sessions. **Why this is the WEBGL-MEMORY-LEAK-WEBGL-LOSE-CONTEXT-CLEANUP-PATTERN dimension distinct from B-131 (WebGL context-loss event handling — EVENT-RECOVERY dimension)**: B-131 is recovering from an UNWANTED loss; B-132 is INTENTIONALLY triggering a loss to CLEAN UP. **Distinct from the gamezipper-qa skill's BGM/setInterval memory-leak pattern (audio interval cleanup)**: that pattern is Web Audio; B-132 is WebGL GPU memory. **Distinct from P-062 (WebGPU three-domain memory leak — WEBGPU dimension)**: P-062 is WebGPU; B-132 is WebGL. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper is a single-page site where users navigate between games WITHOUT a full page reload (the game loads in the same tab) — each game that creates a WebGL context and doesn't clean it up LEAKS GPU memory into the tab, eventually causing the next game to fail to create a context (browsers limit ~16 concurrent WebGL contexts); (b) a user who plays 10+ WebGL games in a session (a common engagement pattern on a game portal) will hit the WebGL-context ceiling and see "Error creating WebGL context" (the documented symptom); (c) the cleanup pattern (`loseContext()` on game exit, before loading the next game) is the canonical fix — but it must be wired into the game-exit/navigation handler, which many games lack; (d) the leak is GPU memory, NOT JS heap — the browser's memory profiler shows normal JS heap while GPU memory grows invisibly, making it hard to diagnose without a GPU-memory tool; (e) on mobile (iOS Safari), the WebGL-context ceiling is even lower (~8-16 contexts), so mobile users hit the ceiling faster. **Test**: (1) for each WebGL game, add an exit/cleanup handler that calls `gl.getExtension('WEBGL_lose_context').loseContext()` and nulls the gl reference before navigating away — verify the WebGL context count (`document.querySelectorAll('canvas').length` of webgl canvases) does not grow unbounded; (2) in a single tab, load and exit 20 WebGL games sequentially and verify no "Error creating WebGL context" error appears; (3) monitor GPU memory (via browser DevTools Performance > GPU, or `performance.memory` where available) across 10 game sessions and confirm it does not grow unbounded; (4) verify the `beforeunload` handler (per the gamezipper-qa skill's rAF/audio cleanup pattern) ALSO cleans up the WebGL context; (5) document the WebGL-context-per-tab ceiling for each target browser (Chrome ~16, Safari ~8-16, Firefox ~32) and set a QA threshold. Source: https://blog.csdn.net/jyl919221lc/article/details/137256813 (April 2024 — "canvas使用webgl导致内存泄漏如何清理" + `gl.getExtension('WEBGL_lose_context').loseContext()` cleanup pattern).

### 56.7 WebAudio AudioContext Autoplay Policy — Suspended-State Resume

- [C-137] **[P2]** developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Media/Guides/Autoplay (September 2025 baseline) and developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Audio_API document the **WebAudio autoplay policy**: all major browsers (Chrome 66+, Safari, Firefox) create an `AudioContext` in a `suspended` state until a USER GESTURE (click, tap, keypress) occurs — calling `audioContext.resume()` outside a user-gesture handler is a no-op. Games that try to play BGM or sound effects on page load (before any user interaction) will have SILENT audio, with no error — the sound simply never plays. **Why this is the WEBAUDIO-AUDIOCONTEXT-AUTOPLAY-POLICY-SUSPENDED-RESUME dimension distinct from S-079 (referenced in G-157 Phaser audio autoplay — FRAMEWORK dimension)**: S-079 is framework-specific; C-137 is the underlying platform policy. **Distinct from the gamezipper-qa skill's BGM/setInterval memory-leak pattern (audio interval cleanup)**: that pattern is cleanup; C-137 is the playback-gate policy. **Distinct from B-131 (WebGL context loss — GRAPHICS dimension)**: B-131 is graphics; C-137 is audio. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper game that plays BGM or a sound effect in `init()` (before the user taps/clicks) will be SILENT on first load — the user perceives a "broken audio" bug, but the game code is technically correct (just policy-gated); (b) the splash-screen dismiss pattern (per the gamezipper-qa skill) IS a user gesture — games that start audio in the splash-dismiss handler work correctly, but games that start audio in `init()` (before splash dismiss) do not; (c) the `audioContext.state` must be checked: if `suspended`, the game must `resume()` on the FIRST user gesture and re-trigger any audio that was attempted during suspension; (d) Safari is the STRICTEST enforcer — it requires the resume() call to be DIRECTLY in the event handler (not in a setTimeout/microtask), so games that defer audio to a promise/timeout will still fail on Safari; (e) the symptom (silent audio, no error) is hard to diagnose — the user reports "no sound" and the developer sees no console error, leading to "works on my machine" false closes. **Test**: (1) for each Top-30 game with audio, load the page and WAIT 5 seconds WITHOUT interacting — verify NO audio plays (expected, policy-gated) and NO error is thrown; (2) perform the first user gesture (tap the splash / click Start) and verify audio NOW plays (the game must call `audioContext.resume()` in the gesture handler); (3) on Safari specifically, verify the `resume()` call is in the DIRECT event handler (not deferred) — deferred resume fails on Safari; (4) check `audioContext.state` after the gesture — it must transition from `suspended` to `running`; (5) document the "first gesture starts audio" requirement in the new-game pipeline so every game with audio wires it correctly from day one. Source: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Media/Guides/Autoplay + https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Audio_API (2025-2026 baseline — autoplay policy + `audioContext.state === 'suspended'` + `audioContext.resume()` on user gesture).

### 56.8 WCAG Mobile Accessibility — Contrast Ratio & Color-Only Information

- [A-044] **[P2]** developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/Guides/Mobile_accessibility_checklist (March 2026 baseline) and w3c.github.io/Mobile-A11y-TF-Note establish the **WCAG mobile accessibility contrast requirements**: (1) contrast ratio of **4.5:1 for normal text** (less than 18pt or 14pt bold), (2) contrast ratio of **3:1 for large text** (at least 18pt or 14pt bold), and (3) **information conveyed via color must also be conveyed via text or pattern** (color-blindness compliance). The civiform WCAG 2.1 issue (github.com/civiform/civiform/issues/5519) documents the common failure: screen readers do not announce error messages on form submission, a WCAG 2.1 violation. **Why this is the WCAG-MOBILE-ACCESSIBILITY-CONTRAST-RATIO-AND-COLOR-ONLY-INFORMATION dimension distinct from A-009 (dark mode mobile OLED — THEME dimension)**: A-009 is theming; A-044 is contrast ratio. **Distinct from B-128 (iOS 26 Liquid Glass Control Center transparency — OVERLAY dimension)**: B-128 is a system overlay; A-044 is the game's own UI. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) many GameZipper casual games use color to convey critical state — a "red = danger, green = safe" tile, a colored health bar, a color-coded match — for color-blind users (8% of males, ~0.5% of females, a significant audience), color-only information is INACCESSIBLE; (b) game HUD text (score, timer, level) often uses low-contrast color schemes for aesthetics (e.g., light-gray text on a white background) that FAIL the 4.5:1 ratio — readable on a calibrated monitor but illegible on a low-brightness phone in sunlight; (c) error states (the gamezipper-qa skill's gz-error-toast) must be announced to screen readers via `role="alert"` (which IS present per the skill) AND have sufficient contrast — a red toast on a red game background fails both; (d) mobile is the majority GameZipper platform — mobile accessibility is not optional; (e) WCAG compliance is increasingly a legal requirement (ADA Title III web-accessibility lawsuit rose in 2025-2026), so non-compliant games are a litigation risk, not just a UX issue. **Test**: (1) for each Top-30 game, run an automated contrast-ratio checker (axe-core, Lighthouse Accessibility audit, or WebAIM Contrast Checker) on all text elements (HUD, instructions, buttons) and flag any below 4.5:1 (normal) or 3:1 (large); (2) for color-coded game state (danger/safe zones, color-matched tiles), verify the state is ALSO conveyed by a non-color cue (pattern, icon, text label, position); (3) simulate color-blindness (Chrome DevTools > Rendering > Emulate vision deficiencies: Protanopia, Deuteranopia, Tritanopia) and verify the game remains playable; (4) verify the gz-error-toast has `role="alert"` (per the skill) AND a contrast ratio ≥ 4.5:1 against its background; (5) run a full Lighthouse Accessibility audit on the homepage and 5 game pages and document the score + specific failures. Source: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/Guides/Mobile_accessibility_checklist (March 2026 — "Contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text" + "Contrast ratio of 3:1 for large text" + "Information conveyed via color must be also [conveyed via text/pattern]").

### 56.9 iOS Safari Input Keyboard Overlap — scrollIntoView Pattern

- [B-133] **[P3]** dvy.com.cn/2016/07/23/5526.html (the canonical iOS Safari keyboard-overlap pattern, re-baselined for 2026) documents that **iOS Safari's virtual keyboard can occlude the focused input field**, especially when the input is near the bottom of the page or when switching input methods (Chinese/English). The `scrollIntoView()` call (with a small setTimeout to let the keyboard animate in) is the canonical fix, but it remains imperfect — switching input methods can still cause partial occlusion. **Why this is the IOS-SAFARI-INPUT-KEYBOARD-OVERLAP-SCROLLINTOVIEW-PATTERN dimension distinct from B-125 (iOS Safari fixed-position bug — FIXED-POSITION dimension)**: B-125 is position:fixed elements; B-133 is the keyboard occluding the focused input. **Distinct from B-128 (iOS 26 Liquid Glass transparency — OVERLAY dimension)**: B-128 is a system-overlay visual; B-133 is a layout occlusion. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) while most GameZipper games are canvas-based (no text inputs), any game with a NICKNAME entry (leaderboard), a SEARCH box (homepage game search), or a SETTINGS input (volume slider with a numeric field) can hit the iOS keyboard-overlap bug — the user types but cannot see what they typed because the keyboard covers the field; (b) the homepage game-search input is the most exposed — on iOS Safari, tapping the search field may occlude it with the keyboard, especially in landscape orientation; (c) the `scrollIntoView()` fix must be called on the input's `focus` event with a 100-300ms setTimeout (to let the keyboard animate), but it must ALSO be re-called on `visualViewport` resize (iOS 26 fires this when the keyboard appears) for robustness; (d) the input-method-switch regression (Chinese/English toggle re-occludes the field) is hard to test without a real iOS device — it often passes desktop QA but fails on device; (e) the iOS 26 Liquid Glass effect (B-128) may compound the issue — a translucent keyboard over a translucent UI reduces what the user can see of the occluded field. **Test**: (1) on a real iOS 26 Safari device, tap the homepage game-search input in PORTRAIT and verify the input remains visible above the keyboard (call `scrollIntoView()` on focus if not); (2) repeat in LANDSCAPE — the keyboard is taller proportionally, occlusion is worse; (3) switch the input method (Chinese/English) while focused and verify the input is re-scrolled into view; (4) for any game with a nickname/leaderboard entry, test the input on iOS Safari and verify `scrollIntoView()` + a `visualViewport` resize listener keeps it visible; (5) add a `position: sticky` bottom padding equal to the keyboard height as a fallback when `scrollIntoView` is insufficient. Source: https://www.dvy.com.cn/2016/07/23/5526.html (re-baselined 2026 — "iOS下Html页面中input获取焦点弹出键盘时挡住input解决方案" + `scrollIntoView(true)` + setTimeout 100ms + input-method-switch regression).


### 57.1 Chrome "Skills" Browser-Native AI Prompt Store — Runnable on Any Page

- [W-204] **[P2]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_02569df582c01752 (April 15 2026) documents Google's April 14 2026 launch of the **Chrome "Skills"** feature: users can SAVE arbitrary AI prompts and invoke them on ANY webpage via the Chrome browser UI itself — the prompt runs against the current page's content without per-page opt-in. **Why this is the BROWSER-NATIVE-AI-PROMPT-STORE-USER-SAVED-PROMPTS-RUNNABLE-ON-ANY-PAGE dimension distinct from W-193 (Safari Apple Intelligence natural-language commands — one-shot voice/text intent that the OS interprets)**: W-193 is intent interpretation; W-204 is a PERSISTENT USER-CURATED PROMPT LIBRARY. **Distinct from W-125 (Edge Copilot sidebar chat — Q&A only)**: W-125 is conversational; W-204 is scripted-replay. **Distinct from W-158 (Chrome 148 AI address-bar mode — query-only)**: W-158 is one-shot queries;
