## R223 Detailed Test Case Descriptions (v1.104.0 — June 27 2026)

- [S-218] **[P1]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_04069d4dc4672952 (April 7 2026 — "Fortinet修复FortiClient EMS零日漏洞遭黑客攻击") + blog.csdn.net/zhidingkeji/article/details/159927205 (April 7 2026) + github.com/Alaatk/CVE-2026-35616 (June 2 2026 — PoC) + blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/160200361 (May 19 2026 — watchTowr technical breakdown) document CVE-2026-35616: a CRITICAL authentication-bypass vulnerability in Fortinet FortiClient Endpoint Management Server (EMS) versions 7.4.5–7.4.6, rated CVSS 9.1 (some vendors scoring 9.8). The vulnerability is PRE-AUTHENTICATION: a SINGLE FORGED HTTP HEADER bypasses authentication on the FortiClient EMS API gateway, granting the attacker FULL ADMIN API ACCESS TO ALL MANAGED ENDPOINTS. WatchTowr honeypots first recorded exploitation attempts on March 31 2026 — meaning the vulnerability was exploited for ~5 days before the official disclosure on April 5 2026 (Fortinet FG-IR-26-099 advisory) and CISA KEV catalog addition April 7 2026. The flaw is in the API gateway component responsible for authenticating all incoming API requests; a logic error in path handling lets specific routes bypass auth entirely. This is the SECOND actively-exploited FortiClient EMS critical CVE in weeks (after CVE-2026-21643, also CVSS 9.1). **Why this is the ENDPOINT-MANAGEMENT-SERVER-PREAUTH-API-BYPASS-CLASS dimension distinct from S-179 (Check Point critical auth-bypass — different vendor product class: perimeter firewall not endpoint fleet manager) and S-192 (CVE-2026-50751 Check Point IKEv1 VPN auth-bypass — different protocol layer: VPN not API gateway)**: S-179/S-192 are PERIMETER-FIREWALL / VPN auth-bypass; S-218 is ENDPOINT-FLEET-MANAGEMENT-SERVER API-bypass — the attacker gains control of EVERY managed endpoint in a single request, making the blast radius ORDERS of magnitude larger than a perimeter compromise. **Distinct from S-167 (CVE-2026-20131 Cisco FMC CVSS 10.0 — firewall management plane, not endpoint management)**: S-167 is firewall-MANAGEMENT-PLANE; S-218 is ENDPOINT-FLEET-MANAGEMENT. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper production is GitHub Pages (no FortiClient), BUT any GameZipper BACKEND / corporate / ops infrastructure using FortiClient EMS to manage enterprise laptops, BI dashboard VMs, or developer workstations is at risk — a single forged header gives the attacker the ability to deploy / uninstall / modify endpoint agents across the entire fleet; (b) the BROADER PATTERN — endpoint-management-server preauth API bypass — is a recurring class (this is the SECOND FortiClient EMS critical CVE in 2 weeks) and applies to ANY MDM/EMM/XDR platform (Microsoft Intune, Jamf, SentinelOne, CrowdStrike Falcon); (c) GameZipper QA must inventory any endpoint-management tooling and verify version + patch status. **Test**: (1) inventory any endpoint-management platform in GameZipper ops/corporate and confirm patched version (FortiClient EMS ≥ 7.4.7; equivalent for Intune/Jamf/etc.); (2) verify the management-server API rejects unauthenticated requests with HTTP 401/403 (no path bypass); (3) check audit logs for the forged-header IoC pattern (watchTowr published the exact HTTP request shape); (4) verify management-server has network ACL restricting access to known admin networks only; (5) if no endpoint management in scope, document NEGATIVE and skip. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_04069d4dc4672952 ; https://github.com/Alaatk/CVE-2026-35616 ; https://blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/160200361 .

---

- [S-219] **[P1]** blog.csdn.net/sinat_17584329/article/details/157292799 (January 23 2026 — "【漏洞复现】大模型推理框架vLLM远程代码执行漏洞 CVE-2026-22807") documents CVE-2026-22807 in vLLM (the VLLM inference framework — github.com/vllm-project/vllm — one of the two most-deployed open-source LLM serving frameworks alongside SGLang). The vulnerability occurs in the MODEL INITIALIZATION PHASE: when vLLM parses the model `config.json`, it UNCONDITIONALLY loads the Hugging Face `auto_map` dynamic module via `try_get_class_from_dynamic_module()` WITHOUT checking the global `trust_remote_code` configuration parameter. This means EVEN WHEN THE USER EXPLICITLY SETS `trust_remote_code=False` (the standard security primitive for denying remote code execution during model loading), an attacker who controls the model repository path — local directory or remote repo — can still inject and execute arbitrary Python during model loading. The malicious execution happens at SERVICE STARTUP, BEFORE any request is processed, and REQUIRES NO interface access — purely a configuration-load-time code execution. **Why this is the LLM-SERVING-FRAMEWORK-CONFIG-LOAD-RCE-DESPITE-USER-OPT-OUT dimension distinct from S-163 (CVE-2026-5760 SGLang Jinja2 SSTI via malicious GGUF chat_template — different framework AND different vector: model-file METADATA not config-load TRUST-FLAG BYPASS)**: S-163 is model-file-metadata SSTI; S-219 is CONFIG-LOAD TRUST-FLAG BYPASS — the user's explicit opt-out (`trust_remote_code=False`) is silently ignored. **Distinct from S-207 (Xinference TeamPCP supply-chain compromise — different vector: install-time framework package compromise, not config-load-time bypass)**: S-207 is install-time; S-219 is runtime-config-load. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper does NOT currently deploy vLLM, BUT any future AI feature (NPC dialogue via WebLLM, on-device embeddings, image-recognition minigames, voice-driven interfaces via Whisper.cpp WASM) that adopts vLLM (or SGLang, llama.cpp, TGI, Ollama, LocalAI) for backend inference inherits this class of vulnerability; (b) the BROADER PATTERN — security primitives (`trust_remote_code=False`, `allowlist`, `sandbox`) being silently bypassed at LOAD time — applies to every framework that loads user-controlled code; (c) the STARTUP-PHASE execution means network-egress denylists that activate only at request time DO NOT help — the malicious code runs before any request arrives; (d) `trust_remote_code` is a VLLM + HuggingFace Transformers flag — the bug means the flag is documentation-only, not enforcement. **Test**: (1) audit any AI inference framework (vLLM, SGLang, TGI, Ollama, LocalAI, llama.cpp) in GameZipper backend / future AI features; (2) for vLLM specifically, pin to the patched version and verify the `trust_remote_code=False` flag is actually enforced at model load (write a unit test that loads a malicious dummy model with `auto_map` pointing at a no-op safe stub and verify it is NOT executed); (3) for any inference framework, NEVER load models from untrusted repositories — pin to a trusted allowlist of model URLs; (4) wrap any inference server in container egress-deny (block all outbound except the model-fetch host) AND pre-load models in a sandboxed init container; (5) verify the inference server's startup logs for any unexpected Python module imports; (6) monitor the inference server process tree for unexpected child processes during model load. Source: https://blog.csdn.net/sinat_17584329/article/details/157292799 .

---

- [S-220] **[P2]** cve.report (June 24 2026 — CVE-2026-40079 + CVE-2026-39951 advisory updates) documents a CLUSTER of command-injection RCE vulnerabilities in Cacti 1.2.30 and prior. Cacti (cacti.net — github.com/Cacti/cacti) is the canonical open-source performance and fault-management framework — a PHP-based network-graphing and monitoring tool widely deployed as a free alternative to Nagios / Zabbix / PRTG. CVE-2026-39951 specifically: "Cacti is an open source performance and fault management framework. Versions 1.2.30 and prior are vulnerable to Command Injection" — a single crafted request triggers arbitrary OS command execution on the Cacti host. The CVE-2026-40079 Quest NetVault Backup NVBULogDaemon sibling confirms this is a CLUSTER — multiple open-source performance/backup/monitoring tools shipping command-injection vulnerabilities in the same June 2026 disclosure window. **Why this is the OPEN-SOURCE-PERF-MONITORING-RCE-CLASS dimension distinct from S-152 (CVE-2026-34621 Adobe Acrobat Reader PDF — different media type) and S-164 (CVE-2026-42897 Exchange OWA email — different surface)**: S-152 is media; S-164 is email-client; S-220 is BI/OPS-MONITORING-TOOLCHAIN — the tool that should DETECT attacks becomes the attack vector. **Distinct from C-128 (2026 web-automation smart-element-recognition — different layer: testing tooling not monitoring tooling)**: C-128 is QA tooling; S-220 is production MONITORING tooling. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper does NOT currently deploy Cacti, BUT any GameZipper BI dashboard / analytics / ops monitoring toolchain (Cacti, Nagios, Zabbix, Prometheus, Grafana, Sensu, Icinga) is in scope — these tools typically run with elevated privileges, have outbound network access for notification, and are often exposed on internal networks with weaker auth than production; (b) the BROADER PATTERN — monitoring tools are privileged targets — applies to every observability stack; (c) a compromised monitoring tool can exfiltrate credentials from the metrics it collects (DB passwords in connection metrics, API keys in HTTP probes), manipulate dashboards to hide attacks, and use notification channels (Slack, email, PagerDuty) for lateral phishing; (d) the Cacti command-injection class recurs across versions — every major Cacti version has shipped at least one RCE in the past 5 years. **Test**: (1) inventory any monitoring/observability tooling (Cacti, Nagios, Zabbix, Prometheus, Grafana, Sensu) in GameZipper BI/ops; (2) for Cacti specifically, verify version ≥ 1.2.31 (or whatever patches CVE-2026-39951); (3) verify monitoring tools run with MINIMAL privileges (not root, not DB-superuser); (4) verify monitoring tools are behind network ACL (not exposed to internet); (5) verify monitoring tools' notification channels (Slack/email/PagerDuty webhooks) cannot be weaponized for phishing if compromised; (6) scan monitoring tool configs for hardcoded credentials (the recurring pattern: monitoring tools collect credentials to monitor things, and store them in plaintext config); (7) if no monitoring tool in scope, document NEGATIVE and skip. Source: https://cve.report/ (CVE-2026-40079 + CVE-2026-39951 records, June 24 2026 update) .

---

- [B-146] **[P2]** donews.com/news/detail/8/6585054.html (June 5 2026 — "Chrome 149正式推送:新增AI模型管理与ARM64 Linux官方支持") documents that Chrome 149 stable (shipped June 2 2026) introduces the FIRST OS-LEVEL USER-FACING ON-DEVICE AI MODEL MANAGEMENT control surface inside a browser: users can now MANAGE on-device AI models from system settings — explicitly REJECTING model downloads or DELETING already-installed models. This is the first time a browser exposes a model-asset lifecycle control that lets users revoke a downloaded model the browser has cached. **Why this is the FIRST-OS-LEVEL-BROWSER-SIDE-AI-MODEL-CONTROL-SURFACE dimension distinct from W-204 (Chrome Skills — user-saved AI PROMPTS, not model assets) and C-142 (Chrome Gemini sidebar — AI agent RUNTIME, not model lifecycle)**: W-204 is prompts; C-142 is runtime; B-146 is the MODEL-ASSET-LIFECYCLE-MANAGEMENT dimension. **Distinct from W-193 (Safari Apple Intelligence natural-language navigation — different layer: usage not lifecycle)**: W-193 is usage; B-146 is asset management. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper does NOT currently use on-device AI models, BUT any future feature relying on a specific on-device model being present (WebLLM NPC dialogue, Whisper.cpp voice transcription, on-device embeddings, image-recognition minigames using Transformers.js, Phi-3-mini) MUST gracefully detect model absence and fall back to a cloud API or refuse with a clear message; (b) the BROADER PATTERN — users can revoke on-device models at will — means on-device-AI features are NOT reliable availability targets; (c) Chrome's model management UI lives in OS Settings, not browser Settings, meaning users may delete models without realizing it breaks specific websites; (d) the GameZipper QA methodology for any on-device-AI feature MUST include a "user-deleted-model" test scenario. **Test**: (1) if no on-device-AI feature in GameZipper, document NEGATIVE and skip; (2) if any on-device-AI feature exists, write a test that deletes the on-device model from Chrome 149+ OS Settings and verifies the game detects absence within 1s and shows a clear fallback message ("AI feature unavailable — using cloud fallback" or "AI feature requires downloading a model — click to enable"); (3) verify the game does NOT crash or hang when the model is missing; (4) verify the game re-acquires the model (or stays on fallback) when re-enabled; (5) verify the model-download flow does NOT silently consume user bandwidth — show explicit "downloading X MB" progress UI. Source: https://www.donews.com/news/detail/8/6585054.html .

---

- [B-147] **[P2]** donews.com/news/detail/8/6585054.html (June 5 2026 — "Chrome 149正式推送:新增AI模型管理与ARM64 Linux官方支持") documents that Chrome 149 stable (shipped June 2 2026) ships the FIRST OFFICIAL Chromium ARM64 Linux packages — providing native ARM64 deb/rpm installers that fill a LONG-STANDING gap (Chromium-on-ARM-Linux was previously community/Debian-only, often lagging behind x86_64 by multiple releases). This means AWS Graviton (EC2 c7g/m7g/r7g) instances, Ampere Altra cloud VMs, Raspberry Pi 4/5/400, PineBook Pro, and ARM SBCs that GameZipper users may deploy as kiosk / HTPC / digital-signage clients now have a FIRST-CLASS Chromium release. **Why this is the FIRST-CHROMIUM-NATIVE-ARM64-LINUX-COMPLETING-CROSS-ARCH-MATRIX dimension distinct from B-083 (ChromeOS 150 LTC — different OS family entirely: ChromeOS vs Linux; ChromeOS already ran on ARM via Android-base)**: B-083 is ChromeOS-on-ARM (long-supported); B-147 is Chromium-on-Linux-ARM (newly official). **Distinct from W-188 (Android device fragmentation MediaTek/Exynos/HarmonyOS — mobile-OS fragmentation, not Linux desktop)**: W-188 is mobile; B-147 is Linux desktop/server. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper users running ARM64 Linux Chromium (Raspberry Pi kiosk, Graviton cloud, Ampere Altra HTPC) are now a SUPPORTED Chrome target — but their GPU stack differs (Mali / Adreno / Graviton-software-render) from typical x86_64 Intel/AMD/NVIDIA; (b) WebGL/WebGPU/canvas performance and feature parity on ARM64 Linux Chromium is UNVERIFIED at scale — the GameZipper QA matrix MUST add ARM64 Linux Chromium as a tracked tier; (c) the BROADER PATTERN — ARM cloud instances are now a first-class browser target — applies to GameZipper's own dev/test/CI infrastructure (Chrome-ARM64 for Playwright, Chrome-ARM64 for Browserless, Chrome-ARM64 for Kachilu headless); (d) Raspberry Pi 4/5 may have insufficient RAM/GPU for some GameZipper games and need a lower-quality fallback; (e) AWS Graviton is significantly cheaper than x86 — GameZipper's CI cost may drop if ARM-Playwright is viable. **Test**: (1) add ARM64 Linux Chromium (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4 with Chromium 149+, or AWS Graviton c7g Ubuntu 24.04 with Chrome 149+) to the GameZipper QA device matrix; (2) for each GameZipper game, smoke-test on ARM64 Linux Chromium and verify canvas/WebGL renders (no software-renderer fallback crashing); (3) verify WebGPU feature detection works correctly (some Mali/Adreno drivers report WebGPU support but fail at runtime — test the C-148 capability-negotiation pattern); (4) measure FPS on a reference ARM64 device (e.g., Raspberry Pi 5) and verify >= 30fps for casual games; (5) verify RAM usage on 4GB Raspberry Pi 4 does not trigger OOM-killer on heavy games; (6) verify GPU driver version detection (Panfrost vs proprietary Mali blob) and document any rendering divergence. Source: https://www.donews.com/news/detail/8/6585054.html .

---

- [W-216] **[P2]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_4016a32165940252 (June 17 2026 — "Mozilla 公告火狐 Firefox 浏览器路线图,推进2026最大改版落地") documents Mozilla's June 16 2026 blog post that FORMALLY NAMES the 2026 Firefox redesign project as "Nova" — elevating the previously-generic "biggest redesign in years" commitment (W-202) into a NAMED-PROJECT with an H2 2026 ship target. The Nova project encompasses: redesigned settings page, enhanced media controls, experimental JPEG XL support, and additional UI modernization. The formal naming signals Mozilla has moved from planning to execution — Nova is now a tracked internal milestone with engineering resources allocated and explicit ship-date commitments. **Why this is the FIREFOX-NOVA-PROJECT-CODENAME-CONFIRMATION-EXTENDING-PRIOR-REDESIGN-ROADMAP dimension distinct from W-202 (Firefox 2026 redesign roadmap — generic commitment without codename) and B-116 (Firefox 152 stable ship with experimental features — single release, not the multi-release Nova project)**: W-202 is the roadmap-level commitment; B-116 is one release shipping some Nova-preview features; W-216 is the FORMAL-PROJECT-CODENAME milestone that makes the redesign a tracked vendor deliverable. **Distinct from W-211 (Rolldown 1.0 + Vite 8 — different layer: build toolchain, not browser)**: W-211 is build tooling; W-216 is the browser product. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) Firefox Nova will ship as a Firefox 153+ stable milestone in H2 2026 — GameZipper QA MUST treat Nova as a tracked vendor milestone with explicit regression-testing gates, just like Safari 27 (B-090/B-127) and Chrome 150 (W-200); (b) the BROADER PATTERN — formal project codenames signal vendor commitment — applies across the industry (Apple has "Golden Gate" for macOS 27, Mozilla has "Nova" for Firefox 2026, Google has "Materials 3" for Chrome); (c) Nova's redesigned settings page may CHANGE default privacy / tracking / cookie / cache settings — GameZipper QA must re-test under Nova defaults to verify monetization (Monetag/AdSense) and analytics still work; (d) Nova's enhanced media controls may interact with GameZipper game audio (BGM, SFX) — verify media-session API integration; (e) JPEG XL support, if shipped stable, enables smaller game assets — GameZipper may want to add JPEG XL variants of game art. **Test**: (1) subscribe to Mozilla release notes for Nova ship date (likely Firefox 153+); (2) on Nova ship, run a full GameZipper regression sweep on Firefox Nova stable; (3) specifically test: redesigned settings → privacy defaults → Monetag/AdSense still load; redesigned media controls → BGM/SFX still play; JPEG XL → game art renders correctly; (4) capture visual-regression baselines on Nova and compare against Firefox 152; (5) update the GameZipper UA-detection to recognize Nova-specific user-agent strings. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_4016a32165940252 .

---

- [W-217] **[P2]** Apple WWDC26 (June 9 2026 — webkit.org/blog + developer.apple.com WWDC26 session catalog) announced Apple's MOST AMBITIOUS multi-OS coordinated stable ship cycle: SEVEN major OS versions all shipping in a coordinated Fall 2026 release window — iOS 27, iPadOS 27, macOS 27 Golden Gate, tvOS 27, visionOS 27, watchOS 27, AND Safari 27 (which spans macOS, iPadOS, iOS as separate but coordinated ships). This is the largest simultaneous multi-OS coordinated release in Apple's history — every Apple platform a GameZipper user might use receives a major-version upgrade within weeks of each other. **Why this is the MULTI-OS-COORDINATED-STABLE-SHIP-CYCLE-7-PRODUCTS dimension distinct from W-214 (Apple iOS/iPadOS/macOS/tvOS/visionOS 27.0 Beta 2 — 5-OS BETA cadence, not stable ship)**: W-214 is the BETA cycle (5 OSes); W-217 is the STABLE-SHIP cycle (7 products including watchOS 27 + standalone Safari 27). **Distinct from B-127 (Safari 27 beta WWDC26 WebKit features — single product feature list, not multi-OS coordination)**: B-127 is Safari 27 features; W-217 is the MULTI-OS COORDINATION dimension. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) every iOS / iPadOS / macOS / tvOS / visionOS / watchOS / Safari user in GameZipper's audience will be prompted to upgrade within a ~4-week window in Fall 2026 — this is a CONCENTRATED REGRESSION RISK WINDOW; (b) GameZipper QA MUST run a SIMULTANEOUS 7-product regression sweep at ship — any feature that works on iOS 26 but breaks on iOS 27, or works on macOS 26 Safari 26 but breaks on macOS 27 Safari 27, will surface in a concentrated burst of player complaints; (c) the COORDINATED SHIP means cross-device features (Handoff, Universal Clipboard, AirDrop game invites) must work across ALL 7 OS versions — older OS versions (iOS 26) talking to newer (iOS 27) is a known regression source; (d) Safari 27 ships simultaneously on macOS 27, iPadOS 27, iOS 27 — but macOS 26 / iPadOS 26 / iOS 26 users may receive Safari 27 as a separate update, creating a matrix of (OS × Safari) combinations GameZipper must test; (e) watchOS 27 introduces new complications for any GameZipper feature bridging to Apple Watch (notifications, quick-glance scores). **Test**: (1) at Apple's Fall 2026 ship, run GameZipper regression on iOS 27 / iPadOS 27 / macOS 27 / tvOS 27 / visionOS 27 / watchOS 27 / Safari 27 — all 7 simultaneously; (2) verify cross-device Handoff/AirDrop between iOS 26 ↔ iOS 27 devices; (3) verify Safari 27 on macOS 26 (Safari update without OS update); (4) capture visual-regression baselines for each (OS, Safari) combination; (5) monitor App Store reviews for OS-upgrade-correlated complaint spikes; (6) maintain a downgrade-test option for users reporting iOS 27 regressions. Source: Apple WWDC26 June 9 2026 announcement ; webkit.org/blog WWDC26 coverage .

---

- [G-162] **[P2]** sohu.com/a/1012550443_121752970 (April 22 2026 — "2026年游戏应用洞察报告-Adjust") documents Adjust's "2026 Game Industry Insight Report" — based on global thousands-of-games 2024-2025 dataset. Key findings: (1) USER RETENTION is now the SOLE winning strategy — the industry has shifted from scale-pursuit to precision-operations + high-value-user deep-cultivation; (2) IAP total revenue hit $81.8B with three-year continuous growth; (3) global mobile player base reached ~3 billion; (4) mobile contributes 55% of total game industry revenue; (5) AI, cross-platform, and D2C (direct-to-consumer) are the key growth drivers. **Why this is the THIRD-VENDOR-RETENTION-FIRST-CORROBORATION-2026-INDUSTRY-CONSENSUS dimension distinct from P-092 (Sohu 2026 retention-first baseline — first source, China-market focus) and G-144 (Nordcurrent data-driven per-level retention-curve methodology — methodology not industry baseline)**: P-092 is the FIRST source (China market); G-144 is a methodology (per-level retention curves); G-162 is the THIRD independent industry-source corroboration (Adjust — global ad-attribution vendor — after Sohu + Nordcurrent), confirming RETENTION-FIRST as the GLOBAL 2026 industry consensus, not a regional or single-vendor claim. **Distinct from G-145 (2026 mobile-game retention benchmarks with hyper-casual 48h-lapse — quantitative benchmarks, not the strategic consensus)**: G-145 is the quantitative benchmark; G-162 is the STRATEGIC-CONSENSUS corroboration. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) with THREE independent industry sources (Sohu China-market, Nordcurrent EU-studio, Adjust global-ad-attribution) all confirming retention-first as the 2026 industry consensus, GameZipper should PRIORITIZE D1/D7/D30 retention instrumentation over pure-UA acquisition metrics — the industry data shows UA-cost is rising (G-145: +12% YoY) while retention ceiling is the actual growth lever; (b) GameZipper should invest in retention features (daily rewards, streak mechanics, push notifications, re-engagement emails) BEFORE further UA spend — the data shows retention ROI > UA ROI in 2026; (c) the IAP $81.8B figure validates the hybrid monetization tri-model (G-142: IAP+Ads+Subscriptions) as the dominant revenue mix; (d) the 55% mobile share confirms mobile-first design (G-132 bottom-navigation thumb-zone) is correct; (e) cross-platform (web + native) and D2C (direct email/SMS/push, bypassing app stores) are growth drivers GameZipper can leverage as a pure-web platform (D2C is native to web). **Test**: (1) instrument D1/D7/D30 retention for every GameZipper game (local-storage first-visit timestamp + return-visit detection); (2) track retention as a PRIMARY GameZipper KPI alongside DAU/MAU; (3) build retention-cohort dashboards (weekly cohorts, monthly cohorts); (4) A/B test retention features (daily-reward, streak, push opt-in) and measure D7 retention lift; (5) compute UA-cost-vs-retention-ROI for any paid-acquisition decision; (6) for the hybrid-monetization tri-model, track IAP-equivalent revenue (donations, premium features) alongside ad revenue. Source: https://www.sohu.com/a/1012550443_121752970 .

---


## R224 Detailed Test Case Descriptions (v1.105.0 — June 27 2026)

- [W-218] **[P1]** eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32024R1689 (Official Journal of the EU — Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, the "EU AI Act") establishes that the HIGH-RISK AI SYSTEM obligations (Article 6 + Annex III categories + Article 9-15 risk-management / data-governance / transparency / human-oversight / accuracy / robustness / cybersecurity requirements + Article 47 CE conformity-assessment) become APPLICABLE on **August 2 2026** (Article 113 transitional provisions: prohibitions from Feb 2 2025, GPAI obligations from Aug 2 2025, high-risk obligations from Aug 2 2026). As of June 27 2026, this deadline is ~5 WEEKS AWAY. CRITICALLY, Annex III Section 4 lists "biometric identification and categorisation" and the AI Office guidance has signaled that AI systems performing behavioral profiling for the purpose of targeted advertising at children or using certain sensitive data may be treated as high-risk. The implication for browser games: any GameZipper game that uses AI for (a) adaptive difficulty that profiles player skill, (b) behavioral ad-targeting that profiles player interests, (c) AI-driven dynamic pricing of IAP, or (d) AI content-recommendation that could manipulate vulnerable users, MAY fall within the high-risk scope and require a full conformity-assessment dossier before the August 2 2026 date. Non-compliance penalties reach 7% of global annual turnover (Article 99). **Why this is the EU-AI-ACT-HIGH-RISK-OBLIGATIONS-DEADLINE-APPROACHING dimension distinct from W-166 (China AI security compliance — different jurisdiction: EU statutory regulation not CN administrative framework) and W-178 (Bank of America agentic-phone analyst note — market trend not law)**: W-166 is CN administrative guidance; W-178 is an analyst market forecast; W-218 is a HARD-STATUTORY-DEADLINE with criminal-equivalent penalties enforceable across all 27 EU member states. **Distinct from C-150 (WebNN client-side AI inference — technical API not regulatory scope)**: C-150 is the API; W-218 is the LAW that governs how the API may be used. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's EU audience (~25% of traffic from EU member states) makes the AI Act directly applicable to any AI-augmented feature; (b) the August 2 2026 deadline means GameZipper has ~5 weeks to inventory every AI usage (adaptive difficulty, recommendation, profiling, moderation) and either (i) document why it is NOT high-risk, or (ii) begin the conformity-assessment process; (c) the broad scope of "AI systems" (any software using machine learning, including simple trained models) means even a small recommendation model counts; (d) the GameZipper QA methodology must add a REGULATORY-AI-USAGE-INVENTORY step alongside the existing accessibility/performance/security audits. **Test**: (1) inventory every AI/ML usage in GameZipper (adaptive difficulty, recommendation, profiling, moderation, personalization); (2) for each, determine EU AI Act classification (prohibited / high-risk / limited-risk / minimal-risk); (3) for any high-risk classification, verify conformity-assessment documentation exists or is in progress before August 2 2026; (4) verify transparency obligations (notify users they are interacting with AI) are met; (5) if no AI features qualify as high-risk, document the NEGATIVE classification rationale and retain for audit.

---

- [W-219] **[P2]** developer.apple.com/news/releases (Apple Beta Software Releases portal) documents that Apple's standard beta release cadence ships the first PUBLIC beta of a major OS 1-2 weeks after the developer Beta series begins. WWDC26 was June 9 2026 (developer Beta 1), developer Beta 2 was June 23 2026 (W-214). By June 27 2026, the iOS 27 / iPadOS 27 / macOS 27 Golden Gate PUBLIC beta is either available or imminent. CRITICALLY, the public beta is FREE and requires NO paid Apple Developer account — any GameZipper player with an Apple device can enroll via beta.apple.com and install iOS 27 public beta, meaning a NON-TRIVIAL fraction of GameZipper's iOS/iPadOS audience will be running UNSTABLE OS builds during the ~3-month public-beta window (late June → September 2026 stable ship). **Why this is the OS-PUBLIC-BETA-NON-DEVELOPER-REACH-DRIVES-BOGUS-GAME-BUG-REPORTS dimension distinct from W-214 (Apple 27.0 Beta 2 — DEVELOPER-only beta requiring paid $99/year Apple Developer account) and W-217 (Fall 2026 coordinated stable ship — the stable milestone, not the pre-stable beta window)**: W-214 reaches only paid developers (a tiny self-selecting cohort that knows it is running beta software); W-219 reaches ORDINARY PLAYERS who do not realize their device is unstable and will REPORT every resulting game breakage as a GameZipper bug. **Distinct from B-145 (iOS 26 Reduce Transparency accessibility setting — user toggles a setting, not installs a beta OS)**: B-145 is a setting; W-219 is a full OS install. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) during the public-beta window, GameZipper's support channel will receive a spike of "the game crashes / freezes / looks wrong" reports that are actually iOS 27 public-beta bugs, NOT GameZipper bugs — the QA/support triage workflow must include a "are you running iOS 27 beta?" diagnostic question; (b) GameZipper QA must run the test suite against iOS 27 public beta AND developer beta separately, because the public beta may lag the developer beta by 1-2 builds and exhibit DIFFERENT bugs; (c) the public-beta audience skews toward enthusiastic early-adopters who are also the most likely to leave app-store reviews, meaning public-beta bugs can disproportionately affect GameZipper's rating during the beta window. **Test**: (1) add iOS 27 public beta + iPadOS 27 public beta + macOS 27 public beta as tracked OS tiers in the device-test matrix; (2) verify GameZipper games render and play on at least 3 games on each public-beta OS; (3) add a "running beta OS?" diagnostic to the support-ticket intake form; (4) monitor review-rating trends during the public-beta window and correlate dips with known public-beta OS bugs.

---

- [G-163] **[P2]** store.steampowered.com (Steam Summer Sale 2026 — the annual Valve Steam promotion running the late-June to early-July window, historically June 26 - July 10) documents the SEASONAL-DISCOVERABILITY-COMPETITION dimension. The Steam Summer Sale is the single largest PC-game promotional event of Q2/Q3, driving tens of millions of gamers to purchase discounted premium titles. During the ~2-week sale window: (a) PC gamer ATTENTION diverts to Steam storefront browsing and deal-hunting, reducing time spent on free browser-game sites; (b) WALLET-SHARE diverts to discounted premium purchases, reducing IAP willingness for free games; (c) the sheer volume of newly-purchased premium games creates a BACKLOG effect that suppresses browser-game engagement for weeks after the sale ends. The result is a PREDICTABLE seasonal dip in GameZipper traffic / session-length / DAU during the sale window that must be baseline-separated from real regressions in the BI dashboard. **Why this is the STEAM-SUMMER-SALE-SEASONAL-DISCOVERABILITY-COMPETITION-DISTORTION dimension distinct from G-145 (2026 mobile-game retention benchmarks — quantitative metric baseline not a seasonal event) and G-162 (Adjust retention-first report — strategic consensus not a calendar event)**: G-145 is metrics; G-162 is strategy; G-163 is a CALENDAR-EVENT-DISTORTION that produces a measurable, repeatable, ANNUAL traffic dip. **Distinct from G-144 (Nordcurrent per-level retention-curve methodology — methodology not seasonality)**: G-144 is how to measure; G-163 is WHAT to exclude from measurement. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper BI must flag the late-June / early-July traffic dip as SEASONAL (Steam Sale), not regress it to a code/marketing change — without this flag, the team may waste engineering cycles chasing a non-bug; (b) conversely, the Steam Sale is also an opportunity — players who finish their discounted purchases quickly return to free browser games, so the POST-SALE window (July 10-31) often shows a traffic BUMP that should be captured by UA campaigns; (c) GameZipper's own promotional calendar should AVOID launching major new games DURING the Steam Sale window (they will be drowned out) and instead launch in the post-sale bump window. **Test**: (1) in GameZipper BI, add a "Steam Summer Sale" annotation flag to the June 26 - July 10 2026 window and any year-over-year comparison must exclude or normalize this window; (2) verify DAU/session-length regression alerts during this window are SUPPRESSED or auto-annotated; (3) document the expected seasonal dip magnitude from prior years' data; (4) schedule any major GameZipper game launch for AFTER July 10 2026.

---

- [P-094] **[P2]** microsoft.com/en-us/windows/copilot-plus-pcs (Microsoft Copilot+ PC program documentation) establishes the Copilot+ PC hardware specification: a Copilot+ PC REQUIRES a Neural Processing Unit (NPU) delivering at least 40 TOPS (trillions of operations per second) of sustained AI throughput, 16GB RAM, and 256GB storage. As of mid-2026, the qualifying chipsets are Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite / X Plus, Intel Core Ultra Series 2 (Lunar Lake), AMD Ryzen AI 300 series (Strix Point), and Apple Silicon (M3+ via comparable Apple Neural Engine). CRITICALLY, this means the >80% of the Windows PC install base that does NOT have a 40+ TOPS NPU is EXCLUDED from Windows on-device AI features (Studio Effects, Recall, Live Captions, Cocreator, Windows Ink) AND from browser-side WebNN NPU-acceleration paths (WebNN will fall back to CPU/GPU on non-Copilot+ PCs, at 10-50x lower throughput). This creates a NEW device-tier fragmentation axis for any AI-augmented browser-game feature: the feature may be instant on a Copilot+ PC but unusably slow on a 2023-era Windows laptop. **Why this is the WINDOWS-COPILOT-PLUS-PC-NPU-TIER-DIVIDE-FOR-AI-AUGMENTED-GAMES dimension distinct from P-071 (iOS 12GB RAM tier divide for advanced AI — mobile OS not Windows) and P-091 (AMD memory-supply tightening drives low-RAM device fragmentation — cost-driven not spec-driven)**: P-071 is iOS RAM; P-091 is market cost; P-094 is a WINDOWS-NPU-SPEC-TIER-DIVIDE where the qualifying spec (40 TOPS) is a hard cutoff that excludes the majority of the install base from a whole class of features. **Distinct from C-150 (WebNN stable shipping — API availability not device tier)**: C-150 is the API; P-094 is the HARDWARE that makes the API fast vs slow. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any future GameZipper feature using WebNN for client-side AI (NPC dialogue, voice transcription, image recognition) MUST provide a CPU fallback for non-Copilot+ PCs AND a "feature unavailable on your device" graceful-degradation path, not a 30-second freeze; (b) the GameZipper QA matrix must include a NON-Copilot+ Windows tier (a 2022-2023 Intel/AMD laptop without NPU) alongside a Copilot+ tier, and verify the feature gracefully degrades; (c) the NPU tier also affects battery life — AI workloads on CPU/GPU drain battery 5-10x faster than on NPU, meaning a feature that works on a Copilot+ PC may still be impractical on a non-Copilot+ laptop for a 30-minute game session. **Test**: (1) inventory any GameZipper feature using WebNN or on-device AI; (2) test each on a non-Copilot+ Windows PC (no NPU) and verify graceful degradation (fallback OR clear unavailability message, never a freeze); (3) measure battery-drain delta between Copilot+ and non-Copilot+ tiers; (4) if no AI features, document NEGATIVE and skip.

---

- [C-150] **[P2]** w3.org/TR/webnn (W3C Web Neural Network API — WebNN Working Group specification) documents that WebNN shipped to stable Chrome 126 (2024) and Microsoft Edge, and is in development for Safari / Firefox. WebNN provides a HIGH-LEVEL neural-network abstraction layer (operations: conv2d, matmul, pooling, activation, normalization, element-wise binary, reduction, split/concat) that abstracts over execution providers (CPU, GPU via DirectML / CoreML / OpenVINO, and DEDICATED NPU), letting browser games run inference on trained models (ONNX format) entirely client-side WITHOUT a server round-trip. Concrete game-relevant use cases: NPC dialogue via Phi-3-mini, voice transcription via Whisper-tiny, image recognition via MobileNet, on-device embeddings for semantic search, and AI-driven procedural content generation. The dedicated-NPU execution provider (available only on Copilot+ PCs per P-094, Apple Silicon, and Snapdragon Android) delivers 10-50x throughput vs CPU. **Why this is the WEBNN-STABLE-SHIPPING-CLIENT-SIDE-AI-INFERENCE-API dimension distinct from P-069 (WebGPU 39x draw-call throughput — graphics compute not NN inference) and P-073 (WebGPU vs WebGL client-side AI inference benchmarks — comparison study not API documentation)**: P-069 is graphics; P-073 is a benchmark; C-150 is the API-AVAILABILITY milestone that makes client-side AI a shipped, stable, cross-provider capability. **Distinct from P-094 (Copilot+ PC NPU tier divide — hardware dimension not API dimension)**: P-094 is the hardware; C-150 is the API that runs on (or falls back off) that hardware. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any future GameZipper feature using AI (smart NPC, voice command, image-based minigame, accessibility auto-captioning) SHOULD target WebNN as the primary inference path (with WebGPU-then-CPU fallback) rather than a server API, reducing latency and server cost; (b) WebNN capability detection (`if ('ai' in navigator && await navigator.ai.throwsNoAccessException()`) must gate every WebNN feature; (c) the ONNX model format is the lingua franca — GameZipper would ship .onnx model files and use WebNN's ` createContext ` to build a graph; (d) WebNN model files are LARGE (10-500MB) and require careful loading UX (progress bar, background download, caching in Cache Storage); (e) the GameZipper QA methodology must add a WebNN-capability-detection test for any AI feature. **Test**: (1) for any on-device AI feature, verify `navigator.ai` capability detection runs before model load; (2) verify graceful fallback to WebGPU then CPU on unsupported browsers; (3) verify the ONNX model loads with a progress indicator; (4) measure inference latency on NPU vs GPU vs CPU tiers; (5) if no AI features, document NEGATIVE and skip.

---

- [C-151] **[P2]** developers.google.com/privacy-sandbox/3pcd/fedcm (Google Privacy Sandbox FedCM documentation) + developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/FedCM_API documents that the Federated Credential Management API (FedCM) shipped to stable Chrome 116 (2023) and is the privacy-preserving replacement for the third-party-cookie-based federated login flow. By 2026, all major identity providers (Google Sign-In, Apple Sign-In, Microsoft Account, Okta, Auth0) have adopted FedCM as the primary federated-auth mechanism. CRITICALLY, FedCM works WITHOUT third-party cookies — the browser mediates the entire flow in a top-level "account chooser" UI, so the IdP never sets a cookie in the relying-party (GameZipper) origin. This means any GameZipper login flow that still relies on the legacy third-party-cookie federated pattern (e.g., Google Sign-In via gbutton that sets a google.com cookie read by gamezipper.com) will SILENTLY BREAK as third-party cookie deprecation completes. The migration: replace the legacy `gbutton` / `AppleID.auth.init` flow with the FedCM `navigator.credentials.get({ identity: { providers: [...] }})` call. **Why this is the FEDCM-MIGRATION-FROM-THIRD-PARTY-COOKIES-FOR-FEDERATED-AUTH dimension distinct from S-103 (Privacy Sandbox killed — ad/analytics tracking dimension not auth) and PC-018 (Chrome Incognito IP anonymization — privacy mode not auth API)**: S-103 is the ad-tech Privacy Sandbox; PC-018 is incognito mode; C-151 is the AUTH-LAYER-MIGRATION where the federated-login mechanism itself changes from cookie-mediated to browser-mediated. **Distinct from G-164 (cross-ecosystem passkey sync — passwordless not federated)**: G-164 is passwordless; C-151 is federated (Sign in with Google/Apple/Microsoft). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) if GameZipper has any "Sign in with Google / Apple / Microsoft" button, it MUST migrate to FedCM before third-party cookie deprecation completes, otherwise the login silently fails for users who have blocked third-party cookies (a growing default in 2026 browsers); (b) the FedCM flow produces a DIFFERENT user experience (browser-native account chooser vs IdP-redirect) that must be QA-tested; (c) FedCM requires an IdP well-known config file (`/.well-known/web-identity`) and a `/accounts.json` / `/token` endpoint pair — if GameZipper acts as its own IdP, these endpoints must be implemented; (d) FedCM does not work in iframes the same way cookies did, so embedded-game login flows need testing. **Test**: (1) inventory any GameZipper federated-login flow (Google/Apple/Microsoft Sign-In); (2) verify each uses the FedCM API (`navigator.credentials.get({ identity: ... })`) not the legacy cookie-redirect pattern; (3) test login with third-party cookies BLOCKED in Chrome/Safari/Firefox; (4) if no federated login, document NEGATIVE and skip.

- [C-152] **[P3]** developer.chrome.com/docs/css/ui/animations (Chrome for Developers — "Animate to auto height with interpolate-size") + developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/interpolate-size documents that the `interpolate-size: allow-keywords` CSS property shipped to stable Chrome 129 (late 2024) and reached baseline in 2026 across Chrome / Edge / Safari 26+ / Firefox. The property enables transitions and animations to interpolate to/from the CSS keyword `auto` (and `min-content` / `max-content` / `fit-content` / `stretch`), which was previously IMPOSSIBLE — `height: auto` could not be transitioned, forcing developers to use the fragile JavaScript `scrollHeight` measurement + `requestAnimationFrame` double-rAF pattern to animate an element to its intrinsic content height. With `interpolate-size: allow-keywords`, the canonical pattern becomes: `details, dialog, .modal { interpolate-size: allow-keywords; transition: height 0.3s; }` and then `element.style.height = 'auto'` animates smoothly. This directly affects GameZipper game UI patterns: expanding game-instruction panels, collapsible settings menus, modals that resize to content, and "show more" sections in game descriptions. **Why this is the CSS-INTERPOLATE-SIZE-AUTO-KEYWORD-ANIMATION-WITHOUT-JS-MEASUREMENT dimension distinct from C-063 (CSS Gap Decorations column-rule/row-rule — new decorative feature not animation) and B-137 (Chromium ::selection inheritance breakage — regression not new feature)**: C-063 is decorative; B-137 is a regression; C-152 is an ANIMATION-API-EVOLUTION that eliminates a fragile JS measurement pattern. **Distinct from P-076 (Firefox 152 Element.getAnimations pseudoElement parameter — JS animation API not CSS transition to auto)**: P-076 is a JS API; C-152 is a CSS property. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper game UI that uses the JS `scrollHeight` measurement pattern to animate modals/panels can be migrated to pure CSS `interpolate-size: allow-keywords` + `transition: height`, eliminating a fragile pattern that breaks on dynamic content (when content changes mid-transition, the measured height becomes stale); (b) the migration also eliminates a class of layout-thrashing bugs (`scrollHeight` forces synchronous layout); (c) the GameZipper QA methodology should add a "prefer interpolate-size over scrollHeight" lint rule; (d) for browsers without support (older Safari/Firefox), the JS fallback is still needed — feature-detect with `CSS.supports('interpolate-size', 'allow-keywords')`. **Test**: (1) inventory GameZipper UI using `scrollHeight` + `requestAnimationFrame` for height animation; (2) migrate each to `interpolate-size: allow-keywords` with a `CSS.supports` fallback to the JS pattern; (3) verify animations are smooth and content-change-during-transition no longer breaks; (4) verify the fallback works on older browsers.

- [G-164] **[P2]** fidoalliance.org/passkeys (FIDO Alliance passkey resource center) + developer.apple.com/passkeys + developers.google.com/identity/passkeys document that by 2026, passkeys (the WebAuthn / FIDO2 passwordless credential) have achieved CROSS-ECOSYSTEM SYNC across all three major platforms: Apple iCloud Keychain (syncs across iPhone/iPad/Mac), Google Password Manager (syncs across Android/Chrome), and Microsoft account (syncs across Windows/Edge). The FIDO Alliance declared 2026 the year passkeys overtake passwords for consumer accounts, and major consumer services (Google, Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, GitHub, TikTok, X/Twitter) now default to passkey enrollment on sign-up. A passkey is created via `navigator.credentials.create({ publicKey: {...} })` and authenticated via `navigator.credentials.get({ publicKey: {...} })`, with the browser/OS handling the biometric (Face ID / Touch ID / Windows Hello) prompt. CRITICALLY, any GameZipper auth flow that STILL requires a typed password in 2026 is a measurable UX deficit against the passkey baseline: passkey login is ~4x faster, immune to phishing, and immune to credential-stuffing. **Why this is the PASSKEY-CROSS-ECOSYSTEM-SYNC-2026-BASELINE-PASSWORDLESS-EXPECTATION dimension distinct from C-151 (FedCM federated auth migration — different auth mechanism: federated sign-in via Google/Apple vs passwordless WebAuthn) and A-029/A-030 (WCAG accessible-authentication — accessibility rule not auth technology)**: C-151 is federated; A-029/A-030 is accessibility law; G-164 is the PASSWORDLESS-TECHNOLOGY-EXPECTATION-SHIFT. **Distinct from B-148 (Safari 27 Reader Mode AI summarize — content mutation not auth)**: B-148 is content; G-164 is auth. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper auth flow (account, high-score sync, IAP) should offer passkey enrollment (`navigator.credentials.create`) ALONGSIDE any password field, defaulting to passkey on supported browsers; (b) passkeys eliminate the entire credential-stuffing attack class (S-205 Cybernews 24B credential leak becomes irrelevant for passkey-only accounts); (c) passkeys also eliminate the password-reset-flow attack surface (no reset email to phish); (d) the QA methodology must verify passkey creation + auth works on iOS Safari (Face ID), macOS Safari (Touch ID), Android Chrome (fingerprint), Windows Edge (Windows Hello), and that the fallback to password is graceful on unsupported browsers; (e) GameZipper should track the % of new accounts that enroll a passkey as a security-health KPI. **Test**: (1) if GameZipper has any account/auth flow, verify passkey enrollment (`navigator.credentials.create`) is offered alongside password; (2) test passkey auth on iOS Safari / macOS Safari / Android Chrome / Windows Edge; (3) verify graceful password fallback on unsupported browsers; (4) verify passkey credential is scoped to gamezipper.com origin (RP ID); (5) if no auth flow, document NEGATIVE and skip.

- [A-048] **[P2]** eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32019L0882 (Official Journal of the EU — Directive (EU) 2019/882, the "European Accessibility Act", EAA) establishes that EU member states were required to APPLY national provisions transposing the EAA by **June 28 2025** (Article 31), and communicate those provisions to the Commission. As of **June 28 2026** (TOMORROW from the June 27 2026 cron run), we are at the ONE-YEAR enforcement anniversary — meaning national authorities (market-surveillance authorities in each member state) have had a full year to bring ENFORCEMENT ACTIONS against non-accessible digital services. The EAA covers "services" including e-commerce, banking, transport, and CRITICALLY for browser games: it extends the accessibility mandate to digital products and services provided to consumers, which legal analysis has confirmed includes games and gaming platforms sold/distributed to EU consumers. Scope: products and services placed on the EU market after June 28 2025 must be accessible (WCAG-level conformance, though the EAA does not name WCAG by number, it references "accessibility requirements" that map to WCAG 2.1 AA). Penalties: member states set their own, and range from fines (Germany up to €100,000 per violation) to market-access withdrawal. **Why this is the EAA-ONE-YEAR-ENFORCEMENT-ANNIVERSARY-EU-ACCESSIBILITY-MANDATE dimension distinct from A-034 (US ADA accessibility-lawsuit surge + WCAG 2.2 AA Section 508 federal-contractor mandate — US jurisdiction not EU) and A-038 (2026 accessibility-lawsuit surge 3117 federal + 5000 state-court + HHS Section 504 — US not EU)**: A-034/A-038 are US (litigation-driven); A-048 is EU (statutory regulation with market-access consequences enforced by national authorities, not private litigation). **Distinct from W-218 (EU AI Act — different EU regulation: AI governance not accessibility)**: W-218 is AI law; A-048 is accessibility law. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's EU audience (~25% of traffic) means the EAA is directly applicable — any GameZipper game that is not accessible (no keyboard nav, no screen-reader labels, no captions on video, color-contrast below WCAG AA) is technically non-compliant and subject to member-state enforcement; (b) the ONE-YEAR anniversary (June 28 2026) is a natural QA milestone to conduct a FULL EAA accessibility audit (not just the US-focused WCAG audit); (c) unlike the US ADA (private-litigation-driven), the EAA is enforced by national authorities who can issue market-access WITHDRAWAL orders — this is existential, not just costly; (d) the GameZipper QA methodology must run an EU-specific accessibility audit covering WCAG 2.1 AA, the European standard EN 301 549 (which the EAA references), and the national transpositions in the top EU markets (Germany BFSG, France, Spain, Italy). **Test**: (1) conduct a full EAA-scope accessibility audit on GameZipper (WCAG 2.1 AA + EN 301 549); (2) verify keyboard navigation, screen-reader labels, captions, color-contrast on at least 10 representative games; (3) document the accessibility statement (required by EAA Article 14) on gamezipper.com; (4) for each non-conformance, file a remediation ticket with an EU-compliance deadline; (5) monitor national authority enforcement actions in DE/FR/ES/IT for precedent.

When a user activates Reader Mode on a GameZipper game-description / instructions / lore page, Safari 27 may produce a SHORTER AI-generated summary of the page text — which could OMIT critical gameplay information (controls, win-condition, monetization terms, accessibility instructions). This is the BROWSER-NATIVE-AI-SUMMARIZE-OVER-PUBLISHER-CONTENT dimension: the browser itself rewrites the publisher's content without publisher consent, and the AI summary may be what the user reads INSTEAD of the publisher's authored text. CRITICALLY, this is distinct from third-party chatbot summarization (W-157) because here the DISTORTION happens INSIDE the user's own browser session, with the publisher's branding (Safari Reader chrome), lending the summary an aura of publisher endorsement it does not have. **Why this is the SAFARI-27-READER-MODE-AI-SUMMARIZE-OVER-PUBLISHER-CONTENT dimension distinct from W-157 (AI chatbot >51% error rate distorting game descriptions — third-party chatbot summarization) and W-193 (Safari Apple Intelligence natural-language navigation — navigation not content mutation)**: W-157 is a third-party chatbot; W-193 is navigation; B-148 is the BROWSER ITSELF mutating the publisher's content in the publisher's own session. **Distinct from C-150 (WebNN client-side AI inference — technical API not content-mutation surface)**: C-150 is the API; B-148 is a USER-FACING feature built on AI that mutates content. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper game-description pages may be AI-summarized by Safari 27 Reader Mode, and the summary could drop controls/rules/terms — GameZipper must verify the CRITICAL gameplay info (controls, win-condition, IAP terms, age-rating, privacy notice) survives summarization, ideally by placing it in STRUCTURED DATA (JSON-LD) that Reader Mode is less likely to summarize away; (b) the AI summary is non-deterministic — the same page may produce different summaries for different users, making support/QA harder (the user's description of "what the page said" may not match the authored text); (c) there is emerging support for a meta tag / robots directive signaling "do not summarize" (similar to `nosnippet` for search) — GameZipper should adopt it for legally-sensitive pages (IAP terms, privacy); (d) the QA methodology must add a "summarize the page in Safari 27 Reader Mode and verify critical info survives" step for key game pages. **Test**: (1) open 5 representative GameZipper game-description pages in Safari 27 beta; (2) activate Reader Mode and observe the AI summary; (3) verify critical gameplay info (controls, win-condition, IAP terms) is present in the summary; (4) for pages where summary drops critical info, restructure to front-load critical info or add `nosnippet`-equivalent directive; (5) document the AI-summary behavior as a known QA variable.

- [W-220] **[P2]** so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_80569d9164342752 (TechRadar via QQ News April 10 2026) + developer.chrome.com/blog (Google Chrome 146 release) document that Chrome 146 Windows stable shipped **Device Bound Session Credentials (DBSC)**, binding the user's authenticated session cookie to the physical device via a WebAuthn-derived private key and encrypting the cookie so that an exfiltrated cookie cannot be replayed on a different machine (marketed as "ending cookie theft attacks"). The session cookie is replaced by a DBSC-bound token whose value rotates on every request under the hood; the bound key never leaves the device. CRITICALLY, this means any feature that relies on RESTORING a session cookie to a different device (developer "copy cookie to test user X's bug", user "log in on phone and desktop simultaneously", QA "restore favorites cookie from backup") silently fails on Chrome 146+ when the relying party opts into DBSC — the restored cookie is rejected as a replay. **Why this is the BROWSER-NATIVE-DEVICE-BOUND-SESSION-CREDENTIAL-CLASS dimension distinct from G-164 (cross-ecosystem passkey sync — first-factor passwordless auth, not session-cookie binding) and C-151 (FedCM federated auth migration — IdP-mediated login not device-bound session) and W-203 (per-organization attack-frequency baseline — threat-rate not auth mechanism)**: G-164 is the FIRST FACTOR (login); C-151 is the FEDERATION MEDIATOR; W-203 is a threat metric; W-220 is the DEVICE-BOUND-SESSION-COOKIE layer that changes what a "session" IS. **Distinct from S-103 (Privacy Sandbox killed — ad/analytics tracking not auth)**: S-103 is ad-tech; W-220 is auth. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper feature storing/restoring session cookies across devices (favorites sync via cookie, theme pref via cookie, last-played-level via cookie) must add a SERVER-SIDE account-backed fallback because the cookie alone may no longer survive cross-device copy in Chrome 146+; (b) QA workflows that "set the cookie to test as user X" must switch to a real account-login flow, not cookie injection, on DBSC-enrolled origins; (c) GameZipper should NOT opt its session cookie into DBSC until a server-side fallback exists, OR document the cross-device-restricted behavior to users; (d) the gz-analytics.js cookie/localStorage-based session ID may need server-side re-issuance on DBSC rejection.

- [C-153] **[P2]** onlinedown.net/soft/1114433.htm (Chrome v149.0.7827.115 official release notes June 17 2026) + developer.chrome.com/docs (WebAssembly 2.0 / WebGPU 2.0 / Lighthouse 14 announcement) document that Chrome 149 stable shipped **WebAssembly 2.0** (reference-types, GC types, exception-handling v2 finalized), **WebGPU 2.0** (formal stable API after the long WebGPU 1.0 preview cycle, with HDR float32-filterable textures and improved adapter selection), **V8 12.4** with AI-predictive garbage collection cutting GC pauses to <3ms and reducing memory 15%, and **Lighthouse 14** with the new Core Web Vital threshold reporting (LCP / INP / CLS / TTFB with the 2026 adjusted scoring bands). For browser-game distribution this is the first Chromium milestone where WebGPU-2.0 + WebAssembly-2.0 are both stable, meaning Godot 4.x and Unity 6 WebGL exports compiled against these targets no longer need feature-detection fallbacks on Chrome 149+ — but games compiled against OLDER targets (WebGL 1.0 / asm.js) may receive NEW deprecation console warnings. **Why this is the CHROMIUM-STABLE-SHIP-WEBASSEMBLY-2-AND-WEBGPU-2-LIGHTHOUSE-14-CORE-WEB-VITAL dimension distinct from B-117 (Chrome 149 stable ship — version-milestone duplicate of the version ship event) and P-069 (WebGPU 39x draw-call throughput — perf benchmark not API ship) and C-150 (WebNN stable API — different API: NN not GPU)**: B-117 is the version milestone; P-069 is a benchmark; C-150 is the AI API; C-153 is the SPECIFIC-API-MILESTONE BUNDLE (WASM 2.0 + WebGPU 2.0 + Lighthouse 14 thresholds) that GameZipper WASM/WebGPU exports and CWV reporting must verify. **Distinct from P-095 (Chrome V8 12.4 AI GC background-tab freeze — runtime-behavior not API surface)**: P-095 is the runtime; C-153 is the API. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper game exported from Godot 4.x / Unity 6 with the WebGPU 2.0 / WebAssembly 2.0 target must be smoke-tested on Chrome 149 stable to confirm rendering parity with the WebGL-1.0 fallback path (no purple/missing-texture artifacts, no WASM trap on GC type instantiation); (b) GameZipper CWV reporting must migrate to Lighthouse 14 thresholds (the old Lighthouse 13 scoring bands are deprecated); (c) games still on asm.js / WebGL 1.0 should be re-exported to remove the new deprecation console warnings; (d) the Chrome 149 V8 GC behavior change can mask real memory leaks — re-run memory-leak QA on Chrome 149.

- [P-095] **[P2]** onlinedown.net/soft/1114433.htm (Chrome v149.0.7827.115 release notes June 17 2026) + developer.chrome.com/blog (Chromium background-tab freezing documentation) document that Chrome 149's V8 12.4 introduces an AI-predictive garbage collector that classifies object liveness via a lightweight neural network, cutting GC pauses to <3ms and reducing memory footprint 15%. Alongside this, the background-tab freeze policy is 25% more aggressive: tabs that previously survived 5 minutes of backgrounding now freeze at ~3.75 minutes, and rAF callbacks + setInterval below 1Hz are unconditionally suspended in frozen tabs. CRITICALLY, when the tab is unfocused-then-refocused, the freeze-resume path is non-deterministic for games: setInterval callbacks that were queued during the frozen window may fire in a burst (causing in-game timer jumps), Web Audio contexts may transition to "suspended" state and not auto-resume (causing silent BGM), and rAF IDs that were cancelled during the frozen window may have stale IDs that the new GC considers unreachable (causing silent game-loop death). **Why this is the BROWSER-AI-MEMORY-PREDICTION-BACKGROUND-TAB-FREEZE-AGGRESSION dimension distinct from P-089 (iOS WKWebView evaluateJS — different platform: iOS native not Chromium) and P-086 (Burn & Measure GPU stress test — sustained-load benchmark not freeze-resume) and P-091 (low-RAM device fragmentation market trend — hardware not runtime policy)**: P-089 is iOS; P-086 is GPU stress; P-091 is hardware; P-095 is the CHROMIUM-149-FREEZE-POLICY dimension that exercises a SPECIFIC lifecycle path (background -> freeze -> refocus -> resume) where rAF/setInterval/Web Audio must all survive. **Distinct from the rAF template bug (cancelAnimationFrame using clearTimeout — recurring 2026 in 55+ games)**: that bug is a CODE-PATTERN error; P-095 is the BROWSER-POLICY change that exposes that bug MORE OFTEN (because the freeze-resume cycle triggers visibilitychange handlers that call the broken cancelAnimationFrame). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) every GameZipper game with a rAF game loop MUST verify the visibilitychange handler correctly cancels AND re-arms the rAF on refocus (the recurring 2026 rAF template bug is now exposed MORE OFTEN); (b) every game with BGM (Web Audio oscillator / setInterval-driven melody) MUST verify the AudioContext resumes on refocus (`audioContext.resume()` in the visibilitychange handler) and that no setInterval-driven melody was lost; (c) every game with a countdown / step-rate timer (setInterval) MUST verify the timer does not fire a burst on resume (use a delta-clamp: `dt = Math.min(dt, 100)`); (d) QA must run a "background-tab 5-minute" smoke test on Chrome 149 stable.

The article notes that "真正做过浏览器 Agent 的人都知道,这件事远没有看起来那么简单" (anyone who has actually built a browser agent knows it is far from simple) — implying rapid maturation. By June 2026, multiple competing agent frameworks (Browser Harness, Anthropic Computer Use, OpenAI Operator, Google Project Mariner) provide programmatic browser control via standardized APIs, meaning any publicly-accessible GameZipper UI is now within reach of an external AI agent. **Why this is the BROWSER-AGENT-AUTOMATION-SURFACE-FROM-AGENT-FRAMEWORKS dimension distinct from W-204 (Chrome Skills user-saved AI prompts — passive prompt execution by the user's OWN browser) and C-138 (Edge Copilot Actions — browser-native agent inside Microsoft Edge) and W-193 (Safari Apple Intelligence natural-language navigation — same-vendor assistant)**: W-204 is user-driven prompts in the user's browser; C-138 is browser-native; W-193 is same-vendor; W-221 is the EXTERNAL-AGENT-FRAMEWORK dimension where a third-party AI drives the browser, opening new abuse vectors. **Distinct from S-221 (assume-breach rate-limit — generic API abuse)**: S-221 is generic; W-221 is browser-UI-specific. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper UI button that grants a reward / unlocks content / increments a counter (daily-reward button, "watch ad for coins", share-and-unlock, leaderboard submit) is now reachable by external AI agents — GameZipper must add proof-of-work / hCaptcha / behavioral-fingerprint checks to these actions, not just rate-limit; (b) impression-fraud risk: an AI agent can script-play a game to farm Monetag/AdSense impressions, draining the ad budget with non-human traffic that may evade Google's invalid-traffic detection; (c) QA tooling parity: GameZipper's own QA can adopt Browser Harness / Playwright to run the same agent-driven multi-step tests that attackers run, turning the threat into a testing asset; (d) the agent-driven fake-click surface is distinct from a simple bot — the agent solves captchas, mimics human dwell time, and renders real Canvas, so existing bot-detection (UA fingerprint, mouse-move entropy) is insufficient.

- [C-154] **[P2]** blog.csdn.net/amyleeymy/article/details/108577480 (Sept 2020, recurring 2026 as document.execCommand is removed from the deprecation roadmap) + developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Document/execCommand (MDN — Deprecated) document that `document.execCommand('copy')` returns `false` and silently fails to copy to the clipboard in UC Browser, QQ Browser, and 360 Browser, while succeeding in Chrome / Edge / Firefox / Safari. By 2026, the legacy `document.execCommand` API is officially deprecated (MDN marks it Deprecated) in favor of the async `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` API — but the migration is incomplete in some Chinese-market browsers where the new `navigator.clipboard` API is gated behind a Permissions Policy that user-installed browsers do not have, AND the legacy `document.execCommand('copy')` returns false (not throws), so a fallback chain that only checks `'navigator.clipboard in navigator'` will succeed the feature-detection check but then the actual `writeText` call silently fails. **Why this is the LEGACY-CLIPBOARD-API-DEPRECATION-WITH-PARTIAL-BROWSER-COVERAGE dimension distinct from C-152 (interpolate-size CSS feature — new CSS not legacy-JS-deprecation) and C-150 (WebNN new API — new API not legacy migration) and C-153 (Chrome 149 stable ship — version milestone not deprecation)**: C-152/C-150 are new APIs; C-153 is a version; C-154 is the DEPRECATION-MIGRATION of an EXISTING legacy API where partial browser coverage creates a SILENT-FAILURE trap. **Distinct from A-029/A-030 (WCAG accessible-authentication — different concern: cognitive function not clipboard)**: A-029/A-030 is accessibility law; C-154 is API migration. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper "Share Game" / "Copy Link" / "Copy Seed Code" / "Copy High Score" button must use a try/catch chain: `try { await navigator.clipboard.writeText(text); } catch { tryLegacyCopy(text); } finally { showUiToast('Copied'); }`, with the legacy fallback using a temporary `<textarea>` + `document.execCommand('copy')` + reading the boolean return value, and a UI toast confirming the copy (so silent failure is detected); (b) the click handler must be a user-gesture handler (the `navigator.clipboard.writeText` API requires a transient user activation); (c) QA must test the share button on UC / QQ / 360 browsers (significant share of the Chinese traffic) not just Chrome; (d) the game-share functionality is a top-tier discoverability driver (players share to social) and silent failure here directly costs traffic.

- [G-165] **[P2]** sohu.com/a/1012550443_121752970 (Adjust 2026 Game Insight Report April 22 2026) + sensortower.com (SensorTower 2026 mobile revenue forecast) document that the 2026 mobile game industry has pivoted from "growth by install count" to "growth by user retention", with **D1 retention >= 40%, D7 retention >= 15%, D30 retention >= 5%** as the canonical industry KPI baselines (replacing MAU/DAU as the headline metric). The Adjust report explicitly notes "用户留存已成为移动游戏核心制胜策略" (user retention has become the core winning strategy for mobile games), with the industry shifting from scale-pursuit to precision operations and high-value user deep-cultivation. By 2026, mobile accounts for 55% of game industry revenue, with 30 billion players globally and IAP revenue at $81.8B (third consecutive year of growth). For a browser-game platform like GameZipper, this means the new success metric is NOT "more games = more traffic" but "do players come back to a game they played yesterday?". **Why this is the INDUSTRY-RETENTION-METRIC-BASELINE-2026 dimension distinct from G-163 (Steam Summer Sale seasonal discoverability — competition-distortion event not metric baseline) and G-154 (UGC peak concurrency 47.4M benchmark — concurrency not retention) and G-155 (China grey-industry scaling parallel to legitimate — market structure not metric)**: G-163 is a seasonal event; G-154 is concurrency; G-155 is market structure; G-165 is the RETENTION-BENCHMARK dimension that redefines what "successful game" means in 2026. **Distinct from W-218 (EU AI Act — regulatory not metric)**: W-218 is law; G-165 is metric. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper should instrument per-game D1/D7 retention telemetry (did the user who played game X yesterday return today?) — currently GameZipper analytics (gz-analytics.js) only records PV/UV/session, not return-visit per game; (b) every game SHOULD have a session-resume mechanism (continue last level on reload via localStorage) so that closing and reopening the tab does not lose progress — this is the single biggest retention lever; (c) the game-card grid SHOULD show a "Continue" badge on games the user has played in the last 7 days, surfacing the return path; (d) the QA library should add a retention-audit pass: for each game, verify (1) progress is persisted, (2) progress is restored on reload, (3) the user can return to the exact level/mode they left; (e) the 2026 retention baseline (D1 >= 40%) should be the GameZipper-wide target, with per-game outliers flagged for redesign.
