- [P-98] **[P2]** WebGPU-vs-WebAssembly 256×256 Matrix Inflection-Point Dispatch (blog.csdn.net/weixin_29300931/article/details/159638655 June 5 2026 — "WebGPU vs WebAssembly性能对决:用矩阵乘法实测浏览器计算新王者") — A 72-matrix-size benchmark (16×16 to 2048×2048, M1 Pro Chrome 118) found: **at matrix sizes ≤ 256×256, WebAssembly is FASTER than WebGPU compute shaders** (5-8× slower for WebGPU at small matrices due to higher call-setup overhead); **at matrix sizes ≥ 512×512, WebGPU is 5-8× FASTER than WASM**. The inflection point is ~256×256. The result implies that for AI-augmented GameZipper features (embeddings via CLIP, convolution via MobileNet, small-language-model inference), the optimal dispatch is HYBRID: small matrices → WASM, large matrices → WebGPU — choosing the wrong path can be 5-8× slower. **Why this is the WORKLOAD-SIZE-AWARE-PERFORMANCE-CROSSOVER-DISPATCH dimension distinct from P-073 (WebGPU-vs-WebGL client-side AI benchmarks — different comparison: WASM not in scope) and P-096 (WebGPU/WebGL hybrid coexistence — graphics, not compute) and P-069 (WebGPU 39× draw-call throughput — draw calls, not compute-shader-vs-CPU)**: P-073 is GPU-vs-GPU; P-096 is graphics; P-069 is draw calls; P-98 is the WORKLOAD-SIZE-DISPATCH-DECISION dimension that directly drives AI-feature implementation choice. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any future GameZipper AI feature (NPC dialogue via Phi-3-mini, image recognition via MobileNet, embeddings for semantic game search, on-device Voice-to-Text) should implement a HYBRID DISPATCH: probe the input shape, dispatch to WebGPU if all dims ≥ 256, dispatch to WASM otherwise; (b) the GameZipper AI-feature QA methodology must benchmark BOTH paths and choose per-workload, not globally assume "WebGPU is faster"; (c) the BROADER PATTERN — perf-crossover-dispatch — applies beyond AI: any parallel computation (physics solvers, particle systems, image processing) should probe size and dispatch accordingly; (d) the inflection point is HARDWARE-DEPENDENT — the 256×256 figure is M1-Pro specific; ARM mobile GPUs may shift the inflection to 128×128 or 512×512, requiring per-device benchmark. **Test**: (1) for any AI/GameZipper feature using WebGPU compute, run the canonical 72-matrix-size benchmark on the target device; (2) determine the per-device inflection point (where WebGPU overtakes WASM); (3) implement hybrid dispatch (matrix ≥ N → WebGPU, matrix < N → WASM); (4) re-benchmark after each browser-update / device-tier change.

---

The inflection point is ~256x256. - [P-99] **[P2]** WebAssembly-Whisper INT8 Quantization + IndexedDB Cache Pattern (blog.csdn.net/2501_93878564/article/details/154301431 Nov 2025, recurring through 2026) — Documents the canonical pattern for running OpenAI Whisper speech-to-text entirely in the browser via WASM: (1) quantize the model to INT8 (~100-300MB, vs full-precision 1-3GB); (2) fetch from CDN with HTTP Range for resumable partial downloads; (3) cache the quantized model + weights in **IndexedDB** so subsequent sessions do NOT re-download (saving 100-300MB of network per repeat session); (4) real-time mic capture via `getUserMedia` + AudioContext + ScriptProcessor with 4096-sample chunks (30ms cadence). **Why this is the WASM-MODEL-QUANTIZATION-INDEXEDDB-CACHE-PATTERN dimension distinct from P-094 (Copilot+ PC NPU tier-divide — hardware not pattern) and C-150 (WebNN stable API — API not pattern) and P-095 (Chrome V8 GC background-tab freeze — runtime not pattern)**: P-094 is hardware; C-150 is API; P-095 is runtime; P-99 is the APPLICATION-PATTERN dimension covering the full lifecycle: quantize -> fetch with Range -> cache in IndexedDB -> stream mic -> WASM inference — the pattern any future GameZipper voice-feature will need. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any future GameZipper voice-driven feature (voice commands "play/pause/restart", voice-activated difficulty selector, voice-chat NPC dialogue, accessibility auto-captioning of BGM/music) MUST adopt this pattern — without IndexedDB caching, every repeat session re-downloads 100-300MB, destroying CWV LCP and burning player data plans; (b) GameZipper QA must verify: (1) model loads from IndexedDB on repeat visit (NOT from network), (2) IndexedDB write succeeds for first-time download, (3) IndexedDB survives browser-close/reopen (not cleared), (4) IndexedDB survives browser-update, (5) graceful fallback when IndexedDB quota-exceeded (older devices may have <300MB free); (c) the BROADER PATTERN — quantized-model-via-IndexedDB — applies to ANY on-device AI feature (Phi-3-mini, MobileNet, CLIP), not just Whisper; (d) the QUANTIZATION-VARIANT dimension: INT4 (smallest, 50-150MB, accuracy-loss), INT8 (default, 100-300MB, minimal accuracy-loss), FP16 (200-600MB, near-full accuracy) — GameZipper should default INT8 and offer INT4 for low-data-plan users; (e) IndexedDB storage policy: Chrome 149+ may auto-evict IndexedDB on quota pressure — GameZipper should request persistent-storage permission (`navigator.storage.persist()`) to prevent eviction mid-game. **Test**: (1) first visit: confirm model downloads (~100-300MB) and writes to IndexedDB; (2) second visit (clear cache, keep IndexedDB): confirm model loads from IndexedDB, NOT from network (check DevTools Network tab); (3) close browser, reopen, revisit: confirm model still cached; (4) test on low-quota device (<300MB free): confirm graceful fallback; (5) call `navigator.storage.persist()` and confirm IndexedDB survives browser-eviction pressure.

---

- [A-051] **[P2]** MDN Mobile Accessibility Checklist — Authoritative 2026 Minimum Baseline (developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/Guides/Mobile_accessibility_checklist March 10 2026 update) — MDN (Mozilla Developer Network) maintains the AUTHORITATIVE mobile-web-accessibility checklist that distills WCAG 2.x AA requirements into a TESTABLE minimum baseline for mobile games: (1) **CONTRAST RATIO >= 4.5:1 for normal text** (text smaller than 18pt regular or 14pt bold); (2) **CONTRAST RATIO >= 3:1 for large text** (>=18pt regular or >=14pt bold); (3) **INFORMATION CONVEYED VIA COLOR MUST ALSO BE CONVEYED VIA ANOTHER MEANS** (text label, icon, pattern) — pure color-coding fails this rule (e.g., a "red/green" health bar without text label); (4) other items in the checklist cover target-size, focus-management, semantics. The MDN checklist is the de-facto reference cited by Apple/Safari/Chrome/WCAG documentation and is the FIRST thing accessibility auditors check. **Why this is the MDN-AUTHORITATIVE-MOBILE-ACCESSIBILITY-MINIMUM-BASELINE dimension distinct from A-042 (WCAG 2.2 AAA conformance — AAA is the strictest tier, not the MINIMUM baseline) and A-049 (Game Accessibility Guidelines BASIC tier — game-specific guidelines, not the web-baseline) and A-048 (EU EAA one-year enforcement — statutory regulation not checklist)**: A-042 is AAA-strict; A-049 is game-specific; A-048 is regulation; A-051 is the MDN-AUTHORITATIVE-WEB-MOBILE-MINIMUM that GameZipper should verify against as the FIRST accessibility gate — before AAA (A-042), before game-specific (A-049), before regulatory (A-048). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper games using score displays, HUD text, color-coded state indicators (red=damaged, green=full-health) MUST meet the 4.5:1 / 3:1 contrast ratios AND provide non-color cues (text label, icon) — many casual-game HUDs fail this baseline; (b) the GameZipper QA methodology must include a programmatic contrast-check (e.g., Chrome DevTools Lighthouse Accessibility audit, or `getComputedStyle` + WCAG formula) on every text element; (c) the BROADER PATTERN — accessibility baseline as a CI gate — means GameZipper should add an accessibility-check step to the pre-commit hook (Lighthouse a11y audit with min-score 90); (d) the COLOR-MULTI-MODAL-CONVEYANCE rule specifically: any GameZipper game state conveyed by color (health bar, status indicator, danger zone) MUST also convey via text/icon/animation — failing this is the #1 WCAG 2.x AA mobile failure in casual games per the MDN 2026 refresh; (e) MDN is the AUTHORITATIVE reference for legal-defensibility — citing "we conform to MDN mobile accessibility checklist" is a stronger defense than "we conform to WCAG 2.1 AA" because MDN distills the actionable minimum. **Test**: (1) for every GameZipper game, run Lighthouse Accessibility audit on the game page — score must be >=90; (2) programmatic contrast-check on every text element (HUD, score, instructions) — verify >=4.5:1 normal, >=3:1 large; (3) for every color-coded state (health, status, danger), verify there is ALSO a text/icon/animation cue; (4) document any failures in the GameZipper accessibility-issue tracker; (5) verify the same on Safari (Lighthouse is Chromium-only — use Safari Web Inspector Accessibility Audit as second source).


## CATEGORY APPENDIX: R229 New Test Cases (v1.110.0)

> **Dynamic Test Intelligence cron — Cycle 2026-06-29 (late morning)**. 9 new test cases synthesized from late-June 2026 industry research. Search engines: SearXNG (primary) returned 404 for all batch queries; MiniMax (fallback) returned strong coverage of HTML-in-Canvas proposal / iOS 27 Safari AI tab features / Apple April 2026 long-tail security update / WebGPU mobile baseline / Blazor WASM patterns. All 9 cases verified distinct from existing library v1.0.0 -> v1.109.0 (checked against C-162 / S-225 / B-153 / P-99 / G-165 / W-223 / A-051 / PWA-3 et al).

- [C-163] **[P2]** HTML-in-Canvas Experimental Proposal — `drawElementImage` / `layoutsubtree` API Class (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_92969dba69c15252 量子位 April 12 2026; blog.csdn.net/weixin_41961749/article/details/160449948 June 7 2026; sohu.com/a/1008636326_122066678 April 12 2026) — A new W3C-track proposal introduces the ability to render live HTML/DOM subtrees directly INTO a `<canvas>` via a `drawElementImage()` / `layoutsubtree()` API surface, letting developers "control web elements like pixels" inside a Canvas render context. The 量子位 report documents production-grade demos (point-to-select -> instant shatter/fragment visual effects on arbitrary DOM regions) proving the API is moving from concept to implementation. **Why this is the HTML-IN-CANVAS-EXPERIMENTAL-API-CLASS dimension distinct from C-159 (Safari 26 native SVG rendering — static SVG asset rendering, not live-DOM-into-canvas) and G-165 (generic canvas resize — layout not content compositing) and C-162 (WASM 2.0 parity — compute not rendering)**: C-159 is static SVG; G-165 is resize; C-162 is compute; C-163 is the LIVE-DOM-TO-CANVAS-COMPOSITING dimension where a DOM element's rendered output (text, images, interactive widgets) becomes a Canvas texture source. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) once shipped, GameZipper games could render HTML UI overlays (scoreboards, chat, ads) directly into the game Canvas — eliminating the DOM-over-Canvas z-index/pointer-event coordination problems that currently plague HUD overlays; (b) BUT Canvas-rendered HTML is NO LONGER accessible to screen readers / VoiceOver (Canvas pixels are invisible to AT) — any GameZipper feature adopting HTML-in-Canvas MUST mirror the content into an off-screen accessible DOM node with `aria-live`; (c) the screenshot/share feature becomes trivial (single `canvas.toDataURL()` captures the entire composed scene including HTML UI); (d) the QA methodology must add a "HTML-in-Canvas accessibility audit" — verify that any Canvas-composited HTML has an accessible-text mirror; (e) the API is EXPERIMENTAL (behind origin-trial / flag in 2026) so GameZipper MUST feature-detect (`if (ctx.drawElementImage)` not assume availability); (f) pointer-event hit-testing changes — clicks on Canvas-composited HTML may not route to the underlying DOM element, requiring manual hit-region mapping via `addHitRegion()`. **Test**: (1) feature-detect `typeof CanvasRenderingContext2D.prototype.drawElementImage === 'function'`; (2) if available, render a test DOM subtree into canvas and verify pixel output matches a reference screenshot; (3) verify screen-reader can NOT read the Canvas-composited content (confirming the accessibility-mirror requirement); (4) verify pointer events on the Canvas-composited region route correctly (or document the hit-region mapping needed); (5) verify `canvas.toDataURL()` captures the composited HTML; (6) document the feature-detection guard pattern for GameZipper's progressive-enhancement strategy.

- [W-224] **[P2]** Safari iOS/macOS 27 Apple-Intelligence AI Auto-Organize Tabs — Silent Tab Regrouping Breaks Game Session/Visibility Assumptions (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_0556a00a1d430852 May 10 2026 古尔曼; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_6676a2cb3ca28552 June 13 2026; sohu.com/a/1021216335_362225 May 11 2026; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2846a27048114952 June 9 2026) — Safari in iOS 27 / macOS 27 Golden Gate introduces Apple-Intelligence-driven AUTOMATIC TAB GROUPING: the browser analyzes open tabs' content/theme and silently reorganizes them into topic groups (e.g., "shopping" vs "travel planning") without user action. The macOS 27 Safari adds a "智能整理" (Smart Organize) toolbar button for one-click AI grouping. CRITICALLY, this is a NEW BROWSER-INITIATED-DOM-EVENT-CLASS: when Safari moves a GameZipper game tab into an AI-created group, the tab may fire `visibilitychange` (if the move causes a brief hide) or invalidate the `document.visibilityState` assumptions that GameZipper pause-on-blur / rAF-lifecycle / audio-autoplay-gate logic depends on. **Why this is the SAFARI-27-AI-TAB-REGROUPING-VISIBILITY-EVENT-INTRUSION dimension distinct from W-222 (iOS 27 4x6 widget — widget size, not tab lifecycle) and W-223 (Chrome 140 PRT incognito IP anon — privacy not tab lifecycle) and C-160 (iOS 27 split-screen windowing — windowing not tab grouping)**: W-222 is widget; W-223 is privacy; C-160 is windowing; W-224 is the AI-DRIVEN-TAB-SILENT-REGROUPING dimension where a third party (Apple Intelligence) reorganizes the player's tabs without consent, firing unexpected DOM events on the GameZipper page. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper pause-on-blur logic (`document.addEventListener('visibilitychange', pauseGame)`) may FALSE-TRIGGER when Safari moves the tab into a group, pausing the game mid-play even though the user didn't switch away; (b) the rAF lifecycle (`cancelAnimationFrame` on hide, resume on show) may break if the AI regroup fires rapid hide/show pairs; (c) WebAudio autoplay policy re-requires a user gesture after a visibilitychange — if the AI regroup fires visibilitychange, background music may silently stop and not resume; (d) the QA methodology must add a "Safari 27 AI tab regroup simulation" — programmatically fire synthetic visibilitychange pairs and verify the game survives without losing state; (e) the BROADER PATTERN — browser AI agents modifying the user's tab/session state — means GameZipper must treat visibilitychange as UNRELIABLE and add a debounce + state-reconciliation layer (e.g., only pause after visibilitychange has been 'hidden' for >2s). **Test**: (1) on Safari 27 beta, open a GameZipper game in one tab alongside 5+ unrelated tabs; (2) trigger "Smart Organize"; (3) verify the game tab is moved into an AI-created group; (4) verify the game did NOT auto-pause mid-play (or if it did, that it auto-resumes correctly); (5) verify background audio continues or gracefully resumes; (6) verify no game state (score, level, timer) is lost; (7) add a synthetic visibilitychange-pair test (hide->show within 500ms) to the GameZipper QA suite and verify survival.

- [S-226] **[P1]** Apple April 2026 Emergency Security Update Wave for iOS 15-26 — LONG-TAIL-DEVICE-SUPPORT-POLICY Baseline Floor (donews.com/news/detail/8/6511560.html April 15 2026) — On April 15 2026 Apple pushed EMERGENCY security updates spanning iOS 15 through iOS 26 simultaneously, patching web-attack vulnerabilities across a FOUR-YEAR device span. Apple explicitly stated iOS 13/14 users MUST upgrade and offered free in-store upgrade service, but iOS 15-26 devices received the patch in-place. This establishes that Apple's LONG-TAIL security support now covers iOS 15 as the effective floor (devices from ~2021 still receiving patches in 2026), NOT just "current minus 1 or 2". **Why this is the APPLE-LONG-TAIL-IOS-SECURITY-SUPPORT-FLOOR-IOS-15 dimension distinct from S-225 (double WebKit CVE concurrent active exploitation — specific CVE, not support-policy floor) and S-223 (Safari 26.5 20-CVE batch — single release, not multi-year-span) and S-222 (Ivanti EPMM MDM CVE — different vendor/surface)**: S-225 is a specific CVE; S-223 is a single release; S-222 is MDM; S-226 is the SUPPORT-POLICY-FLOOR dimension that defines the MINIMUM iOS version GameZipper must continue testing against — iOS 15 (released 2021) is still in Apple's security-patch scope as of April 2026. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's minimum-supported-iOS-version policy MUST be iOS 15 (not iOS 17/18/19) — real players on iPhone 6s/7/8/SE2020 running iOS 15 are still in Apple's security scope and still visiting GameZipper; (b) the QA device matrix MUST include an iOS 15 device (or Xcode iOS 15 simulator) — GameZipper games using APIs newer than iOS 15 (e.g., Web Push, Push API, certain CSS features) MUST have graceful fallback or a soft-warning; (c) the April 2026 wave signals Apple will continue patching iOS 15 for the foreseeable future, so the floor is DURABLE — GameZipper should not raise the minimum above iOS 15 without explicit data; (d) the BROADER PATTERN — vendor long-tail security support — means GameZipper's browser-support policy must track the vendor's patch floor, not the "current version"; (e) GameZipper analytics should report the iOS-version distribution to confirm the iOS 15 floor is real (if <0.1% traffic, the floor can be raised). **Test**: (1) check GameZipper analytics for iOS 15 / 16 traffic share over last 30 days; (2) if >0.5%, add an iOS 15 Safari device (or Xcode simulator) to the QA matrix; (3) load 5 representative GameZipper games on iOS 15 Safari — document any API-not-supported errors; (4) for any unsupported API, add feature-detection + graceful fallback; (5) document the iOS 15 floor in the GameZipper browser-support policy; (6) re-evaluate the floor quarterly against Apple's patch-scope announcements.

- [B-154] **[P3]** Chrome iOS 149+ Lock-Screen Notification for Cross-Device Synced Tabs — Game-Title/URL Preview Leak (itunes.apple.com/cn/app/id535886823 Chrome App Store release notes June 10 2026, v149.0.7827.137) — Chrome iOS 149 introduced a feature where tabs received from other devices (Chrome desktop "Send to your devices" / Handoff) trigger a LOCK-SCREEN NOTIFICATION showing the tab's title and URL preview. CRITICALLY, when a player sends a GameZipper game link from desktop to their iPhone, the lock-screen notification preview displays the game title (e.g., "Bubble Shooter — GameZipper") and a URL snippet — visible to anyone glancing at the locked phone (bystander, coworker, family member). **Why this is the CHROME-IOS-CROSS-DEVICE-TAB-LOCK-SCREEN-PREVIEW-LEAK dimension distinct from B-153 (Chrome 150 Wallet autofill PII misattribution — autofill not notification) and B-152 (Chrome 148 bookmark bar resurrection — viewport not privacy) and S-225 (WebKit CVE — security vuln not UX privacy)**: B-153 is autofill PII; B-152 is viewport; S-225 is vuln; B-154 is the LOCK-SCREEN-NOTIFICATION-PREVIEW-LEAK dimension where the player's game-playing activity is broadcast on the lock screen without their explicit consent at send-time. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) the lock-screen preview may reveal a player's game activity to bystanders — a privacy concern for players who share devices or are in public/work settings; (b) the game TITLE in the `<title>` tag becomes the notification preview text — GameZipper should ensure titles are not embarrassing or sensitive (current titles like "Bubble Shooter" are benign, but future adult-themed games would be a concern); (c) the URL preview may leak the game slug which could be a discovery/SEO signal competitors could scrape; (d) the QA methodology must verify Chrome iOS 149+ behavior: send a GameZipper tab from desktop, observe the lock-screen notification, verify the preview text is appropriate; (e) the BROADER PATTERN — cross-device sync surfaces activity on lock screens — means GameZipper should set `<meta name="theme-color">` and a clean `<title>` so the notification preview is branded correctly; (f) players concerned about privacy can disable Chrome's cross-device sync, but GameZipper cannot control that. **Test**: (1) on Chrome desktop (signed in), open a GameZipper game page; (2) use "Send to your devices" -> select an iPhone running Chrome iOS 149+; (3) observe the iPhone lock screen; (4) verify the notification preview shows the game title and URL; (5) confirm the title is appropriate/benign (not sensitive); (6) verify the notification tap opens the correct game page; (7) document the privacy implication in the GameZipper privacy policy.

- [P-100] **[P2]** WebGPU Mobile Baseline — Chrome 121+ Default-Enabled on Android 12+ Qualcomm/ARM GPU (sohu.com/a/753242250_121124365 Jan 21 2024; developer.chrome.com/blog/new-in-webgpu-121 baseline through 2026) — Chrome 121 (Jan 2024) shipped WebGPU DEFAULT-ENABLED on Android 12+ devices with Qualcomm Adreno or ARM Mali GPUs, with Google's plan to expand to Android 11. By mid-2026, the majority of Android 12+ devices in GameZipper's audience now have WebGPU available by default — establishing WebGPU as a REAL MOBILE rendering baseline, not a desktop-only experiment. Google reported WebGPU reduces JS graphics workload and improves ML inference 3x+ vs WebGL on these mobile GPUs. **Why this is the WEBGPU-MOBILE-DEFAULT-ENABLED-ANDROID-12-QUALCOMM-ARM-GPU-BASELINE dimension distinct from P-096 (WebGPU/WebGL hybrid coexistence — production pattern, not the device-tier-availability dimension) and P-068 (WebGPU streaming buffers — pattern within WebGPU, not availability) and C-162 (WASM 2.0 parity — compute not graphics)**: P-096 is coexistence pattern; P-068 is intra-WebGPU pattern; C-162 is compute; P-100 is the DEVICE-TIER-AVAILABILITY dimension defining which real mobile devices in GameZipper's audience now have WebGPU by default. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper WebGPU games now have a REAL mobile audience (Android 12+ with Qualcomm/ARM GPU = the majority of mid-range+ Android phones in 2026) — the QA matrix MUST include a real WebGPU-capable Android device (Pixel 6+, Samsung S21+, OnePlus 9+); (b) the mobile WebGPU path may have DIFFERENT performance characteristics than desktop WebGPU (mobile GPUs have lower fill-rate, less VRAM, thermal throttling) — games must adapt (lower resolution, simpler shaders, particle-count scaling); (c) the ML inference 3x+ speedup claim means on-device AI features (image recognition, voice) are now viable on mobile, not just desktop; (d) GameZipper QA must verify WebGPU games run on a THERMALLY-CONSTRAINED device for 10+ minutes without throttling-induced frame drops; (e) the BROADER PATTERN — mobile WebGPU reaching default-enabled status — means GameZipper can ship WebGPU-first games with WebGL fallback, not WebGL-first; (f) the device-fragmentation risk: older Android (10/11) and budget GPUs (PowerVR, Mali-G52 and below) may NOT have WebGPU — the fallback path is still required. **Test**: (1) identify 3 representative WebGPU-capable Android devices (Pixel 7, Samsung S23, OnePlus 11); (2) load 3 WebGPU GameZipper games on each; (3) verify WebGPU context initializes (`navigator.gpu !== undefined` and `adapter.requestAdapter()` succeeds); (4) run a 10-minute sustained-play session and verify no thermal-throttling frame drops (sustained >=30fps); (5) on a non-WebGPU Android (Android 10 or budget GPU), verify the WebGL fallback renders correctly; (6) benchmark ML inference (if any AI feature) WebGPU vs WebGL on mobile — confirm the 3x+ speedup holds.

- [G-166] **[P2]** WASM Game Loading-State Async-Disposable Pattern (blog.csdn.net/AlgoFun/article/details/160335021 April 20 2026 — Blazor WASM + WebGPU real-time rendering interview-prep, documenting the 2026-recommended async-disposable pattern) — The 2026 production pattern for WASM game initialization adopts the `IAsyncDisposable` / `[Symbol.asyncDispose]` pattern for loading-state lifecycle management: a `LoadingScope` wrapper tracks async resource acquisition (WASM module fetch, GPU adapter request, texture upload) and ensures deterministic cleanup on early-exit or error, preventing the "half-initialized game" state where the WASM module loaded but the GPU context failed, leaving orphaned resources. **Why this is the WASM-GAME-LOADING-ASYNC-DISPOSABLE-LIFECYCLE-PATTERN dimension distinct from G-165 (generic canvas resize — layout not resource lifecycle) and P-099 (WASM-Whisper IndexedDB cache — model cache pattern, not loading-scope lifecycle) and C-162 (WASM 2.0 parity — perf not lifecycle)**: G-165 is layout; P-099 is model cache; C-162 is perf; G-166 is the LOADING-SCOPE-RESOURCE-LIFECYCLE dimension covering the async init -> cleanup path that prevents resource leaks on init failure. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper WASM game (Unity/Godot export, Rust-WASM) that fetches a large module + initializes GPU/audio context MUST wrap the init in an async-disposable scope — if the player navigates away mid-init, the partially-loaded WASM module and half-open GPU adapter must be cleaned up, not leaked; (b) the current GameZipper games (mostly JS, not WASM) don't have this problem yet, but any future WASM export will; (c) the QA methodology must add a "navigate-away-during-WASM-init" test — start loading a WASM game, navigate away after 500ms, verify no console errors and no leaked GPU adapters (check `performance.memory` or chrome://gpu); (d) the async-disposable pattern also enables graceful game-restart (dispose old scope -> create new) without page reload; (e) the BROADER PATTERN — async resource lifecycle in games — applies beyond WASM to any game using WebGPU/WebAudio/IndexedDB init sequences; (f) the `Symbol.asyncDispose` (ES2024+) and `using`/`await using` declarations are the modern JS equivalent of C# `IAsyncDisposable` — GameZipper should adopt `await using` for scoped resources. **Test**: (1) for any WASM GameZipper game, wrap init in an async-disposable scope; (2) start loading, navigate away after 500ms, verify no leaked GPU adapters (re-open and check adapter count stable); (3) verify no console errors on early-exit; (4) test graceful restart (dispose + re-init) without page reload — verify state is clean; (5) verify the pattern works on Chrome 149+ (supports `await using`) and Safari 26+ (may need transpilation).

- [C-164] **[P3]** Safari 27 Natural-Language Browser-Extension Generation — Player-Generated Extensions Inject Arbitrary JS into GameZipper Pages (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2846a27048114952 June 9 2026; sohu.com/a/1021216335_362225 May 11 2026) — Safari 27 (iOS/macOS) introduces Apple-Intelligence-powered NATURAL-LANGUAGE EXTENSION GENERATION: a user can type a natural-language description ("block all ads on this site", "auto-click the play button", "change all colors to dark mode") and Safari generates + installs a custom browser extension that executes that behavior on matching pages. CRITICALLY, this is a NEW PLAYER-GENERATED-CODE-EXECUTION-ON-GAMEZIPPER-PAGES vector: a player can generate an extension that injects arbitrary JS into gamezipper.com pages — potentially breaking ad attribution (Monetag/AdSense), cheating at games (auto-play, score-inflation), or breaking the game-footer/related-games navigation. **Why this is the SAFARI-27-NL-EXTENSION-GENERATION-PLAYER-INJECTED-JS dimension distinct from S-224 (CSP bypass via window.open — attacker vector, not player-self-generated) and C-163 (HTML-in-Canvas — platform API, not player-injected code) and W-221 (external browser-agent frameworks — automation tooling, not AI-generated extensions)**: S-224 is attacker CSP-bypass; C-163 is platform API; W-221 is agent tooling; C-164 is the PLAYER-SELF-GENERATED-EXTENSION dimension where the player intentionally (or accidentally) generates an extension that modifies GameZipper's behavior — a vector that did not previously exist at scale (writing extensions required dev skill; now it requires a sentence). **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper cannot prevent player-generated extensions (they run with user consent), but MUST detect when an extension has modified the page (e.g., ad containers removed, game-footer hidden, score DOM tampered) and degrade gracefully; (b) ad-attribution integrity: a player-generated "block ads" extension may remove Monetag/AdSense iframes, breaking revenue — GameZipper should detect ad-container removal and show a soft "ads support free games" message (not a hard block); (c) anti-cheat: for any competitive GameZipper feature (leaderboards, tournaments), a player-generated "auto-play" extension could inflate scores — GameZipper must validate scores server-side, not trust client DOM; (d) the QA methodology must add a "Safari 27 NL-extension simulation" — inject common extension behaviors (ad-block, auto-click, dark-mode-forced) and verify GameZipper degrades gracefully; (e) the BROADER PATTERN — AI-lowered-barrier-to-browser-modification — means GameZipper must assume a growing fraction of players run AI-generated modifications; (f) GameZipper should NOT rely on client-side DOM integrity for any security/critical path. **Test**: (1) in Safari 27 beta, generate a "block all ads on this site" extension via natural language; (2) navigate to a GameZipper game page; (3) verify the extension removes Monetag/AdSense iframes; (4) verify GameZipper detects the removal and shows a graceful soft-message (not a crash); (5) generate an "auto-click the play button" extension; (6) verify it doesn't break the game (or if it does, document the failure); (7) for any leaderboard, verify scores are server-validated and a client-side auto-play extension cannot inflate them.

- [A-052] **[P3]** Safari 27 Apple-Intelligence Page Summarization on Minimal-Text Game Pages — AI-Generated Misleading Descriptions (apple.com/macos/ macOS 27 Golden Gate; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2846a27048114952 June 9 2026) — Safari 27 ships Apple-Intelligence-powered PAGE SUMMARIZATION: the browser reads the page's DOM text and generates a summary the user sees in the tab/reader/spotlight. CRITICALLY, GameZipper game pages are CANVAS-HEAVY with MINIMAL DOM TEXT (the game logic renders to canvas, not DOM) — so the AI summarizer has very little text to work with and may generate NONSENSICAL, MISLEADING, or EMPTY summaries that harm game discovery and player perception. For example, a canvas-only puzzle game page whose only DOM text is "GameZipper — Loading..." and the footer links may get summarized as "A loading screen with navigation links" — completely misrepresenting the game. **Why this is the SAFARI-27-AI-SUMMARIZATION-MINIMAL-TEXT-CANVAS-GAME-MISREPRESENTATION dimension distinct from A-050 (VoiceOver curtain-screen audio-only — assistive-tech, not AI-summarization) and A-051 (MDN mobile a11y contrast — visual, not AI-content) and C-164 (NL extension generation — code injection, not content summarization)**: A-050 is assistive-tech; A-051 is visual contrast; C-164 is code injection; A-052 is the AI-CONTENT-SUMMARIZATION dimension where a third-party AI misrepresents the game to the player before they even play it. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper MUST ensure every game page has a RICH, DESCRIPTIVE DOM text layer (not just canvas) — a hidden `<section>` with a 2-3 sentence game description, controls explanation, and genre tag that the AI summarizer can read; (b) the `<meta name="description">` and Open Graph tags are the PRIMARY source for the summarizer — they must be accurate and compelling; (c) the QA methodology must add a "Safari 27 AI summary audit" — open each game page, trigger the AI summary, verify the summary accurately describes the game (not "loading screen" or empty); (d) the BROADER PATTERN — AI summarizers as a discovery surface — means GameZipper's SEO strategy must now optimize for AI-summarizer readability, not just search-engine crawlers; (e) canvas-only games are the WORST case — they have near-zero extractable text; (f) the summarizer may also pull from `aria-label` / `alt` text — GameZipper should add descriptive aria-labels to the canvas element. **Test**: (1) for 5 representative canvas-heavy GameZipper games, open in Safari 27 beta; (2) trigger the AI page summary (tab preview / reader / spotlight); (3) verify the summary accurately describes the game (genre, objective, controls); (4) if summary is empty/misleading, add a hidden descriptive `<section>` + meta description + canvas aria-label; (5) re-test until summary is accurate; (6) document the AI-summary-optimization checklist for all future game pages.

- [PWA-4] **[P2]** Safari 27 AI Tab Grouping Breaks PWA "Continue Playing" Single-Tab Session Assumption (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_6676a2cb3ca28552 June 13 2026; sohu.com/a/1021216335_362225 May 11 2026) — When Safari 27's AI auto-grouping moves a tab that has an INSTALLED PWA service-worker association (or an active service-worker-controlled session) into an AI-created tab group, the service worker's `clients.claim()` scope and the PWA's session continuity may be affected. CRITICALLY, the GameZipper PWA's "continue playing" feature (which relies on the service worker caching the last-played game state in Cache API / IndexedDB and restoring it when the user reopens the PWA) assumes a STABLE single-tab session — but AI tab grouping can clone/move the tab, creating a SECOND service-worker-controlled client that competes for the same IndexedDB game-state record, causing a write-write conflict or stale-read. **Why this is the SAFARI-27-AI-TAB-GROUP-PWA-SERVICE-WORKER-SESSION-CONFLICT dimension distinct from W-224 (AI tab regroup visibility events — DOM events, not service-worker session) and PWA-3 (Firefox 142 PWA installability — install, not session) and PWA-1 (general installability — install, not AI-disruption)**: W-224 is DOM visibility events; PWA-3 is Firefox install; PWA-1 is general install; PWA-4 is the AI-TAB-GROUP-INDUCED-SERVICE-WORKER-CLIENT-CONFLICT dimension where the AI creates a duplicate client that races on shared state. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) if GameZipper has a PWA with "continue playing" (or plans one), the service worker must handle MULTIPLE concurrent clients writing to the same IndexedDB game-state record — use a last-write-wins with timestamp, or a client-id-scoped state; (b) the QA methodology must add a "Safari 27 AI tab group PWA session" test — install the PWA, start a game, trigger AI grouping (which may duplicate the tab), verify both tabs don't corrupt the shared state; (c) the BROADER PATTERN — browser AI agents creating duplicate clients — means any service-worker state must be multi-client-safe (assume N clients, not 1); (d) the `BroadcastChannel` API can synchronize state across the duplicate clients in real-time; (e) if GameZipper does NOT have a PWA yet, this is a forward-looking test case to apply when one is built. **Test**: (1) if GameZipper has a PWA, install it on Safari 27 beta; (2) start a game, reach a mid-game state; (3) trigger AI tab grouping that moves/duplicates the PWA tab; (4) verify both tabs (original + duplicate) do not corrupt the IndexedDB game-state; (5) close one tab, verify the other retains correct state; (6) if state corruption occurs, implement last-write-wins + BroadcastChannel sync; (7) if GameZipper has no PWA, document this as a future-test-case for when one is built.


## CATEGORY APPENDIX: R230 New Test Cases (v1.111.0)

> **Dynamic Test Intelligence cron — Cycle 2026-06-29 (afternoon)**. 8 new test cases synthesized from late-June 2026 industry research. Search engines: SearXNG (primary) returned 404 for all batch queries; MiniMax (fallback) returned strong coverage of CVE-2026-5281 Chrome Dawn WebGPU UAF / CVE-2026-2441 CSS @property+paint() exfiltration / Apple BSI mechanism / iOS 26 Liquid Glass / CVE-2026-20643 WebKit navigation API. All 8 cases verified distinct from existing library v1.0.0 -> v1.110.0 (checked against S-224 / S-225 / S-226 / B-153 / B-154 / C-163 / C-164 / W-224 / P-100 / G-166 / A-052 et al).

- [S-227] **[P1]** CVE-2026-5281 — Chrome Dawn WebGPU Use-After-Free, 4th In-The-Wild Chrome 0day of 2026 (toutiao.com/article/7624119670857122304 April 2 2026) — Google confirmed CVE-2026-5281 is EXPLOITED IN THE WILD. The vulnerability is a use-after-free weakness in **Dawn**, the cross-platform implementation of the WebGPU standard used by the Chromium project. An attacker who compromises a WebGPU shader or buffer can trigger a Dawn-layer memory-corruption leading to arbitrary code execution chain, escaping the renderer sandbox via the GPU process. **Why this is the CHROMIUM-DAWN-WEBGPU-USE-AFTER-FREE-IN-WILD-EXPLOITATION dimension distinct from S-225 (double WebKit UAF — different engine: WebKit not Chromium-Dawn) and S-226 (Apple long-tail iOS support floor — policy not specific CVE) and P-100 (WebGPU mobile baseline — availability not vulnerability)**: S-225 is WebKit; S-226 is policy; P-100 is availability; S-227 is the CHROMIUM-DAWN-LAYER-SPECIFIC dimension where the WebGPU implementation (not the API surface) has an in-wild UAF — meaning any GameZipper game that uses WebGPU (or loads a third-party WebGPU ad/visualization) on an unpatched Chrome is a potential RCE vector. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper players on Chrome versions prior to the Dawn patch are running a KNOWN-EXPLOITABLE device class when visiting any page that initializes a WebGPU context — the ad networks (Monetag/AdSense) could theoretically serve a malicious WebGPU payload; (b) GameZipper QA must add a minimum-Chrome-version check for WebGPU-enabled games and gracefully fall back to WebGL2/Canvas when the Chrome version is below the Dawn patch; (c) the broader pattern — WebGPU Dawn is now a PRIMARY exploitation target (4th Chrome 0day of 2026) — means GameZipper should treat WebGPU as a SECURITY-SENSITIVE surface, not just a performance feature; (d) verify: load a WebGPU game on Chrome pre-patch, confirm no crash; load on patched Chrome, confirm normal render; check navigator.gpu availability gating.

---

- [S-228] **[P1]** CVE-2026-2441 — CSS-Only Exfiltration via @property Registration + paint() Worklet Chaining (sitepoint.com/zero-day-css-cve-2026-2441-security-vulnerability March 20 2026; sitepoint.com/css-zero-day-cve-2026-2441-browser-sandbox-escape Feb 19 2026) — CVE-2026-2441 (CVSS 6.5 Medium) is a zero-day in Chrome's Blink rendering engine that allows attackers to STEAL SENSITIVE DOM CONTENT (CSRF tokens, hidden form values, attribute values) using PURE CSS — **no JavaScript execution required**. The exploit chains @property registration with paint() worklet and @import redirects plus attribute selectors to trigger sequential network requests to attacker-controlled servers, exfiltrating data character-by-character via CSS selector matching. **Why this is the CSS-ONLY-NO-JAVASCRIPT-DATA-EXFILTRATION-VIA-PROPERTY-AND-PAINT-WORKLET dimension distinct from S-224 (CSP bypass via window.open+document.write — requires JS, different vector) and S-227 (Dawn WebGPU UAF — GPU layer not CSS parser) and S-225 (WebKit UAF — memory corruption not data exfiltration)**: S-224 requires JS; S-227 is GPU; S-225 is memory; S-228 is the CSS-PARSER-AS-EXFILTRATION-CHANNEL dimension where the assumption "CSS is harmless" is PROVEN FALSE — a CSS file (loaded via @import or inline style) can read DOM attribute values and phone them home WITHOUT any JS. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper pages that load ANY third-party CSS (ad network stylesheets, Monetag/AdSense CSS, CDN-hosted frameworks) are potentially vulnerable if the CSS source is compromised — the CSS can read data-* attributes, hidden input values, and CSRF tokens from the DOM; (b) GameZipper CSP must include style-src restrictions (not just script-src) — a CSP that only restricts scripts but allows style-src wildcard leaves the S-228 vector open; (c) the QA methodology must add a CSS-exfiltration audit — verify no CSS rule uses attribute selectors that match sensitive attributes (input[value^="..."], [data-token], [name="csrf"]) combined with background-image: url(attacker.com/...) or @import; (d) verify GameZipper CSP style-src directive is restricted to trusted origins, not wildcard.

---

- [W-225] **[P2]** Apple Background Security Improvement (BSI) — Silent Update Mechanism Replacing Rapid Security Response (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_08569b9e6fa30652 March 18 2026; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_65569bcad1337152 March 20 2026) — Apple shipped the FIRST Background Security Improvement (BSI) update (version 26.3.1) for macOS Tahoe, iOS, and iPadOS, explicitly REPLACING the prior Rapid Security Response (RSR) mechanism. BSI updates are applied SILENTLY in the background without requiring user interaction or device restart, fixing WebKit cross-domain security vulnerabilities. This is a NEW UPDATE-MECHANISM-CLASS that changes the GameZipper QA assumptions: a player's Safari/WebKit version can change BETWEEN QA test runs WITHOUT the player explicitly updating, meaning a game that passed QA yesterday may encounter a different WebKit behavior today because BSI silently patched the engine overnight. **Why this is the APPLE-BSI-SILENT-BACKGROUND-UPDATE-MECHANISM-REPLACING-RSR dimension distinct from S-226 (Apple long-tail iOS 15 support floor — version-range policy, not update mechanism) and C-161 (Safari 26.4 191-bug-fix batch — explicit dot-release, not silent BSI) and S-223 (Safari 26.5 20-CVE batch — explicit release)**: S-226 is policy; C-161/S-223 are explicit releases; W-225 is the SILENT-UPDATE-MECHANISM dimension where the browser engine version changes without user action or visible notification — a QA-timing hazard. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper QA can no longer assume the player's Safari version is stable between sessions — BSI may silently change WebKit behavior between a player's morning and afternoon sessions; (b) bug reports from players may reference WebKit behaviors that changed via BSI overnight — QA must check the player's exact WebKit build (navigator.userAgentData or Safari version string) not just the major iOS version; (c) the QA methodology must add a BSI-version-aware step — record the exact Safari/WebKit build number (not just major version) in bug reports; (d) verify: GameZipper games survive a simulated BSI update (change WebKit feature flags between sessions) without state corruption.

---

- [C-165] **[P2]** iOS 26 Liquid Glass / Control Center Over-Transparency — Game UI Compositing Legibility Regression (tenorshare.tw/ios-update-problems.html June 8 2026; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news multiple iOS 26 bug reports June 2026) — iOS 26 introduced the Liquid Glass (液态玻璃) visual design language with heavy translucency/blur effects. Users report the Control Center becomes SO transparent that it merges with the home screen, reducing clarity. For browser games, this is a COMPOSITING-LAYER-TRANSPARENCY regression: when a GameZipper game canvas is rendered behind iOS 26's translucent system UI overlays (Control Center, Notification Center, multi-tasking view), the game's HUD/score/text may become ILLEGIBLE because the translucency blends game pixels with whatever is behind/above the canvas. **Why this is the IOS-26-LIQUID-GLASS-TRANSLUCENT-COMPOSITING-LEGIBILITY-REGRESSION dimension distinct from C-160 (iOS 27 split-screen windowing — windowing axis not translucency) and A-051 (MDN contrast ratio — static contrast, not dynamic translucency-over-content) and C-161 (Safari 26.4 bug batch — explicit bug fixes, not new visual design language)**: C-160 is windowing; A-051 is static contrast; C-161 is bug fixes; C-165 is the NEW-VISUAL-DESIGN-LANGUAGE-TRANSLUCENCY dimension where iOS system chrome overlays render translucently OVER game content, reducing effective contrast dynamically. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper games with light/white HUD text on light backgrounds will become illegible when iOS 26 translucent Control Center overlays them — the QA must test games with Control Center / Notification Center partially visible; (b) games using position: fixed HUD overlays may z-index-conflict with iOS 26 system translucent layers; (c) the QA methodology must add a translucent-overlay legibility test — trigger iOS 26 Control Center swipe (half-open), verify game HUD remains readable; (d) verify the Reduce Transparency accessibility setting restores legibility.

---

- [B-155] **[P2]** CVE-2026-20643 — WebKit Navigation API Vulnerability (First BSI Patch Target) (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_65569bcad1337152 March 20 2026) — CVE-2026-20643, discovered by security researcher Thomas Espach, affects the WebKit browser engine's NAVIGATION API — the API surface governing window.location, history.pushState, history.replaceState, and the NavigationObserver interface. This was the FIRST vulnerability patched via Apple's new BSI (Background Security Improvement) mechanism. A navigation-API vulnerability can allow a malicious page to manipulate the browser's history stack, spoof the URL bar, or intercept navigation events — directly relevant to any GameZipper feature that uses history.pushState for SPA-style routing or window.location for game-deep-linking. **Why this is the WEBKIT-NAVIGATION-API-VULNERABILITY-FIRST-BSI-TARGET dimension distinct from S-225 (WebKit UAF in content processing — different API surface: content not navigation) and W-225 (BSI mechanism itself — mechanism not specific CVE) and S-227 (Chrome Dawn — different engine entirely)**: S-225 is content-processing; W-225 is the mechanism; S-227 is Chrome; B-155 is the WEBKIT-NAVIGATION-API-SPECIFIC dimension where history/location manipulation can spoof URLs or intercept navigation — a DIRECT attack surface for GameZipper's deep-linking and SPA routing. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper games using history.pushState() for level/scene routing (common in puzzle games with shareable level URLs) may be vulnerable to history-injection on unpatched Safari <26.3.1 — an attacker could inject fake history entries that spoof the game URL; (b) window.location redirects (e.g., game-over to share page) may be interceptable; (c) the QA methodology must verify: (1) history.pushState on unpatched Safari doesn't allow cross-origin URL spoofing, (2) game deep-links (/game/#level=5) survive navigation-API manipulation, (3) the popstate event fires correctly after BSI update.

---

- [P-101] **[P2]** WebGPU Dawn Layer Crash-Signature Detection — Verify GameZipper WebGPU Games Survive Dawn UAF Exploit Attempts (derived from CVE-2026-5281 S-227; developer.chrome.com/blog/new-in-webgpu Dawn architecture docs) — Following CVE-2026-5281 (S-227), the Dawn WebGPU implementation layer is now a CONFIRMED in-the-wild exploitation target. GameZipper games that initialize a WebGPU context (navigator.gpu.requestAdapter()) on an unpatched Chrome version are running on a known-vulnerable Dawn layer. The QA methodology must verify that GameZipper WebGPU games (a) gracefully detect and fall back when Dawn crashes (WebGPU context loss), and (b) do not propagate Dawn crash signatures to the player as an unhandled exception. **Why this is the WEBGPU-DAWN-CRASH-SIGNATURE-DETECTION-AND-FALLBACK dimension distinct from P-100 (WebGPU mobile baseline — availability, not crash handling) and P-096 (WebGPU/WebGL coexistence — rendering strategy, not crash resilience) and P-069 (WebGPU 39x draw-call throughput — performance, not stability)**: P-100 is availability; P-096 is strategy; P-069 is performance; P-101 is the CRASH-RESILIENCE-AND-FALLBACK dimension specific to the Dawn implementation layer. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) any GameZipper WebGPU game must register a gpuDevice.lost promise handler that falls back to WebGL2 or Canvas2D — NOT just log the error; (b) the QA methodology must simulate Dawn crash (via device.destroy() or WEBGPU_debug harness) and verify the game continues rendering via fallback; (c) verify no unhandled GPUDeviceLostInfo rejection reaches the player as a blank canvas; (d) the broader pattern — WebGPU is now a security-sensitive surface (S-227) — means crash-resilience is not optional.

---

- [G-167] **[P3]** CSS Houdini @property + paint() Worklet Adoption Guard — Verify No Exfiltration Vector If GameZipper Adopts CSS Houdini (derived from CVE-2026-2441 S-228; developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/@property Houdini docs) — CVE-2026-2441 (S-228) proved that CSS @property registration plus paint() worklet can be chained for no-JS data exfiltration. If GameZipper EVER adopts CSS Houdini (the @property API, paint() worklet, layout() worklet) for advanced visual effects (animated CSS custom properties, custom paint effects for game UI), the QA must verify the adoption does not introduce an exfiltration vector. **Why this is the CSS-HOUDINI-ADOPTION-SECURITY-GUARD dimension distinct from S-228 (the CVE itself — vulnerability, not adoption guard) and C-163 (HTML-in-Canvas — different experimental API) and G-166 (WASM async-disposable — resource lifecycle, not CSS security)**: S-228 is the vulnerability; C-163 is a different API; G-166 is resource lifecycle; G-167 is the PROACTIVE-ADOPTION-GUARD dimension — if/when GameZipper adopts CSS Houdini, verify the usage pattern cannot be chained for exfiltration. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) currently GameZipper does NOT use CSS Houdini (verified: no @property or paint() in the codebase as of June 2026), but future UI enhancements (animated gradient backgrounds, custom paint effects for game cards) may adopt it; (b) the QA methodology must add a pre-adoption security review: any PR introducing @property or paint() must be audited for exfiltration risk (attribute selectors plus external URL refs); (c) verify CSP style-src blocks external @import in worklet context; (d) the broader pattern — treat any CSS feature that can read DOM state (attribute selectors, @property, paint()) as security-sensitive.

---

- [A-053] **[P3]** iOS 26 Reduce Transparency Accessibility Setting — Verify Game UI/HUD Legibility Under Liquid-Glass Compositing (derived from C-165; apple.com iOS 26 accessibility docs; tenorshare.tw June 8 2026) — iOS 26 Liquid Glass design (C-165) introduces heavy translucency that reduces game UI legibility. Apple provides the Reduce Transparency (降低透明度) accessibility setting (Settings then Accessibility then Display and Text Size then Reduce Transparency) that DISABLES the translucency and restores solid backgrounds. The QA must verify GameZipper games render correctly BOTH with and without Reduce Transparency enabled, because players with visual impairments will have it ON (solid backgrounds) while typical players have it OFF (translucent system UI). **Why this is the IOS-26-REDUCE-TRANSPARENCY-ACCESSIBILITY-SETTING-LEGIBILITY-VERIFICATION dimension distinct from A-051 (MDN static contrast ratio — programmatic contrast, not OS-level translucency toggle) and C-165 (Liquid Glass regression — the problem, not the accessibility mitigation) and A-050 (VoiceOver curtain — audio-only, not visual translucency)**: A-051 is static contrast; C-165 is the problem; A-050 is audio; A-053 is the ACCESSIBILITY-SETTING-MITIGATION-VERIFICATION dimension — confirming the OS-provided accessibility toggle actually fixes the legibility issue for GameZipper games. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper games must be tested with iOS 26 Reduce Transparency BOTH ON and OFF — the game UI must be legible in both states; (b) if the game uses its own translucency (backdrop-filter: blur(), rgba() backgrounds), verify those also respect the Reduce Transparency preference (iOS does NOT auto-disable web CSS translucency — the game must check prefers-reduced-transparency media query); (c) the QA methodology must add @media (prefers-reduced-transparency: reduce) CSS that replaces translucent backgrounds with solid ones; (d) verify the media query is supported on iOS 26 Safari (check window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-transparency: reduce)').matches).


---

## v1.112.0 — R231 Dynamic Test Intelligence (2026-06-29)

**Version Note**: v1.112.0 = R231 Dynamic Test Intelligence cron — 8 new test cases from late-June 2026 industry research (June 29 2026). Search engine status: SearXNG (primary) returned 404 for all 5 batch queries; MiniMax fallback used successfully. Distinctness analysis performed against the full prior library (v1.0.0 → v1.111.0, prior 111 versions, ~900 test cases).

- [S-229] **[P1]** CVE-2026-32202 — Windows Shell 0-Click LNK NTLMv2 Credential Theft (APT28 / Fancy Bear, actively exploited in the wild; CISA KEV April 28 2026, FCEB patch deadline May 12 2026) — sohu.com/a/1020020937_114765 May 8 2026; blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/160645609 May 24 2026; blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/160753163 May 4 2026 (攻击链 Shell 0-Click → CVE-2026-33825 BlueHammer LPE → full control). The vulnerability is a ZERO-CLICK protection-mechanism-failure (CWE-693) in Windows Shell (shell32.dll, Explorer.exe): merely opening a folder containing a malicious .LNK shortcut file in Windows Explorer auto-triggers an icon-load request that leaks the victim's NTLMv2 credential hash to an attacker-controlled SMB server — NO user click required. Affecting all Windows 10 21H2+, Windows 11 21H2+, Windows Server. **Why this is the ZERO-CLICK-LNK-NTLMV2-CREDENTIAL-THEFT-VIA-FOLDER-OPEN dimension distinct from S-203 (NGINX Rift server-side RCE — different layer: server not client OS) and S-191 (Windows 11 kernel ProbeForWrite LPE via Chrome NtQuerySystemInformation — different Windows subsystem: kernel info-API not Shell) and S-222 (Ivanti EPMM MDM server RCE — different vector: server RCE not client credential theft)**: S-203 is server-side RCE; S-191 is kernel-info-API LPE; S-222 is MDM server takeover; S-229 is the CLIENT-OS-SHELL-ZERO-CLICK-CREDENTIAL-THEFT dimension where the mere act of viewing a folder in Explorer exfiltrates NTLM hashes — a credential-theft primitive that chains into pass-the-hash attacks against every authenticated service the victim can reach, including GameZipper admin/BI/analytics dashboards protected by NTLM/SSO. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper ops/admin team members on Windows who open a folder containing a malicious LNK (delivered via phishing, shared drive, or USB) silently leak their NTLMv2 hash to an attacker — this hash can then be used in pass-the-hash attacks against any Windows-authenticated GameZipper backend (BI dashboard, analytics, admin); (b) GameZipper must NOT use NTLM/NTLMv2 authentication on any internal service — enforce Kerberos or modern OAuth/federated auth only; (c) GameZipper Windows endpoint policy must block outbound SMB (port 445) to non-corporate IPs to prevent the LNK icon-load callback from reaching the attacker's SMB server; (d) the QA/ops must verify all Windows endpoints accessing GameZipper admin/BI have the April 14 2026 patch (KB) installed; (e) the broader pattern — zero-click credential theft primitives invalidate the assumption "the user didn't click anything, so they're safe" — GameZipper security training must explicitly warn that opening a folder (not clicking a file) can compromise credentials.

---

- [B-156] **[P2]** iOS 27 Siri Camera Mode Replacing Visual Intelligence — Camera Control Long-Press Gesture Remapping (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_0946a3cd29601952 June 25 2026 — "iOS 27相机应用新增Siri模式 视觉智能功能全面整合"; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2616a2f978c89352 June 15 2026 — "iOS 27将Siri摄像头模式整合进相机应用,大幅优化Camera Control体验"; new.qq.com/rain/a/20260615A05F0200 June 15 2026; sohu.com/a/1016449451_121956424 April 30 2026) — In iOS 18 and iOS 26, the Camera Control hardware button (on iPhone 16/17 with the capacitive side button) had a DUAL-LOGIC gesture: single-click launches the Camera app; long-press triggers "Visual Intelligence" (the Apple Intelligence feature that uses the camera to identify objects, look up restaurants, scan receipts). In iOS 27, the long-press gesture is REMAPPED: it now launches the new in-app "Siri Camera Mode" instead of the standalone Visual Intelligence overlay. The Siri Camera Mode is a full camera shooting mode (alongside Photo/Video/Portrait/Pano) embedded in the Camera app, with a new Apple-Intelligence-branded shutter button. **Why this is the IOS-27-CAMERA-CONTROL-LONG-PRESS-GESTURE-REMAPPING-VISUAL-INTELLIGENCE-TO-SIRI-CAMERA-MODE dimension distinct from C-160 (iOS 27 split-screen windowing — viewport, not gesture remapping) and W-224 (Safari 27 AI tab grouping — tab behavior, not hardware-button gesture) and B-150 (iOS 27 lock-screen AI wallpaper — lock screen, not camera)**: C-160 is viewport; W-224 is tab behavior; B-150 is lock screen; B-156 is the HARDWARE-BUTTON-GESTURE-SEMANTICS dimension where a physical button's long-press action changes meaning across iOS versions — relevant to GameZipper because any game that integrates with the Camera Control API (rare but possible for AR/scan-based games) or relies on the gesture for screenshots/visual-search must handle the remapping. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) if GameZipper EVER ships an AR/scan-based game (e.g., "scan a real-world object to unlock a game item"), the code that expected iOS 18/26 long-press → Visual Intelligence must be updated to handle iOS 27 long-press → Siri Camera Mode (different API, different UI flow, different intent-delivery mechanism); (b) the QA methodology must test camera-related GameZipper features on BOTH iOS 26 (Visual Intelligence gesture) and iOS 27 (Siri Camera Mode gesture) — the same physical gesture triggers different code paths; (c) the broader pattern — Apple is consolidating Visual Intelligence INTO the Camera app rather than keeping it as a separate overlay — this changes the API surface for any third-party camera-integration code.

---

- [S-230] **[P1]** cPanel & WHM Authentication Bypass — CVE-2026-41940 (CVSS 9.8), 44,000+ Hosts Compromised, SORRY Ransomware + Mirai Deployment (blog.csdn.net/weixin_41905135/article/details/160880815 May 22 2026 — "2026年5月第2周网络安全形势周报"; blog.csdn.net/weixin_45635831/article/details/160686133 May 1 2026 — "每日安全情报报告"; download.csdn.net/blog/column/11594790/160880815) — CVE-2026-41940 is a CVSS 9.8 authentication-bypass vulnerability in cPanel & WebHost Manager (WHM) versions 11.40 through 136.0.4. The attack uses CRLF injection to forge admin session files, allowing UNAUTHENTICATED remote attackers to impersonate any user including root. Shadow Server confirmed 44,000+ IPs compromised; Censys found 8,859 hosts with ".sorry" ransomware-extension artifacts. The attack chain: scan exposed cPanel ports (2083/2087/2095/2096) → CRLF-inject to forge root session → deploy SORRY ransomware (Go, encrypts files) → delete backups → deploy Mirai botnet variant "nuclear.x86" for DDoS. The exploit was in-the-wild since February 2026 with a 2-month patch gap. cPanel hosts ~70 million domains globally. **Why this is the HOSTING-CONTROL-PANEL-AUTH-BYPASS-RANSOMWARE-BOTNET-DEPLOYMENT-CHAIN dimension distinct from S-203 (NGINX Rift — web server binary, not hosting control panel) and S-222 (Ivanti EPMM — MDM server, not hosting panel) and S-229 (Windows Shell — client OS, not hosting infrastructure)**: S-203 is the web server; S-222 is MDM; S-229 is client OS; S-230 is the HOSTING-CONTROL-PANEL dimension — if GameZipper's hosting provider (or any future GameZipper self-hosted infrastructure) uses cPanel/WHM, this CVE is a direct existential threat: full root takeover, ransomware encryption of all game files, and botnet recruitment of the server. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper is currently hosted on GitHub Pages (not cPanel), so the direct exposure is ZERO as of June 2026 — BUT any future migration to a cPanel/WHM-based VPS/dedicated server (common for game backends, analytics, multiplayer servers) must verify cPanel version ≥ the patched release; (b) GameZipper ops must NEVER expose cPanel/WHM admin ports (2083/2087/2095/2096) to the public internet — restrict to VPN/bastion host; (c) if GameZipper uses any third-party service hosted on cPanel (analytics, ads, CDN origin), verify the provider patched CVE-2026-41940; (d) the broader pattern — hosting control panels are HIGH-VALUE targets because one compromise = thousands of domains — GameZipper vendor risk assessment must include hosting-panel-CVE tracking.

---

- [W-226] **[P2]** cPanel cPanel/WHM Hosting Control Panel Class — Vendor Surface For Future GameZipper Backend Hosting (derived from CVE-2026-41940 S-230; cpanel.net platform docs) — CVE-2026-41940 (S-230) demonstrated that hosting control panels (cPanel/WHM, Plesk, DirectAdmin, etc.) are a DISTINCT attack-surface class from web servers (NGINX/Apache), MDM servers (Ivanti), or client OSes (Windows). GameZipper is currently on GitHub Pages (no control panel), but any future backend (multiplayer, analytics, user accounts) may use a hosting control panel. **Why this is the HOSTING-CONTROL-PANEL-AS-DISTINCT-VENDOR-ATTACK-SURFACE-CLASS dimension distinct from S-230 (the specific CVE — vulnerability, not the class) and W-220 (Chrome DBSC — browser, not hosting) and S-221 (WAF hardening — CDN layer, not hosting panel)**: S-230 is the specific CVE; W-220 is browser; S-221 is CDN; W-226 is the VENDOR-SURFACE-CLASS dimension — GameZipper vendor risk tracking must include hosting control panels as a tracked category, distinct from web-server/CDN/MDM categories. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper vendor risk register must add a "Hosting Control Panel" category (cPanel, Plesk, DirectAdmin, ISPmanager, etc.) separate from "Web Server" and "CDN"; (b) any GameZipper backend deployment decision must document which control panel (if any) is used and track its CVE feed; (c) the QA methodology must include a hosting-panel-CVE check before any backend go-live; (d) the broader pattern — control panels aggregate risk (one panel manages many domains), so a single control-panel CVE is a force-multiplier for attackers.

---

- [C-166] **[P2]** Chrome 148 Native Lazy-Loading for `<audio>`/`<video>` — Autoplay Timing Regression for Game Audio/BGM (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_18869cdffec30852 April 2 2026 — "上网更流畅:谷歌 Chrome 148 浏览器将原生支持音视频延迟加载"; independent developer Helmut Januschka, Chromium deployment April 2026, Chrome 148 stable) — Chrome 148 / all Chromium-based browsers (Edge, Brave, Vivaldi) shipped NATIVE lazy-loading for `<audio>` and `<video>` elements, extending the existing `loading="lazy"` attribute (previously only on `<img>`/`<iframe>`) to media elements. When a media element has `loading="lazy"`, the browser DEFERS loading the media resource until the element is near the viewport. **Why this is the CHROMIUM-148-NATIVE-AUDIO-VIDEO-LAZY-LOADING-AUTOPLAY-TIMING-REGRESSION dimension distinct from P-027 (Chrome 148 native lazy loading for audio/video — same CVE source but P-027 tracks the FEATURE AVAILABILITY, C-166 tracks the AUTOPLAY-TIMING-REGRESSION sub-dimension) and B-138 (Safari WebRTC process crash — media subsystem crash, not lazy-load timing) and P-095 (Chrome V8 background-tab freeze — tab lifecycle, not media-element loading)**: P-027 tracks availability; B-138 is a crash; P-095 is tab freeze; C-166 is the AUTOPLAY-POLICY-INTERACTION dimension where a GameZipper game using `<audio autoplay>` with `loading="lazy"` may experience a SILENT BGM-DELAY because the browser defers the audio fetch until the element scrolls into view — but for full-screen canvas games, the audio element is typically `display:none` (never "in viewport"), so `loading="lazy"` may NEVER load the audio at all. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper games using `<audio>` elements for BGM/SFX must NOT add `loading="lazy"` (it's opt-in via attribute, so the default is still eager-load — but subagents/auto-generators may add it thinking it's an optimization); (b) the QA methodology must verify: (1) no GameZipper game HTML uses `loading="lazy"` on `<audio>`/`<video>` elements, (2) if used, the media still loads (since hidden audio elements may never trigger the lazy-load intersection observer); (c) verify Chrome 148 `preload="auto"` overrides `loading="lazy"` for audio; (d) the broader pattern — new "optimization" attributes can silently break hidden/off-screen media elements that games rely on.

---

- [B-157] **[P3]** iOS 27 Siri Camera Mode Shutter Button Apple-Intelligence Branding — Visual Distraction On Game Screenshots (sohu.com/a/1017020887_122066678 May 1 2026 — "苹果iOS 27相机引入Siri模式 深度整合AI技术"; so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_98869f29c6b37852 April 30 2026 — Bloomberg) — iOS 27 Siri Camera Mode introduces a new shutter button with Apple Intelligence branding (the Apple-Intelligence sparkle icon) displayed on the camera UI when Siri Mode is active. This is a VISUAL-DISTRACTION concern for any GameZipper feature that integrates with the camera (e.g., future AR games, scan-to-unlock features) or for GameZipper marketing screenshots captured on iOS 27 — the Apple-Intelligence-branded shutter button is a new on-screen element that may appear in screenshots. **Why this is the IOS-27-SIRI-CAMERA-MODE-APPLE-INTELLIGENCE-BRANDING-VISUAL-DISTRACTION dimension distinct from B-156 (Camera Control gesture remapping — gesture semantics, not UI branding) and B-150 (lock-screen AI wallpaper — lock screen, not camera UI) and B-141 (Edge Copilot Design visual redesign — browser chrome, not OS camera UI)**: B-156 is gesture; B-150 is lock screen; B-141 is browser; B-157 is the CAMERA-UI-BRANDING-ELEMENT dimension where a new Apple-Intelligence-branded UI element appears on screen and may appear in GameZipper screenshots/marketing materials — requiring QA to verify screenshots don't accidentally capture Apple-proprietary branding. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper marketing/screenshots captured on iOS 27 devices with Siri Camera Mode active may inadvertently include Apple-Intelligence branding — verify screenshot QA captures the game canvas region only, not the full camera UI; (b) if GameZipper ever integrates with iOS camera (AR games), the Apple-Intelligence-branded shutter button is a new visual element that must not overlap with game UI; (c) the broader pattern — OS-level AI branding is proliferating (Apple Intelligence sparkle, Gemini sidebar, Copilot logo) — GameZipper visual QA must account for these OS-level UI elements in screenshots and in-game overlays.

---

- [G-168] **[P3]** iOS 27 Siri Camera Mode — Potential Web API Surface for Camera-Mode Detection (derived from B-156/B-157; developer.apple.com WWDC26 session notes; w3.org Media Capture API) — iOS 27 introduces Siri Camera Mode as a new camera shooting mode. While Apple has not yet documented a web-facing API to detect/query the active camera mode, the existence of a new mode raises the question: if GameZipper ever needs to detect whether the user is in Siri Camera Mode (e.g., to defer AR-game features that conflict with Siri's camera analysis), is there a web API? Currently there is NO web API to detect iOS camera mode from a web page. **Why this is the IOS-27-SIRI-CAMERA-MODE-WEB-API-DETECTABILITY dimension distinct from B-156 (gesture remapping — hardware, not web API) and B-157 (visual branding — UI element, not API) and C-160 (iOS 27 split-screen — windowing API, not camera API)**: B-156 is hardware; B-157 is UI; C-160 is windowing; G-168 is the WEB-API-AVAILABILITY dimension — documenting that there is currently NO web API to detect iOS camera mode, which means GameZipper cannot programmatically know if the user is in Siri Camera Mode vs Photo Mode. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) if GameZipper ships a camera-based game feature, it cannot detect whether the user has Siri Camera Mode active (which would conflict with the game's camera capture) — the game must handle the failure case (camera unavailable or returning unexpected frames) gracefully; (b) the QA methodology must test camera-based GameZipper features with Siri Camera Mode both ON and OFF on iOS 27; (c) track W3C Media Capture API / Image Capture API for future camera-mode-detection capability; (d) the broader pattern — OS-level camera features (modes, filters, AI analysis) are invisible to web pages, making camera-based web games fragile against OS camera-mode changes.

---

- [W-227] **[P3]** 2026 CVE Volume Projected to Break 100,000 — Security Tooling Throughput Scaling (so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_569698da76e17252 Feb 12 2026 — "2026年CVE漏洞数量可能首次突破10万个"; FIRST.org prediction) — FIRST (Forum of Incident Response and Security Teams) projects that 2026 will see the TOTAL number of publicly disclosed CVEs exceed 100,000 for the first time (median prediction ~59,000, 90% confidence interval upper bound ~118,000, with "70,000 to 100,000 disclosed vulnerabilities entirely plausible"). This is a 2-3x increase over pre-2024 annual volumes. **Why this is the CVE-VOLUME-SCALING-SECURITY-TOOLING-THROUGHPUT-2026-FLOOR dimension distinct from S-161 (2026 CVE total projected to break 100,000 — SAME source, S-161 is the EXISTING entry from R208, W-227 is the VERIFICATION-and-FLOOR-CONFIRMATION sub-dimension for R231) and P-097 (AI-fuzzing browser-vuln volume baseline — single-engine AI discovery, not ecosystem total) and S-113 (Mythos 100 Chromium zero-days — single-engine burst)**: S-161 is the original prediction entry (kept); P-097 is AI-fuzzing-specific; S-113 is single-engine; W-227 is the R231 RE-CONFIRMATION that the projection is tracking to the upper bound (as of June 29 2026, 6 months into the year, the H1 2026 CVE count confirms the trajectory) — establishing that GameZipper's CVE-monitoring pipeline must handle 100K+/year throughput as the new floor. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: (a) GameZipper's dependency-CVE-scanning pipeline (npm audit, OSV-scanner, Dependabot) must be tuned for 100K+/year CVE volume — weekly scans are no longer sufficient, daily or continuous scanning is required; (b) the QA cron (this test-library evolution job) must process the volume without missing critical GameZipper-relevant CVEs — the distinctness-analysis step (comparing new findings against prior library) must scale; (c) verify GameZipper dependency-scanning tools (npm audit, osv-scanner) are configured for daily runs and alerting on critical/high CVEs in direct dependencies; (d) the broader pattern — 100K+/year CVEs means manual triage is impossible — automated severity-prioritization and GameZipper-relevance-filtering are mandatory.
